explanation: Use this to put a descriptive word or action word in front of a noun — like saying 'a small house' or 'a person who eats.' In English we just put adjectives before nouns, but Korean needs this ending to link them. Descriptive verbs (like 크다, 작다) take 은 or ㄴ. Action verbs (like 가다, 먹다) always take 는.

rules.title: Descriptive verb + consonant → + 은
rules.description: When the stem ends in a consonant, attach 은.

examples.english: 작은 고양이 (a small cat)
맛있는 음식 (delicious food)
추운 날씨 (cold weather)

rules.title: Descriptive verb + vowel → + ㄴ
rules.description: When the stem ends in a vowel, attach ㄴ directly.

examples.english: 큰 나무 (a big tree)
예쁜 꽃 (a pretty flower)
비싼 커피 (expensive coffee)

rules.title: Action verb → + 는 (always)
rules.description: Action verbs always take 는, no matter what the stem ends with.

examples.english: 가는 사람 (a person who is going)
먹는 것 (something you eat)
공부하는 학생 (a student who studies)

notes.title: descriptive vs. action
notes.text: If the word describes a quality (big, small, pretty), use 은/ㄴ. If it describes an action (go, eat, study), use 는. When in doubt, ask: can I do this? If yes, it's 는.

notes.title: Makes relative clauses
notes.text: This is how Korean creates 'who/which/that' clauses. 'The book that I read' = 내가 읽는 책. 'The friend who sings well' = 노래를 잘 하는 친구.

notes.title: Don't use with past/future meaning
notes.text: This only shows present meaning before a noun. For past-modified nouns, use -(으)ㄴ (different from present 은). For future-modified nouns, use -(으)ㄹ.

exceptions.name: 있다 / 없다
exceptions.description: These are descriptive verbs but they use 있는 / 없는, not 있은 / 없은. Think of them as special fixed forms.
exceptions.example: 돈이 있는 사람 (a person who has money), 시간이 없는 사람 (a person who has no time)

exceptions.name: ㄹ-ending stems
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ drop the ㄴ before adding 는.
exceptions.example: 만들다 → 만드는 것 (something you make), 살다 → 사는 집 (the house where someone lives)

explanation: This is the past tense. Attach it to any verb or descriptive verb to say something happened or was true before now. It's built from two parts: 았 (for ㅏ/ㅗ stems) or 었 (for everything else), plus an ending like -어요. 하다 verbs are special — they become 했.

rules.title: Stem with ㅏ or ㅗ → + 았
rules.description: If the last vowel in the stem is ㅏ or ㅗ, attach 았. Then add the appropriate ending (-어요, -어, etc.).

examples.english: 갔어요 — I went / he went.
좋았어요 — It was good.
만났어요 — I met (someone).

rules.title: Stem with other vowels → + 었
rules.description: For ㅓ, ㅜ, ㅡ, ㅣ, and all other vowels, attach 었.

examples.english: 먹었어요 — I ate.
마셨어요 — I drank.
썼어요 — I wrote.

rules.title: 하다 verbs → + 였 (했)
rules.description: All 하다 verbs contract to 했. This is the most common verb ending in Korean, so memorizing this pattern saves a lot of time.

examples.english: 공부했어요 — I studied.
요리했어요 — I cooked.
청소했어요 — I cleaned.

notes.title: Completion focus
notes.text: Past tense emphasizes the action is finished. If you want to say you 'used to' do something (but don't anymore), use -았/었/였었어요 instead.

notes.title: Experience is different
notes.text: To say you have experienced something before (e.g., 'I have been to Korea'), use -아/어 보다, not simple past tense. 가 봤어요 = I've been there (experience). 갔어요 = I went (specific past event).

notes.title: Don't overuse for states
notes.text: For states that continued in the past, like 'I was tired,' simple past is fine: 피곤했어요. But for ongoing past actions, use past progressive: 먹고 있었어요.

exceptions.name: 이다 → 이었/였
exceptions.description: The 'to be' verb 이다 becomes 이었어요 after consonants and 였어요 after vowels. In speech, 이었 often contracts to 였 after vowel-ending nouns.
exceptions.example: 학생이었어요 (I was a student), 의자였어요 (It was a chair)

exceptions.name: 아니다 → 아니었
exceptions.description: The negative 'to be' 아니다 becomes 아니었어요. This is completely regular once you know the pattern.

exceptions.name: 가다 / 오다 contraction
exceptions.description: 가 + 았 → 갔 and 오 + 았 → 왔 are common contractions you should memorize early.

explanation: Use this to talk about things that haven't happened yet — plans, predictions, or intentions. It's like saying 'I will...' or 'I'm going to...' in English. The core is 거예요 (polite), 거야 (casual), or 겁니다 (formal). The part before that is just the verb stem + 을/ㄹ.

rules.title: Verb stem + consonant → + 을 거예요
rules.description: When the verb stem ends in a consonant, attach 을 before 거예요.

examples.english: 먹을 거예요 — I'll eat. / I'm going to eat.
읽을 거예요 — I'll read it.
만들 거예요 — I'll make it.

rules.title: Verb stem + vowel → + ㄹ 거예요
rules.description: When the verb stem ends in a vowel, attach ㄹ directly before 거예요.

examples.english: 갈 거예요 — I'll go.
살 거예요 — I'll buy it.
볼 거예요 — I'll see / watch it.

rules.title: Speech level variants
rules.description: The politeness level changes the ending after 을/ㄹ, but the intention stays the same.

examples.english: Standard for daily conversation.
Use with close friends, younger people, or family.
Use in presentations, speeches, or very formal writing.

notes.title: Plans and predictions
notes.text: This form works for both definite plans (내일 한국에 갈 거예요 — I'm going to Korea tomorrow) and guesses (비가 올 거예요 — It will probably rain). Context tells the difference.

notes.title: Not for adjectives
notes.text: This future form is mainly for action verbs. For descriptive verbs in future meaning, use the plain present (since descriptions in future are often talked about in present tense in Korean) or 겠- for supposition.

notes.title: First-person intention
notes.text: When used with first person (I), it sounds like a plan or promise. With third person, it sounds like a prediction about someone else. With second person in a question, it's a soft invitation: 갈 거예요? (Will you go?).

exceptions.name: ㄹ-ending stems
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ before adding ㄹ 거예요. So you don't get double ㄹ.
exceptions.example: 만들다 → 만들 거예요 (I'll make), 살다 → 살 거예요 (I'll live)

exceptions.name: Irregular ㄷ verbs
exceptions.description: A few verbs ending in ㄷ change to ㄹ before adding this ending.
exceptions.example: 듣다 → 들을 거예요 (I'll listen), 걷다 → 걸을 거예요 (I'll walk)

explanation: 겠 is a versatile ending that sounds more formal and assertive than -(으)ㄹ 거예요. Use it when you want to make a polite offer, express strong intention, or guess what might happen. It's one syllable attached directly to the stem — no consonant/vowel splitting needed — which makes it simple to form.

rules.title: Attach 겠 directly to any stem
rules.description: No vowel harmony rules, no consonant checks. Just add 겠 and then your speech-level ending.

examples.english: 가겠습니다 — I shall go. (very formal/polite)
먹겠어요 — I'll eat. (polite, determined)
도와주겠습니다 — I'll help you. (offer)

rules.title: Common endings after 겠
rules.description: 겠 pairs with different endings depending on how formal or soft you want to sound.

examples.english: Formal promise or offer. The standard business/presentation form.
Polite but firm intention. Sounds more committed than 할 거예요.
Soft realization or guess. 'I guess it will...' / 'I see that it will...'

notes.title: The polite offer
notes.text: 겠습니다 is the go-to form for offering to do something in a polite way. At a restaurant: 제가 계산하겠습니다 (I'll pay). In a meeting: 제가 준비하겠습니다 (I'll prepare it). It sounds helpful and responsible.

notes.title: Supposition / guess
notes.text: When you observe something and make a guess, 겠네요 is natural. Looking at dark clouds: 비가 오겠네요 (I guess it's going to rain). Tasting food: 맛있겠네요 (This looks delicious — I bet it tastes good).

notes.title: Stronger than 거예요
notes.text: 겠 carries more weight. A promise with 겠 sounds more binding. An intention with 겠 sounds more definite. Don't use it casually for trivial things — it can sound oddly serious.

explanation: This is the progressive form — the Korean equivalent of '-ing' in English. It describes an action happening right now. Form it by adding -고 to the verb stem, then using the helper verb 있다 (to exist). You then conjugate 있다 into whatever politeness level you need.

rules.title: Verb stem + 고 + 있다 (conjugated)
rules.description: Attach 고 to the verb stem, then conjugate 있다. The most common form is 있어요.

examples.english: 먹고 있어요 — I'm eating (right now).
공부하고 있어요 — I'm studying.
일하고 있어요 — I'm working.

rules.title: Past progressive: 고 있었어요
rules.description: To say something was in progress in the past, conjugate 있다 to past tense.

examples.english: 자고 있었어요 — I was sleeping.
듣고 있었어요 — I was listening.

notes.title: Right now
notes.text: Use this when the action is in progress at the moment of speaking. 'What are you doing?' 'I'm reading.' — 뭐 해요? 책을 읽고 있어요.

notes.title: Wearing / putting on
notes.text: With verbs like 입다 (to wear), 신다 (to wear on feet), 쓰다 (to wear on head), 끼다 (to wear on hands), progressive describes a continuing state: 안경을 쓰고 있어요 means 'I'm wearing glasses' (not necessarily putting them on right now).

notes.title: Don't use with descriptive verbs
notes.text: You can't say 예쁘고 있어요. Descriptive verbs don't have progressive forms because they describe states, not ongoing actions.

exceptions.name: Posture verbs
exceptions.description: 앉다 (sit), 서다 (stand), 눕다 (lie down) can use progressive, but in modern Korean they often use the simple form to describe current posture. Both are acceptable.
exceptions.example: 앉아 있어요 = I'm seated. 앉고 있어요 = I'm in the process of sitting down. The nuance is slightly different.

exceptions.name: 가다 / 오다
exceptions.description: '가고 있어요' means 'I'm on my way.' This is a very common phrase in daily life.
exceptions.example: 지금 가고 있어요 — I'm on my way now.

explanation: This is the 'past-recollection' or 'past-past' form. It describes something that was true in the past but is no longer true now. Think of it as 'used to be' or 'had been.' You form it by taking the regular past tense and adding another layer of past on top.

rules.title: Past tense stem + 었/았/였었어요
rules.description: First make the regular past tense, then add 었/았/였었. The result emphasizes that the state existed in the past but has since changed.

examples.english: 학생이었었어요 — I used to be a student (but I'm not anymore).
많았었어요 — There used to be a lot (but now there isn't).
비가 왔었어요 — It had rained / It rained at some point before.

notes.title: Retrospective nuance
notes.text: This form always carries the feeling of looking back. Compare 갔어요 (I went — simple fact) vs 갔었어요 (I had gone / I went at some point — implies distance from now).

notes.title: Used to be
notes.text: For describing past states that no longer exist, this is the most natural form: 예뻤었어요 (She used to be pretty), 작았었어요 (It used to be small).

notes.title: Not for very recent events
notes.text: Using this for something that just happened sounds odd. Use simple past for recent events. Reserve this for things further back in time.

exceptions.name: 이다 double contraction
exceptions.description: 이다 becomes 이었었어요, which can feel long. In casual speech, people sometimes simplify but the full form is standard.
exceptions.example: 학생이었었어요 (I used to be a student)

explanation: This is the polite 'to be' for nouns — the Korean equivalent of 'is / am / are.' Attach 이에요 after nouns ending in a consonant, and 예요 after nouns ending in a vowel. It's the most common way to identify or describe something in daily conversation.

rules.title: Noun ending in consonant → + 이에요
rules.description: If the noun ends with a consonant sound, 이에요 is needed as a bridge.

examples.english: 책이에요 — It's a book.
학생이에요 — I'm a student. / She's a student.
한국 사람이에요 — I'm Korean.

rules.title: Noun ending in vowel → + 예요
rules.description: If the noun ends with a vowel sound, 예요 attaches directly without 이.

examples.english: 의자예요 — It's a chair.
사과예요 — It's an apple.
우산이에요 — It's an umbrella.

notes.title: 해요체 standard
notes.text: 이에요 / 예요 is the default polite form you'll use 90% of the time. Master this before worrying about formal or casual variants.

notes.title: Question form
notes.text: To ask a question, just raise your intonation: 학생이에요? (Are you a student?) No extra grammar needed.

notes.title: Only for nouns
notes.text: Never use 이에요 / 예요 with adjectives or action verbs. For verbs/adjectives, use their own sentence endings like -아/어요.

exceptions.name: 아니다 → 아니에요
exceptions.description: The negative form uses 아니에요 regardless of what came before. It doesn't follow the 이에요 / 예요 split.
exceptions.example: 학생이 아니에요 — I'm not a student.

explanation: These are casual forms of 'to be' for nouns. Use 이야 after consonant-ending nouns and 야 after vowel-ending nouns. (이)지 is the plain form used for emphasis or strong assertion — like saying 'It IS a book' rather than just 'It's a book.'

rules.title: Noun + consonant → 이야 / 이지
rules.description: When the noun ends in a consonant, 이 acts as a bridge.

examples.english: 책이야 — It's a book. (casual)
친구야 — It's a friend. / Hey friend!
시간이지 — It IS time. (assertive)

rules.title: Noun + vowel → 야 / 지
rules.description: When the noun ends in a vowel, attach directly.

examples.english: 의자야 — It's a chair. (casual)
우리 집이야 — It's my house.

notes.title: Casual speech only
notes.text: Use 이야 / 야 with close friends, family, and people younger than you. With strangers or superiors, stick to 이에요 / 예요.

notes.title: (이)지 for emphasis
notes.text: Adding 지 makes the statement stronger or self-evident. 맞지? means 'Right?' or 'Isn't it?' and expects agreement.

notes.title: Don't use in writing
notes.text: These forms are spoken-language only. For essays, news, or formal writing, use 이다 or 입니다.

explanation: This is the formal 'to be' for nouns. Use it in presentations, business meetings, speeches, news broadcasts, and any highly formal setting. Unlike 이에요 / 예요, 입니다 attaches directly to all nouns without any vowel/consonant splitting.

rules.title: Any noun + 입니다
rules.description: Attach 입니다 directly to the noun. No 받침 check needed.

examples.english: 학생입니다 — I am a student. (formal)
의자입니다 — It is a chair. (formal)
한국입니다 — It is Korea. (formal)

notes.title: Formal standard
notes.text: This is the standard for business Korean, public announcements, and academic presentations. If in doubt whether a situation is formal enough, using this is safer than being too casual.

notes.title: Question form: 입니까?
notes.text: For formal questions, replace 다 with 까: 학생입니까? (Are you a student?). This is the respectful way to ask in professional settings.

notes.title: Too stiff for friends
notes.text: Using 입니다 with close friends creates distance and can sound cold or sarcastic. Reserve it for professional or public contexts.

explanation: This is how you negate 'to be' in Korean. The structure is: noun + 이/가 아니다 (plain), 아니에요 (polite), or 아니야 (casual). 이/가 is the subject marker, and 아니다 is the negation word. Think of it as 'is not' broken into two parts.

rules.title: Noun + 이/가 아니다 / 아니에요 / 아니야
rules.description: Use 이/가 as the subject marker, then choose your speech level.

examples.english: 학생이 아니에요 — I'm not a student. (polite)
의자가 아니에요 — It's not a chair. (polite)
한국 사람이 아니야 — I'm not Korean. (casual)

notes.title: Always needs 이/가
notes.text: You cannot drop the subject marker. 학생 아니에요 is grammatically incomplete. Always use 학생이 아니에요 or 학생이 아닙니다.

notes.title: Formal: 아닙니다
notes.text: In formal speech, 아니다 becomes 아닙니다. 학생이 아닙니다 is the formal negation.

notes.title: Not for verbs/adjectives
notes.text: This negation is only for nouns (copula). For verbs, use 안 or -지 않다. For adjectives, use 안 or -지 않다.

exceptions.name: 아니에요 contraction
exceptions.description: In rapid speech, 아니에요 can sound like 아니에요 with a slight contraction, but write it fully.

explanation: This is the formal declarative ending for verbs and descriptive verbs. It elevates your speech to the highest politeness level (합쇼체). Use it in presentations, business, public speaking, and when addressing large groups. For stems with no 받침 (vowel ending), use ㅂ니다. For stems with a 받침 (consonant ending), use 습니다.

rules.title: Stem with no 받침 → + ㅂ니다
rules.description: When the verb or adjective stem ends in a vowel, attach ㅂ니다.

examples.english: 갑니다 — I go. / He goes. (formal)
옵니다 — I come. (formal)
큽니다 — It is big. (formal)

rules.title: Stem with 받침 → + 습니다
rules.description: When the stem ends in a consonant, attach 습니다.

examples.english: 먹습니다 — I eat. (formal)
작습니다 — It is small. (formal)
공부합니다 — I study. (formal)

notes.title: Formal standard
notes.text: This is the ending you hear in news broadcasts, official speeches, and business presentations. It shows maximum respect to the listener.

notes.title: 하다 verbs
notes.text: All 하다 verbs become 합니다. This is the most common verb type in Korean, so memorize this pattern early: 공부합니다, 요리합니다, 청소합니다.

notes.title: Too formal for daily chat
notes.text: Using this with friends or family creates excessive distance. In casual settings, use -아/어요 or -아/어 instead.

exceptions.name: ㄹ-ending stems
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ before adding ㅂ니다.
exceptions.example: 살다 → 삽니다 (I live), 만들다 → 만듭니다 (I make)

explanation: This is the formal question ending. It has the exact same structure as (스)ㅂ니다 but with 까 instead of 다. Use it to ask questions in professional or public contexts. It sounds respectful and appropriate for superiors, clients, or audiences.

rules.title: Stem with no 받침 → + ㅂ니까?
rules.description: For vowel-ending stems, attach ㅂ니까.

examples.english: 갑니까? — Are you going? (formal)
좋습니까? — Is it good? (formal)
먹습니까? — Do you eat it? (formal)

notes.title: Formal questions only
notes.text: This is the most polite way to ask a question. Use it with teachers, bosses, customers, or in any professional interaction.

notes.title: No tonal change needed
notes.text: Unlike 해요체 where questions rely on intonation, this form is unambiguously a question because of the 까 ending.

notes.title: Not for casual use
notes.text: Asking friends 갑니까? sounds comically stiff. With peers, use -아/어요 with rising intonation or -아/어? with friends.

explanation: These are honorific command and request forms. (으)세요 is the polite way to ask someone to do something — softer than a command, more like a request. (으)십시오 is a stronger formal command, used in instructions, signs, and formal directives. Both show respect to the person being addressed.

rules.title: Consonant ending → 으세요 / 으십시오
rules.description: When the stem ends in a consonant, add 으 as a bridge.

examples.english: 드세요 — Please eat. (honorific)
읽으십시오 — Please read. (formal command)

rules.title: Vowel ending → 세요 / 십시오
rules.description: When the stem ends in a vowel, attach directly.

examples.english: 가세요 — Please go. / Goodbye. (honorific)
오세요 — Please come. / Welcome. (honorific)
보세요 — Please look. / Here you go. (honorific)

notes.title: Honorific built in
notes.text: These forms automatically raise the listener's status. 가세요 is not just 'go' — it's 'please go (respectfully).' This is why 안녕히 가세요 means goodbye to someone leaving.

notes.title: 십시오 for signs and rules
notes.text: You'll see 십시오 on official signs: 입장하십시오 (Enter), 제출하십시오 (Submit). It's the standard written command form.

notes.title: Not for yourself
notes.text: You don't use this form to describe your own actions. It's only for telling or asking others to do something.

exceptions.name: Honorific verb substitution
exceptions.description: Some verbs have special honorific forms. 먹다 becomes 드세요 (not 먹으세요) when speaking to someone you respect. 마시다 becomes 드세요 as well.
exceptions.example: 드세요 (Please eat/drink — honorific), 주무세요 (Please sleep — honorific for 자다)

explanation: These are 'let's' forms. (으)ㅂ시다 is the formal/polite suggestion — use it with colleagues, strangers, or groups. 자 is the casual version — use it with close friends and people younger than you. Both mean 'Let's do X together.'

rules.title: Formal: consonant → 읍시다, vowel → ㅂ시다
rules.description: Attach 읍시다 after consonant-ending stems, ㅂ시다 after vowel-ending stems.

examples.english: 갑시다 — Let's go. (formal/polite)
먹읍시다 — Let's eat. (formal/polite)
공부합시다 — Let's study. (formal/polite)

rules.title: Casual: any stem + 자
rules.description: 자 attaches directly to any stem without modification.

examples.english: 가자 — Let's go. (casual)
먹자 — Let's eat. (casual)
보자 — Let's see. (casual)

notes.title: Inclusion
notes.text: Both forms include the speaker. You're not commanding someone to go — you're suggesting that you both go together.

notes.title: 갑시다 as encouragement
notes.text: In formal contexts, 갑시다 can also mean 'Let's get started' or 'Shall we begin?' — a polite way to move things forward.

notes.title: Not for boss to employee
notes.text: A boss wouldn't say 가자 to an employee — that's too casual. They'd use 가세요 (command/request) or 갑시다 (let's go together, implying teamwork).

explanation: This is the most important sentence ending in Korean. It's the polite present tense (해요체) — what you'll use in 90% of daily conversations. The ending changes based on vowel harmony: if the stem's last vowel is ㅏ or ㅗ, use 아요. For all other vowels, use 어요. 하다 verbs are special — they become 해요.

rules.title: Stem with ㅏ or ㅗ → + 아요
rules.description: When the last vowel is ㅏ or ㅗ, attach 아요.

examples.english: 가요 — I go. / He goes. (polite)
좋아요 — It's good. (polite)
사요 — I buy it. (polite)

rules.title: Stem with other vowels → + 어요
rules.description: For ㅓ, ㅜ, ㅡ, ㅣ, and all other vowels, use 어요.

examples.english: 먹어요 — I eat. (polite)
써요 — I write. (polite)
마셔요 — I drink. (polite)

rules.title: 하다 verbs → + 해요
rules.description: All 하다 verbs contract to 해요. This is extremely common.

examples.english: 공부해요 — I study. (polite)
요리해요 — I cook. (polite)
울어요 — I cry. (polite)

notes.title: Question = rising tone
notes.text: 가요 with falling intonation is a statement ('I go'). 가요? with rising intonation is a question ('Are you going?'). The grammar doesn't change — only your voice does.

notes.title: Command = stern tone
notes.text: With a firm, falling tone, -아/어요 can be a gentle command: 앉아요 ('Sit down' — polite but firm).

notes.title: Don't drop 요
notes.text: Dropping 요 makes it casual: 가 ('Go' — casual/command). Keep 요 for polite conversation.

exceptions.name: 가다 / 오다 contraction
exceptions.description: 가 + 아요 contracts to 가요 (not 가아요). 오 + 아요 contracts to 와요 (not 오아요). These are standard contractions.

exceptions.name: 하다 contraction
exceptions.description: All 하다 verbs become 해요. This is a fixed rule with no exceptions.

explanation: This is the casual speech ending — what you use with close friends, family, and people younger than you. It follows the exact same vowel harmony as -아/어요 (ㅏ/ㅗ → 아, others → 어, 하다 → 해), but without the 요. It can be a statement, a question (with rising tone), or a direct command.

rules.title: Same vowel harmony as -아/어요, minus 요
rules.description: Follow the same rules as polite -아/어요, just drop the 요 at the end.

examples.english: 가 — Go. / I go. (casual)
먹어 — Eat. / I eat. (casual)
공부해 — Study. / I study. (casual)

notes.title: Casual intimacy
notes.text: Using this form signals closeness. If someone uses it with you, they consider you a friend or junior. If you use it with a stranger, it can be rude.

notes.title: Questions with rising tone
notes.text: 가? (Going?), 먹어? (Eating?), 공부해? (Studying?). Same grammar, just raise your voice at the end.

notes.title: Direct commands
notes.text: Without 요, this sounds like an order. 앉아 ('Sit') is a command to a friend. 앉아요 ('Please sit') is polite. Be careful with the tone.

explanation: This is the plain declarative form (핵심 is 합쇼체 but this is 항라체... actually 항라체 is wrong, it's 항라체? No, it's 항라체/핵심체? Let me think. The plain form is called 항라체 or 항라체? Actually it's called 항라체 when written, but commonly referred to as the 'plain form' or 'dictionary form style.' For verbs, you add 는다. For descriptive verbs (adjectives), you typically just use the dictionary form ( stem + 다), but some grammars include adjectives under this pattern too.

rules.title: Action verbs → + 는다
rules.description: All action verbs take 는다 in the plain declarative form.

examples.english: 간다 — goes. (plain)
먹는다 — eats. (plain)
한다 — does. (plain)

rules.title: Descriptive verbs → dictionary form (stem + 다)
rules.description: Descriptive verbs in plain declarative typically just use their dictionary form.

examples.english: 크다 — is big. (plain)
작다 — is small. (plain)
예쁘다 — is pretty. (plain)

notes.title: Written language
notes.text: This form is standard in novels, newspapers, essays, and diary entries. It feels objective and neutral — the writer is stating facts without involving the reader emotionally.

notes.title: Plain speech to close friends
notes.text: Some men use this form in very casual spoken conversation with close male friends: 간다! ('I'm off!'), 먹는다 ('I'm eating'). It sounds blunt and masculine.

notes.title: Not for polite conversation
notes.text: Using this with strangers or superiors is extremely rude. It's either written-style or very casual spoken style among close peers.

exceptions.name: ㄹ-ending verbs
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ before adding 는다.

exceptions.name: 있다 / 없다
exceptions.description: These use 있다 / 없다 in plain form (same as dictionary form). They do not take 는다.

explanation: This is the plain quoted speech form. Use it to report what someone said, thought, or heard, in written or blunt spoken style. It's the plain-form equivalent of -아/어요 + quotative forms. For verbs, add 는다고. For descriptive verbs, add 다고 directly.

rules.title: Action verbs → + 는다고
rules.description: For action verbs, attach 는다고 to report speech in plain style.

examples.english: 간다고 — (someone) says they're going.
먹는다고 — (someone) says they're eating.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs → + 다고
rules.description: For descriptive verbs, attach 다 directly, then 고.

examples.english: 크다고 — (someone) says it's big.
좋다고 — (someone) says it's good.
아니라고 — (someone) says it's not.

notes.title: Indirect quotes
notes.text: This is the most common way to report speech in writing: 친구가 간다고 했다 (My friend said he's going). The structure is [subject] + [plain quoted verb] + 했다 / 말했다.

notes.title: Thoughts and perceptions
notes.text: You can also report thoughts and perceptions: 그렇다고 생각한다 (I think so), 좋다고 느낀다 (I feel that it's good).

notes.title: Don't use in polite speech
notes.text: In polite conversation, use -아/어요 + quotative forms instead: 간다고요? (Did you say he's going?). The plain form is too blunt for most spoken contexts.

exceptions.name: 아니다 → 아니라고
exceptions.description: The negation 아니다 becomes 아니라고 in quoted speech, not 아니다고.
exceptions.example: 아니라고 했어요 — They said no / They said it's not.

explanation: This is the casual command form — the blunt, direct way to tell someone to do something. It follows the same vowel harmony as -아/어 (ㅏ/ㅗ → 아라, others → 어라, 하다 → 항라), but adds 라 at the end. Use it with close friends, younger people, or in emergencies when politeness doesn't matter.

rules.title: ㅏ/ㅗ stem → + 아라
rules.description: When the stem's last vowel is ㅏ or ㅗ, attach 아라.

examples.english: 가라 — Go! (casual command)
봐라 — Look! (casual command)
앉아라 — Sit down! (casual command)

rules.title: Other vowels → + 어라
rules.description: For all other vowels, attach 어라.

examples.english: 먹어라 — Eat! (casual command)
들어라 — Listen! (casual command)
읽어라 — Read! (casual command)

rules.title: 하다 → 항라
rules.description: All 하다 verbs contract to 항라.

examples.english: 공부항라 — Study! (casual command)
일항라 — Work! (casual command)

notes.title: Parent to child
notes.text: This is the standard way parents speak to children: 먹어라, 가라, 공부항라. It sounds caring rather than harsh in a family context.

notes.title: Can sound aggressive
notes.text: Between adults who aren't close, this is a direct order and can sound confrontational. Use carefully outside of family or very close friends.

notes.title: Not for polite requests
notes.text: Never use this to ask a favor from a stranger or superior. For polite requests, use -아/어요 or -(으)세요.

exceptions.name: 가다 / 오다
exceptions.description: 가 + 아라 → 가라 (go), 오 + 아라 → 와라 (come). These are common contractions.

exceptions.name: 말다 → 마라
exceptions.description: The negative command 'don't' uses 마라: 가지 마라 (don't go), 먹지 마라 (don't eat).

explanation: This form expresses a first-person promise, decision, or offer. It's like saying 'I'll do it' or 'I've decided to do it.' It always implies the speaker is the one doing the action, and it carries a soft, cooperative tone. Think of it as making a voluntary commitment.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을게요
rules.description: When the stem ends in a consonant, attach 을게요.

examples.english: 먹을게요 — I'll eat. (promise/offer)
읽을게요 — I'll read it. (promise/offer)
볼게요 — I'll see. / I'll take care of it. (promise/offer)

rules.title: Vowel ending → + ㄹ게요
rules.description: When the stem ends in a vowel, attach ㄹ게요.

examples.english: 갈게요 — I'll go. (promise/offer)
도와줄게요 — I'll help you. (offer)
기다릴게요 — I'll wait. (promise)

notes.title: First person only
notes.text: You can only use this when YOU are the subject. You cannot say 'He will go' with 갈게요. For third person, use -(으)ㄹ 거예요 instead.

notes.title: Helpful and soft
notes.text: This form makes you sound considerate. When a friend is struggling, saying 제가 도와줄게요 ('I'll help you') sounds warmer than 제가 도와줄 거예요.

notes.title: Not a guess
notes.text: Unlike -(으)ㄹ 거예요 which can be a prediction, -(으)ㄹ게요 is always a deliberate decision or promise.

exceptions.name: ㄹ-ending stems
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ before adding ㄹ게요.
exceptions.example: 만들다 → 만들게요 (I'll make), 살다 → 살게요 (I'll live)

explanation: This form is used to make soft suggestions or ask about someone's intention. It's casual and friendly — like saying 'Do you want to...?' or 'Shall we...?' The 요 version is polite-casual, and without 요 it's purely casual.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을래요 / 을래
rules.description: When the stem ends in a consonant, attach 을래요 or 을래.

examples.english: 먹을래요? — Do you want to eat? / Shall we eat?
갈래? — Wanna go? (casual)

rules.title: Vowel ending → + ㄹ래요 / ㄹ래
rules.description: When the stem ends in a vowel, attach ㄹ래요 or ㄹ래.

examples.english: 볼래요? — Do you want to see it?
마실래? — Wanna drink? (casual)

notes.title: Friendly suggestion
notes.text: This is perfect for asking friends to hang out: 영화 볼래? (Wanna see a movie?), 밥 먹을래? (Wanna grab food?).

notes.title: Self-question too
notes.text: You can also use it to ask yourself: 뭐 먹을까? What should I eat? → 뭐 먹을래? What do I feel like eating?

notes.title: Not for formal settings
notes.text: This is too casual for business or formal situations. With superiors, use -(으)실래요? (extremely rare) or rephrase with -(으)ㄹ까요?.

exceptions.name: 하다 verbs
exceptions.description: All 하다 verbs become 할래요 / 할래.

explanation: This versatile form can be a suggestion ('Shall we...?'), a self-directed question ('I wonder if I should...'), or a soft question to someone else. It's polite but not as formal as 습니까. The 요 version is standard polite; without 요 it's casual.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을까요 / 을까
rules.description: When the stem ends in a consonant, attach 을까요 or 을까.

examples.english: 먹을까요? — Shall we eat? / Should I eat it?
갈까? — Should I go? (casual)

rules.title: Vowel ending → + ㄹ까요 / ㄹ까
rules.description: When the stem ends in a vowel, attach ㄹ까요 or ㄹ까.

examples.english: 볼까요? — Shall we see? / Should I look?
주문할까요? — Shall I order?

notes.title: Three meanings in one
notes.text: Context determines the meaning. 같이 갈까요? with a friend = 'Shall we go together?' 갈까? to yourself = 'Should I go?' 엄마, 이거 먹을까요? = 'Mom, should I eat this?'

notes.title: Soft and indecisive
notes.text: This form sounds thoughtful and unhurried. It's less pushy than a direct command. Perfect for when you want to be polite but not too formal.

notes.title: Not for facts
notes.text: Don't use this for things that are objectively true. 'Is this a book?' is 이거 책이에요? or 이거 책입니까? — never 이거 책일까요?

exceptions.name: 하다 verbs
exceptions.description: All 하다 verbs become 할까요 / 할까.
exceptions.example: 공부할까요? (Shall we study?), 전화할까? (Should I call?)

explanation: This is the plain-form future marker. It's the written-style equivalent of -(으)ㄹ 거예요. Use it in essays, novels, news articles, and objective statements. 거다 is a contracted version of 것이다 — you'll see both, but 거다 is more common in spoken plain style while 것이다 is more formal and written.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 것이다 / 을 거다
rules.description: When the stem ends in a consonant, attach 을 before 것이다 or 거다.

examples.english: 먹을 것이다 — He will eat. (plain/formal written)
읽을 거다 — I'll read it. (plain/spoken blunt)

rules.title: Vowel ending → + ㄹ 것이다 / ㄹ 거다
rules.description: When the stem ends in a vowel, attach ㄹ directly.

examples.english: 갈 것이다 — He will go. (plain/formal written)
올 거다 — He'll come. (plain/spoken blunt)

notes.title: Objective prediction
notes.text: In news and essays, this sounds neutral and factual: 날씨가 좋을 것이다 (The weather will be good). It removes the speaker's personal involvement.

notes.title: 거다 in casual speech
notes.text: Men sometimes use 거다 in very casual conversation with friends: 간다고 했는데 안 올 거야 (He said he's coming but he probably won't).

notes.title: Not polite
notes.text: This is either written-style or blunt spoken style. For polite conversation, always use -(으)ㄹ 거예요.

exceptions.name: 하다 verbs
exceptions.description: All 하다 verbs become 할 것이다 / 할 거다.
exceptions.example: 공부할 것이다 (will study), 요리할 거다 (will cook)

explanation: This form sets up a background situation and then contrasts it with what follows. 텐데(요) softens a statement and implies 'I was going to... but' or 'It would... but.' 테니(까) gives a reason: 'Since I will..., [you should too].' Both come from 터 (a plan/intention) + ㄴ/은데 or 니까.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 텐데(요) / 을 테니(까)
rules.description: When the stem ends in a consonant, attach 을 before 텐데 or 테니까.

examples.english: 갈 텐데... — I would go, but... / I was going to go, but...
먹을 테니까 기다려요 — I'll eat, so please wait.

rules.title: Vowel ending → + ㄹ 텐데(요) / ㄹ 테니(까)
rules.description: When the stem ends in a vowel, attach ㄹ directly.

examples.english: 비가 올 텐데 우산을 가져가세요 — It looks like it'll rain, so take an umbrella.
피곤할 텐데 일찍 자세요 — You must be tired, so go to bed early.

notes.title: 텐데 = soft background
notes.text: Use 텐데 to provide context before making a request or statement: 내일 갈 텐데 같이 갈래? (I'm planning to go tomorrow — wanna come?). It softens the invitation.

notes.title: 테니까 = reason/cause
notes.text: Use 테니까 when your intention is the reason for what follows: 제가 할 테니까 걱정 마세요 (I'll do it, so don't worry).

notes.title: Advanced nuance
notes.text: This is slightly advanced for pure beginners. It combines future meaning with contrastive or causal connectors. Master basic -아/어요 and -(으)ㄹ 거예요 first.

explanation: This form expresses that you're considering or thinking about doing something, but haven't decided yet. It's tentative and soft — perfect for when you want to mention a possible plan without committing to it. 싶다 adds a layer of personal feeling ('I feel like I might...').

rules.title: Stem + (으)ㄹ까 하다
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ까 to the stem, then add 하다. You're literally saying 'I'm doing the act of thinking about whether to...'

examples.english: 한국에 갈까 해요 — I'm thinking of going to Korea.
커피를 마실까 해요 — I'm thinking of having coffee.

rules.title: Stem + (으)ㄹ까 싶다
rules.description: Similar meaning but with 싶다 (to want/feel like), making it more subjective and emotional.

examples.english: 집에 일찍 갈까 싶어요 — I feel like going home early.
그 영화를 볼까 싶어요 — I feel like watching that movie.

notes.title: Tentative plans
notes.text: Use this when you want to mention a plan without sounding definite: 주말에 쇼핑을 할까 해요 (I'm thinking of shopping this weekend). It leaves room for change.

notes.title: 싶다 is more emotional
notes.text: 할까 싶다 sounds more like a whim or feeling, while 할까 하다 sounds more like a deliberate consideration. Both are soft and uncommitted.

notes.title: Not for definite plans
notes.text: If you've already decided, use -(으)ㄹ 거예요 or -(으)ㄹ게요 instead. This form explicitly signals uncertainty.

explanation: This form adds the possibility that something 'might' happen or 'may be' true. 수 means 'ability/way,' 도 means 'also/even,' and 있다 means 'exists.' Together: 'there is also a way that...' It's softer and more tentative than a direct statement.

rules.title: Stem + (으)ㄹ 수도 있다
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ 수도 있다 to any verb or descriptive verb stem.

examples.english: 비가 올 수도 있어요 — It might rain.
늦을 수도 있어요 — I might be late.
좋을 수도 있어요 — It might be good.

notes.title: Soft possibility
notes.text: Use this when you want to suggest something without being pushy. 'It might be good' (좋을 수도 있어요) is much softer than 'It's good' (좋아요).

notes.title: Speculation
notes.text: Perfect for guessing: 그 사람이 올 수도 있어요 (That person might come). It shows you're not certain.

notes.title: Not for ability
notes.text: Don't confuse this with -(으)ㄹ 수 있다 (can/be able to). 수도 adds 'also/maybe,' changing the meaning from ability to possibility.

explanation: This is a strong negation of possibility. 리가 없다 literally means 'there is no reason/logic for it.' Use it when you want to say 'no way,' 'impossible,' or 'there's no chance.' It's much stronger than a simple 'no' and often expresses disbelief or strong disagreement.

rules.title: Stem + (으)ㄹ 리가 없다
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ 리가 없다 to any verb or descriptive verb stem.

examples.english: 그런 일이 일어날 리가 없어요 — There's no way that would happen.

examples.breakdown: 그 사람이 거짓말을 할 리가 없어요
examples.english: 그 사람이 거짓말을 할 리가 없어요 — He would never lie. / There's no way he'd lie.
비가 올 리가 없어요 — There's no way it'll rain. (strong disbelief)

notes.title: Strong disbelief
notes.text: Use this when you're confident something is NOT true: 내가 잘못 본 리가 없어 (There's no way I saw wrong). It shows strong conviction.

notes.title: Defending someone
notes.text: When someone accuses your friend, you can defend them: 걔가 그럴 리가 없어 (There's no way he'd do that).

notes.title: Can sound argumentative
notes.text: In a debate, this can sound forceful. Use with care in professional settings where you want to stay diplomatic.

explanation: This form describes a near-miss — something that almost happened but didn't. It's like saying 'I almost fell' or 'I nearly forgot.' Use it when you want to express relief that a bad outcome was narrowly avoided. The feeling is usually 'whew, that was close.'

rules.title: Verb stem + (으)ㄹ 뻔하다
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ 뻔하다 to a verb stem. The subject is usually the speaker, expressing their own near-miss.

examples.english: 넘어질 뻔했어요 — I almost fell.
잊을 뻔했어요 — I almost forgot.
늦을 뻔했어요 — I was almost late.

notes.title: Relief and surprise
notes.text: This always carries a sense of relief. You almost experienced something negative, but you didn't. It's a common topic in casual conversation.

notes.title: Past tense only
notes.text: 뻔하다 is almost always used in past tense (뻔했어요) because you're talking about something that nearly happened. Present or future usage is extremely rare.

notes.title: Negative events only
notes.text: You don't use this for positive near-misses. 'I almost won' is technically possible but sounds odd. Stick to bad things that were avoided.

exceptions.name: 뻔했다 contraction
exceptions.description: In casual speech, 뻔했어요 is standard. 뻔했다 is the plain form. Both are common.
exceptions.example: 죽을 뻔했어요 (I almost died — exaggeration for 'that was scary')

explanation: This is the quickest way to say 'not' in Korean. Just put 안 directly in front of a descriptive verb or action verb. It's short, casual, and extremely common in daily conversation. Use it when you want to negate something simply and directly.

rules.title: 안 + verb or descriptive verb
rules.description: Place 안 immediately before the word you want to negate. No conjugation needed.

examples.english: 안 가요 — I'm not going.
안 먹어요 — I don't eat it. / I'm not eating it.
안 좋아요 — It's not good.
안 예뻐요 — It's not pretty.

notes.title: Most common negation
notes.text: This is what you'll hear most often in spoken Korean. It's faster than -지 않다 and sounds natural in almost all casual and polite contexts.

notes.title: Can modify nouns too
notes.text: In casual speech, 안 can sometimes modify noun phrases: 안 그래요? (Isn't that so?), 안 돼요 (It doesn't work / No way).

notes.title: Don't use with nouns (copula)
notes.text: You cannot say 안 학생이에요. For noun negation, use 아니에요: 학생이 아니에요 (I'm not a student).

exceptions.name: 있다 / 없다
exceptions.description: You cannot say 안 있다. Use 없다 instead, which already means 'does not exist.' 안 없다 is a double negative and incorrect.
exceptions.example: 없어요 (There isn't any / I don't have it)

exceptions.name: 알다 / 모르다
exceptions.description: You can say 안 알아요 in some casual contexts, but 몰라요 (I don't know) using 모르다 is more natural.
exceptions.example: 몰라요 (I don't know) — more common than 안 알아요

explanation: Use 못 to express inability or impossibility — 'cannot' rather than 'do not.' It means you want to do something but you can't, either because of circumstances, lack of skill, or external obstacles. Place it directly before the verb, just like 안.

rules.title: 못 + action verb
rules.description: Place 못 immediately before the action verb. It only works with action verbs, not descriptive verbs.

examples.english: 못 가요 — I can't go. (circumstances prevent it)
못 먹어요 — I can't eat it. (allergy, diet, skill)
못 해요 — I can't do it. (lack of ability)
못 만나요 — I can't meet (them). (scheduling conflict)

notes.title: Cannot vs. do not
notes.text: 못 가요 = I can't go (I want to but something stops me). 안 가요 = I'm not going (I choose not to). This distinction is crucial in Korean.

notes.title: External obstacles
notes.text: Use 못 when the reason is outside your control: 비가 와서 못 갔어요 (I couldn't go because it rained). For internal choice, use 안.

notes.title: Not for descriptive verbs
notes.text: You cannot say 못 좋아요. Descriptive verbs describe states, not actions, so inability doesn't apply. Use 안 좋아요 instead.

exceptions.name: 모르다
exceptions.description: While 못 알아요 is technically possible, 모르다 already means 'cannot know.' Just use 몰라요.
exceptions.example: 몰라요 (I don't know / I can't know)

exceptions.name: 알다 with 못
exceptions.description: In some contexts, 못 알아들었어요 means 'I couldn't understand (what I heard).' This emphasizes the failure to comprehend, not refusal.
exceptions.example: 빨리 말해서 못 알아들었어요 (You spoke fast so I couldn't understand)

explanation: This is the long negation form. While 안 is quick and casual, -지 않다 sounds slightly more formal, literary, or emphatic. It's also necessary when you want to add extra endings after the negation, since 안 can't take additional grammar. Attach -지 않다 directly to the verb or descriptive verb stem.

rules.title: Verb/descriptive verb stem + 지 않다
rules.description: Attach 지 않다 directly to any stem. Then conjugate 않다 like a normal descriptive verb.

examples.english: 가지 않아요 — I do not go. (slightly formal)
먹지 않아요 — I do not eat it. (neutral/formal)
좋지 않아요 — It is not good. (slightly formal)
예쁘지 않아요 — It is not pretty. (neutral)

notes.title: More formal than 안
notes.text: In business emails, presentations, or formal writing, -지 않아요 is preferred over 안. It sounds more composed and less blunt.

notes.title: Can add extra grammar
notes.text: Because -지 않다 is a full verb form, you can attach more endings to it: 가지 않았어요 (didn't go), 가지 않을 거예요 (won't go), 가지 않아도 돼요 (don't have to go).

notes.title: Don't overuse in casual chat
notes.text: With close friends, 안 가 is natural. 가지 않아 sounds stiff and bookish. Reserve -지 않다 for formal situations or when you need extra grammar attached.

exceptions.name: 알다 → 알지 못하다 (not 않다)
exceptions.description: For 알다 (to know), the natural negative is 모르다, not 알지 않다. 알지 않다 exists but sounds very stiff.
exceptions.example: 몰라요 (I don't know) — natural. 알지 않아요 — awkward/stiff.

explanation: This is the long-form way to say 'cannot.' It's the more formal and complete version of 못 + verb. Use it in writing, formal speech, or when you want to sound more deliberate. It emphasizes that ability or circumstance is lacking.

rules.title: Verb stem + 지 못하다
rules.description: Attach 지 못하다 to any action verb stem. Then conjugate 못하다 like a normal verb.

examples.english: 가지 못해요 — I am unable to go. (formal)
먹지 못해요 — I am unable to eat it. (formal)
만나지 못했어요 — I was unable to meet them. (formal/polite)

notes.title: Formal inability
notes.text: This is what you'd write in a formal apology: 시간이 없어서 참석하지 못했습니다 (I was unable to attend due to lack of time). It sounds more regretful than 못 갔어요.

notes.title: Can attach extra endings
notes.text: Like -지 않다, this form can take additional grammar: 하지 못할 거예요 (won't be able to do it), 하지 못하게 됐어요 (became unable to do it).

notes.title: Don't use with descriptive verbs
notes.text: Just like 못, -지 못하다 only works with action verbs. For descriptive verbs, use -지 않다 instead.

exceptions.name: 모르다
exceptions.description: 모르다 already contains the meaning of 'cannot know.' Using 알지 못하다 is possible in very formal writing but 모르다 is standard.
exceptions.example: 알지 못했다 (formal written: did not know) vs 몰랐다 (natural spoken/written)

explanation: This is the casual negative command — the direct way to tell someone not to do something. Add 지 마 to any verb stem for a casual prohibition, or 지 마라 for a stronger, more forceful command. Use these only with close friends, younger people, or in situations where politeness is not expected.

rules.title: Verb stem + 지 마 / 지 마라
rules.description: Attach 지 마 for casual 'don't,' or 지 마라 for stronger/commanding 'don't.'

examples.english: 가지 마 — Don't go. (casual)
먹지 마 — Don't eat it. (casual)
하지 마라 — Don't do it. (strong/command)
울지 마 — Don't cry. (casual, comforting)

notes.title: Standard casual prohibition
notes.text: Between friends, 가지마 and 먹지마 are completely natural. They sound direct but not necessarily rude if the relationship is close.

notes.title: 마라 sounds harsher
notes.text: 지 마라 is more forceful and commanding. A parent might say 하지 마라 to a child. Between peers, it can sound confrontational.

notes.title: Never to superiors
notes.text: Using this with a boss, teacher, or elder is extremely disrespectful. For polite negative commands, use -지 마세요 or -지 말아요.

explanation: These are the polite and formal ways to tell someone not to do something. -지 마세요 is the standard polite prohibition — use it with strangers, elders, or anyone you want to show respect to. -지 맙시다 is the formal 'let's not' — use it in business or public settings to suggest a group should refrain from something.

rules.title: Verb stem + 지 마세요
rules.description: The honorific polite negative command. Appropriate for most everyday situations where you need to be respectful.

examples.english: 가지 마세요 — Please don't go. (polite)
먹지 마세요 — Please don't eat it. (polite)
만지지 마세요 — Please don't touch it. (polite)

rules.title: Verb stem + 지 맙시다
rules.description: The formal 'let's not' — suggests that the group (including speaker) should refrain.

examples.english: 가지 맙시다 — Let's not go. (formal)
걱정하지 맙시다 — Let's not worry. (formal)

notes.title: 마세요 is standard polite
notes.text: This is what you see on signs and hear in public announcements: 만지지 마세요 (Do not touch), 사진 찍지 마세요 (No photography). It's firm but respectful.

notes.title: 맙시다 for group decisions
notes.text: In a meeting, someone might say 그만하지 맙시다 (Let's not stop yet) or 그렇게 하지 맙시다 (Let's not do it that way). It includes the speaker in the prohibition.

notes.title: Not for casual friends
notes.text: With close friends, -지 마세요 sounds comically stiff. Use -지 마 instead. Save -지 마세요 for strangers, elders, and public contexts.

explanation: This is a softer, more gentle way to say 'don't' in polite speech. It sits between the blunt casual -지 마 and the formal -지 마세요. Use it when you want to be polite but not overly stiff — for example, with acquaintances or in semi-formal situations where -지 마세요 feels too strong.

rules.title: Verb stem + 지 말아요
rules.description: Attach 지 말아요 to any verb stem. This form is less common in textbooks but widely used in real-life polite conversation.

examples.english: 가지 말아요 — Please don't go. (soft polite)
먹지 말아요 — Please don't eat it. (soft polite)
걱정하지 말아요 — Please don't worry. (gentle)

notes.title: Gentle and soft
notes.text: This sounds warmer than -지 마세요. A mother might say 걱정하지 말아 to her adult child — caring but not commanding.

notes.title: Semi-formal sweet spot
notes.text: Use this when you want to be polite but intimate. With someone you know but aren't close to, -지 말아요 sounds friendlier than -지 마세요.

notes.title: Less common in writing
notes.text: You'll hear this more than you'll see it written. Textbooks often skip it in favor of -지 마세요, but native speakers use it naturally in spoken polite contexts.

explanation: This form asks for or gives permission. It's like saying 'Is it okay if I...?' or 'You may...' The core meaning is 'even if you do X, it's fine.' Use it to check if something is allowed, or to reassure someone that their action is acceptable.

rules.title: Verb/descriptive verb + 아/어도 괜찮다 / 되다
rules.description: Attach 아/어도 to the stem, then add 괜찮다 (it's okay) or 되다 (it's allowed/fine).

examples.english: 가도 괜찮아요 — It's okay to go. / You may go.
먹어도 돼요 — It's okay to eat. / You may eat it.
늦어도 괜찮아요 — It's okay to be late.
작아도 돼요 — It's okay if it's small.

notes.title: Asking permission
notes.text: Raise your intonation to ask: 먹어도 돼요? (Is it okay to eat?). This is the polite way to check before doing something.

notes.title: Giving reassurance
notes.text: Use the statement form to reassure someone: 걱정하지 않아도 괜찮아요 (It's okay, don't worry). 아파도 괜찮아요 (It's okay even if it hurts — e.g., the pain is manageable).

notes.title: Don't confuse with obligation
notes.text: -아/어도 돼요 means 'may' (permission). -(아/어)야 돼요 means 'must' (obligation). Be careful: 가도 돼요 = you may go. 가야 돼요 = you must go.

exceptions.name: 하다 verbs
exceptions.description: All 하다 verbs become 항라도 괜찮아요 / 항라도 돼요.
exceptions.example: 공부항라도 돼요 (It's okay to study), 쇼핑항라도 괜찮아요 (It's okay to shop)

explanation: This form expresses prohibition or asks whether something is allowed. -(으)면 안 되다 means 'if you do X, it's not okay' — in other words, you must not do X. -(으)면 돼요? with rising intonation asks 'Is it okay if...?' Use these to set boundaries or check rules.

rules.title: Stem + (으)면 안 되다
rules.description: Attach (으)면 안 되다 to prohibit something. 'If you do X, it won't work / it's not allowed.'

examples.english: 가면 안 돼요 — You must not go. / Don't go.
먹으면 안 돼요 — You must not eat it.
만지면 안 돼요 — You must not touch it.

rules.title: Stem + (으)면 돼요?
rules.description: With rising intonation, this asks for permission: 'Is it okay if I do X?'

examples.english: 가면 돼요? — Is it okay if I go?
먹으면 돼요? — Is it okay if I eat it?
여기에 앉으면 돼요? — Is it okay if I sit here?

notes.title: Strong prohibition
notes.text: -(으)면 안 돼요 is stronger than -지 마세요. It implies there will be consequences: 여기서 smoking 하면 안 돼요 (You can't smoke here — it's against the rules).

notes.title: Casual variant: -(으)면 안 돼
notes.text: Drop the 요 for casual speech: 가면 안 돼 (You can't go), 먹으면 안 돼 (Don't eat it). This is common between friends.

notes.title: Don't use for invitations
notes.text: Never say 가면 안 돼요? to invite someone. That means 'Is it not okay to go?' which is confusing. For invitations, use -(으)ㄹ까요? or -(으)ㄹ래요?

exceptions.name: 되다 contractions
exceptions.description: 안 돼요 (It doesn't work / No way) and 돼요 (It works / It's okay) are extremely common standalone expressions.
exceptions.example: 안 돼요! (No! / Don't do it!), 돼요? (Is it okay? / Does it work?)

explanation: This is a stronger, more emphatic prohibition than -(으)면 안 되다. The -서는 adds emphasis: 'Doing X is absolutely not acceptable.' Use it when you want to be very clear about boundaries, rules, or strong advice. It sounds more serious and deliberate.

rules.title: Verb/descriptive verb + 아/어서는 안 되다
rules.description: Attach 아/어서는 to the stem, then add 안 되다. The -서는 emphasizes that the action itself is the problem.

examples.english: 가서는 안 돼요 — You absolutely must not go. (strong)
먹어서는 안 돼요 — You absolutely must not eat it. (strong)
늦어서는 안 돼요 — You absolutely must not be late. (strong)

notes.title: Emphatic prohibition
notes.text: Use this when the rule is serious: 거짓말해서는 안 돼요 (You must not lie). It carries more weight than 거짓말하면 안 돼요.

notes.title: Parental scolding
notes.text: Parents use this to emphasize rules: 그렇게 하면 안 돼 (Don't do that) vs 그렇게 해서는 안 돼 (You absolutely must not do that). The second is sterner.

notes.title: Too strong for casual requests
notes.text: Don't use this for minor requests among friends. It sounds like you're setting a hard rule. Use -지 마 or -(으)면 안 돼 instead.

explanation: 은/는 is the topic particle. It tells the listener what you're talking about — the main theme of your sentence. Think of it as 'as for...' or 'speaking of...' It doesn't mark the grammatical subject (that's 이/가). Instead, it marks what the sentence is ABOUT. Use 은 after consonant-ending nouns, 는 after vowel-ending nouns.

rules.title: Noun + consonant → + 은
rules.description: Attach 은 when the noun ends in a consonant.

examples.english: 책은 — As for the book...
학생은 — As for the student...
한국은 — As for Korea...

rules.title: Noun + vowel → + 는
rules.description: Attach 는 when the noun ends in a vowel.

examples.english: 의자는 — As for the chair...
사과는 — As for the apple...
우산은 — As for the umbrella...

notes.title: Topic vs. subject
notes.text: 저는 학생입니다 (As for me, I'm a student) — 는 marks the topic, 이 is the subject marker in 이다. The topic is what you're discussing; the subject is what does the action or has the property.

notes.title: Contrast and emphasis
notes.text: 은/는 creates contrast: 저는 커피를 좋아해요 (I like coffee — implying others might not, or as for me...). It adds a nuance of comparison.

notes.title: Don't overuse in lists
notes.text: In simple lists, don't mark every item with 은/는. Use it for the main topic, then use other particles for details within the sentence.

exceptions.name: 저는 / 나는
exceptions.description: First-person pronouns almost always take 는/은. 저는 is polite; 나는 is casual. These are among the first words every learner memorizes.

explanation: 이/가 is the subject particle. It marks WHO or WHAT is doing the action or possessing the property in a sentence. Use 이 after consonant-ending nouns, 가 after vowel-ending nouns. If you're not sure whether to use 은/는 or 이/가, ask: is this word the doer/haver of something? If yes, use 이/가.

rules.title: Noun + consonant → + 이
rules.description: Attach 이 when the noun ends in a consonant.

examples.english: 책이 — The book [does/is something].
학생이 — The student [does/is something].
비가 와요 — It's raining. (rain is the subject)

rules.title: Noun + vowel → + 가
rules.description: Attach 가 when the noun ends in a vowel.

examples.english: 의자가 커요 — The chair is big.
사과가 맛있어요 — The apple is delicious.
누가 왔어요? — Who came?

notes.title: New information
notes.text: 이/가 often introduces new information into the conversation. When you first mention something, use 이/가. Once it's established as the topic, switch to 은/는.

notes.title: Existence and possession
notes.text: 이/가 is required for 있다/없다/많다/적다: 책이 있어요 (There is a book / I have a book). You cannot say 책은 있어요 in standard Korean.

notes.title: Don't double-mark
notes.text: A noun cannot have both 은/는 and 이/가. Choose one: 책은 or 책이, never 책은이.

exceptions.name: 누가
exceptions.description: 누구 (who) + 가 contracts to 누가. This is the only form — you never say 누구가.

explanation: 을/를 is the object particle. It marks the noun that receives the action of the verb — the thing being eaten, seen, bought, or loved. Use 을 after consonant-ending nouns, 를 after vowel-ending nouns. Every transitive verb (a verb that acts on something) needs an object marked with 을/를.

rules.title: Noun + consonant → + 을
rules.description: Attach 을 when the noun ends in a consonant.

examples.english: 책을 읽어요 — I read a book.
밥을 먹어요 — I eat rice.
한국어를 공부해요 — I study Korean.

rules.title: Noun + vowel → + 를
rules.description: Attach 를 when the noun ends in a vowel.

examples.english: 사과를 사요 — I buy apples.
커피를 마셔요 — I drink coffee.
영화를 봐요 — I watch a movie.

notes.title: Transitive verbs need objects
notes.text: Verbs like 먹다 (eat), 마시다 (drink), 보다 (see), 사다 (buy), 읽다 (read) all need objects. You cannot just say 먹어요 without context — you need to specify WHAT you're eating.

notes.title: Drop in casual speech
notes.text: In very casual speech, Koreans sometimes drop 을/를: 밥 먹었어? (Did you eat?). But as a learner, always use it — dropping particles requires native intuition.

notes.title: Not for subjects
notes.text: Never use 을/를 for the doer of the action. The subject gets 이/가 or 은/는. 을/를 is only for the target of the action.

exceptions.name: 가다 / 오다 are intransitive
exceptions.description: These verbs don't take objects because you don't 'go something.' You go TO a place (marked with 에/로). Saying 집를 가요 is wrong — say 집에 가요.
exceptions.example: 집에 가요 (I go home) — 에 marks direction, not 을/를.

explanation: 의 is the possessive particle — the Korean equivalent of apostrophe-s ('s) in English. It shows that one noun belongs to or is associated with another noun. Unlike 은/는/이/가, 의 has only one form and never changes.

rules.title: Possessor + 의 + possessed
rules.description: Place 의 between the owner and the thing owned. The order is always owner → 의 → owned thing.

examples.english: 저의 책 — My book.
학생의 가방 — The student's bag.
한국의 음식 — Korean food. / Korea's food.

notes.title: No vowel/consonant split
notes.text: 의 never changes form. It doesn't matter if the noun before it ends in a consonant or vowel — it's always 의.

notes.title: Can be dropped in speech
notes.text: In casual conversation, Koreans often drop 의: 내 책 (my book) instead of 나의 책, 우리 집 (our house) instead of 우리의 집. But write it fully in formal contexts.

notes.title: Don't confuse with 이
notes.text: 의 (possessive) looks similar to 이 (subject particle) but they're completely different. 의 is always about belonging or association.

exceptions.name: 저의 / 제
exceptions.description: 저의 contracts to 제 in humble speech: 제 책 (my book). This is more polite than 저의 책.
exceptions.example: 제 이름은... (My name is...) — standard self-introduction.

exceptions.name: 나의 / 내
exceptions.description: 나의 contracts to 내 in casual speech: 내 친구 (my friend), 내 집 (my house).

exceptions.name: 우리의 / 우리
exceptions.description: 우리 often drops 의 even in writing: 우리 학교 (our school), 우리 나라 (our country).
exceptions.example: 우리 가족 (our family) — no 의 needed.

explanation: 도 means 'also,' 'too,' or 'even.' It adds the meaning of inclusion — the noun before it is included in whatever was just mentioned. Unlike English where 'too' goes at the end, Korean 도 attaches directly to the noun. It replaces other particles (은/는, 이/가, 을/를) rather than stacking on top of them.

rules.title: Noun + 도
rules.description: Attach 도 directly to the noun. It replaces the particle that noun would normally have.

examples.english: 저도 — Me too.
커피도 — Coffee too. / Even coffee.
한국도 — Korea too. / Even Korea.
오늘도 — Today too. / Again today.

notes.title: Replaces other particles
notes.text: You cannot say 저는도 or 저를도. 도 takes the place of the original particle. Compare 저는 갔어요 (I went) → 저도 갔어요 (I went too).

notes.title: Even / as much as
notes.text: In negative contexts, 도 can mean 'even': 커피도 없어요 (There's not even coffee). This adds emphasis — not just no milk, but not even coffee.

notes.title: Not for verbs directly
notes.text: You cannot attach 도 directly to a verb stem. For 'I eat too,' you need 저도 먹어요 — the 도 is on the subject, not the verb.

exceptions.name: 누구도 / 아묵도
exceptions.description: 누구 + 도 = 누구도 (anyone / no one, depending on context). With negatives, it means 'no one': 누구도 없어요 (There's no one).
exceptions.example: 아무것도 몰라요 (I don't know anything) — 아무것 + 도.

explanation: 에 is the location/time particle. It marks WHERE something is (static location) or WHEN something happens (time). Think of it as 'at,' 'in,' 'on,' or 'to' depending on context. It does NOT mark the location where an action happens — that's 에서.

rules.title: Place + 에 = static location
rules.description: Use 에 to say something exists at a place, or to mark a destination you're going toward.

examples.english: 집에 있어요 — I'm at home. / It's at home.
학교에 가요 — I go to school.
서울에 살아요 — I live in Seoul.

rules.title: Time + 에 = when
rules.description: Use 에 to mark when something happens.

examples.english: 오늘에? — Rare! 오늘 is usually used without 에.
3시에 만나요 — Let's meet at 3 o'clock.
월요일에 시작해요 — It starts on Monday.

notes.title: Destination marker
notes.text: 에 marks where you're going: 집에 가요 (going home), 한국에 왔어요 (came to Korea). The movement is TOWARD the place.

notes.title: Existence
notes.text: For 있다/없다, use 에 to mark location: 집에 있어요 (at home), 가방 안에 있어요 (inside the bag).

notes.title: Not for action location
notes.text: If you're describing where an action TAKES PLACE (not where you're going), use 에서 instead. Compare 학교에 가요 (going to school) vs 학교에서 공부해요 (studying at school).

exceptions.name: Time words without 에
exceptions.description: Some time words don't need 에: 오늘 (today), 내일 (tomorrow), 어제 (yesterday), 지금 (now). Saying 오늘에 is unnatural.
exceptions.example: 오늘 만나요 (Let's meet today) — no 에 needed.

exceptions.name: 에 / 로 for direction
exceptions.description: Some destinations take 로 instead of 에: 집으로 가요 (going toward home) emphasizes direction. Both are acceptable in most cases.
exceptions.example: 학교에 가요 = 학교로 가요 (both mean going to school)

explanation: 에서 marks the location WHERE an action takes place. If 에 answers 'where to?' then 에서 answers 'where at?' Use it when you're describing studying at school, working at an office, eating at a restaurant, or any action that happens AT a location. It can also mean 'from' when describing origin.

rules.title: Location + 에서 = action happens there
rules.description: Use 에서 to mark the place where an action is performed.

examples.english: 학교에서 공부해요 — I study at school.
집에서 일해요 — I work at home.
카페에서 커피를 마셔요 — I drink coffee at a cafe.

rules.title: Location + 에서 = from
rules.description: When describing movement away from a place, 에서 means 'from.'

examples.english: 한국에서 왔어요 — I came from Korea.
집에서 출발했어요 — I departed from home.

notes.title: 에 vs. 에서
notes.text: This is the most important particle distinction for beginners. 에 = destination / static location. 에서 = action location / origin. If there's a verb happening AT the place, use 에서.

notes.title: From + to pattern
notes.text: When describing a journey, use 에서 for departure and 에/로 for arrival: 집에서 학교에 가요 (I go from home to school).

notes.title: Not for existence
notes.text: Don't use 에서 with 있다/없다. Say 집에 있어요 (at home), not 집에서 있어요. Existence uses 에, actions use 에서.

exceptions.name: 에서 → 에게서 / 한테서
exceptions.description: When the starting point is a person, use 에게서 or 한테서 instead of 에서: 친구에게서 편지를 받았어요 (I got a letter from a friend).
exceptions.example: 선생님한테서 들었어요 (I heard it from the teacher)

explanation: 에게 and 한테 both mean 'to' when directing an action toward a person. 에게 is slightly more formal and standard; 한테 is casual and very common in spoken Korean. Use them when giving something to someone, talking to someone, or directing any action toward a person.

rules.title: Person + 에게 / 한테
rules.description: Attach 에게 or 한테 to the person who receives the action.

examples.english: 친구에게 줬어요 — I gave it to a friend.
엄마한테 말했어요 — I told Mom.
선생님에게 물어봤어요 — I asked the teacher.

notes.title: 에게 is standard
notes.text: 에게 works in all contexts — formal writing, essays, polite conversation. It's the safe choice when you're unsure.

notes.title: 한테 is casual
notes.text: 한테 is what you hear in daily conversation: 누구한테 들었어? (Who did you hear it from?). It's friendly and natural but slightly less formal.

notes.title: Only for people
notes.text: Don't use 에게/한테 for places or things. For objects and locations, use 에: 책상에 놓았어요 (I put it on the desk), not 책상에게.

exceptions.name: Honorific: 께
exceptions.description: For people you deeply respect (grandparents, president, CEO), use 께 instead of 에게: 할머니께 드렸어요 (I gave it to Grandma — honorific).
exceptions.example: 선생님께 여쭤봤어요 (I asked the teacher — honorific)

explanation: 에게서 and 한테서 mean 'from' when the source is a person. Use them when receiving something, hearing something, or getting an action from someone. 에게서 is standard; 한테서 is casual. Think of them as the opposite direction of 에게/한테 — those mean 'to a person,' these mean 'from a person.'

rules.title: Person + 에게서 / 한테서
rules.description: Attach 에게서 or 한테서 to the person who is the source.

examples.english: 친구에게서 받았어요 — I received it from a friend.
엄마한테서 들었어요 — I heard it from Mom.
선생님에게서 배웠어요 — I learned it from the teacher.

notes.title: From people only
notes.text: These particles are specifically for human sources. For non-human origins (countries, stores), use 에서: 한국에서 왔어요 (from Korea), 가게에서 샀어요 (from a store).

notes.title: 에게서 vs. 에서
notes.text: Some Koreans use 에서 even for people in casual speech: 친구에서 들었어. This is common but technically less precise. 에게서/한테서 is grammatically correct.

notes.title: Don't use for things
notes.text: A book cannot give you something. Don't say 책에게서 배웠어요. Say 책에서 배웠어요 (I learned it from a book) — using 에서 for non-human sources.

explanation: 와/과 means 'and' or 'with' in formal and written contexts. It connects nouns together or marks accompaniment. Use 과 after consonant-ending nouns, 와 after vowel-ending nouns. It's the standard form for essays, news, business documents, and formal speech.

rules.title: Noun + consonant → + 과
rules.description: Attach 과 when the noun ends in a consonant.

examples.english: 책과 펜 — A book and a pen.
한국과 일본 — Korea and Japan.
친구와 같이 — Together with a friend.

rules.title: Noun + vowel → + 와
rules.description: Attach 와 when the noun ends in a vowel.

examples.english: 의자와 책상 — A chair and a desk.
엄마와 살아요 — I live with Mom.
사과와 바나나 — Apples and bananas.

notes.title: Formal and written
notes.text: This is what you see in news headlines, academic papers, and formal speeches. It sounds precise and educated.

notes.title: With = accompaniment
notes.text: When 와/과 means 'with,' it usually appears with 같이 (together): 친구와 같이 갔어요 (I went with a friend). Without 같이, it can mean 'and' or 'with' depending on context.

notes.title: Too stiff for friends
notes.text: Saying 친구과 밥을 먹었어요 to a friend sounds weirdly formal. Use 하거나 이랑 instead in casual contexts.

explanation: 하고 means 'and' or 'with' in casual spoken Korean. It's the everyday equivalent of 와/과. Use it when listing things, describing who you did something with, or connecting nouns in conversation. It has only one form and never changes.

rules.title: Noun + 하고
rules.description: Attach 하고 to any noun. No consonant/vowel split needed.

examples.english: 친구하고 놀았어요 — I hung out with a friend.
밥하고 김치 — Rice and kimchi.
엄마하고 쇼핑했어요 — I went shopping with Mom.

notes.title: Most common 'and'
notes.text: In spoken Korean, 하고 is what you'll hear most often. It's natural, flexible, and works in almost any casual context.

notes.title: Can list multiple items
notes.text: You can chain 하고 multiple times: 사과하고 바나나하고 딸기 — apples, bananas, AND strawberries. Koreans use this naturally in speech.

notes.title: Not for formal writing
notes.text: Avoid 하고 in essays, business reports, or news articles. Use 와/과 for formal contexts. 하고 can make your writing look childish or sloppy.

explanation: (이)랑 is the most casual way to say 'and' or 'with.' It's even more informal than 하고 and is common among children, close friends, and family. Use 이랑 after consonant-ending nouns, 랑 after vowel-ending nouns. It has a cute, friendly, slightly childish feeling.

rules.title: Noun + consonant → + 이랑
rules.description: Attach 이랑 when the noun ends in a consonant.

examples.english: 책이랑 공책 — A book and a notebook. (very casual)
친구랑 놀았어 — I played with a friend. (very casual)

rules.title: Noun + vowel → + 랑
rules.description: Attach 랑 when the noun ends in a vowel.

examples.english: 엄마랑 갔어 — I went with Mom. (very casual)
사과랑 바나나 — Apples and bananas. (very casual)

notes.title: Very casual
notes.text: This is what children use and what adults use when being playful or extremely relaxed. It has a warm, intimate feeling.

notes.title: Cute connotation
notes.text: Women sometimes use 랑 to sound cute or friendly: 나랑 갈래? (Wanna go with me?). Men can use it too with very close friends.

notes.title: Avoid in professional contexts
notes.text: Using 랑 in a job interview or business email is a serious mistake. It sounds childish and unprofessional. Stick to 와/과 in formal settings.

explanation: 처럼 means 'like' or 'as if' — it compares one thing to another. Use it when you want to say someone or something resembles another person or thing in appearance, behavior, or quality. It creates similes.

rules.title: Noun + 처럼
rules.description: Attach 처럼 to the thing being compared to. The noun before 처럼 is the model; the subject of the sentence is what resembles it.

examples.english: 엄마처럼 예뻐요 — She's pretty like Mom.
새처럼 날아요 — It flies like a bird.
꿈처럼 느껴요 — It feels like a dream.

notes.title: Similes
notes.text: 처럼 is your go-to for comparisons: 바람처럼 빨라요 (Fast like the wind), 아이처럼 웃어요 (Smiles like a child).

notes.title: Behavior and manner
notes.text: You can describe how someone acts: 선생님처럼 말해요 (Speaks like a teacher). This focuses on manner, not just appearance.

notes.title: Not for exact equality
notes.text: 처럼 means 'similar to,' not 'exactly the same as.' For exact equality, use 같이 or 같다.

explanation: 같이 has two main meanings: (1) 'like' — similar to 처럼 but more about equality or shared quality; (2) 'together' — doing something with someone. Context tells you which meaning is intended. It's one of the most versatile particles in Korean.

rules.title: Noun + 같이 = like / as
rules.description: When meaning 'like,' 같이 compares things more strongly than 처럼 — it suggests shared properties or exact similarity.

examples.english: 형 같이 생겼어요 — He looks exactly like his older brother.
한국 사람 같이 말해요 — Speaks like a Korean.

rules.title: Noun + 같이 = together
rules.description: When meaning 'together,' 같이 indicates joint action.

examples.english: 친구하고/와 같이 갔어요 — I went together with a friend.
우리 같이 가요 — Let's go together.

notes.title: Two meanings, one particle
notes.text: 같이 is ambiguous without context. 우리 같이 밥을 먹어요 can mean 'We eat rice together' OR 'We eat rice like each other.' Usually context makes it clear.

notes.title: 같이 vs. 처럼
notes.text: 같이 suggests stronger similarity or equality. 형 같이 = 'exactly like his brother.' 형처럼 = 'similar to his brother.' The difference is subtle but important.

notes.title: Don't confuse with 같은
notes.text: 같은 is an adjective meaning 'same' that comes BEFORE a noun: 같은 책 (the same book). 같이 is a particle/adverb that comes AFTER a noun.

explanation: 보다 means 'than' — it introduces the standard of comparison. Use it when comparing two things: 'A is bigger THAN B,' 'A is more expensive THAN B.' The noun before 보다 is the thing being compared against (the baseline).

rules.title: Baseline + 보다
rules.description: Attach 보다 to the noun that serves as the comparison baseline. The subject of the sentence exceeds or falls short of this baseline.

examples.english: 한국보다 커요 — It's bigger than Korea.
어제보다 추워요 — It's colder than yesterday.
친구보다 잘해요 — Does it better than a friend.

notes.title: Comparative standard
notes.text: The word before 보다 is always the baseline: 나보다 키가 커요 (Taller than me). 나 is the baseline; someone else is taller.

notes.title: 더 / 훨씬 / 많이
notes.text: Add 더 (more), 훨씬 (much more), or 많이 (a lot) before the adjective for emphasis: 어제보다 훨씬 추워요 (Much colder than yesterday).

notes.title: No verb change needed
notes.text: Unlike English which adds -er or 'more,' Korean adjectives don't change form for comparisons. You just add 보다 to the baseline noun.

exceptions.name: 보다도 for emphasis
exceptions.description: Adding 도 after 보다 strengthens the comparison: 생각보다도 쉬워요 (Easier than I thought — emphasizing the gap).

explanation: 만 means 'only' — it restricts the noun to nothing else. When you add 만 to a noun, you're saying 'just this one, nothing else.' It replaces other particles (like 도 does) rather than stacking on top of them.

rules.title: Noun + 만
rules.description: Attach 만 to any noun. It replaces whatever particle that noun would normally have.

examples.english: 저만 — Only me.
커피만 — Only coffee.
오늘만 — Only today.
한 번만 — Just once.

notes.title: Exclusive focus
notes.text: 만 narrows the scope: 저만 알아요 (Only I know). Others don't know. This creates a feeling of exclusivity.

notes.title: Replaces particles
notes.text: Like 도, 만 replaces other particles. You cannot say 저는만 or 저를만. 저만 is correct.

notes.title: Not for 'only' as an adverb
notes.text: For 'only' modifying a verb (not a noun), use -기만 하다 or 그냥: 그냥 가요 (I'm just going). 만 only attaches to nouns.

exceptions.name: 만 with numbers
exceptions.description: 만 is very common with quantities: 두 개만 (only two), 한 번만 (just once), 오늘만 (just today).
exceptions.example: 물 한 잔만 주세요 (Just a glass of water, please)

explanation: 밖에 means 'nothing but' or 'only' — but it ALWAYS requires a negative verb. It expresses that nothing else exists or is possible except the noun before it. The feeling is stronger and more restrictive than 만. Think of it as 'there is nothing EXCEPT...'

rules.title: Noun + 밖에 + negative verb
rules.description: Attach 밖에 to a noun, then use a negative verb (없다, 안 되다, 못 하다, 모르다, etc.).

examples.english: 커피밖에 없어요 — There's nothing but coffee. / Only coffee exists.
한국어밖에 몰라요 — I only know Korean. (I don't know anything else)
오늘밖에 안 돼요 — Only today works. (Other days don't work)

notes.title: Must use negative verb
notes.text: 밖에 without a negative verb is grammatically wrong. Say 커피밖에 없어요 (correct), not 커피밖에 있어요 (wrong). The negative is required.

notes.title: Stronger than 만
notes.text: 밖에 feels more dramatic and absolute. 커피만 마셔요 (I only drink coffee — my preference). 커피밖에 못 마셔요 (I can drink nothing but coffee — restriction/limitation).

notes.title: Not for positive statements
notes.text: You cannot use 밖에 to say 'I only like this' in a positive sense. For positive exclusivity, use 만: 저만 좋아해요 (I only like it).

exceptions.name: 밖에 vs. 만
exceptions.description: This is a classic learner confusion point. 만 works with positive and negative verbs. 밖에 ONLY works with negative verbs and sounds more absolute.
exceptions.example: 물만 있어요 (There's only water — neutral). 물밖에 없어요 (There's nothing but water — dramatic/exclusive).

explanation: 까지 means 'until,' 'to,' or 'even.' It marks the endpoint of a range, the extent of something, or adds emphasis by including something unexpected. Use it for time (until 5 o'clock), place (to Seoul), or emphasis (even me).

rules.title: Time/place + 까지 = until / to
rules.description: Use 까지 to mark the endpoint of a time period or the destination of movement.

examples.english: 5시까지 — Until 5 o'clock.
서울까지 — To Seoul. / As far as Seoul.
내일까지 — By tomorrow.

rules.title: Noun + 까지 = even
rules.description: Use 까지 to emphasize inclusion of something unexpected.

examples.english: 저까지 — Even me.
아이까지 — Even a child.
여기까지 — Up to here. / Even here.

notes.title: From... until pattern
notes.text: Use 부터 for starting point, 까지 for ending point: 월요일부터 금요일까지 (From Monday until Friday). This pair is inseparable in Korean.

notes.title: Even = emphasis
notes.text: 까지 for emphasis adds surprise: 그 사람은 돈까지 줬어요 (That person even gave money). It highlights that something extra or unexpected happened.

notes.title: Not for starting point
notes.text: Don't use 까지 for starting points. 'From Monday' is 월요일부터, never 월요일까지. 까지 is always the END of something.

exceptions.name: 까지도 for extra emphasis
exceptions.description: Adding 도 after 까지 strengthens the 'even' meaning: 저까지도 몰라요 (Even I don't know — emphasizing that my knowledge is limited too).
exceptions.example: 아이까지도 할 수 있어요 (Even a child can do it)

explanation: 부터 marks the starting point of a time period, place, or sequence. It's the partner of 까지 (until). Use it to say 'from Monday,' 'from Seoul,' or 'from the beginning.' It establishes where something begins.

rules.title: Time/place/sequence + 부터
rules.description: Attach 부터 to mark the beginning point.

examples.english: 월요일부터 — From Monday.
지금부터 — From now.
처음부터 — From the beginning.
여기부터 — From here.

notes.title: Partner of 까지
notes.text: 부터 and 까지 are a fixed pair. You almost never see one without the other in formal contexts: 9시부터 5시까지 (From 9 to 5).

notes.title: Sequence order
notes.text: Use 부터 to establish order: 저부터 할게요 (I'll go first — literally 'from me'). This is common in group activities.

notes.title: Not for destination
notes.text: Don't use 부터 for endpoints. 'To Seoul' is 서울까지 or 서울로, never 서울부터. 부터 is always the START.

explanation: 마다 means 'every.' It distributes the action across all instances of the noun. Use it with time words (every day), places (every country), or any noun where you want to express repetition or universality.

rules.title: Noun + 마다
rules.description: Attach 마다 to any noun to mean 'every [noun].'

examples.english: 날마다 — Every day.
주마다 — Every week.
사람마다 — Every person. / Each person.
나라마다 — Every country.

notes.title: Repetition
notes.text: 마다 emphasizes that something happens repeatedly: 매일 울어요 (I cry every day) vs 날마다 울어요 (Day after day, I cry). 날마다 feels more literary.

notes.title: Diversity
notes.text: 마다 can express variety: 사람마다 다르다 (Different for each person). 나라마다 문화가 달라요 (Every country has different culture).

notes.title: Not for 'each' as a quantity
notes.text: For 'each' as a specific quantity (each apple = 100 won), use 씩: 사과 한 개씩 (one apple each). 마다 is about repetition across instances, not individual distribution.

exceptions.name: 매 + time word
exceptions.description: Instead of 날마다, Koreans more commonly say 매일 (every day), 매주 (every week), 매년 (every year). These 매- compounds are more natural in daily speech.
exceptions.example: 매일 아침 (every morning), 매주 월요일 (every Monday)

explanation: (이)나 has multiple meanings: (1) 'or' — listing alternatives; (2) 'as much as' / 'even' — emphasizing a surprisingly large amount. The context tells you which meaning applies. Use 이나 after consonant-ending nouns, 나 after vowel-ending nouns.

rules.title: Noun + (이)나 = or
rules.description: List alternatives. The noun before (이)나 is one option among others.

examples.english: 커피나 차 — Coffee or tea.
월요일이나 화요일 — Monday or Tuesday.
집이나 학교 — Home or school.

rules.title: Noun + (이)나 = as much as / even
rules.description: Emphasize that the amount or degree is surprisingly large.

examples.english: 한 시간이나 걸렸어요 — It took as much as an hour. (surprisingly long)
열 개나 샀어요 — I bought as many as ten. (surprisingly many)

notes.title: Or in lists
notes.text: (이)나 is the standard 'or' for nouns: 밥이나 빵 (rice or bread). For verbs, use -거나: 먹거나 마시다 (eat or drink).

notes.title: Surprising quantity
notes.text: When (이)나 means 'as much as,' it always carries surprise: 커피만 마셔요 (I only drink coffee — preference). 커피를 세 잔이나 마셨어요 (I drank as many as three cups of coffee — surprise at the amount).

notes.title: Don't confuse with 밖에
notes.text: (이)나 with large quantities is POSITIVE surprise. 밖에 with negatives is RESTRICTION. They look similar in function but have opposite emotional tones.

exceptions.name: (이)나 with minimal quantities
