exceptions.description: Surprisingly, (이)나 can also emphasize that something is surprisingly small or easy: 한 번이나 항봤어요 (I tried even once — sarcastic / disappointed tone). Context is everything.
exceptions.example: 그것도 모륶나? (You don't even know that?)

explanation: (이)나마 means 'even if only' or 'at least.' It expresses that although the noun isn't ideal, it's better than nothing. The tone is resigned but grateful — like saying 'I'll take what I can get.' Use it when you want to acknowledge that something modest is still worthwhile.

rules.title: Noun + (이)나마
rules.description: Attach 이나마 after consonant-ending nouns, 나마 after vowel-ending nouns.

examples.english: 잠이나마 잤어요 — I slept at least a little. / Even if only sleep, I got some.
물이나마 마셔요 — Drink at least some water.
시간이나마 있어서 다행이에요 — I'm glad we have at least some time.

notes.title: Better than nothing
notes.text: (이)나마 always carries the feeling of settling for less. 커피가 없어서 물이나마 마셨어요 (There was no coffee, so I drank water at least).

notes.title: Gratitude in hardship
notes.text: In difficult situations, (이)나마 softens the blow: 돈이 없어도 건강이나마 있어요 (Even if I have no money, at least I have my health).

notes.title: Not for optimistic contexts
notes.text: Don't use (이)나마 when things are genuinely good. Saying 맛있는 음식이나마 먹었어요 sounds odd — just say 맛있는 음식을 먹었어요. (이)나마 implies compromise.

explanation: 만큼 means 'as much as' — it compares quantities or degrees. Use it when you want to say someone has as much of something as someone else, or an action happens to the same degree. It creates balanced comparisons.

rules.title: Noun + 만큼
rules.description: Attach 만큼 to the standard of comparison. The subject matches this standard in quantity or degree.

examples.english: 형만큼 커요 — He's as big as his older brother.
어제만큼 추워요 — It's as cold as yesterday.
생각만큼 쉬워요 — It's as easy as I thought.

notes.title: Equal comparison
notes.text: 만큼 creates equality: 나만큼 잘해요 (Does it as well as me). This is different from 보다 (than), which creates inequality.

notes.title: Degree and extent
notes.text: You can describe how much something matches expectations: 기대만큼 좋아요 (It's as good as expected). 예상만큼 빨라요 (It's as fast as expected).

notes.title: Not for 'as much as' with surprise
notes.text: For 'as many as' with surprise (emphasizing a large number), use (이)나: 열 개나 샀어요 (I bought as many as ten). 만큼 is neutral comparison, not surprise.

exceptions.name: 만큼 with verbs
exceptions.description: You can use 만큼 with verb stems by adding ㄴ/은: 하는 만큼 (as much as one does), 먹은 만큼 (as much as one ate).
exceptions.example: 먹은 만큼 살쪄요 (You gain weight as much as you eat)

explanation: 께 is the honorific version of 에게. Use it when the recipient of an action is someone you deeply respect — grandparents, parents (in very formal situations), teachers, bosses, or anyone of significantly higher status. It elevates the recipient to a position of honor.

rules.title: Honorific person + 께
rules.description: Attach 께 to the respected person who receives the action.

examples.english: 할머니께 드렸어요 — I gave it to Grandma. (honorific)
선생님께 여쭤봤어요 — I asked the teacher. (honorific)
사장님께 말씀드렸어요 — I spoke to the CEO. (honorific)

notes.title: Maximum respect
notes.text: 께 is the highest level of respect for the recipient. Using it shows deep deference. In business, using 께 with a client can make a very positive impression.

notes.title: Pair with honorific verbs
notes.text: 께 naturally pairs with honorific verbs: 드리다 (give), 여쭈다 (ask), 말씀드리다 (speak). Don't use 줬어요 with 께 — say 드렸어요 instead.

notes.title: Don't overuse
notes.text: Using 께 for peers or casual acquaintances sounds excessive and can be awkward. Reserve it for people who genuinely deserve elevated respect.

explanation: 께서 is the honorific subject particle. It's the respectful version of 이/가. Use it when the subject of the sentence — the person doing the action or possessing the quality — is someone you deeply respect. It elevates the subject's status in the sentence.

rules.title: Honorific person + 께서
rules.description: Attach 께서 to a respected person who is the subject of the sentence.

examples.english: 할머니께서 오셨어요 — Grandma has come. (honorific)
선생님께서 가르쳐 주셨어요 — The teacher taught me. (honorific)
사장님께서 말씀하셨어요 — The CEO spoke. (honorific)

notes.title: Honorific subject
notes.text: 께서 marks that the subject is worthy of respect. The verb that follows should also be honorific: 오셨어요 (came — honorific), not 왔어요.

notes.title: Can combine with 도
notes.text: You can say 께서도 to mean 'even [respected person]': 선생님께서도 모르셨어요 (Even the teacher didn't know).

notes.title: Not for yourself
notes.text: Never use 께서 for yourself. It's only for people you respect. For yourself, use 이/가 or 저는.

explanation: (으)로 has two main meanings: (1) direction/toward — indicating where something is headed; (2) means/method — indicating how something is done. Use 로 after vowel-ending nouns, 으로 after consonant-ending nouns. It's a versatile particle that covers both physical direction and abstract methods.

rules.title: Direction: noun + (으)로
rules.description: Mark the direction something is moving toward.

examples.english: 학교로 가요 — I go toward school.
집으로 왔어요 — I came toward home.
오른쪽으로 도세요 — Turn to the right.

rules.title: Means/method: noun + (으)로
rules.description: Mark the tool, method, or material used to do something.

examples.english: 버스로 가요 — I go by bus.
한국어로 말해요 — I speak in Korean.
현금으로 샀어요 — I bought it with cash.

notes.title: Direction emphasis
notes.text: (으)로 emphasizes the direction of movement more than 에. 학교에 가요 (going to school — destination). 학교로 가요 (heading toward school — direction). The difference is subtle.

notes.title: Material and ingredient
notes.text: (으)로 can describe what something is made of: 나무로 만들었어요 (Made of wood), 금으로 된 반지 (A ring made of gold).

notes.title: ㄹ-ending nouns
notes.text: Nouns ending in ㄹ usually take 로, not 으로: 서울로 (to Seoul), not 서을으로. This is a standard exception.

exceptions.name: ㄹ-ending nouns
exceptions.description: Nouns ending in ㄹ take 로, not 으로: 서울로, 길로, 저울로.

explanation: 에다(가) means 'onto' or 'in addition to.' Use it when you're putting something ON a surface or adding something to an existing thing. The (가) is optional and often dropped in speech. It emphasizes the surface or location where something is being added or placed.

rules.title: Surface/location + 에다(가)
rules.description: Mark the surface or place where something is put or added.

examples.english: 책상에다가 놓았어요 — I put it on the desk. (emphasizing the surface)
종이에다가 썼어요 — I wrote on the paper.
빵에다가 버터를 발랐어요 — I spread butter on the bread.

notes.title: Surface emphasis
notes.text: 에다(가) emphasizes that something is being placed ON or INTO something else. 책상에 놓았어요 (I put it on the desk) is neutral. 책상에다가 놓았어요 emphasizes the act of placing it on that specific surface.

notes.title: Adding to existing things
notes.text: Use 에다(가) when adding to something that already exists: 밥에다가 김치를 올렸어요 (I put kimchi on top of the rice).

notes.title: Not for destinations
notes.text: Don't use 에다(가) for movement destinations. 'I'm going to school' is 학교에 가요 or 학교로 가요, never 학교에다가 가요.

explanation: 에 대해(서) means 'about' or 'concerning.' Use it when you're talking or thinking ABOUT a topic, person, or thing. The 서 is optional and commonly dropped in casual speech. It's the standard way to introduce what a conversation, book, or thought is about.

rules.title: Topic + 에 대해(서)
rules.description: Attach 에 대해(서) to the topic being discussed.

examples.english: 한국에 대해 알고 싶어요 — I want to know about Korea.
그 사람에 대해서 말했어요 — I spoke about that person.
이 문제에 대해 생각해요 — I think about this problem.

notes.title: Standard 'about'
notes.text: This is the most common way to say 'about' in Korean. Use it with 말하다 (speak), 생각하다 (think), 알다 (know), 배우다 (learn), etc.

notes.title: 서 is optional
notes.text: Both 에 대해 and 에 대해서 are correct. 에 대해 is more common in speech; 에 대해서 is slightly more formal and common in writing.

notes.title: Only for topics
notes.text: Don't use this for physical direction. 'About 5 o'clock' uses 쯤 or 정도, not 에 대해. 에 대해 is only for abstract topics.

explanation: 에 관해(서) also means 'about' or 'regarding,' but it sounds slightly more formal and objective than 에 대해(서). Use it in academic writing, news reports, and formal presentations. The nuance is 'in relation to' rather than 'talking about.' Again, 서 is optional.

rules.title: Topic + 에 관해(서)
rules.description: Attach 에 관해(서) to the topic being discussed in a formal or objective manner.

examples.english: 환경에 관해 연구해요 — I research regarding the environment.
역사에 관해서 썼어요 — I wrote regarding history.
법에 관해 발표했어요 — I presented regarding the law.

notes.title: Formal and objective
notes.text: 에 관해 sounds more scholarly and detached. Use it in academic papers, official reports, and news articles.

notes.title: 에 대해 vs. 에 관해
notes.text: The difference is subtle. 에 대해 is more general 'about.' 에 관해 is more formal 'regarding / in relation to.' In daily speech, stick to 에 대해.

notes.title: Interchangeable in most contexts
notes.text: Native speakers often use them interchangeably. Don't stress too much about the distinction — just know that 에 관해 is slightly more formal.

explanation: 을/를 위해(서) means 'for the sake of' or 'in order to.' It expresses purpose, benefit, or intention. Use 을 after consonant-ending nouns, 를 after vowel-ending nouns. The 서 is optional. This particle shows that an action is done FOR someone or FOR a purpose.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 위해(서)
rules.description: Attach 을/를 위해(서) to the beneficiary or purpose.

examples.english: 가족을 위해 일해요 — I work for my family.
건강을 위해 운동해요 — I exercise for my health.
미래를 위해 공부해요 — I study for the future.

notes.title: Purpose and benefit
notes.text: This is the standard way to express purpose: 시험을 위해 공부해요 (Studying for the exam), 엄마를 위해 요리했어요 (I cooked for Mom).

notes.title: 위해서 is slightly more formal
notes.text: Both 위해 and 위해서 are correct. 위해서 sounds a bit more complete and is common in writing. 위해 is more common in speech.

notes.title: Not for 'in order to' with verbs
notes.text: For 'in order to' with verb phrases, use -(으)려고 or -기 위해: 한국에 가려고 돈을 모았어요 (I saved money to go to Korea). 을/를 위해 only attaches to nouns.

explanation: 때문에 means 'because of.' It attaches to a noun to explain the REASON for something. Unlike English where 'because' comes before a clause, 때문에 comes after the noun that caused the situation. Use it when the cause is a noun, not a whole sentence.

rules.title: Cause noun + 때문에
rules.description: Attach 때문에 to the noun that is the cause or reason.

examples.english: 비 때문에 늦었어요 — I'm late because of the rain.
아픔 때문에 못 왔어요 — I couldn't come because of illness.
교통 때문에 늦었어요 — I'm late because of traffic.

notes.title: Noun-based reason
notes.text: Use 때문에 when the cause is a thing or state: 돈 때문에 (because of money), 시간 때문에 (because of time), 날씨 때문에 (because of the weather).

notes.title: Negative connotation
notes.text: 때문에 often carries a negative or problematic feeling. For neutral or positive reasons, -(으)니까 or -아/어서 are more common: 좋아서 했어요 (I did it because I like it — positive).

notes.title: Not for verb clauses
notes.text: For 'because' with full clauses (verbs), use -아/어서 or -(으)니까: 비가 와서 늦었어요 (I'm late because it rained). Don't say 비가 때문에.

exceptions.name: -기 때문에
exceptions.description: You can turn a verb into a noun with -기, then add 때문에: 늦게 일어나기 때문에 늦었어요 (Because of waking up late). This is grammatically correct but slightly formal.
exceptions.example: 돈이 없기 때문에 못 샀어요 (Because of not having money, I couldn't buy it)

explanation: (으)로서 means 'as' or 'in the capacity of.' It describes someone's role, position, or identity when performing an action. Use it when you want to emphasize that someone is acting in a specific capacity — as a teacher, as a friend, as a parent.

rules.title: Role + (으)로서
rules.description: Attach (으)로서 to the role or capacity. Use 으로서 after consonants, 로서 after vowels.

examples.english: 선생님으로서 말할게요 — I'll speak as a teacher.
친구로서 충고해요 — I give advice as a friend.
한국 사람으로서 자랑스러워요 — I'm proud as a Korean.

notes.title: Identity and role
notes.text: (으)로서 emphasizes the hat someone is wearing: 학생으로서 (as a student), 회사원으로서 (as an employee). It defines the perspective.

notes.title: Don't confuse with (으)로써
notes.text: (으)로써 means 'by means of' (method). 선생님으로서 (as a teacher) vs 인터넷으로써 (by means of the internet). The difference is role vs. method.

notes.title: Formal contexts
notes.text: This is formal and somewhat literary. In casual speech, people often just say 선생님으로 or use simpler structures.

explanation: 치고(는) means 'for a...' or 'considering...' It introduces a comparison against expectations based on someone's identity or category. Use it when you want to say that someone or something is unusual for their type. The 는 is optional and adds contrast.

rules.title: Category + 치고(는)
rules.description: Attach 치고(는) to the category or type being compared against expectations.

examples.english: 학생치고는 돈이 많아요 — For a student, they have a lot of money.
한국 사람치고 매운 음식을 못 먹어요 — For a Korean, they can't eat spicy food.
첫 작품치고는 아주 좋아요 — For a first work, it's very good.

notes.title: Against expectations
notes.text: 치고(는) always compares against a stereotype or expectation. You're saying 'considering what they are, this is surprising.'

notes.title: 늘은 adds contrast
notes.text: Adding 는 makes the contrast stronger: 학생치고는 (for a student — and this is noteworthy). Without 는, it's slightly more neutral.

notes.title: Can sound critical
notes.text: Be careful — this can sound like you're judging someone against a stereotype. Use it thoughtfully, especially when talking about people directly.

explanation: 대로 means 'as' or 'just as' — it indicates that something follows exactly the pattern, instructions, or state of the noun before it. Use it when you're following instructions, copying someone, or describing something that remains unchanged.

rules.title: Noun + 대로
rules.description: Attach 대로 to the model, instruction, or state being followed.

examples.english: 말한 대로 했어요 — I did it just as I said.
생각한 대로 됐어요 — It turned out just as I thought.
원래대로 — As it was originally. / Back to normal.

notes.title: Following instructions
notes.text: Use 대로 when obeying or following: 시키는 대로 했어요 (I did as told), 레시피대로 만들었어요 (I made it according to the recipe).

notes.title: Unchanged state
notes.text: 대로 can describe things staying the same: 그대로 있어요 (It's exactly as it was / Stay like that). 그대로 is a fixed expression meaning 'just like that.'

notes.title: Not for similarity
notes.text: For 'similar to,' use 처럼 or 같이. 대로 means 'exactly as,' not 'similar to.' There's no room for variation with 대로.

exceptions.name: 그대로
exceptions.description: 그대로 is an extremely common expression meaning 'just like that' or 'as is.' Leave it 그대로 (Don't change it!).
exceptions.example: 그대로 두세요 (Please leave it as it is)

explanation: 쯤 means 'about,' 'approximately,' or 'around.' It softens a number, time, or quantity to show it's not exact. Use it when you don't want to be precise or when you're estimating. It makes statements sound more natural and less rigid.

rules.title: Number/time/quantity + 쯤
rules.description: Attach 쯤 to any number, time, or quantity to make it approximate.

examples.english: 3시쯤 — Around 3 o'clock.
스무 살쯤 — About twenty years old.
만 원쯤 — About 10,000 won.

notes.title: Softens precision
notes.text: Without 쯤, 3시에 왔어요 means 'came at exactly 3.' With 쯤, 3시쯤 왔어요 means 'came around 3.' It makes you sound more natural and less robotic.

notes.title: Estimates and guesses
notes.text: When you're not sure, always use 쯤: 한 5명쯤 있었어요 (There were about 5 people). 한 adds extra softness to the estimate.

notes.title: Not for exact facts
notes.text: Don't use 쯤 for exact numbers. If a meeting starts at exactly 3:00, say 3시에. Saying 3시쯤 when it's exactly 3:00 sounds like you don't know the schedule.

exceptions.name: 한 + quantity + 쯤
exceptions.description: Adding 한 before a number with 쯤 makes the estimate even softer and more natural: 한 30분쯤 (about 30 minutes or so).
exceptions.example: 한 열 개쯤 샀어요 (I bought about ten or so)

explanation: 씩 means 'each' or 'per.' It distributes an amount evenly across members of a group. Use it when everyone gets the same amount, or when describing a repeated rate. It turns a total amount into an individual share.

rules.title: Quantity + 씩
rules.description: Attach 씩 to a quantity to show it's distributed per person or per instance.

examples.english: 한 개씩 — One each.
하루에 세 번씩 — Three times per day.
두 명씩 — Two people each / In pairs.

notes.title: Distribution
notes.text: 씩 divides equally: 사과를 두 개씩 줬어요 (I gave two apples to each person). Without 씩, 두 개 sounds like a total amount.

notes.title: Rate and frequency
notes.text: Use 씩 for rates: 일주일에 한 번씩 (once a week), 매달 만 원씩 (10,000 won per month). It establishes a regular pattern.

notes.title: Must follow a quantity
notes.text: 씩 cannot stand alone. It needs a number or counter before it: 한 개씩 (correct), 씩 줬어요 (wrong — no quantity specified).

exceptions.name: 조금씩 / 천천히
exceptions.description: 씩 can attach to 조금 (a little) to mean 'little by little': 조금씩 먹어요 (I eat little by little). This describes gradual action rather than distribution.
exceptions.example: 조금씩 배우고 있어요 (I'm learning little by little)

explanation: 동안 means 'during' or 'for the duration of.' It marks a period of time throughout which an action or state continues. Use it when you want to describe what happens within a time frame — what you did during vacation, what changes during a period, etc.

rules.title: Time period + 동안
rules.description: Attach 동안 to a noun that represents a duration of time.

examples.english: 방학 동안 — During vacation.
1시간 동안 — For one hour.
어린 시절 동안 — During childhood.

notes.title: Duration marker
notes.text: 동안 tells you that something happened throughout the entire period: 방학 동안 집에 있었어요 (I was home during the whole vacation).

notes.title: While / as long as
notes.text: 동안 can also mean 'while' when followed by a contrasting action: 제가 없는 동안 잘 지내세요 (Please take care while I'm away).

notes.title: Not for specific points in time
notes.text: Don't use 동안 for single moments. 'At 3 o'clock' is 3시에, not 3시 동안. 동안 requires a duration, not a point.

exceptions.name: 낂 동안
exceptions.description: Some time expressions naturally pair with 동안: 잠깐 동안 (for a short while), 오랫동안 (for a long time), 낂 동안 (for a moment).
exceptions.example: 오랫동안 기다렸어요 (I waited for a long time)

explanation: -고 is the simplest way to say 'and' or 'and then' in Korean. Use it to connect two actions, states, or facts together in a single sentence without needing a strong cause-and-effect relationship.

rules.title: Verb/Adjective Stem + 고
rules.description: Attach 고 directly to the dictionary stem, regardless of whether it ends in a vowel or a consonant (받침).

examples.english: 밥을 먹고 자요 — I eat and then sleep.
크고 싸요 — It is big and cheap.

notes.title: Tense usually goes at the end
notes.text: When listing actions, you usually only apply the past or future tense to the final verb. 밥을 먹고 갔어요 (I ate and went).

notes.title: Don't use for strong reasons
notes.text: If the first action causes the second action, use -아/어서 instead of -고.

explanation: -아/어서 is a heavy lifter in Korean. It means 'because' (giving a reason) or 'and then' (when the first action is logically required to do the second action, like opening a door and then going inside).

rules.title: Stem with ㅏ or ㅗ + 아서
rules.description: If the last vowel of the stem is ㅏ or ㅗ, add 아서.

examples.english: 학교에 가서 공부해요 — I go to school and study.
작아서 안 맞아요 — It doesn't fit because it's small.

rules.title: Other vowels + 어서
rules.description: If the last vowel is anything other than ㅏ or ㅗ (like ㅓ, ㅜ, ㅣ), add 어서.

examples.english: 많이 먹어서 배불러요 — I'm full because I ate a lot.

rules.title: 하다 verbs → 해서
rules.description: Any verb or adjective ending in 하다 automatically becomes 해서.

examples.english: 피곤해서 일찍 잤어요 — I slept early because I was tired.

notes.title: No past tense before 어서
notes.text: Never use past tense markers (-았/었-) before 아/어서. Say 바빠서 못 갔어요 (Because I was busy I couldn't go), not 바빴어서.

notes.title: No commands or suggestions
notes.text: You cannot use 아/어서 with imperative (-(으)세요) or propositive (-(으)ㅂ시다) endings. Use -(으)니까 for those instead.

exceptions.name: Irregular ㅂ
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅂ drop the ㅂ and add 워서.
exceptions.example: 추워서 집에 있었어요 (I stayed home because it was cold).

explanation: -지만 means 'but' or 'although.' Use it to link two clauses where the second part is unexpected or opposite to the first part. It provides a very direct and clear contrast.

rules.title: Stem + 지만
rules.description: Attach 지만 directly to the verb or adjective stem.

examples.english: 비싸지만 살 거예요 — It is expensive, but I will buy it.
작지만 강해요 — It's small but strong.

rules.title: Past Tense + 지만
rules.description: Unlike -고 or -아/어서, you can (and should) attach tense markers before 지만 if the first action is in the past.

examples.english: 약은 먹었지만 아직 아파요 — I took medicine, but I'm still sick.

notes.title: 지만 vs 는데
notes.text: -지만 is a stronger, more direct 'but.' -는데 is softer and often just provides background information that happens to contrast.

explanation: -는데 is the ultimate background setter in Korean, often translating to 'but,' 'and,' or 'so...' Use it to paint the picture or set the scene before making a request, asking a question, or sharing a contrasting fact.

rules.title: Action Verbs + 는데
rules.description: Attach 는데 to all action verb stems, regardless of vowels or consonants.

examples.english: 지금 가는데, 어디에요? — I'm going now, so where are you?
밥을 먹는데 전화가 왔어요 — I was eating, and/but the phone rang.

rules.title: Adjectives (no 받침) + ㄴ데
rules.description: Attach ㄴ데 to descriptive verbs (adjectives) that end in a vowel.

examples.english: 이 옷이 큰데, 다른 사이즈 있어요? — This shirt is big, so do you have another size?

rules.title: Adjectives (with 받침) + 은데
rules.description: Attach 은데 to descriptive verbs (adjectives) that end in a consonant.

examples.english: 방은 작은데, 깨끗해요 — The room is small, but it's clean.

notes.title: Trailing off softly
notes.text: Koreans frequently end sentences with -는데요 to soften their statement or invite the listener to respond. It sounds less aggressive than finishing a firm sentence.

notes.title: Past tense rules
notes.text: When using past tense (-았/었-), ALWAYS use 는데, even for adjectives (예뻤는데 - it was pretty, but...).

exceptions.name: 있다/없다
exceptions.description: Even though they describe states, 있다 and 없다 always take 는데, never 은데.
exceptions.example: 시간이 없는데 빨리 가요 (I don't have time, so let's go quickly).

explanation: -(으)면 means 'if' or 'when.' Reach for this when you want to set a condition: 'If A happens, then B will happen.'

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 면
rules.description: Attach 면 when the stem ends in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ.

examples.english: 비가 오면 집에 있을 거예요 — If it rains, I will stay home.
한국에 살면 한국어를 잘할 거예요 — If you live in Korea, you'll speak Korean well.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 으면
rules.description: Attach 으면 when the stem ends in any consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 많이 먹으면 배가 아파요 — If you eat a lot, your stomach will hurt.

notes.title: If vs. When
notes.text: -(으)면 can mean 'when' for general facts (When it's night, it gets dark) or future conditions. But for a specific past 'when' event (When I was young...), use -(으)ㄹ 때 instead.

notes.title: Not for impossible past regrets
notes.text: Don't use this to say 'If I HAD studied...' For impossible past conditions, advanced learners use -았/었더라면.

exceptions.name: Irregular ㄷ
exceptions.description: ㄷ changes to ㄹ before 으면.
exceptions.example: 음악을 들으면 기분이 좋아요 (When/if I listen to music, I feel good).

explanation: -(으)니까 means 'because' or 'since.' Unlike -아/어서, you MUST use this grammar when the second half of your sentence is a suggestion (let's...), a command (please do...), or a subjective opinion.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 니까
rules.description: Attach 니까 if the stem ends in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ. (Note: the ㄹ drops out!).

examples.english: 바쁘니까 내일 만나요 — Since I'm busy, let's meet tomorrow.
제가 만드니까 걱정하지 마세요 — I'm making it, so don't worry.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 으니까
rules.description: Attach 으니까 if the stem ends in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 날씨가 좋으니까 산책합시다 — Since the weather is nice, let's take a walk.

notes.title: Perfect for commands
notes.text: Always pair -(으)니까 with -(으)세요 (please do) and -(으)ㅂ시다 (let's). You cannot use -아/어서 for these.

notes.title: Past tense is allowed
notes.text: Unlike -아/어서, you CAN use past tense markers before -(으)니까. (e.g., 어제 늦게 잤으니까 — Because I slept late yesterday).

explanation: -거나 means 'or.' Use it when you are giving two or more verb or adjective options and stating that any of them are fine.

rules.title: Stem + 거나
rules.description: Simply attach 거나 to the verb or adjective stem.

examples.english: 주말에는 자거나 영화를 봐요 — On weekends, I sleep or watch movies.
책을 읽거나 음악을 들으세요 — Read a book or listen to music.

notes.title: Verbs vs. Nouns
notes.text: Use -거나 for verbs and adjectives. If you want to say 'or' between two nouns (like 'coffee or tea'), use -(이)나 instead (커피나 차).

notes.title: Tense is at the end
notes.text: You usually only mark the past or future tense on the final verb, not on the -거나.

explanation: -(으)면서 means 'while.' Use it when the exact same person is doing two actions at the exact same time, like listening to music while studying.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 면서
rules.description: Attach 면서 to stems ending in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ.

examples.english: 운전하면서 전화하지 마세요 — Don't talk on the phone while driving.
놀면서 배워요 — We learn while playing.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 으면서
rules.description: Attach 으면서 to stems ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 밥을 먹으면서 TV를 봐요 — I watch TV while eating.

notes.title: Same subject required
notes.text: The person doing Action A MUST be the same person doing Action B. You can't say 'While I ate, he slept' using -(으)면서.

notes.title: What about -(으)며?
notes.text: -(으)며 has the exact same meaning, but it is mostly used in writing, news, or formal speeches. Stick to -(으)면서 for daily conversation.

explanation: -아/어도 means 'even if' or 'even though.' Use it when you want to emphasize that regardless of the condition in the first clause, the result in the second clause won't change.

rules.title: ㅏ or ㅗ vowels → + 아도
rules.description: If the last vowel of the stem is ㅏ or ㅗ, add 아도.

examples.english: 작아도 아주 비싸요 — Even though it's small, it's very expensive.

rules.title: Other vowels → + 어도
rules.description: If the last vowel is not ㅏ or ㅗ, add 어도.

examples.english: 많이 먹어도 배가 고파요 — Even if I eat a lot, I'm hungry.

rules.title: 하다 verbs → + 해도
rules.description: 하다 verbs become 해도.

examples.english: 피곤해도 운동을 해요 — Even though I'm tired, I exercise.

notes.title: Pairing with 아무리
notes.text: You will often hear 아무리 (no matter how much) at the start of the sentence to add dramatic emphasis. 아무리 바빠도... = No matter HOW busy you are...

explanation: -(으)ㄴ 후에 and -고 나서 both mean 'after doing [Verb].' Use them to clearly map out the timeline of your actions.

rules.title: Vowel ending → + ㄴ 후에
rules.description: If the verb ends in a vowel, attach ㄴ 후에.

examples.english: 퇴근한 후에 친구를 만나요 — After getting off work, I meet a friend.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 은 후에
rules.description: If the verb ends in a consonant, attach 은 후에.

examples.english: 밥을 먹은 후에 약을 드세요 — Take the medicine after eating.

rules.title: Any Stem + 고 나서
rules.description: Attach 고 나서 directly to the dictionary stem for a slightly more natural spoken feel.

examples.english: 씻고 나서 잘 거예요 — I will sleep after washing up.

notes.title: 후에 vs 고 나서
notes.text: -고 나서 emphasizes that the first action is completely 100% finished before the next one starts. Both are highly interchangeable in daily life.

notes.title: Using with nouns
notes.text: For nouns, drop the (으)ㄴ and just use 후에. 식사 후에 (after the meal).

explanation: -기 전에 means 'before doing [something].' Use it to explain that the second action in your sentence happens prior to the first one.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 기 전에
rules.description: Simply attach 기 전에 to the verb stem, regardless of vowels or consonants.

examples.english: 자기 전에 책을 읽어요 — I read a book before sleeping.
친구가 오기 전에 청소했어요 — I cleaned before my friend came.

notes.title: No past tense here!
notes.text: Never conjugate the verb into past tense before -기 전에. Tense is only shown on the final verb of the sentence.

notes.title: Using with nouns
notes.text: Just like 후에, you can attach 전에 directly to nouns. 회의 전에 (before the meeting).

explanation: -자마자 means 'as soon as.' Reach for this when you want to show that Action B happens immediately, almost instantly, right after Action A finishes.

rules.title: Stem + 자마자
rules.description: Attach 자마자 directly to the verb stem.

examples.english: 집에 도착하자마자 잤어요 — As soon as I arrived home, I slept.
일어나자마자 물을 마셔요 — As soon as I wake up, I drink water.

notes.title: No restrictions
notes.text: Unlike some other grammars, -자마자 can be used with any tense, and the subjects of the two clauses can be different (As soon as mom left, the baby cried).

notes.title: vs. -(으)ㄴ/는 대로
notes.text: -(으)는 대로 also means 'as soon as' (e.g., 도착하는 대로 연락할게요 - I'll contact you as soon as I arrive). However, -자마자 sounds slightly more immediate and dramatic.

explanation: -는 동안 means 'while' or 'during.' Unlike -(으)면서, you use this when Person A is doing one thing while Person B is doing something else, or for an action spanning a block of time.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 는 동안
rules.description: Attach 는 동안 directly to action verb stems.

examples.english: 기다리는 동안 커피를 마셨어요 — I drank coffee while waiting.
제가 자는 동안 비가 왔어요 — While I was sleeping, it rained.

notes.title: Different subjects are okay
notes.text: This is the perfect grammar to use when two different people are doing things simultaneously. (While mom cooked, I cleaned).

notes.title: Using with nouns
notes.text: You can attach 동안 directly to time-duration nouns. 방학 동안 (during vacation), 3일 동안 (for 3 days).

exceptions.name: ㄹ 받침 drops
exceptions.description: Verbs ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ before adding 는 동안.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 때 means 'when' or 'at the time of.' Use it to describe the timeframe, condition, or moment in which an action or state occurs.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 때
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 때 if the stem ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 피곤할 때 커피를 마셔요 — When I am tired, I drink coffee.
친구랑 놀 때 제일 행복해요 — When I play with my friends, I'm the happiest.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 때
rules.description: Attach 을 때 if the stem ends in a consonant.

examples.english: 밥을 먹을 때 말하지 마세요 — Don't talk when eating.

rules.title: Past Tense → 았/었을 때
rules.description: If you are talking about a completed action or a period in the past, add -았/었- before 을 때.

examples.english: 어렸을 때 미국에 살았어요 — When I was young, I lived in the US.

notes.title: Don't use with specific time words
notes.text: Do not use -때 with specific days, dates, or clock times (like Monday, 2 PM, or yesterday). Use 에 for those instead.

notes.title: Using with nouns
notes.text: Can attach directly to event nouns: 방학 때 (during vacation), 회의 때 (at the time of the meeting).

explanation: -고 싶다 means 'want to'. It is the standard way to express your own personal desires or to ask the listener what they want to do. Use this only for actions, not wanting objects.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 고 싶다
rules.description: Attach 고 싶다 directly to any action verb stem.

examples.english: 피자를 먹고 싶어요 — I want to eat pizza.
한국에 가고 싶어요 — I want to go to Korea.

notes.title: Only for 1st/2nd person
notes.text: Do not use -고 싶다 for a third person (like 'my friend wants to'). For that, you must use -고 싶어하다.

notes.title: Verbs only, not nouns
notes.text: You cannot say '커피고 싶어요' to mean 'I want coffee'. You must add a verb: '커피를 마시고 싶어요' (I want to drink coffee).

explanation: -고 싶어하다 means 'wants to', but it is strictly used for third-party subjects (he, she, they, my friend). In Korean, you cannot claim to know someone else's internal feelings 100%, so this grammar literally implies 'shows signs of wanting to'.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 고 싶어하다
rules.description: Attach 고 싶어하다 to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 제 친구가 그 영화를 보고 싶어해요 — My friend wants to see that movie.
아기가 자고 싶어해요 — The baby wants to sleep.

notes.title: Subject marker match
notes.text: Because -고 싶어하다 is treated as an action verb showing visible signs, the object they want is often marked with 을/를, not 이/가.

explanation: -아/어 보다 literally means 'to do and see'. It translates to 'try doing' something. Use it when suggesting someone try an action, or when talking about your own past experiences (having tried or experienced something).

rules.title: ㅏ or ㅗ vowels → + 아 보다
rules.description: If the last vowel is ㅏ or ㅗ, add 아 보다.

examples.english: 제주도에 가 봤어요? — Have you ever been to (tried going to) Jeju Island?

rules.title: Other vowels → + 어 보다
rules.description: If the last vowel is anything else, add 어 보다.

examples.english: 이거 한번 먹어 보세요 — Please try eating this.

rules.title: 하다 → 해 보다
rules.description: Verbs ending in 하다 change to 해 보다.

examples.english: 내일 연락해 볼게요 — I will try contacting you tomorrow.

notes.title: Polite suggestions
notes.text: Combining this with -(으)세요 creates -아/어 보세요, which is the most natural, polite way to recommend someone do something (e.g., 읽어 보세요 - Please read it).

notes.title: Don't use with 보다
notes.text: You generally don't attach -아/어 보다 to the verb 보다 (to see) itself. It sounds awkward to say 보아 보세요.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 수 있다/없다 expresses ability ('can') or possibility ('it is possible'). Use this to explain whether you have the physical ability, time, or permission to do something.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 수 있다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 수 있다/없다 if the stem ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 내일 파티에 올 수 있어요? — Can you come to the party tomorrow?
저는 케이크를 만들 수 없어요 — I cannot make a cake.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 수 있다
rules.description: Attach 을 수 있다/없다 if the stem ends in a consonant.

examples.english: 매운 음식을 먹을 수 있어요? — Can you eat spicy food?

notes.title: vs. 못
notes.text: In casual spoken Korean, people often use 못 + verb instead of -(으)ㄹ 수 없다 because it's shorter. (e.g., 못 먹어요 is more common than 먹을 수 없어요).

notes.title: Spacing matters
notes.text: Make sure to leave a space before 수 and after 수. It is 할 수 있다, not 할수있다.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 줄 알다/모르다 means 'to know (or not know) how to do something'. While -(으)ㄹ 수 있다 is about general possibility or having the time, this grammar specifically focuses on whether you have LEARNED the skill or method.

rules.title: Vowel/ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 줄 알다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 줄 알다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 수영할 줄 알아요? — Do you know how to swim?

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 줄 알다
rules.description: Attach 을 줄 알다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 한글을 읽을 줄 알아요 — I know how to read Hangul.

notes.title: Ability vs. Skill
notes.text: If you broke your leg, you would say 수영할 수 없어요 (I can't swim right now). You wouldn't say 수영할 줄 몰라요 (I don't know how to swim) because you still possess the skill, just not the physical ability today.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 수밖에 없다 means 'to have no choice but to' or 'to be bound to'. Use it when a certain action or outcome is the only logical option or unavoidable result.

rules.title: Vowel/ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 수밖에 없다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 수밖에 없다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 너무 어려워서 포기할 수밖에 없었어요 — It was so hard I had no choice but to give up.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 수밖에 없다
rules.description: Attach 을 수밖에 없다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 이 식당은 맛있어서 인기가 좋을 수밖에 없어요 — This restaurant is delicious, so it's bound to be popular.

notes.title: Etymology
notes.text: 밖에 means 'outside of' or 'other than', and 없다 means 'none'. So literally, 'Other than doing this, there is no way'.

explanation: -아/어도 되다 is the standard way to ask for or grant permission. It literally means 'even if you do this, it is okay', translating smoothly to 'You may...' or 'Is it okay if I...?'

rules.title: ㅏ/ㅗ vowels → + 아도 되다
rules.description: Use 아도 되다 for stems ending in ㅏ or ㅗ.

examples.english: 여기 앉아도 돼요? — Is it okay if I sit here? / May I sit here?

rules.title: Other vowels → + 어도 되다
rules.description: Use 어도 되다 for other vowels.

examples.english: 창문을 열어도 돼요? — May I open the window?

rules.title: 하다 → 해도 되다
rules.description: 하다 verbs become 해도 되다.

examples.english: 이제 시작해도 돼요 — You may start now.

notes.title: Answering yes
notes.text: To say 'Yes, you may', simply repeat the grammar back: 네, 앉아도 돼요 (Yes, you may sit).

explanation: -(으)면 안 되다 is the direct opposite of -아/어도 되다. It means 'you must not' or 'it is not allowed.' It literally translates to 'if you do this, it is not okay.'

rules.title: Vowel/ㄹ ending → + 면 안 되다
rules.description: Attach 면 안 되다 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 작품을 만지면 안 돼요 — You must not touch the artwork.
지금 문을 열면 안 돼요 — You shouldn't open the door now.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 으면 안 되다
rules.description: Attach 으면 안 되다 to stems ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 여기서 사진을 찍으면 안 돼요 — You must not take photos here.

notes.title: Answering a permission question
notes.text: If someone asks 앉아도 돼요? (May I sit?), and you want to say no, use this grammar: 아니요, 앉으면 안 돼요 (No, you must not sit).

explanation: -아/어야 되다 (or 하다) means 'must', 'have to', or 'should'. It is the most common way to express obligation or necessity in Korean.

rules.title: ㅏ/ㅗ vowels → + 아야 되다
rules.description: Add 아야 되다/하다 if the stem ends in ㅏ or ㅗ.

examples.english: 내일 일찍 가야 돼요 — I have to go early tomorrow.

rules.title: Other vowels → + 어야 되다
rules.description: Add 어야 되다/하다 for other vowels.

examples.english: 이 약을 먹어야 해요 — You must take this medicine.

rules.title: 하다 → 해야 되다
rules.description: 하다 becomes 해야 되다/하다.

examples.english: 시험이 있어서 공부해야 돼요 — I have a test, so I must study.

notes.title: 되다 vs 하다
notes.text: 되다 and 하다 are virtually interchangeable here. 되다 is more common in casual spoken Korean, while 하다 sounds slightly more formal.

notes.title: Adding 겠다
notes.text: Using -아야겠다 (e.g., 가야겠어요) adds a personal resolve: 'I guess I should go' or 'I better go'.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ까 하다 means 'I am thinking of doing...' Use this for vague plans or intentions that you haven't 100% committed to yet.

rules.title: Vowel/ㄹ ending → + ㄹ까 하다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ까 하다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 일본어를 배울까 해요 — I'm thinking of learning Japanese.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을까 하다
rules.description: Attach 을까 하다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 오늘 저녁은 피자를 먹을까 해요 — I'm thinking of having pizza for dinner tonight.

notes.title: First person only
notes.text: This grammar is only used for your own thoughts and plans. You cannot use it to say 'My friend is thinking of...'

notes.title: 싶다 instead of 하다
notes.text: You can swap 하다 for 싶다 (-(으)ㄹ까 싶다). It means the exact same thing but sounds slightly more passive or uncertain.

explanation: -(으)면 좋겠다 means 'I wish' or 'It would be nice if...' Use it to express a hope or desire for a certain situation to happen.

rules.title: Present Stem + (으)면 좋겠다
rules.description: Attach directly to the present tense stem for a straightforward wish.

examples.english: 비행기 표가 싸면 좋겠어요 — I wish the flight tickets were cheap.

rules.title: Past Stem + 았/었으면 좋겠다
rules.description: Koreans very frequently attach the past tense marker before -(으)면 좋겠다. This doesn't make it past tense; it just makes the wish sound stronger and more earnest.

examples.english: 빨리 끝났으면 좋겠어요 — I really wish it would finish quickly.
내일 비가 안 왔으면 좋겠어요 — I hope it doesn't rain tomorrow.

notes.title: Past tense is preferred
notes.text: In daily conversation, the past tense form (-았/었으면 좋겠다) is much more natural and commonly used than the present tense form.

explanation: -는 게 좋다 means 'It is good to...' or 'You should...'. It is used to give soft advice or recommendations. -는 게 낫다 means 'It is better to...', used when comparing two options.

rules.title: Action Verbs + 는 게 좋다/낫다
rules.description: Attach 는 게 좋다 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 오늘은 집에서 쉬는 게 좋아요 — It is good to (you should) rest at home today.
택시를 타는 게 나아요 — It is better to take a taxi.

notes.title: What does '게' mean?
notes.text: 게 is a contraction of 것이 (the thing/the act). So 쉬는 게 좋아요 literally means 'The act of resting is good'.

notes.title: 낫다 conjugation
notes.text: 낫다 (to be better) has an irregular ㅅ. When conjugating to polite form, it becomes 나아요, not 낫아요.

exceptions.name: ㄹ drop
exceptions.description: If the verb ends in ㄹ, drop the ㄹ before adding 는 게 좋다.
exceptions.example: 만들다 → 만드는 게 좋아요 (It's better to make it).

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 필요가 있다/없다 translates to 'there is a need to' or 'there is no need to'. Use it to express whether an action is necessary or completely unnecessary.

rules.title: Vowel/ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 필요가 있다/없다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 필요가 있다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 서두를 필요가 없어요 — There is no need to rush.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 필요가 있다/없다
rules.description: Attach 을 필요가 있다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 허락을 받을 필요가 있어요 — You need to get permission.

notes.title: Alternative to 안 해도 되다
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 필요가 없다 has a very similar meaning to -아/어도 되지 않다 or 안 -아/어도 되다 (don't have to do it). They are often interchangeable.

explanation: -아/어 주다 means 'to do something for someone'. Adding 주다 (to give) to a verb softens the action, making it a favor or a service being offered or requested.

rules.title: ㅏ/ㅗ vowels → + 아 주다
rules.description: Add 아 주다 to stems ending in ㅏ or ㅗ.

examples.english: 문 좀 닫아 주세요 — Please close the door (for me).

rules.title: Other vowels → + 어 주다
rules.description: Add 어 주다 for other vowels.

examples.english: 친구가 케이크를 만들어 줬어요 — My friend made a cake for me.

rules.title: 하다 → 해 주다
rules.description: 하다 becomes 해 주다.

examples.english: 다시 설명해 줄 수 있어요? — Can you explain it again (for me)?

notes.title: Essential for polite requests
notes.text: When asking someone to do something, simply using -(으)세요 can sound like a bossy command. Using -아/어 주세요 sounds much more polite, like 'Please do me the favor of...'.

explanation: -아/어 드리다 is the humble (polite) version of -아/어 주다. You use it when YOU (or someone of lower status) are doing a favor for someone of HIGHER status (like a boss, teacher, or elder).

rules.title: Verb Stem + 아/어 드리다
rules.description: Follow the standard 아/어 rules, but attach 드리다 instead of 주다.

examples.english: 제가 도와 드릴게요 — I will help you (Sir/Ma'am).
가방을 들어 드릴까요? — Shall I carry your bag for you?

notes.title: Don't use for yourself
notes.text: Never say '저한테 만들어 드리세요' (Make it for me). 드리다 is only for giving favors UP the social ladder, not for receiving them.

notes.title: 주다 vs 드리다 vs 주시다
notes.text: 주다 (I do for equal/lower), 드리다 (I do for boss), 주시다 (Boss does for me).

explanation: -아/어 주시다 is the honorific version of -아/어 주다. You use it when someone of HIGHER status (like a boss, teacher, or elder) is doing a favor for YOU.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 아/어 주시다
rules.description: Follow the standard 아/어 rules, and attach 주시다.

examples.english: 선생님이 한국어를 가르쳐 주셨어요 — The teacher taught me Korean.
조금만 기다려 주시겠어요? — Could you wait a moment for me, please?

notes.title: The ultimate polite request
notes.text: When making requests to strangers, customers, or elders, -아/어 주시겠어요? (Would you please do this for me?) is the gold standard for politeness.

explanation: -아/어 받다 literally means 'to receive an action' (like being taught or being given advice). However, it is not very common in Korean because Koreans usually prefer to use the active voice with -아/어 주다 (Someone gave/taught me) instead.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 아/어 받다
rules.description: Attach to the verb using the standard 아/어 rules.

examples.english: 파티에 초대받았어요 — I received an invitation to the party.
직원에게 안내를 받았어요 — I was guided by the staff.

notes.title: Natural usage
notes.text: Words like 초대받다 (be invited), 안남받다 (be guided), and 버림받다 (be abandoned) are established words. But don't force -아/어 받다 onto every verb just to translate the English passive voice.

explanation: -아/어 올리다 is an extremely polite, humble expression that means 'to offer' or 'to present' an action up to someone much higher in status. It is even more formal than 드리다.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 아/어 올리다
rules.description: Use standard 아/어 rules and attach 올리다.

examples.english: 사장님께 보고해 올리겠습니다 — I will report this to the boss.

notes.title: Highly formal
notes.text: You will mostly hear this in formal business settings, historical K-dramas, or religious contexts. In daily life, -아/어 드리다 is usually polite enough.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ래요? means 'Would you like to...?' or 'Will you...?'. When combined with -아/어 주다 (-아/어 줄래요?), it becomes a friendly, casual way to ask someone for a favor.

rules.title: Vowel/ㄹ ending → + ㄹ래요
rules.description: Attach ㄹ래요 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 저 좀 도와줄래요? — Would you mind helping me?

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을래요
rules.description: Attach 을래요 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 이것 좀 읽어 줄래요? — Could you read this for me?

notes.title: Politeness level
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ래요? is friendly but casual. It's great for friends or close coworkers. For elders or strangers, use -아/어 주시겠어요? instead.

explanation: -(으)려고 means 'in order to' or 'with the intention of'. Use it when you perform an action in the second half of the sentence specifically to achieve the goal stated in the first half.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 려고
rules.description: Attach 려고 to stems ending in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ.

examples.english: 선물을 사려고 백화점에 가요 — I am going to the department store in order to buy a gift.
케이크를 만들려고 계란을 샀어요 — I bought eggs to make a cake.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 으려고
rules.description: Attach 으려고 to stems ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 지갑을 찾으려고 경찰서에 갔어요 — I went to the police station to find my wallet.

notes.title: No commands or suggestions
notes.text: You cannot use -(으)려고 with imperative (-(으)세요) or propositive (-(으)ㅂ시다) endings. Use -(으)러 or -기 위해서 instead.

notes.title: No tense markers before it
notes.text: Do not use past or future tense markers before -(으)려고. The tense is determined by the final verb of the sentence.

explanation: -(으)려고 하다 means 'plan to' or 'intend to'. Use this to express a future plan or resolution that you have already spent some time thinking about.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 려고 하다
rules.description: Attach 려고 하다 to stems ending in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ.

examples.english: 내년부터 피아노를 배우려고 해요 — I plan to learn the piano starting next year.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 으려고 하다
rules.description: Attach 으려고 하다 to stems ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 이번 주말에는 책을 많이 읽으려고 해요 — I plan to read a lot of books this weekend.

notes.title: vs. -(으)ㄹ 거예요
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 거예요 is a firm future fact. -(으)려고 하다 is a personal intention that could still change.

notes.title: Past tense usage
notes.text: It is very commonly used in the past tense (-(으)려고 했어요) to mean 'I was planning to... (but didn't or couldn't)'.

explanation: -(으)려면 is a contraction of -(으)려고 하면, meaning 'if you intend to'. Use it to set a condition or requirement for achieving a goal.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 려면
rules.description: Attach 려면 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 살을 빼려면 운동을 해야 돼요 — If you intend to lose weight, you must exercise.
시험에 합격하려면 열심히 공부하세요 — If you want to pass the exam, please study hard.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 으려면
rules.description: Attach 으려면 to stems ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 이 책을 읽으려면 시간이 많이 필요해요 — If you intend to read this book, you need a lot of time.

notes.title: Natural pairings
notes.text: -(으)려면 is almost always followed by advice, commands, or necessities like -아/어야 되다 (must) or -(으)세요 (please).

explanation: -(으)러 means 'in order to', but it is exclusively used with movement verbs like 가다 (go) and 오다 (come). Use it to express the purpose of your travel or movement.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 러
rules.description: Attach 러 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 친구를 만나러 카페에 가요 — I am going to the cafe to meet a friend.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 으러
rules.description: Attach 으러 to stems ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 책을 찾으러 도서관에 왔어요 — I came to the library to look for a book.

notes.title: Movement verbs only
notes.text: The second verb MUST be a movement verb (가다, 오다, 다니다). You cannot say '밥을 먹으러 돈을 내요' (I pay money to eat).

notes.title: Commands are okay
notes.text: Unlike -(으)려고, you can use -(으)러 with commands and suggestions (e.g., 밥 먹으러 갑시다 - Let's go eat).

explanation: -기 위해서 means 'for the sake of' or 'in order to'. It is a slightly more formal, clear, and emphatic way to state your purpose compared to -(으)려고.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 기 위해서
rules.description: Attach 기 위해서 directly to any action verb stem.

examples.english: 성공하기 위해서 열심히 일해요 — I work hard in order to succeed.
살기 위해서 먹어요 — I eat to live.

notes.title: Using with nouns
notes.text: You can use this with nouns by dropping '기' and adding '을/를'. For example: 가족을 위해서 (for the sake of my family).

notes.title: Highly flexible
notes.text: It can be used freely with commands, suggestions, and any type of ending verb, making it very versatile.

explanation: -도록 means 'so that', 'in a way that', or 'to the point where'. It focuses on the degree, result, or intended goal of an action.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 도록
rules.description: Attach 도록 directly to the verb stem.

examples.english: 모두가 이해하도록 쉽게 설명했어요 — I explained it easily so that everyone could understand.
목이 아프도록 울었어요 — I cried to the point where my throat hurt.

notes.title: Giving instructions
notes.text: Often combined with 하다 (-도록 하다) to strongly instruct someone to do something: 내일까지 끝낙도록 하세요 (Make sure you finish by tomorrow).

notes.title: vs. -게
notes.text: Similar to -게 (so that), but -도록 is slightly more formal and places heavier emphasis on the extent or limit of the action.

explanation: -게 attached to an adjective turns it into an adverb (like '-ly' in English). When attached to an action verb, it means 'so that' and describes the intended result of an action.

rules.title: Stem + 게
rules.description: Attach 게 directly to the verb or adjective stem.

examples.english: 머리를 예쁘게 잘랐어요 — I cut my hair beautifully.
잘 들리게 크게 말해 주세요 — Please speak loudly so that it can be heard well.

notes.title: Common fixed expressions
notes.text: Koreans use this constantly in daily phrases like 맛있게 드세요 (Eat deliciously / Enjoy your meal) and 늦게 (late).

explanation: -기로 하다 means 'to decide to' do something. -기로 되다 means 'it has been decided that' (usually by a group, a rule, or destiny).

rules.title: Verb Stem + 기로 하다/되다
rules.description: Attach 기로 하다 or 기로 되다 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 내년부터 담배를 끊기로 했어요 — I decided to quit smoking starting next year.
회의 시간이 바뀌기로 되었어요 — It has been decided that the meeting time will change.

notes.title: Always past tense
notes.text: You will almost always use this in the past tense (-기로 했어요) because the decision was already made before speaking.

notes.title: Making promises
notes.text: If you are promising someone else, use 약속하다 instead: 주말에 만나기로 약속했어요 (We promised to meet on the weekend).

explanation: This grammar turns verbs into nouns, similar to adding '-ing' or saying 'the fact that' in English. It allows you to use entire verb phrases as subjects or objects in a sentence.

rules.title: Present → + 는 것
rules.description: For an action happening now or a general fact, attach 는 것.

examples.english: 한국어를 배우는 것은 재미있어요 — Learning Korean is fun.

rules.title: Past → + (으)ㄴ 것
rules.description: For an action that is already finished, attach (으)ㄴ 것.

examples.english: 어제 만난 것을 잊어버렸어요 — I forgot the fact that we met yesterday.

rules.title: Future → + (으)ㄹ 것
rules.description: For an action that will happen, attach (으)ㄹ 것.

examples.english: 내일 먹을 것을 샀어요 — I bought the things (we will) eat tomorrow.

notes.title: Shortening to 거
notes.text: In spoken Korean, '것' is almost always shortened to '거'. For example, '먹는 것' becomes '먹는 거'.

notes.title: Particle contraction
notes.text: When attached to particles, it shrinks further: 하는 것은 → 하는 건, 하는 것을 → 하는 걸, 하는 것이 → 하는 게.

exceptions.name: ㄹ drop
exceptions.description: If the verb stem ends in ㄹ, drop the ㄹ before adding 는 것.

explanation: This pattern translates to 'it seems like' or 'I think'. Koreans use this constantly to sound polite, soften their opinions, or when they aren't 100% certain about a fact.

rules.title: Adjectives → + (으)ㄴ 것 같다
rules.description: For adjectives, use (으)ㄴ 것 같다 for present descriptions.

examples.english: 이 옷은 조금 비싼 것 같아요 — I think this shirt is a little expensive.

rules.title: Verbs Present → + 는 것 같다
rules.description: For verbs happening now, use 는 것 같다.

examples.english: 지금 비가 오는 것 같아요 — It seems like it's raining right now.

rules.title: Verbs Past / Future
rules.description: Use (으)ㄴ 것 같다 for past actions, and (으)ㄹ 것 같다 for future guesses.

examples.english: 벌써 다 먹은 것 같아요 — It seems like they already ate it all.
내일 날씨가 좋을 것 같아요 — I think the weather will be nice tomorrow.

notes.title: Softening speech
notes.text: Even if you are completely sure of something, using this grammar makes you sound less direct, less aggressive, and more polite.

explanation: This is the core rule for modifying nouns in Korean. Unlike English where adjectives can stand alone or come after the noun, Korean modifiers always come directly before the noun they describe.

rules.title: Adjectives → + (으)ㄴ
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ to adjectives to describe a noun.

examples.english: 작은 가방 — a small bag

rules.title: Verbs Present → + 는
rules.description: Attach 는 to action verbs happening now.

examples.english: 자는 아기 — a sleeping baby

rules.title: Verbs Past / Future
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ for completed actions, and (으)ㄹ for future actions.

examples.english: 어제 읽은 책 — the book I read yesterday
내일 마실 우유 — the milk I will drink tomorrow

notes.title: No -는 with adjectives
notes.text: Never use -는 with adjectives. It is '예쁜 꽃' (pretty flower), not '예쁘는 꽃'.

notes.title: 있다/없다 rule
notes.text: For 있다 and 없다, always use -는 (재미있는 영화 = interesting movie), even though they translate like adjectives.

explanation: -는 중이다 means 'in the middle of doing'. It puts a strong emphasis on the fact that an action is currently in progress at this exact moment and you are busy with it.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 는 중이다
rules.description: Attach 는 중이다 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 지금 도서관에서 공부하는 중이에요 — I am in the middle of studying at the library right now.
지금 가는 중이에요 — I am on my way (in the middle of going).

notes.title: vs. -고 있다
notes.text: They both mean 'am doing', but -는 중이다 sounds busier and more active, like 'I am right in the middle of...'. You cannot use -는 중이다 for wearing clothes (use 입고 있다).

notes.title: Noun usage
notes.text: You can drop the verb completely and attach 중이다 directly to action nouns: 회의 중이에요 (I am in a meeting), 공사 중 (Under construction).

explanation: These are formal nominalizers that turn verbs into nouns. -기 is used for lists, goals, or interface buttons. -ㅁ/음 is used for memos, document titles, or brief text messages.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 기
rules.description: Attach 기 directly to the stem. Common in apps, buttons, and casual instructions.

examples.english: 보내기 — Send (App button)
달리기 — Running (as a sport/noun)

rules.title: Verb Stem + ㅁ/음
rules.description: Attach ㅁ after vowel-ending stems, 음 after consonant-ending stems. Common in formal writing, titles, and memos.

examples.english: 감 — Going (formal memo title)
읽음 — Reading (formal record of having read)

notes.title: Digital Korean
notes.text: You will see -기 all over Korean apps and websites: 더보기 (See more), 취소하기 (Cancel), 설정하기 (Settings).

notes.title: Formal -ㅁ/음
notes.text: -ㅁ/음 sounds objective and formal. You'll see it in news headlines, report summaries, and read receipts: 확인함 (Confirmed), 도착함 (Arrived).

explanation: -던 and -았/었던 are used to recall the past. -던 is for recalling an action you used to do repeatedly or an action you didn't finish. -았/었던 is for a completely finished past action.

rules.title: Stem + 던
rules.description: Attach 던 for a habit or unfinished past.

examples.english: 제가 자주 가던 카페예요 — This is the cafe I used to go to often.
아까 마시던 커피 어딨어요? — Where is the coffee I was drinking earlier (but didn't finish)?

rules.title: Stem + 았/었던
rules.description: Attach 았/었던 for a one-time, completely finished past event.

examples.english: 우리가 처음 만났던 곳 — The place where we first met (one time event).

notes.title: Nostalgic feeling
notes.text: Using -던 naturally adds a sense of nostalgia or reminiscence to your sentence.

explanation: This is a specific application of noun modifiers where verbs describe common structural nouns like 사람 (person), 것 (thing), or 데 (place/situation).

rules.title: Modifier + 사람/것/데
rules.description: Use the standard modifier rules (-는, -(으)ㄴ, -(으)ㄹ) before the noun.

examples.english: 아픈 데 없어요? — Is there any place that hurts?
모르는 것이 많아요 — There are many things I don't know.
저를 가르치는 사람이에요 — That is the person who teaches me.

notes.title: 데 as 'situation'
notes.text: '데' usually means 'place', but it can also mean 'situation' or 'case': 옷을 사는 데 돈을 다 썼어요 (I spent all my money on buying clothes).

explanation: -(으)ㄴ 적이 있다/없다 means 'have/haven't experienced' doing something. Use it to talk about your past experiences, similar to 'Have you ever...?' in English.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ 적이 있다
rules.description: Attach ㄴ 적이 있다 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 한국에 간 적이 있어요 — I have been to Korea.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 은 적이 있다
rules.description: Attach 은 적이 있다 to stems ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 낙지를 먹은 적이 없어요 — I have never eaten octopus.

notes.title: Pairing with -아/어 보다
notes.text: This is very commonly combined with -아/어 보다 (try doing) to form -아/어 본 적이 있다 (Have the experience of trying). 제주도에 가 본 적이 있어요? (Have you ever tried going to Jeju?).

notes.title: Not for daily routines
notes.text: Do not use this for everyday tasks. Don't say '아침을 먹은 적이 있어요?' to mean 'Did you eat breakfast?'. Use standard past tense for that.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ 지 (시간) 되다 means 'It has been [time] since [action]'. Use it to express how long it has been since you started doing something or since an event occurred.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ 지 되다
rules.description: Attach ㄴ 지 되다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 한국어를 배운 지 1년 됐어요 — It has been 1 year since I learned (started learning) Korean.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 은 지 되다
rules.description: Attach 은 지 되다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 밥을 먹은 지 3시간 됐어요 — It has been 3 hours since I ate.

notes.title: Spacing is important
notes.text: Make sure to leave a space before '지'. It is '(으)ㄴ 지', not '(으)는지'. The latter is a different grammar point entirely!

notes.title: Asking questions
notes.text: You can use this in question form to ask how long it's been: 한국에 온 지 얼마나 됐어요? (How long has it been since you came to Korea?).

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는지 알다/모르다 translates to 'know / don't know whether' or 'know / don't know if'. Use it to express whether you possess the knowledge about a specific fact, who did something, or where something is.

rules.title: Verbs (Present) → + 는지 알다
rules.description: Attach 는지 알다/모르다 to action verbs.

examples.english: 그 사람이 어디에 가는지 알아요? — Do you know where that person is going?

rules.title: Adjectives → + (으)ㄴ지 알다
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ지 알다/모르다 to adjectives.

examples.english: 이게 얼마나 비싼지 몰라요 — You don't know how expensive this is.

rules.title: Past Tense → + 았/었는지
rules.description: For past tense facts, always attach 는지 after the past tense marker 았/었.

examples.english: 누가 피자를 먹었는지 몰라요 — I don't know who ate the pizza.

notes.title: Question words
notes.text: This grammar is almost always paired with question words like 누구 (who), 어디 (where), 언제 (when), or 얼마나 (how much).

explanation: -아/여 있다 expresses a continuous state resulting from a completed action. It means something happened and it remains in that state, like a door that was opened and is STILL open.

rules.title: ㅏ or ㅗ vowels → + 아 있다
rules.description: Use 아 있다 for verbs ending in ㅏ or ㅗ.

examples.english: 학생들이 의자에 앉아 있어요 — The students are (in the state of) sitting on the chairs.

rules.title: Other vowels → + 어 있다
rules.description: Use 어 있다 for other vowels.

examples.english: 문이 엽려 있어요 — The door is open.

notes.title: vs -고 있다
notes.text: -고 있다 is the active process of doing it (문을 열고 있어요 = I am opening the door right now). -아/어 있다 is the quiet result (문이 엽려 있어요 = The door is resting in an open state).

notes.title: Only for passive or state verbs
notes.text: You cannot use this with active verbs that require a direct object. You can't say '밥을 먹어 있다' (I am in a state of having eaten). It is mostly for posture (sit, stand) or passive verbs (opened, broken).

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 때 means 'when' or 'at the time of'. Similar to ID 89, it is the standard way to express the time or condition during which an action or state occurs.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 때
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 때 if the stem ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 슬플 때 이 노래를 들어요 — I listen to this song when I am sad.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 때
rules.description: Attach 을 때 if the stem ends in a consonant.

examples.english: 사진을 찍을 때 웃으세요 — Please smile when taking a picture.

notes.title: Contextual usage
notes.text: While ID 89 introduced the general rule, remember that -(으)ㄹ 때 can be used for both specific past moments (어렸을 때) and general facts (피곤할 때).

explanation: -기 시작하다 means 'to begin to' or 'to start doing'. It highlights the moment an action initiates.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 기 시작하다
rules.description: Attach 기 시작하다 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 비가 내리기 시작했어요 — It started to rain.
어제부터 일하기 시작했어요 — I started working yesterday.

notes.title: Very straightforward
notes.text: This grammar has no complex rules for vowels or consonants. Just attach it to the stem and conjugate 시작하다 based on the tense.

explanation: -게 되다 means 'to become', 'to get to', or 'to end up doing'. It indicates a change in situation or circumstances, often due to outside forces rather than your own will.

rules.title: Verb/Adjective Stem + 게 되다
rules.description: Attach 게 되다 directly to the stem.

examples.english: 그 소식을 알게 되었어요 — I got to know (found out) the news.
매운 음식을 잘 먹게 됐어요 — I became able to eat spicy food well.

notes.title: Natural flow of events
notes.text: Koreans love using -게 되다 to sound humble. Instead of saying 'I got a job in Korea', saying '한국에서 일하게 됐어요' (I ended up getting to work in Korea) sounds more modest, attributing the success to circumstances.

explanation: -아/어지다 is attached to adjectives to mean 'to become[adjective]'. It describes a gradual change of state over time, like 'getting cold' or 'becoming pretty'.

rules.title: ㅏ or ㅗ vowels → + 아지다
rules.description: Add 아지다 to adjective stems ending in ㅏ or ㅗ.

examples.english: 날씨가 좋아졌어요 — The weather has gotten better.

rules.title: Other vowels → + 어지다
rules.description: Add 어지다 to other vowels.

examples.english: 머리가 길어졌어요 — My hair got longer.

rules.title: 하다 → 해지다
rules.description: 하다 adjectives become 해지다.

examples.english: 바람이 시원해졌어요 — The wind became cool.

notes.title: Usually past tense
notes.text: Because the change of state has usually already happened by the time you notice it, this is most commonly used in the past tense (-아/어졌어요).

notes.title: Adjectives only!
notes.text: This specific meaning ('to become') only applies to adjectives. If you attach -아/어지다 to an ACTION verb, it becomes the passive voice (e.g., 만들어지다 - to be made).

explanation: -아/어 보이다 means 'to look' or 'to seem'. You attach it to adjectives to express your impression of someone or something based on its visual appearance.

rules.title: ㅏ or ㅗ vowels → + 아 보이다
rules.description: Use 아 보이다 for adjectives ending in ㅏ or ㅗ.

examples.english: 옷이 작아 보여요 — The clothes look small.

rules.title: Other vowels → + 어 보이다
rules.description: Use 어 보이다 for other vowels.

examples.english: 케이크가 맛있어 보여요 — The cake looks delicious.

rules.title: 하다 → 핮 보이다
rules.description: 하다 adjectives become 핮 보이다.

examples.english: 오늘 피곤해 보여요 — You look tired today.

notes.title: Only visual
notes.text: This grammar strictly means 'LOOKS like'. You cannot use it for things you hear, smell, or deduce logically. For general 'seems like', use -(으)ㄴ/는 것 같다 instead.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는/(으)ㄹ 줄 알다/모르다 translates to 'to know/not know THAT [fact]'. Unlike ID 94 which is about knowing 'how to' do a skill, this is about possessing knowledge of a situation.

rules.title: Present Verb → + 는 줄 알다
rules.description: Attach 는 줄 알다 to a present action.

examples.english: 비가 오는 줄 알았어요? — Did you know that it is raining?

rules.title: Adjectives / Past Verbs → + (으)ㄴ 줄 알다
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 줄 알다 to adjectives or past verbs.

examples.english: 이게 비싼 줄 몰랐어요 — I didn't know that this was expensive.

notes.title: Skill vs Fact
notes.text: 수영할 줄 알아요? = Do you know HOW to swim? (Skill). 수영하는 줄 알아요? = Do you know THAT he is swimming? (Fact).

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 줄 알았다/몰랐다 expresses an expectation vs reality. It means 'I thought that it would...' (but it didn't) or 'I didn't think that it would...' (but it did).

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 줄 알았다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 줄 알았다/몰랐다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 비가 올 줄 알았어요 — I thought it would rain (but it didn't / or confirming a guess).

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 줄 몰랐다
rules.description: Attach 을 줄 몰랐다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 이렇게 어려울 줄 몰랐어요 — I didn't think/know it would be this hard (but it is).

notes.title: Expressing surprise
notes.text: This is the most natural way to express surprise in Korean when reality doesn't match your expectations. 'I had no idea you would come!' = 올 줄 몰랐어요!

exceptions.name: Irregular ㅂ
exceptions.description: ㅂ changes to 우 before ㄹ.
exceptions.example: 어렵다 → 어려울 줄 알았어요 (I thought it would be difficult).

explanation: -기도 하다 means 'sometimes do' or 'also do'. It implies that while a certain action or state is not the primary one, it does occur on occasion or is an additional trait.

rules.title: Verb/Adjective Stem + 기도 하다
rules.description: Attach 기도 하다 directly to the stem.

examples.english: 가끔 슬플 때 울기도 해요 — Sometimes, when I'm sad, I also cry.
그 신발은 편하고 예쁘기도 해요 — Those shoes are comfortable, and they are pretty too.

notes.title: Adding emphasis
notes.text: By adding the particle 도 (also/even) to the nominalized verb (기), you are essentially saying 'the act of doing this ALSO happens'.

explanation: -기 쉽다 / 어렵다 means 'it is easy to' or 'it is hard to' do something. It evaluates the difficulty level of an action.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 기 쉽다/어렵다
rules.description: Attach 기 쉽다 or 기 어렵다 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 이 앱은 사용하기 쉬워요 — This app is easy to use.
그 책은 이해하기 어려워요 — That book is difficult to understand.

notes.title: Versatile combinations
notes.text: You can replace 쉽다/어렵다 with many other adjectives: -기 좋다 (good for doing), -기 편하다 (comfortable/convenient to do), -기 싫다 (hate doing).

explanation: -네요 is an exclamation ending. Use it when you are expressing mild surprise, realization, or admiration about something you are directly experiencing or noticing in the present moment.

rules.title: Verb/Adjective Stem + 네요
rules.description: Attach 네요 directly to the stem, regardless of vowels or consonants.

examples.english: 와, 이 케이크 정말 맛있네요! — Wow, this cake is really delicious! (Tasting it right now)
생각보다 비싸네요 — It is more expensive than I thought. (Looking at the price tag)

notes.title: Direct experience only
notes.text: You can only use -네요 for things you are perceiving yourself right now. You cannot use it for things you merely heard about from someone else.

notes.title: Agreeing with someone
notes.text: If someone says '오늘 날씨 좋죠?' (The weather is nice today, right?), you can agree by saying '네, 정말 좋네요!' (Yes, it really is!).

exceptions.name: ㄹ drop
exceptions.description: If the stem ends in ㄹ, the ㄹ is dropped before adding 네요.

explanation: -군요 and -(는)구나 are exclamation endings used when you discover a new fact. While -네요 is for direct sensory experience, you use this when you suddenly realize something, often after hearing new information or figuring something out.

rules.title: Adjectives → + 군요 / 구나
rules.description: Attach 군요 (polite) or 구나 (casual) directly to adjective stems.

examples.english: 아, 이 시계는 아주 비싸군요 — Ah, so this watch is very expensive (I just realized).

rules.title: Verbs → + 는군요 / 는구나
rules.description: Attach 는군요 (polite) or 는구나 (casual) to action verb stems.

examples.english: 한국에 사는군요! — Oh, so you live in Korea! (I just found out).

rules.title: Past Tense → 았/었군요
rules.description: For past tense, attach 군요/구나 to the past tense marker (for both verbs and adjectives).

examples.english: 어제 야근했군요 — Ah, I see you worked late yesterday.

notes.title: Talking to yourself
notes.text: -(는)구나 is extremely common when muttering to yourself upon realizing something. (e.g., 비가 오는구나... - Ah, it's raining...).

notes.title: Nouns
notes.text: For nouns, use (이)군요 / (이)구나. 학생이군요 (Ah, you're a student).

exceptions.name: ㄹ drop (Verbs)
exceptions.description: Action verbs ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ before 는군요.

explanation: -지요? (often shortened to -죠?) is a tag question ending meaning 'right?' or 'isn't it?'. Use it when you are already pretty sure of the answer and just want the listener to agree or confirm.

rules.title: Verb/Adjective Stem + 지요?
rules.description: Attach 지요? or 죠? directly to the stem.

examples.english: 오늘 날씨가 춥지요? — The weather is cold today, isn't it?
제 이름 알죠? — You know my name, right?

rules.title: Past/Future + 지요?
rules.description: Attach 지요? after tense markers.

examples.english: 어제 학교에 갔죠? — You went to school yesterday, right?

notes.title: Softening a statement
notes.text: Even when not asking a question, Koreans end sentences with -죠 to sound friendly, cooperative, and soft (e.g., 제가 할게요 -> 제가 하죠 = Sure, I'll do it).

notes.title: Nouns
notes.text: For nouns, use (이)지요? / (이)죠? (e.g., 학생이죠? = You're a student, right?).

explanation: -(ㄴ/는)가(요)? is a soft, indirect way to ask a question. Instead of asking directly with -아/어요?, using this makes you sound gentler, more polite, and less demanding.

rules.title: Verbs → + 는가(요)?
rules.description: Attach 는가요 to action verbs.

examples.english: 언제 오시는가요? — When are you coming? (Very soft)

rules.title: Adjectives → + (으)ㄴ가(요)?
rules.description: Attach ㄴ가요 to vowel stems, and 은가요 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 오늘 많이 바쁜가요? — Are you very busy today?
사이즈가 작은가요? — Is the size small?

notes.title: Casual vs Polite
notes.text: Drop the 요 to use it with friends or when wondering to yourself: 이게 진짜 좋은가? (Is this really good?).

notes.title: Interchangeable with -나요?
notes.text: -나요? is another soft question ending that is mostly used for action verbs, but is largely interchangeable with -는가요 in modern Korean.

explanation: -잖아(요) means 'you know...' or 'come on, as you already know...'. Use it to remind the listener of a shared fact, or to gently correct them by pointing out the obvious.

rules.title: Stem + 잖아요
rules.description: Attach 잖아요 directly to the verb or adjective stem.

examples.english: 이 옷이 예쁘잖아요 — Come on, this dress is pretty (and you know it).
어제는 비쌌잖아요 — It was expensive yesterday, remember?

notes.title: Watch your tone
notes.text: If spoken aggressively, -잖아요 can sound annoyed, like 'I TOLD you!'. If spoken softly, it's just a friendly reminder.

notes.title: Origin
notes.text: It comes from -지 않아요 (isn't it?), but contracted to 잖아요 over time to form its own unique nuance.

explanation: -(ㄴ/는)다고(요)? is used to ask for clarification or express surprise at what someone just said. It translates to 'Are you saying that...?' or 'You mean...?'

rules.title: Action Verbs → + ㄴ/는다고요?
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다고요? (vowel) or 는다고요? (consonant) to action verbs.

examples.english: 내일 미국에 간다고요? — You mean you're going to the US tomorrow?!
벌써 다 먹는다고요? — You're saying you're eating it all already?

rules.title: Adjectives & Past Tense → + 다고요?
rules.description: Attach 다고요? directly to adjectives and past tense markers.

examples.english: 오늘 바쁘다고요? — Are you saying you're busy today?
이미 끝났다고요? — You mean it's already finished?!

notes.title: Indirect quotation
notes.text: This grammar is just a question form of the indirect quoting rule (He said that...). You are literally quoting them back to themselves as a question.

explanation: -거든(요) is attached to the end of a sentence to provide a reason or add background information that the listener doesn't know yet. It loosely translates to 'You see...' or 'Because...'.

rules.title: Stem + 거든요
rules.description: Attach 거든요 directly to the stem, regardless of vowels or consonants.

examples.english: 지금 갈 수 없어요. 많이 바쁘거든요 — I can't go right now. I'm very busy, you see.
배 안 고파요. 아까 먹었거든요 — I'm not hungry. I ate earlier, because/you see.

notes.title: Don't use for shared facts
notes.text: Only use this when giving new information the listener is unaware of. If they already know it, use -(으)니까 or -잖아요 instead.

notes.title: Can sound defensive
notes.text: If overused or said with a sharp tone, it can sound defensive or annoyed, like 'Well, excuse me, but I did it already'.

explanation: -자 is the casual (반말) way to say 'Let's'. It is ONLY used with close friends, people younger than you, or family members. Never use this with a boss, elder, or stranger.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 자
rules.description: Attach 자 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 빨리 가자! — Let's go quickly!
점심 먹자 — Let's eat lunch.

notes.title: No adjectives
notes.text: You cannot use -자 with adjectives. You can't say '예쁘자' (Let's be pretty).

notes.title: Negative form
notes.text: To say 'Let's not', use -지 말자. (e.g., 오늘은 만나지 말자 - Let's not meet today).

explanation: -(으)ㅂ시다 is the formal way to say 'Let's'. It is used in groups, meetings, or public situations where you are suggesting a joint action.

rules.title: Vowel ending → + ㅂ시다
rules.description: Attach ㅂ시다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 회의를 시작합시다 — Let's start the meeting.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 읍시다
rules.description: Attach 읍시다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 같이 밥을 먹읍시다 — Let's eat a meal together.

notes.title: Not for elders or bosses
notes.text: Even though it is formal, -(으)ㅂ시다 sounds mildly authoritative. Do not say '갑시다' to your boss or grandparents. Instead, use -(으)실까요? or -아/어요.

notes.title: Negative form
notes.text: To say 'Let's not', use -지 맙시다. (e.g., 싸우지 맙시다 - Let's not fight).

exceptions.name: ㄹ drop
exceptions.description: Verbs ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ and take ㅂ시다.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ까요? means 'Shall we?' or 'I wonder...'. Use it to suggest doing something together, ask for someone's opinion, or wonder out loud.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ까요
rules.description: Attach ㄹ까요 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 내일 같이 영화 볼까요? — Shall we go watch a movie together tomorrow?
창문을 열까요? — Shall I open the window?

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을까요
rules.description: Attach 을까요 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 오늘 점심은 뭐 먹을까요? — What shall we eat for lunch today?

notes.title: Wondering out loud
notes.text: When used with 3rd person subjects or adjectives, it means 'I wonder if...'. (e.g., 내일 비가 올까요? = I wonder if it will rain tomorrow?).

explanation: -(으)ㄹ래요? is a friendly, conversational way to ask 'Do you want to...?' or state your own intent 'I will...'. It is softer than -(으)ㄹ 거예요 but should only be used with peers or close acquaintances.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ래요
rules.description: Attach ㄹ래요 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 커피 마실래요? — Do you want to drink coffee?
저는 피곤해서 먼저 잘래요 — I'm tired, so I will sleep first.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을래요
rules.description: Attach 을래요 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 뭐 먹을래요? — What do you want to eat?

notes.title: 1st person vs 2nd person
notes.text: In a statement, the subject must be 'I' (I will do this). In a question, the subject must be 'You' (Do you want to do this?).

notes.title: Too casual for bosses
notes.text: Do not ask your boss '커피 마실래요?'. Use '커피 드시겠어요?' instead.

explanation: -아/어도 돼요? means 'Is it okay if I...?' or 'May I...?'. It is the standard structure for asking permission politely.

rules.title: ㅏ or ㅗ vowels → + 아도 돼요?
rules.description: Attach 아도 돼요 to vowel stems ending in ㅏ or ㅗ.

examples.english: 여기 앉아도 돼요? — May I sit here?

rules.title: Other vowels → + 어도 돼요?
rules.description: Attach 어도 돼요 for other vowels.

examples.english: 창문을 열어도 돼요? — Is it okay if I open the window?

rules.title: 하다 → 해도 돼요?
rules.description: 하다 verbs become 해도 돼요.

examples.english: 하나 질문해도 돼요? — May I ask one question?

notes.title: Granting permission
notes.text: To reply yes, just remove the question mark: 네, 앉아도 돼요 (Yes, you may sit).

notes.title: Review
notes.text: This relies on the grammar -아/어도 (even if) + 되다 (to be okay/possible). Literally: 'Even if I do this, is it okay?'

explanation: Korean question words (누구, 무엇, 어디, 언제, 왜, 어떻게, 어느, 몇) behave just like nouns. They can take particles like 이/가, 은/는, and 을/를 depending on their role in the sentence.

rules.title: 누구 (Who)
rules.description: When used as a subject, 누구 + 가 becomes 누가. When used as an object, it's 누구를.

examples.english: 누가 피자를 먹었어요? — Who ate the pizza?

rules.title: 무엇 / 뭐 (What)
rules.description: 무엇 is formal, 뭐 is conversational.

examples.english: 뭘 보고 있어요? — What are you looking at?

rules.title: 어디 / 언제 / 왜 (Where, When, Why)
rules.description: Used directly in the sentence, often without particles.

examples.english: 어디 가요? — Where are you going?
언제 도착해요? — When do you arrive?

notes.title: 어느 vs 무슨
notes.text: 어느 (Which) is used to choose between specific options (어느 나라 - which country). 무슨 (What kind of) is open-ended (무슨 책 - what kind of book).

notes.title: 몇 (How many)
notes.text: 몇 must always be followed by a counter word. 몇 시 (what time), 몇 명 (how many people).

explanation: These are indefinite pronouns used to mean 'anybody', 'nobody', 'everybody', etc. They are created by combining question words with particles like -나 or -도.

rules.title: Question Word + (이)나 (Anyone, Anything)
rules.description: Adding -(이)나 creates a positive, inclusive meaning ('any-').

examples.english: 누구나 할 수 있어요 — Anyone can do it.
아무 데나 앉으세요 — Sit anywhere.

rules.title: Question Word + 도 (No one, Nothing)
rules.description: Adding -도 MUST be followed by a negative verb (없다, 안, 못).

examples.english: 방에 아무도 없어요 — There is nobody in the room.
아무것도 안 먹었어요 — I didn't eat anything.

notes.title: Never use 아무도 with positives
notes.text: You cannot say '아무도 왔어요' to mean someone came. 아무도 requires a negative verb.

notes.title: 모든 (Every)
notes.text: 모든 is used to mean 'every' or 'all' and must precede a noun. 모든 사람 (everyone), 모든 것 (everything).

explanation: -아/어지다 transforms an active verb into a passive verb. It means an action is 'being done' or 'gets done' to the subject, without explicitly stating who is doing it.
