rules.title: ㅏ or ㅗ vowels → + 아지다
rules.description: Attach 아지다 to action verbs with ㅏ or ㅗ.

examples.english: 종이가 잘라졌어요 — The paper got cut.

rules.title: Other vowels → + 어지다
rules.description: Attach 어지다 to other vowels.

examples.english: 이 가방은 이탈리아에서 만들어졌어요 — This bag was made in Italy.
전화가 끊어졌어요 — The phone call got disconnected.

notes.title: Adjectives vs Verbs
notes.text: Remember ID 131! When -아/어지다 attaches to an ADJECTIVE (예뻐지다), it means 'to become'. When attached to a VERB (만들어지다), it creates the passive voice.

notes.title: Usually past tense
notes.text: Because passive states are usually observed after they happen, this is mostly used in the past tense (-아/어졌어요).

explanation: -게 하다 is the causative form. It means 'to make someone do something' or 'to let/allow someone to do something'. You are the outside force causing the action.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 게 하다
rules.description: Attach 게 하다 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 엄마가 아이에게 따뜻한 옷을 입게 했어요 — The mom made (or let) the child wear warm clothes.
오래 기다리게 해서 죄송합니다 — I'm sorry for making you wait a long time.

notes.title: Make vs Let
notes.text: Depending on the context, this can mean forcing someone to do it, or giving them permission to do it. The context clarifies which one it is.

notes.title: vs -게 되다
notes.text: -게 하다 means YOU caused it. -게 되다 (ID 130) means FATE or circumstances caused it to happen.

explanation: These are short, irregular suffixes (-이-, -히-, -리-, -기-) inserted into the middle of a verb to make it either Passive (to be done) or Causative (to make someone do). They must be memorized individually.

rules.title: Passive Examples (To be done)
rules.description: The subject receives the action.

examples.english: 산이 보여요 — The mountain is seen / can be seen.
문이 열렸어요 — The door opened (was opened).

rules.title: Causative Examples (Make someone do)
rules.description: The subject forces or helps someone else do the action.

examples.english: 아기에게 밥을 먹였어요 — I fed (made eat) the baby.
손님을 의자에 앉혔어요 — I seated (made sit) the guest.

notes.title: No strict rules
notes.text: There is no phonetic rule to guess whether a verb takes 이, 히, 리, or 기. You simply have to learn these as new vocabulary words.

explanation: -도록 하다 means 'to make sure that you do...' or 'to try your best to do...'. It is a formal, polite way to instruct someone or to promise that you will do something.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 도록 하다
rules.description: Attach 도록 하다 to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 내일은 늦지 않도록 하세요 — Please make sure you are not late tomorrow.
오늘까지 끝내록 하겠습니다 — I will make sure to finish it by today.

notes.title: Polite commands
notes.text: Saying -도록 하세요 is softer and more professional than just saying -(으)세요. You will hear doctors say 약을 잘 드록 하세요 (Make sure to take your medicine).

explanation: -시키다 is a causative verb ending attached primarily to noun-based verbs. It replaces '하다' to mean 'to make someone do [Noun]' or 'to order[Noun]'.

rules.title: Noun + 시키다
rules.description: Replace 하다 with 시키다.

examples.english: 엄마가 아이를 억지로 공부시켰어요 — The mom forced the child to study.
부모님을 실망시키고 싶지 않아요 — I don't want to disappoint (make disappointed) my parents.

notes.title: Ordering food
notes.text: In daily life, 시키다 is most commonly used to mean 'to order food' at a restaurant (e.g., 피자를 시킬까요? = Shall we order pizza?).

notes.title: Not for native verbs
notes.text: You cannot attach 시키다 to native Korean verbs like 먹다 or 가다. Use -게 하다 for those.

explanation: -아/어 놓다 and -아/어 두다 both mean to do an action in advance and leave it in that completed state for later use. It implies preparation or maintaining a state.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 아/어 놓다(두다)
rules.description: Use standard 아/어 conjugation rules before attaching 놓다 or 두다.

examples.english: 환기를 위해 창문을 열어 놓았어요 — I opened the window (and left it open) for ventilation.
미리 호텔을 예약해 두었어요 — I booked the hotel in advance (and kept the reservation).

notes.title: 놓다 vs 두다
notes.text: They are nearly identical. 두다 implies keeping the state for a slightly longer term, while 놓다 is slightly more casual and short-term.

notes.title: Spoken contraction
notes.text: In fast spoken Korean, -아/어 놓다 often shrinks. (e.g., 해 놓았어요 -> 해 놨어요).

explanation: -아/어 버리다 indicates that an action is completely finished. It often carries an emotional undertone: either relief that a burden is gone, or regret/sadness that something is irreparably lost.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 아/어 버리다
rules.description: Use standard 아/어 rules and attach 버리다.

examples.english: 동생이 제 케이크를 다 먹어 버렸어요 — My younger sibling ate up my entire cake (regret/anger).
숙제를 빨리 끝내 버렸어요 — I finished off my homework quickly (relief).

notes.title: Common vocabulary
notes.text: The word 잊어버리다 (to forget) and 잃어버리다 (to lose) are built using this grammar. They imply the information or item is completely gone.

explanation: -아/어 오다 and -아/어 가다 express actions continuing through time. -아/어 오다 means you 'have been doing' it from the past up to now. -아/어 가다 means you 'will continue doing' it into the future, or it is nearing completion.

rules.title: Past to Present → 아/어 오다
rules.description: Attach 아/어 오다 for actions that started in the past and continue today.

examples.english: 10년 동안 한국어를 공부해 왔어요 — I have been studying Korean for 10 years.

rules.title: Present to Future → 아/어 가다
rules.description: Attach 아/어 가다 for ongoing progression toward an end point.

examples.english: 숙제가 거의 다 끝나 가요 — The homework is almost finishing up (nearing the end).

notes.title: Time travel
notes.text: Literally, 오다 (come) means time is 'coming' to you from the past. 가다 (go) means time is 'going' away from you into the future.

explanation: -들 is the plural marker for nouns. However, unlike English, Korean does not strictly require plural markers. If the context makes it clear there is more than one, you can usually drop it.

rules.title: Noun + 들
rules.description: Attach 들 directly to the noun.

examples.english: 여기에 사람들이 많아요 — There are many people here.
학생들이 공부하고 있어요 — The students are studying.

notes.title: Don't use with numbers
notes.text: If you already specify a number or a word like 'many' (많이), you do not need 들. '사과 3개' (3 apples) is perfect. '사과들 3개' sounds unnatural.

notes.title: Mostly for people
notes.text: Koreans primarily use 들 for people or animals. They rarely attach it to inanimate objects like chairs or books.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 걸(요) is used to express an unconfirmed assumption, meaning 'It probably is...' or 'I bet...'. It is a casual way to state your guess to a friend.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 걸(요)
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 걸요 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 그 식당은 너무 비쌀 걸요? — I bet that restaurant is too expensive.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 걸(요)
rules.description: Attach 을 걸요 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 사이즈가 좀 작을 걸요 — The size will probably be a bit small.

rules.title: Past Tense → + 았/었을 걸(요)
rules.description: You can attach this to the past tense to guess about a past event.

examples.english: 지금쯤 도착했을 걸요? — They probably arrived by now.

notes.title: Not for facts
notes.text: This grammar strictly shows a guess. If you actually know for sure, use -(으)ㄹ 거예요 instead.

notes.title: Intonation matters
notes.text: The sentence often ends with a slight rising intonation, like you are tossing your opinion out for consideration.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 테니(까) has two different uses. The first use is to volunteer for a task while asking the listener to do something else. It translates to 'I will do X, so you do Y.'

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 테니까
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 테니까 to vowel stems. The subject must be 'I'.

examples.english: 제가 먼저 갈 테니까 천천히 오세요 — I will go first, so please come slowly.
제가 청소할 테니까 쉬세요 — I will clean, so you rest.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 테니까
rules.description: Attach 을 테니까 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 제가 식당을 찾을 테니까 걱정하지 마세요 — I will find a restaurant, so don't worry.

notes.title: Commands and suggestions
notes.text: The second half of the sentence is almost always a command (-(으)세요) or a suggestion (-(으)ㅂ시다).

notes.title: First person only
notes.text: For this specific 'intention' meaning, the person doing the action before 테니까 MUST be you.

explanation: -고 보면 means 'If you do [Verb] and then reflect on it' or 'If you look at it after doing'. Use it to express a realization or a truth you discover only after a continuous action.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 고 보면
rules.description: Attach 고 보면 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 알고 보면 좋은 사람이에요 — If you get to know him (and look closely), he is a good person.
살고 보면 다 익숙해져요 — As you live (and experience it), you get used to everything.

notes.title: vs -고 보니(까)
notes.text: -고 보면 is for general truths or future conditional realizations (If you do this, you will realize). -고 보니까 is for a specific past realization (I did this, and I realized).

explanation: -고 보니(까) means 'Having done [Verb], I realized that...'. Use it to share a sudden discovery or realization that occurred directly after completing an action.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 고 보니까
rules.description: Attach 고 보니까 to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 먹고 보니까 유통기한이 지났어요 — Having eaten it, I realized it was expired.
그 말을 듣고 보니 네 말이 맞네 — Having heard that, I realize you are right.

notes.title: Often shortened
notes.text: In conversation, the '까' is frequently dropped, leaving just -고 보니.

notes.title: No past tense before it
notes.text: Do not attach the past tense marker (-았/었-) before 고 보니까. Even though the action is in the past, it is attached to the dictionary stem.

explanation: -다가 indicates an interruption. It means 'while doing A, I stopped and shifted to doing B'. The action in the first half is cut short or transitions into the second half.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 다가
rules.description: Attach 다가 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 학교에 가다가 친구를 만났어요 — While going to school (I stopped/transitioned), I met a friend.
공부하다가 잠이 들었어요 — While studying, I fell asleep.

notes.title: Same subject
notes.text: The subject of the first action must be the exact same as the subject of the second action.

notes.title: vs -(으)면서
notes.text: -(으)면서 means doing two things at the exact same time (eating while watching TV). -다가 means shifting from one to another (watching TV, then stopping to eat).

explanation: -다가(는) acts as a warning. It translates to 'If you keep doing[Verb], a negative result will happen'. The 는 adds emphasis to the warning.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 다가는
rules.description: Attach 다가는 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 계속 놀다가는 시험에 떨어질 거예요 — If you keep playing around, you will fail the test.
이렇게 많이 먹다가는 배탈이 날 거예요 — If you keep eating this much, you'll get a stomachache.

notes.title: Always negative
notes.text: The second half of the sentence using -다가는 will always contain a negative consequence or a bad prediction.

explanation: -(ㄴ/는)다면 is the hypothetical 'if'. While -(으)면 is for normal conditions (If it rains...), this grammar is used for unlikely, dramatic, or entirely imaginary scenarios.

rules.title: Action Verbs → + ㄴ/는다면
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다면 to vowel stems and 는다면 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 다시 태어난다면 새가 되고 싶어요 — If I were born again, I'd want to be a bird.

rules.title: Adjectives & Past Tense → + 다면
rules.description: Attach 다면 directly to adjectives or the past tense marker.

examples.english: 그게 너무 비싸다면 안 살게요 — If (it turns out that) it's too expensive, I won't buy it.
어제 거기에 갔다면 그를 만났을 거예요 — If you had gone there yesterday, you would have met him.

notes.title: With nouns
notes.text: For nouns, use (이)라면. (e.g., 내가 부자라면... = If I were rich...).

explanation: N-(이)라도 means 'at least [Noun]' or 'even [Noun]'. Use it to suggest a second-best option when your first choice isn't available, showing that it will suffice.

rules.title: Noun ending in a vowel → + 라도
rules.description: Attach 라도 to nouns without a final consonant.

examples.english: 밥이 없으면 커피라도 마실래요? — If there's no food, do you want to at least drink coffee?

rules.title: Noun ending in a consonant → + 이라도
rules.description: Attach 이라도 to nouns with a final consonant.

examples.english: 라면이라도 먹자 — Let's at least eat ramen (since there's nothing else).

notes.title: With question words
notes.text: When combined with question words, it means 'any'. 누구라도 (anyone), 언제라도 (anytime), 어디라도 (anywhere).

explanation: -뿐(만 아니라) means 'Not only A, but also B'. It shows that the situation includes the first item and extends to an additional item.

rules.title: Noun + 뿐만 아니라
rules.description: Attach 뿐만 아니라 directly to nouns.

examples.english: 한국어뿐만 아니라 영어도 잘해요 — He speaks not only Korean, but also English well.

rules.title: Verb + (으)ㄹ 뿐만 아니라
rules.description: Attach ㄹ/을 뿐만 아니라 to verb stems.

examples.english: 그녀는 똑똑할 뿐만 아니라 친절해요 — She is not only smart, but also kind.

notes.title: Using 뿐이다 alone
notes.text: If you drop '만 아니라' and just use 'N-뿐이다', it means 'There is ONLY [Noun]'. (e.g., 나에겐 너뿐이야 = I only have you).

explanation: -조차 and -마저 both translate to 'even', but they are used in extreme or negative situations. They emphasize that out of all possibilities, even the most basic or unexpected thing is included.

rules.title: Noun + 조차
rules.description: Often used with a negative ending to mean 'not even the most basic thing'.

examples.english: 너무 당황해서 이름조차 기억나지 않았어요 — I was so panicked I couldn't even remember my name.

rules.title: Noun + 마저
rules.description: Often implies that the LAST remaining thing has also crossed over to a negative state.

examples.english: 너마저 나를 떠나는구나 — Even you (the last person I trusted) are leaving me.

notes.title: vs -도
notes.text: -도 is the normal 'also/even'. -조차 and -마저 are dramatic and carry heavy emotional weight, usually sadness, shock, or disappointment.

explanation: -대신(에) means 'instead of'. You can use it to swap one noun for another, or to compensate for an action.

rules.title: Noun + 대신(에)
rules.description: Attach 대신(에) directly to a noun.

examples.english: 커피 대신에 차를 마실게요 — I will drink tea instead of coffee.

rules.title: Verb + 는 대신(에)
rules.description: Attach 는 대신(에) to an action verb.

examples.english: 제가 도와주는 대신에 밥을 사 주세요 — Instead of (in exchange for) me helping you, buy me a meal.

notes.title: Dropping 에
notes.text: The '에' is completely optional and frequently dropped in spoken Korean. '커피 대신' sounds perfectly natural.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는 데(에) 좋다/나쁘다 means 'to be good/bad for doing [Verb]'. Here, '데' means 'situation' or 'the case of'.

rules.title: Action Verbs → + 는 데 좋다
rules.description: Attach 는 데 좋다/나쁘다 to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 이 차는 잠을 자는 데에 좋아요 — This tea is good for sleeping.
드라마는 한국어 배우는 데 도움이 돼요 — Dramas are helpful for learning Korean.

notes.title: Spacing is crucial!
notes.text: You MUST put a space before 데 (는 데). If you write it together (-는데), it becomes the grammar point for 'but/and' (ID 79).

notes.title: Other adjectives
notes.text: You can use this with other adjectives like 필요하다 (This is necessary for...) or 쓰다 (I spent money on/for...).

explanation: -는 법이다 translates to 'It is a rule that...' or 'It is natural that...'. Use it to state a universal truth, a law of nature, or a proverb.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 는 법이다
rules.description: Attach 는 법이다 to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 시간이 지나면 잊혀지는 법이에요 — It is natural to be forgotten as time passes.
물가는 항상 오르는 법이에요 — It is a rule (fact of life) that prices always go up.

notes.title: Literal meaning
notes.text: 법 literally means 'law' or 'rule'. So you are saying 'It is the law of the universe that...'

explanation: -는 척하다 means 'to pretend to'. Use it when someone is faking an action or a state to deceive others.

rules.title: Present Verb → + 는 척하다
rules.description: Attach 는 척하다 to an action verb happening right now.

examples.english: 아기가 자는 척해요 — The baby is pretending to sleep.

rules.title: Adjectives & Past Verbs → + (으)ㄴ 척하다
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 척하다 to adjectives or past completed actions.

examples.english: 모를면서 아는 척하지 마세요 — Don't pretend to know when you don't.
친한 척했어요 — We pretended to be close.

notes.title: Interchangeable with 체하다
notes.text: You will sometimes see -는 체하다 in books. It means the exact same thing as -는 척하다, but 척하다 is much more common in speaking.

exceptions.name: ㄹ drop
exceptions.description: Verbs ending in ㄹ drop it before adding ㄴ 척하다.
exceptions.example: 알다 (to know) → 아는 척하다 (pretend to know).

explanation: -는 통에 and -(으)ㄴ 탓에 both mean 'because of [X]', but they exclusively carry a negative, blaming nuance. -는 통에 specifically implies a noisy, chaotic, or distracting situation.

rules.title: Action Verb + 는 통에
rules.description: Use for a chaotic, distracting action causing a bad result.

examples.english: 사람들이 떠드는 통에 집중할 수 없었어요 — Because people were chatting loudly, I couldn't focus.

rules.title: Adjectives/Verbs + (으)ㄴ/는 탓에
rules.description: Use 탓에 to directly point the finger of blame at a cause.

examples.english: 비가 오는 탓에 행사가 취소됐어요 — The event was canceled due to the rain.

notes.title: Never use for good results
notes.text: Do not say 'I passed the test 탓에/통에'. Blame words are only for bad outcomes.

notes.title: With Nouns
notes.text: You can attach 탓에 directly to nouns. (e.g., 날씨 탓에 = due to the weather).

explanation: -는 덕분에 is the exact opposite of 탓에. It means 'thanks to' and is exclusively used when a positive cause brings about a positive, beneficial result.

rules.title: Past Verbs → + (으)ㄴ 덕분에
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 덕분에 to a past helpful action.

examples.english: 도와주신 덕분에 빨리 끝났어요 — Thanks to you helping me, it finished quickly.

rules.title: Nouns → + 덕분에
rules.description: Attach 덕분에 directly to nouns.

examples.english: 선생님 덕분에 합격했어요 — Thanks to the teacher, I passed.

notes.title: Not for negative results
notes.text: It sounds very sarcastic if you use this for a bad outcome. Don't say 'Thanks to the rain, we couldn't go'.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는 걸 보니(까) means 'Judging from the fact that...'. Use it when you observe a piece of visual evidence and draw a logical conclusion from it.

rules.title: Action Verbs → + 는 걸 보니까
rules.description: Attach 는 걸 보니까 to an action you are observing.

examples.english: 웃는 걸 보니까 기분이 좋은가 봐요 — Judging from the fact that he's smiling, he seems to be in a good mood.

rules.title: Adjectives → + (으)ㄴ 걸 보니까
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 걸 보니까 to state an observed fact.

examples.english: 사람이 많은 걸 보니까 맛집인가 봐요 — Judging from the fact that there are many people, it must be a good restaurant.

notes.title: Natural pairings
notes.text: The second half of the sentence is almost always a guess, so it pairs perfectly with -나 보다, -(으)ㄹ 것 같다, or -(으)ㄹ 텐데.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ 채(로) means 'while in the state of having done [X]'. It is used to describe doing a second action while maintaining the physical state or result of the first action.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ 채로
rules.description: Attach ㄴ 채로 to vowel stems.

examples.english: TV를 켠 채로 잠이 들었어요 — I fell asleep with the TV (left) on.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 은 채로
rules.description: Attach 은 채로 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 신발을 신은 채로 방에 들어갔어요 — I went into the room with my shoes on.

notes.title: vs -(으)면서
notes.text: -(으)면서 is for two active motions happening at once (eating while walking). -(으)ㄴ 채로 is for a frozen state (eyes closed, glasses on, door open) while doing something else.

explanation: -곤 하다 expresses a habit. It means 'I often do...' or 'I tend to...'. It shows that an action is repeated frequently, but not necessarily on a strict schedule.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 곤 하다
rules.description: Attach 곤 하다 to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 주말에는 혼자 영화를 볼러 가곤 해요 — On weekends, I often go to watch movies alone.

rules.title: Past Tense → + 곤 했다
rules.description: Use the past tense to talk about a habit you used to have, meaning 'used to'.

examples.english: 어렸을 때 이 공원에서 놀곤 했어요 — When I was young, I used to play in this park.

notes.title: Origin
notes.text: -곤 is a contraction of -고는. It literally means 'doing this and then...' implying a recurring sequence.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 만하다 means 'worth doing' or 'bearable'. Use it to recommend something because it has enough value to justify doing it, or to say a bad situation isn't entirely terrible.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 만하다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 만하다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 제주도는 한 번쯤 갈 만해요 — Jeju Island is worth going to at least once.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 만하다
rules.description: Attach 을 만하다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 이 식당 음식은 먹을 만해요 — This restaurant's food is bearable (decent enough to eat).

notes.title: Nuance check
notes.text: Depending on your tone, 먹을 만해요 can mean 'It's a highly recommended dish' OR 'It's not amazing, but it's okay enough to eat'.

explanation: -다시피 means 'as you [know/see/hear]'. It confirms that the listener is already aware of the fact you are about to state.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 다시피
rules.description: Attach 다시피 to verbs related to perception (know, see, hear, feel).

examples.english: 아시다시피, 저는 외국인이에요 — As you know, I am a foreigner.
보시다시피, 비가 많이 오고 있어요 — As you can see, it is raining a lot.

notes.title: Honorifics are standard
notes.text: Because you are usually addressing someone else politely, it is almost always combined with the honorific -(으)시. (알다 -> 아시다시피, 보다 -> 보시다시피).

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는/(으)ㄹ 셈이다 means 'It is practically the same as' or 'It amounts to'. You use this when a situation isn't 100% exactly X, but mathematically or logically, it is so close that you can consider it X.

rules.title: Adjectives & Past Verbs → + (으)ㄴ 셈이다
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 셈이다 to past actions.

examples.english: 일주일에 5번 봤으니까 매일 만난 셈이에요 — Since we saw each other 5 times a week, it's practically like we met every day.

rules.title: Present Verbs → + 는 셈이다
rules.description: Attach 는 셈이다 to present facts.

examples.english: 돈을 안 썼으니까 돈을 버는 셈이에요 — Since I didn't spend money, it's basically the same as earning money.

notes.title: Similar to
notes.text: This is very similar to -와/과 마찬가지다 (It is the same as), but 셈 focuses more on the calculation or logical conclusion.

explanation: -나 보다 / -(으)ㄴ가 보다 means 'It seems like' or 'I guess'. While -(으)ㄹ 것 같다 is just a gut feeling, you use this grammar when you have observed a CLUE or piece of evidence that makes you assume something.

rules.title: Action Verbs → + 나 보다
rules.description: Attach 나 보다 to action verbs.

examples.english: 우산이 있는 걸 보니, 비가 오나 봐요 — Seeing that people have umbrellas, I guess it's raining.

rules.title: Adjectives → + (으)ㄴ가 보다
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ가 보다 to adjectives.

examples.english: 약국에 가는 걸 보니, 아픈가 봐요 — Seeing him go to the pharmacy, he must be sick.

rules.title: Past Tense → + 았/었나 보다
rules.description: Always use 나 보다 after the past tense marker.

examples.english: 불이 꺼진 걸 보니, 끝났나 봐요 — Seeing the lights are off, I guess it ended.

notes.title: Not for personal plans
notes.text: You cannot use this to guess about your own actions. You can't say 'I guess I am eating' (내가 먹나 봐요).

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는/(으)ㄹ 듯하다 means 'It seems as if...'. It is a slightly more formal, literary, or written equivalent of -(으)ㄴ/는 것 같다.

rules.title: Modifiers + 듯하다
rules.description: Use standard noun modifier rules (는, (으)ㄴ, (으)ㄹ) and attach 듯하다.

examples.english: 내일은 비가 올 듯합니다 — It seems as if it will rain tomorrow.
요즘 많이 바쁜 듯해요 — It appears that you are very busy lately.

notes.title: Spoken contraction
notes.text: In conversation, 듯하다 is often shortened to just -는 듯. (e.g., 비가 올 듯 = looks like rain).

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 리가 없다 means 'There is no way that...' or 'It's impossible that...'. Use it to strongly deny a possibility based on your logic or disbelief.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 리가 없다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 리가 없다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 그 사람이 그런 말을 할 리가 없어요 — There is no way that person would say such a thing.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 리가 없다
rules.description: Attach 을 리가 없다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 사이즈가 작을 리가 없어요. 제일 큰 옷이에요 — It's impossible for it to be small. It's the biggest size.

notes.title: Question form
notes.text: You can turn it into a rhetorical question: 그럴 리가요? (How could that be? / No way!).

explanation: The second use of -(으)ㄹ 테니(까) is to express a strong assumption. It means 'I expect/assume it will be X, so you should do Y'. Use it to give advice based on your logical guess.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 테니까
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 테니까 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 지금 바쁠 테니까 나중에 전화하세요 — I assume he is busy right now, so call later.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 테니까
rules.description: Attach 을 테니까 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 퇴근 시간이라 길이 막힐 테니까 지하철을 타세요 — I expect traffic will be bad since it's rush hour, so take the subway.

notes.title: vs. First-person use
notes.text: Remember ID 161! If the subject is 'I', it means 'I WILL do this'. If the subject is anything else (weather, a third person, traffic), it means 'I ASSUME it will be this'.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 텐데(요) means 'I expect/guess that... but/so...'. It is used to show a strong assumption while introducing a related concern, suggestion, or contrasting background information.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 텐데
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 텐데 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 많이 피곤하실 텐데 일찍 쉬세요 — You must be very tired, so please rest early.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 텐데
rules.description: Attach 을 텐데 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 지금 가면 늦을 텐데요... — I expect you'll be late if you go now (trailing off with concern).

notes.title: Trailing off
notes.text: It is extremely common to end sentences with -(으)ㄹ 텐데요. It leaves the sentence open, expressing worry or hesitation without directly stating the bad news.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 걸 그랬다 is the standard way to express regret. It translates to 'I should have done [X]'. It literally implies 'It would have been better if I had done it'.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 걸 그랬다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 걸 그랬다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 세일할 때 살 걸 그랬어요 — I should have bought it when it was on sale.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 걸 그랬다
rules.description: Attach 을 걸 그랬다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 밥을 더 먹을 걸 그랬어요 — I should have eaten more food.

notes.title: Expressing 'I shouldn't have'
notes.text: To regret something you DID do, use the negative -지 말 걸 그랬다. (e.g., 말하지 말 걸 그랬어요 = I shouldn't have said it).

explanation: -기 마련이다 means 'It is bound to happen' or 'It is natural that'. It is used to state inevitable outcomes or general truths of human nature and the world.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 기 마련이다
rules.description: Attach 기 마련이다 directly to the action verb stem.

examples.english: 사람은 누구나 실수하기 마련이에요 — Everyone is bound to make mistakes.
시간이 흐를면 마음도 변하기 마련이죠 — As time passes, it's natural for feelings to change.

notes.title: Similar to
notes.text: It has almost the exact same meaning and usage as -는 법이다 (ID 172). Both point out universal truths.

explanation: -기 십상이다 means 'Very likely to' or 'It is easy to [make a mistake]'. It is almost always used to warn about a negative probability or an easy trap to fall into.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 기 십상이다
rules.description: Attach 기 십상이다 directly to the verb stem.

examples.english: 얇게 입으면 감기에 걸리기 십상이에요 — If you dress lightly, you are very likely to catch a cold.
메모를 안 하면 잊어버리기 십상이에요 — If you don't take notes, it's easy to forget.

notes.title: Etymology
notes.text: 십상 comes from the Hanja phrase '십중팔구' (Eight or nine out of ten). So it literally means a 80-90% chance of a bad thing happening.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 뻔하다 means 'almost did' or 'nearly happened'. You use this when a drastic event almost took place, but you narrowly escaped it. It is always used in the past tense.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 뻔했다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 뻔했다 to vowel stems.

examples.english: 사고가 날 뻔했어요 — I almost got into an accident.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 뻔했다
rules.description: Attach 을 뻔했다 to consonant stems.

examples.english: 너무 매워서 죽을 뻔했어요 — It was so spicy I almost died.

notes.title: Always past tense
notes.text: Because the danger has already been avoided by the time you speak, it must always end in 뻔했다, never 뻔하다.

notes.title: Adding 하마터면
notes.text: You will often hear the adverb 하마터면 (by a hair's breadth) at the start of the sentence for dramatic effect. 하마터면 늦을 뻔했어요! (I was THIS close to being late!).

explanation: -게 하다 means 'make someone do' or 'let someone do.' It expresses causation: the subject causes, allows, or instructs another person to perform an action. Use it when talking about permissions, orders, or indirect actions performed through someone else.

rules.title: V/A-게 하다
rules.description: Attach 게 하다 directly to the verb or adjective stem. The form is the same for all verbs and adjectives. 하다 itself conjugates normally (해요, 했어요, etc.).

examples.english: 선생님이 학생들을 교실에 가게 했어요 — The teacher made the students go to the classroom.
엄마가 아이에게 약을 먹게 했어요 — Mom made the child take the medicine.
선생님이 매일 일기를 쓰게 하셔요 — The teacher has us write a diary every day.

notes.title: Force vs. permission
notes.text: -게 하다 covers both making someone do something (strong) and letting someone do something (permissive). Tone and context tell you which meaning is intended.

notes.title: Cross-reference: passive pair
notes.text: See ID 193 for the passive counterpart (-아/여지다). Causative and passive are often taught together because they transform the direction of the action.

explanation: -아/여지다 is the regular passive ending. It turns an active verb into a passive one, meaning the subject receives the action instead of performing it. Use it to describe something being done by an unspecified agent, or to express possibility ('can be done').

rules.title: V-아지다
rules.description: For verb stems with ㅏ or ㅗ, attach 아지다.

examples.english: 문이 자동으로 닫아져요 — The door closes automatically.
이 책은 한국어로 쓰여졌어요 — This book was written in Korean.

rules.title: V-어지다
rules.description: For verb stems with other vowels, attach 어지다.

examples.english: 이 약은 물 없이도 쉽게 먹어져요 — This medicine can be taken easily even without water.
이 케이크는 집에서도 쉽게 만들어져요 — This cake can be easily made at home, too.

rules.title: V-여지다 / 해지다
rules.description: For 하다 verbs, 하 + 여지다 contracts to 해지다.

examples.english: 이 프로그램은 물론 물에서도 사용해져요 — This program can naturally be used underwater, too.

notes.title: Passive or potential?
notes.text: -아/여지다 can express both passive ('is eaten') and potential ('can be eaten'). Context usually makes the meaning clear.

notes.title: Special passive forms
notes.text: Some verbs have dedicated passive forms that are more common: 팔다 → 팔리다, 듣다 → 들리다, 보이다 (inherent passive). These often appear instead of the regular -아/여지다 form.

notes.title: Cross-reference: causative pair
notes.text: See ID 191 for the causative counterpart (-게 하다).

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅂ change to 우 before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄷ change to ㄹ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅅ drop the ㅅ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㅡ drop
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅡ, the ㅡ drops and the vowel harmonizes.

exceptions.name: 르 irregular
exceptions.description: For 르-ending stems, an extra ㄹ is inserted into the previous syllable before 아/어.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는 대로 has two main uses. First, it means 'as soon as' — the second action happens immediately after the first. Second, it means 'the way' or 'according to' — doing something exactly as instructed, seen, or observed. Use the present form (-는 대로) for future actions or general manner, and the past form (-(으)ㄴ 대로) when the first action is completed or describes a specific past instance.

rules.title: V-는 대로
rules.description: Attach 는 대로 to the verb stem. Use this for 'as soon as' with future actions, or for 'the way' in general or habitual situations.

examples.english: 시험이 끝나는 대로 집에 갈 거예요 — I'll go home as soon as the exam ends.
요리사가 보여주는 대로 따라 하세요 — Follow along just as the chef shows you.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ 대로
rules.description: Attach ㄴ 대로 to stems ending in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ. Use this for 'as soon as' when the first action is completed, or for 'the way' something was done in a specific past instance.

examples.english: 온 대로 돌아가세요 — Go back the way you came.
도착한 대로 연락해 주세요 — Please contact me as soon as you arrive.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 은 대로
rules.description: Attach 은 대로 to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ). Use this for completed actions, just like the rule above.

examples.english: 약을 먹은 대로 효과가 나타났어요 — The effect appeared right after I took the medicine.
들은 대로 전해 드릴게요 — I'll pass on the message exactly as I heard it.

notes.title: Two distinct meanings
notes.text: The same form covers both time ('as soon as') and manner ('the way'). Context usually makes the meaning clear. If the second clause is a sequential action, it's usually 'as soon as'. If the second clause describes imitation or obedience, it's 'the way'.

notes.title: Also works with nouns
notes.text: You can attach 대로 directly to nouns for 'according to': 약속한 대로 (as promised), 원래 대로 (as it was originally), 생각한 대로 (just as I thought).

explanation: -자마자 means 'as soon as' or 'the moment that'. It emphasizes that the second action happens with absolutely no gap after the first action is finished. The subject of both clauses is usually the same person, and it is used only with action verbs.

rules.title: All action verb stems + 자마자
rules.description: Attach 자마자 directly to the verb stem. There are no 받침-related changes.

examples.english: 집에 도착하자마자 잠이 들었어요 — I fell asleep as soon as I arrived home.
밥을 먹자마자 운전하지 마세요 — Don't drive right after eating.
그 사진을 보자마자 눈물이 났어요 — Tears came the moment I saw that photo.

notes.title: No commands or future intentions
notes.text: You cannot use -자마자 with imperative (-세요), propositive (-ㅂ시다), or intention endings like -겠다 in the second clause. It strictly describes something that actually happened.

notes.title: Same subject preferred
notes.text: Both clauses usually share the same subject. If you need different subjects for 'as soon as', use -(으)ㄴ/는 대로 (ID 197) instead.

explanation: -는 길에 means 'on the way to doing something'. Use it when you are going somewhere or doing something, and something else happens incidentally along the way. The main clause usually describes a past event that you noticed or did while moving.

rules.title: All action verb stems + 는 길에
rules.description: Attach 는 길에 to the stem of an action verb. It is most natural with movement verbs like 가다, 오다, and 다니다.

examples.english: 회사에 가는 길에 커피를 샀어요 — I bought coffee on my way to work.
집에 오는 길에 비가 왔어요 — It rained on my way home.
학교에 다니는 길에 늘 그 카페를 지나가요 — I always pass by that cafe on my way to school.

notes.title: Movement context required
notes.text: -는 길에 strongly implies physical movement or going somewhere. It feels unnatural with stationary actions like 공부하다 or 자다 unless there is a clear destination involved.

notes.title: Main clause is usually past tense
notes.text: Since you are reporting what happened along the way, the main verb is most commonly in the past tense. Future tense is possible but far less common.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는 김에 means 'while I'm at it' or 'since I'm already doing this'. Use it when you take advantage of a current situation to do something else that is convenient or related. It carries a light, practical tone of doing two things at once.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ 김에
rules.description: Attach ㄴ 김에 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. This past form is used when the first action is already done or when the state has been reached.

examples.english: 온 김에 밥이라도 먹고 가세요 — Since you're here, at least have a meal before you go.
간 김에 장도 보고 왔어요 — While I was out, I went grocery shopping too.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 은 김에
rules.description: Attach 은 김에 to stems ending in a consonant. Use this for the same 'already done' nuance as above.

examples.english: 점심을 먹은 김에 커피도 마셨어요 — Since I was already having lunch, I had coffee too.
지갑을 찾은 김에 명함도 챙겼어요 — Since I was already looking for my wallet, I grabbed my business cards too.

rules.title: All action verb stems → + 는 김에
rules.description: Attach 는 김에 for ongoing or future actions. This means 'since I'm going to do X anyway'.

examples.english: 나가는 김에 쓰레기도 버려 주세요 — Since you're going out, please take out the trash too.
커피를 마시는 김에 잠깐 이야기해요 — While we're having coffee, let's chat for a bit.

notes.title: Seize the opportunity
notes.text: This grammar carries the nuance of killing two birds with one stone. The second action feels efficient or sensible given the first action. It would sound odd if the two actions were completely unrelated.

notes.title: Casual tone
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ/는 김에 is casual and friendly. It would sound odd in very formal written reports or serious speeches. Use -는 동안에 or -기 때문에 for more neutral alternatives.

explanation: -는 사이에 means 'in the meantime' or 'while'. It describes something that happened during the time that the first action was taking place. Often the second event happens unexpectedly or without the subject noticing, as time simply passed by.

rules.title: All action verb stems + 는 사이에
rules.description: Attach 는 사이에 directly to the verb stem. 사이 literally means 'interval' or 'space between'.

examples.english: 잠깐 자는 사이에 비가 왔어요 — It rained while I was briefly sleeping.
기다리는 사이에 친구를 만났어요 — I met a friend while waiting.
이야기하는 사이에 시간이 다 갔어요 — Time flew by while we were talking.

notes.title: Unexpected results
notes.text: -는 사이에 is perfect for situations where something happened without you paying attention. It emphasizes that time passed unnoticed and something changed in the background.

notes.title: Can be used with nouns too
notes.text: You can say 사이에 after time expressions or nouns: 한 달 사이에 (within a month), 방학 사이에 (during vacation), 그 사이에 (in the meantime).

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는 채(로) means 'while in a state of' or 'while keeping something as-is.' It describes doing something while maintaining the state resulting from a previous action. Use it when you want to emphasize that an action was performed without changing from a particular condition.

rules.title: V-(으)ㄴ 채(로)
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 채(로) to the verb stem. Use this for completed states that are maintained. 받침 있음 → + 은 채(로); 받침 없음 or ㄹ → + ㄴ 채(로).

examples.english: 의자에 앉은 채로 잠이 들었어요 — I fell asleep while sitting in the chair.
두 시간 동안 선 채로 일했어요 — I worked while standing for two hours.
눈을 감은 채로 명상했어요 — I meditated with my eyes closed.

notes.title: 로 is optional
notes.text: Both 앉은 채 and 앉은 채로 are correct. Adding 로 gives a slight nuance of 'in that manner.'

notes.title: Action verbs only
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ/는 채(로) primarily attaches to action verbs. For adjectives describing a maintained state, use A-(으)ㄴ 상태로 instead.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄷ change to ㄹ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄹ ending
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ before ㄴ.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅅ drop the ㅅ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: 르 irregular
exceptions.description: For 르-ending stems, the modifier form becomes 고른, 마른, etc.

explanation: -고 나서 means 'after doing'. It clearly marks that the first action is completely finished before the second action begins. Use it when you want to emphasize the completion and sequence of events, especially when the second action is a reaction or consequence.

rules.title: All action verb stems + 고 나서
rules.description: Attach 고 나서 directly to the verb stem. There are no conjugation changes based on the stem ending.

examples.english: 밥을 먹고 나서 산책했어요 — I took a walk after eating.
영화를 보고 나서 감동받았어요 — I was moved after watching the movie.
집에 가고 나서 연락할게요 — I'll contact you after I get home.

notes.title: Emphasizes completion
notes.text: -고 나서 is stronger than simple -아/어서 for sequence. It insists that the first action is 100% finished. You cannot use it if the actions overlap.

notes.title: Often used with emotional reactions
notes.text: Because it marks a clear endpoint, -고 나서 pairs naturally with reactions or realizations that come after finishing something: 보고 나서 알았어요 (I realized after seeing it).

explanation: -고 나면 means 'once done' or 'once someone does'. It combines the completion meaning of -고 나서 with the conditional meaning of -(으)면. Use it to describe a natural result, discovery, or habitual outcome that follows after completing an action.

rules.title: All action verb stems + 고 나면
rules.description: Attach 고 나면 directly to the verb stem. The 나면 part comes from 나서 + (으)면.

examples.english: 이 영화를 보고 나면 생각이 바뀔 거예요 — Once you see this movie, your thinking will change.
한국에 가고 나면 김치를 매일 먹게 돼요 — Once you go to Korea, you end up eating kimchi every day.
약을 먹고 나면 좀 나아질 거예요 — Once you take the medicine, you'll feel a bit better.

notes.title: Result or discovery
notes.text: -고 나면 is perfect for sentences where the second clause is a natural consequence, new understanding, or predictable outcome. It feels less like a command and more like a statement of cause and effect.

notes.title: See ID 203 for the difference
notes.text: -고 나서 is purely about sequence: 'I did A, then I did B.' -고 나면 adds a conditional nuance: 'Once A is done, B will naturally follow.' Use -고 나면 for predictions, general truths, and habitual outcomes.

explanation: -고 나니(까) means 'having done... I found that' or 'after doing... I realized'. Use it when you discover or realize something new as a natural consequence of completing an action. It often carries a sense of surprise or unexpected realization.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 고 나니(까)
rules.description: Attach 고 나니 or 고 나니까 directly to the verb stem of the completed action. 나니까 is a slightly more causal version, while 나니 is more reflective.

examples.english: 영화를 보고 나니까 기분이 좋아졌어요 — After watching the movie, I found that my mood had improved.
약을 먹고 나니 마음이 한결 편안해졌어요 — Having taken the medicine, I felt much more at ease.
한국에 가고 나니까 문화가 정말 다를더라고요 — After going to Korea, I realized the culture is really different.

notes.title: Same subject preferred
notes.text: The subject of both clauses is usually the same person. The first action must be completed before the realization in the second clause occurs.

notes.title: Nuanced discovery
notes.text: Unlike simple -고 나서 (after doing), -고 나니(까) emphasizes a discovery or change in perception that results from the completed action.

explanation: -다가 means 'while doing, then...' It describes an interruption or sudden change: the first action was in progress when something else happened. Use it when you want to tell a story about an unexpected shift in activity.

rules.title: V-다가
rules.description: Remove 다 from the dictionary form and attach 다가. This form is the same for all verbs.

examples.english: 길을 걷다가 넘어졌어요 — While walking down the road, I fell.
밥을 먹다가 친구한테 전화가 왔어요 — While eating, I got a call from a friend.
책을 읽다가 잠이 들었어요 — While reading a book, I fell asleep.

notes.title: Same subject or different?
notes.text: The two clauses usually share the same subject, but they can differ if the context is clear. The key is that the first action is interrupted.

notes.title: Cross-reference: warning form
notes.text: See ID 207 for -다가(는), which warns about negative consequences rather than describing a past interruption.

explanation: -다가(는) means 'if you keep doing...' It warns that continuing the first action will lead to a negative result. Use it for giving warnings, advice, or predictions about bad outcomes.

rules.title: V-다가(는)
rules.description: Remove 다 from the dictionary form and attach 다가(는). The 는 is optional but adds emphasis to the warning.

examples.english: 이렇게 먹다가는 살이 찔 거예요 — If you keep eating like this, you'll gain weight.
늦게까지 공부하다가는 아플 거예요 — If you keep studying until late, you'll get sick.
매일 놀다가는 시험에 떨어질 거예요 — If you keep playing every day, you'll fail the exam.

notes.title: Negative result required
notes.text: The second clause must contain a negative or undesirable outcome. You cannot use -다가(는) to describe positive results.

notes.title: (는) adds weight
notes.text: Without 는, -다가 can simply describe an interruption (see ID 206). Adding 는 shifts the meaning to a clear warning.

notes.title: Cross-reference: interruption form
notes.text: See ID 206 for -다가, which describes a past interruption rather than giving a warning.

explanation: -(으)면 means 'if' or 'when'. Use it to set up a condition: if the situation in the first clause occurs, then the result in the second clause follows. It is one of the most versatile and frequently used conditional forms in Korean.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 면
rules.description: Attach 면 directly to stems ending in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ.

examples.english: 시장에 가면 생선이 많아요 — When you go to the market, there is a lot of fish.
직접 만들면 더 건강해요 — If you make it yourself, it's healthier.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 으면
rules.description: Attach 으면 to stems ending in a consonant (other than ㄹ).

examples.english: 매운 걸 먹으면 눈물이 나요 — If I eat spicy food, I tear up.
책을 찾으면 바로 알려 주세요 — If you find the book, please let me know right away.

rules.title: Habitual 'whenever'
rules.description: -(으)면 can also express a habitual or general truth, similar to 'whenever' in English.

examples.english: 비가 오면 기분이 우울해져요 — Whenever it rains, I feel down.

notes.title: If vs. when
notes.text: -(으)면 blends 'if' and 'when'. Context and intonation determine whether it is hypothetical or refers to a recurring situation.

notes.title: Tense goes at the end
notes.text: Do not put tense markers before -(으)면. The tense of the entire sentence is expressed in the final verb.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅂ change to 우 before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄷ change to 르 before vowel-initial endings.

explanation: -(으)려면 means 'if you want to' or 'if you intend to.' It sets up a necessary condition for achieving a goal. Use it when giving instructions, requirements, or advice about what someone must do to accomplish something.

rules.title: V-(으)려면
rules.description: Attach (으)려면 to the verb stem. 받침 있음 → + 으려면; 받침 없음 → + 려면. Stems ending in ㄹ keep the ㄹ: 살다 → 살 + 려면 = 살려면.

examples.english: 한국에 가려면 비자가 필요해요 — If you want to go to Korea, you need a visa.
이걸 먹으려면 포크가 필요해요 — If you want to eat this, you need a fork.
이 버스를 타려면 얼마나 기다려야 해요? — If you want to take this bus, how long do you have to wait?

notes.title: Necessary conditions
notes.text: -(으)려면 sets up a requirement. The second clause almost always contains instructions, advice, or factual requirements.

notes.title: Often paired with -아/여야 돼요
notes.text: Sentences with -(으)려면 frequently end with -아/여야 돼요 or a similar obligation pattern to strengthen the necessity.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅂ change to 우 before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄷ change to ㄹ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅅ drop the ㅅ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄹ ending
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ do not drop the ㄹ before 려면.

explanation: -기 전에 means 'before.' It indicates that one action happens prior to another. Use it for sequencing events, giving instructions about order, or describing routines.

rules.title: V-기 전에
rules.description: Attach 기 전에 directly to the verb stem. This form is the same for all verbs and adjectives.

examples.english: 자기 전에 이를 닦아요 — Before sleeping, I brush my teeth.
한국에 가기 전에 한국어를 공부했어요 — Before going to Korea, I studied Korean.
밥을 먹기 전에 손을 씻어요 — Before eating, I wash my hands.

notes.title: Opposite: -고 나서
notes.text: The opposite of -기 전에 is -고 나서 ('after'). These two are often taught together for talking about sequence.

notes.title: Also used with adjectives
notes.text: Although less common, adjectives can also attach 기 전에: 어렵기 전에 ('before it becomes difficult').

explanation: -는 동안 means 'during' or 'while'. Use it when two actions or states overlap in time. It emphasizes that something happens within the timeframe of another ongoing action.

rules.title: Verb Stem + 는 동안
rules.description: Attach 는 동안 directly to the verb stem. This form is identical for all verbs regardless of the final sound.

examples.english: 아이가 자는 동안 청소를 했어요 — I cleaned while the baby was sleeping.
버스를 기다리는 동안 음악을 들었어요 — I listened to music while waiting for the bus.
친구가 공부하는 동안 나는 게임을 했어요 — While my friend was studying, I played games.

notes.title: Duration matters
notes.text: -는 동안 is best used with actions or states that have a clear duration. It sounds awkward with instantaneous actions like 'to arrive' or 'to die'.

notes.title: Subjects can differ
notes.text: Unlike -다가, the subjects of the two clauses can easily be different people. This is very common with -는 동안.

notes.title: Difference from -(으)ㄹ 때
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 때 simply points to a moment in time, while -는 동안 stresses the overlapping duration of two events.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 때 means 'when'. Use it to refer to a specific time, occasion, or circumstance in which something happens. It is extremely versatile and works for past, present, and future contexts.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 때
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 때 to stems ending in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ.

examples.english: 한국에 갈 때 비행기를 탈 거예요 — When I go to Korea, I'll take a plane.
김치를 만들 때 재료가 아주 중요해요 — When making kimchi, the ingredients are very important.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 때
rules.description: Attach 을 때 to stems ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 점심을 먹을 때 같이 이야기해요 — We talk together when we eat lunch.
이 옷을 입을 때 기분이 좋아요 — I feel good when I wear this.

rules.title: Past tense modifier → + 았/었을 때
rules.description: To talk about a time in the past, attach 을 때 to the past tense stem.

examples.english: 어렸을 때 할머니 집에서 살았어요 — When I was young, I lived at my grandmother's house.

notes.title: Habitual or specific
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 때 can refer to a single future event or a habitual situation. Context makes it clear which one is meant.

notes.title: Adjectives work too
notes.text: Although this entry is categorized under verbs, adjectives also take -(으)ㄹ 때 (e.g., 바쁠 때, 피곤할 때).

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅂ change to 우 before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄷ change to 르 before vowel-initial endings.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ 후에 means 'after'. Use it when one action is fully completed before another action begins. It is more formal and slightly more explicit than -고 나서, making it common in written Korean and polite speech.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ 후에
rules.description: Attach ㄴ 후에 to stems ending in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ.

examples.english: 회의가 끝난 후에 점심을 먹으러 갔어요 — After the meeting ended, we went to eat lunch.
케이크를 만든 후에 사진을 찍었어요 — After making the cake, I took a photo.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 은 후에
rules.description: Attach 은 후에 to stems ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 밥을 먹은 후에 바로 운 동하지 마세요 — Don't exercise right after eating.
책을 읽은 후에 독후감을 써야 해요 — I have to write a book report after reading the book.

notes.title: More formal than -고 나서
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ 후에 is preferred in essays, reports, and formal speech. In casual conversation, -고 나서 is far more common.

notes.title: Action must be completed
notes.text: The first action must be finished before the second action begins. Do not use this for simultaneous or interrupted actions.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅂ change to 우 before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄷ change to 르 before vowel-initial endings.

explanation: -지만 means 'but' or 'although.' It connects two contrasting ideas. Use it when you want to acknowledge something while presenting an opposing fact or feeling. It is one of the most common contrast connectors in Korean.

rules.title: A/V-지만
rules.description: Attach 지만 directly to the verb or adjective stem. The form is identical for all verbs and adjectives.

examples.english: 비가 오지만 나가야 해요 — Although it's raining, I have to go out.
배가 고프지만 참고 있어요 — Although I'm hungry, I'm holding it in.
한국어가 어렵지만 재미있어요 — Korean is difficult, but it's fun.

notes.title: Soft contrast
notes.text: -지만 presents contrast in a neutral, balanced way. It does not emphasize the second clause as strongly as other contrast forms.

notes.title: Cross-reference: concession
notes.text: See ID 219 for -아/어도 ('even if'), which expresses a stronger sense of concession and hypothetical situations.

explanation: -는데 provides background information before introducing the main point. Depending on context, it can mean 'but,' 'and,' or simply set the scene. It is softer and more conversational than -지만, making it the go-to connector in everyday Korean speech.

rules.title: Action Verb Present + 는데
rules.description: Attach -는데 to present tense action verbs.

examples.english: 시장에 가는데 사람이 많았어요 — I'm going to the market, and there were a lot of people.
점심을 먹는데 전화가 왔어요 — I was eating lunch, but a call came in.

rules.title: Descriptive Verb + 은/ㄴ데
rules.description: Attach -은데 to stems ending in a consonant, and -ㄴ데 to stems ending in a vowel.

examples.english: 방이 큰데 정리가 안 돼 있어요 — The room is big, but it's not organized.
가방이 작은데 많이 들어가요 — The bag is small, but it holds a lot.

rules.title: Past Tense + 았/었는데
rules.description: Attach -는데 to the past tense form.

examples.english: 공항에 도착했는데 비행기가 지연됐어요 — I arrived at the airport, but the flight was delayed.
어제 피곤했는데 잠을 못 잤어요 — I was tired yesterday, but I couldn't sleep.

rules.title: 이다 → 인데
rules.description: The copula 이다 becomes 인데.

examples.english: 학생인데 돈이 많아요 — They're a student, but they have a lot of money.

notes.title: Softer than -지만
notes.text: -는데 is less confrontational than -지만. Use it when you want to gently introduce contrasting information or simply provide background before your main point.

notes.title: Main clause can be omitted
notes.text: In casual speech, the main clause is often left out when the meaning is clear from context. For example, '맛있는데?' implies 'It's delicious, but... (isn't it?)' or 'It's delicious, (so why aren't you eating?)'.

explanation: -기는 하지만 acknowledges that something is true or did happen, but then introduces a contrasting point. It emphasizes the concession more strongly than simple -지만, meaning 'it is true that... but...' or 'although... indeed...'. Use it when you want to admit a fact before presenting a reservation or criticism.

rules.title: Verb/Adjective Stem + 기는 하지만
rules.description: Attach -기는 하지만 directly to the verb or adjective stem. The 기는 portion emphasizes the concession.

examples.english: 가기는 하지만 좀 늦을 거예요 — I am going, but I'll be a bit late.
먹기는 하지만 입에 안 맞아요 — I do eat it, but it doesn't suit my taste.
비싸기는 하지만 가치가 있어요 — It is true that it's expensive, but it's worth it.

notes.title: Softens criticism
notes.text: -기는 하지만 is perfect for softening negative feedback. Instead of saying '맛없어요' (it's not tasty), saying '먹기는 하지만 맛없어요' (I do eat it, but it's not tasty) sounds more polite and nuanced.

notes.title: Can shorten to -기는 하다
notes.text: In some contexts, 하지만 can be replaced with 하다 or 해도. For example, '가기는 해' (I do go) or '가기는 해도' (even though I do go). See ID 214 for simple -지만 and ID 219 for -아/어도.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는 반면(에) means 'whereas' or 'on the other hand.' It is used to compare two contrasting situations, people, or things side by side in an objective way. It is more formal and literary than -지만, making it ideal for essays, reports, and structured comparisons.

rules.title: Present Action Verb + 는 반면(에)
rules.description: Attach -는 반면(에) to present tense action verbs.

examples.english: 동생은 운동을 잘하는 반면에 저는 못 해요 — My younger sibling is good at sports, whereas I can't do them.
여름은 더운 반면에 겨울은 추워요 — Summer is hot, whereas winter is cold.

rules.title: Descriptive Verb + (으)ㄴ 반면(에)
rules.description: Attach -은 반면(에) to stems ending in a consonant, and -ㄴ 반면(에) to stems ending in a vowel.

examples.english: 이 지역은 교통이 편리한 반면에 집값이 비싸요 — This area is convenient in terms of transportation, whereas housing prices are expensive.
한국은 인구가 적은 반면에 일본은 인구가 많아요 — Korea has a small population, whereas Japan has a large one.

rules.title: Past Action Verb + (으)ㄴ 반면(에)
rules.description: Use the past tense modifier form before 반면(에).

examples.english: 동료는 열심히 준비한 반면에 저는 소홀했어요 — My colleague prepared hard, whereas I was negligent.

notes.title: Objective comparison
notes.text: Use -(으)ㄴ/는 반면(에) when making objective, factual comparisons rather than emotional contrasts. It sounds more analytical than -지만 or -는데.

notes.title: 반면 and 반면에 are interchangeable
notes.text: Both forms are correct. Adding 에 simply makes the contrast slightly more explicit, but the meaning is identical.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For descriptive verbs ending in ㅂ, drop ㅂ and add 운 before ㄴ.
exceptions.example: 덥다 → 더우 + ㄴ 반면에 → 더운 반면에

explanation: -(으)ㄹ지라도 means 'even if' with a stronger, more formal or literary nuance than -아/어도. It acknowledges a hypothetical situation but insists that the result will not change. Use it in written language, speeches, or when making strong assertions and warnings.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ지라도
rules.description: Attach -ㄹ지라도 to verb stems ending in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ.

examples.english: 아무리 멀리 갈지라도 연락할게요 — No matter how far I go, I will contact you.
혼자 만들지라도 완성할 수 있어요 — Even if I make it alone, I can finish it.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을지라도
rules.description: Attach -을지라도 to verb stems ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 약을 먹을지라도 낫지 않을 거예요 — Even if I take medicine, I won't get better.
지갑을 찾을지라도 돈은 없을 거예요 — Even if I find my wallet, the money will be gone.

notes.title: Formal and emphatic
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ지라도 is much more formal than -아/어도. It is often paired with 아무리 (no matter how), 절대 (never), or 비록 (although) for extra emphasis.

notes.title: Hypothetical focus
notes.text: This grammar is primarily used for hypothetical situations rather than factual ones. For everyday concessions, use -아/어도 (ID 219) instead.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For verbs ending in ㅂ, drop ㅂ and add 울지라도.

explanation: -아/어도 means 'even if' or 'even though.' It describes a hypothetical or actual situation that does not change the outcome. Use it when you want to say that something remains true regardless of conditions.

rules.title: A/V-아도
rules.description: For stems with ㅏ or ㅗ, attach 아도.

examples.english: 비가 와도 나갈 거예요 — Even if it rains, I'll go out.
봐도 모르겠어요 — Even if I look, I don't understand.

rules.title: A/V-어도
rules.description: For stems with other vowels, attach 어도.

examples.english: 먹어도 배가 고파요 — Even though I eat, I'm still hungry.
추워도 문을 열어 두세요 — Even if it's cold, please keep the door open.

rules.title: A/V-해도 → 해도? No, 해도
rules.description: For 하다 verbs, 하 + 여도 contracts to 해도? No, 해도.

examples.english: 공부해도 시험에 떨어질 수 있어요 — Even if you study, you might fail the exam.

notes.title: Hypothetical or actual
notes.text: -아/어도 works for both hypothetical situations ('even if') and actual ones ('even though'). Context tells you which meaning applies.

notes.title: Cross-reference: simple contrast
notes.text: See ID 214 for -지만 ('but'), which presents a simple contrast without the 'regardless' nuance of -아/어도.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅂ change to 우 before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄷ change to ㄹ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅅ drop the ㅅ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㅡ drop
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅡ, the ㅡ drops and the vowel harmonizes.

exceptions.name: 르 irregular
exceptions.description: For 르-ending stems, an extra ㄹ is inserted into the previous syllable before 아/어.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는데도 means 'even though' and combines the background-providing function of -는데 with the concessive -도. It expresses stronger surprise or frustration that the expected result didn't happen despite the situation. Use it when you want to emphasize that something should have happened but didn't.

rules.title: Present Action Verb + 는데도
rules.description: Attach -는데도 to present tense action verbs.

examples.english: 병원에 가는데도 안 나아요 — Even though I go to the hospital, I'm not getting better.
약을 먹는데도 효과가 없어요 — Even though I take medicine, there's no effect.

rules.title: Adjective + (으)ㄴ데도
rules.description: Attach -은데도 to stems ending in a consonant, and -ㄴ데도 to stems ending in a vowel.

examples.english: 가격이 비싼데도 사람들이 많이 사요 — Even though the price is expensive, many people buy it.
집이 작은데도 가족이 행복해요 — Even though the house is small, the family is happy.

rules.title: Past Tense + 았/었는데도
rules.description: Attach -는데도 to the past tense form for both verbs and adjectives.

examples.english: 친구가 왔는데도 같이 안 놀았어요 — Even though my friend came, I didn't hang out with them.
어제 피곤했는데도 잠을 못 잤어요 — Even though I was tired yesterday, I couldn't sleep.

notes.title: Expectation vs. reality
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ/는데도 carries a sense of surprise or disappointment. The speaker expected one outcome but got the opposite. See ID 219 for simple concession without this emotional weight.

notes.title: Not for softening
notes.text: Unlike -는데 (ID 215), -(으)ㄴ/는데도 is not used to soften statements. It always carries a strong contrast.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는 반대로 means 'contrary to' or 'unlike.' It states that the second clause is the opposite of what the first clause describes. Use it when comparing expectations with reality, or when describing behavior that goes against a prior situation or command.

rules.title: Present Action Verb + 는 반대로
rules.description: Attach -는 반대로 to present tense action verbs, often with an implied noun.

examples.english: 생각하는 반대로 일이 흘러갔어요 — Things went contrary to what I thought.
부모님이 원하는 반대로 전공을 선택했어요 — I chose my major contrary to what my parents wanted.

rules.title: Past Action Verb + (으)ㄴ 반대로
rules.description: Use the past tense modifier form before 반대로.

examples.english: 예상한 반대로 결과가 나왔어요 — The result came out contrary to expectations.
약속한 반대로 행동했어요 — They acted contrary to what was promised.

notes.title: Often follows mental verbs
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ/는 반대로 works best with verbs like 생각하다, 예상하다, 원하다, and 말하다, because the contrast is between a thought and a reality.

notes.title: Can also use N + 과 반대로
notes.text: You will often see 반대로 used with nouns and the particle 과/와: 예상과 반대로 (contrary to expectations), 내 말과 반대로 (contrary to my words).

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는 탓에 means 'due to' or 'because of,' but exclusively for negative results. It places blame on the cause stated in the first clause. The result is always something bad or undesirable. Use it when you want to attribute a problem to a specific cause.

rules.title: Present Action Verb + 는 탓에
rules.description: Attach -는 탓에 to present tense action verbs.

examples.english: 밤늦게까지 일하는 탓에 건강이 나빠졌어요 — My health worsened due to working until late at night.
너무 과하게 운동하는 탓에 다쳤어요 — I got injured due to exercising too excessively.

rules.title: Adjective / Past Action Verb + (으)ㄴ 탓에
rules.description: Attach -은 탓에 to stems ending in a consonant, and -ㄴ 탓에 to stems ending in a vowel.

examples.english: 날씨가 추운 탓에 감기에 걸렸어요 — I caught a cold because of the cold weather.
늦잠을 잔 탓에 지각했어요 — I was late due to oversleeping.
사람이 많은 탓에 사고가 났어요 — An accident happened due to there being too many people.

notes.title: Negative results only
notes.text: Never use -(으)ㄴ/는 탓에 for positive outcomes. For neutral or positive causes, use -아/어서 or -(으)니까 instead.

notes.title: Can replace with 탓이다
notes.text: You can restructure with 탓이다 at the end: 지각한 탓이다 (it's because of being late). This emphasizes blame even more strongly.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For adjectives ending in ㅂ, drop ㅂ and add 운 before ㄴ.
exceptions.example: 춥다 → 추 + 운 탓에 → 추운 탓에

explanation: -(이)나마 means 'even if it's only' or 'at least.' It softens a noun, suggesting that even this small or modest thing is meaningful. Use it when you want to emphasize that something minimal is still better than nothing.

rules.title: N-(이)나마
rules.description: Attach (이)나마 to a noun. 받침 있음 → + 이나마; 받침 없음 → + 나마.

examples.english: 잠깐이나마 쉴 수 있어서 다행이에요 — I'm glad I can rest even for a short while.
그 사람 이름이나마 기억하고 싶어요 — I want to remember even his name.
밥이나마 제대로 먹어요 — At least eat your meals properly.

notes.title: Formal and slightly literary
notes.text: -(이)나마 is more formal than -(이)라도. It often appears in written Korean, speeches, and formal expressions of gratitude or regret.

notes.title: Don't confuse with -(이)라도
notes.text: -(이)라도 is more common in spoken Korean and has a broader meaning ('anything,' 'at least'). -(이)나마 is narrower and more emphatic.

explanation: -(은/는)커녕 means 'let alone' or 'not to mention.' It emphatically denies the first noun (as being too difficult or impossible) and then states that even the second, easier thing is also not true. The second clause must always be negative. Use it for strong, emphatic denial.

rules.title: Noun with 받침 → + 은커녕
rules.description: Attach -은커녕 to nouns ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 밥은커녕 물도 못 마셨어요 — I couldn't even drink water, let alone eat rice.
잠은커녕 쉴 시간도 없었어요 — I didn't even have time to rest, let alone sleep.
한국은커녕 일본도 안 가봤어요 — I haven't even been to Japan, let alone Korea.

rules.title: Noun without 받침 → + 는커녕
rules.description: Attach -는커녕 to nouns ending in a vowel.

examples.english: 친구는커녕 이웃도 안 만나요 — I don't even meet my neighbors, let alone friends.
커피는커녕 물도 안 마셨어요 — I didn't even drink water, let alone coffee.

notes.title: Second clause must be negative
notes.text: The sentence following -(은/는)커녕 must contain a negative expression: 못, 안, 없다, or a negated verb. Saying '밥은커녕 물도 마셨어요' is grammatically incorrect.

notes.title: First item is 'harder'
notes.text: The noun before 커녕 is presented as the more difficult or unlikely scenario. The second noun is something easier, yet even that didn't happen.

explanation: -(으)니까 means 'because' or 'since.' It expresses a reason and often leads to a suggestion, command, or new discovery in the result clause. Use it when the reason is personal, subjective, or when you want to give advice based on that reason.

rules.title: A/V-(으)니까
rules.description: Attach (으)니까 to the verb or adjective stem. 받침 있음 → + 으니까; 받침 없음 or ㄹ → + 니까. Note: ㄹ drops before 니까.

examples.english: 비가 오니까 우산을 가져가세요 — Because it's raining, take an umbrella.
배가 고프니까 밥을 먹어요 — Because I'm hungry, I'll eat.
추우니까 문을 닫아 주세요 — Because it's cold, please close the door.

notes.title: Commands and suggestions love 니까
notes.text: -(으)니까 pairs naturally with imperative and propositive endings (-세요, -ㅂ시다, -아/어요). -(으)니까 emphasizes that the suggestion follows logically from the reason.

notes.title: Cross-reference: objective because
notes.text: See ID 231 for -기 때문에, which is more objective and formal. Use 니까 for subjective reasons and 기 때문에 for objective explanations.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅂ change to 우 before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄷ change to ㄹ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄹ ending
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ before ㄴ.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅅ drop the ㅅ before vowel-initial endings.

explanation: -는 바람에 means 'due to' or 'because of,' usually describing a sudden, unexpected cause that leads to a negative result. Use it when the reason was unplanned and the consequence was unfortunate.

rules.title: V-는 바람에
rules.description: Attach 는 바람에 to the verb stem. This form is the same for all action verbs. Adjectives can also attach, but the form becomes 운 바람에 for ㅂ irregular adjectives.

examples.english: 급하게 뛰어가는 바람에 넘어졌어요 — Because I was running in a hurry, I fell.
비를 맞는 바람에 감기에 걸렸어요 — Because I got rained on, I caught a cold.
알람을 못 듣는 바람에 지각했어요 — Because I didn't hear the alarm, I was late.

notes.title: Often negative
notes.text: -는 바람에 strongly prefers negative or unfortunate results. Using it with positive outcomes can sound unnatural.

notes.title: Sudden and unplanned
notes.text: This grammar emphasizes that the cause was sudden or outside the subject's control. It is not used for deliberate reasons.

notes.title: Cross-reference: similar negative causes
notes.text: See ID 228 (-는 통에, disruptive cause) and ID 233 (-(으)ㄴ/는 탓에, negative result) for related expressions.

exceptions.name: ㄹ ending
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ before ㄴ.
exceptions.example: 살다 → 사는 바람에, 울다 → 우는 바람에

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular (adjectives)
exceptions.description: Adjective stems ending in ㅂ change to 우 before ㄴ.
exceptions.example: 춥다 → 추운 바람에, 어렵다 → 어려운 바람에

explanation: -는 통에 means 'because of' and describes a disruptive, ongoing cause that prevents something else from happening properly. Use it when an action or state is so distracting or obstructive that it interferes with another activity.

rules.title: V-는 통에
rules.description: Attach 는 통에 to the verb stem. This form is the same for all action verbs. It emphasizes that the ongoing action was a nuisance or obstacle.

examples.english: 옆집에서 공사하는 통에 잠을 못 잤어요 — Because of the construction next door, I couldn't sleep.
아이가 울고 있는 통에 전화를 못 받았어요 — Because the baby was crying, I couldn't answer the phone.
사람들이 떠드는 통에 집중이 안 됐어요 — Because people were talking loudly, I couldn't concentrate.

notes.title: Disruption, not just cause
notes.text: -는 통에 specifically implies that the first action was disruptive, noisy, or obstructive. It is stronger than a simple 'because.'

notes.title: Negative result expected
notes.text: Like -는 바람에, 통에 typically leads to a negative result: something failed, was prevented, or could not be done.

notes.title: Cross-reference: sudden cause
notes.text: See ID 227 (-는 바람에) for sudden causes and ID 233 (-(으)ㄴ/는 탓에) for blaming a negative outcome on something.

exceptions.name: ㄹ ending
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ before ㄴ.
exceptions.example: 살다 → 사는 통에, 놀다 → 노는 통에

explanation: -기 때문에 means 'because.' It is the standard, objective way to state a reason. Use it in formal writing, logical explanations, and when you want to present a reason without implying a command or suggestion.

rules.title: A/V-기 때문에
rules.description: Attach 기 때문에 directly to the verb or adjective stem. The form is identical for all verbs and adjectives.

examples.english: 시험이 내일이기 때문에 오늘 공부해야 해요 — Because the exam is tomorrow, I have to study today.
길이 막히기 때문에 일찍 출발했어요 — Because the road was congested, I left early.
건강이 좋지 않기 때문에 운동을 시작했어요 — Because my health is not good, I started exercising.

notes.title: Objective and neutral
notes.text: -기 때문에 feels more objective and formal than -(으)니까. It is preferred in essays, reports, and formal speech.

notes.title: Cross-reference: subjective because
notes.text: See ID 226 for -(으)니까, which is more subjective and often pairs with commands or suggestions.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅂ change to 우 before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄷ change to ㄹ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅅ drop the ㅅ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄹ ending
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ do not drop before 기.

explanation: -느라고 means 'because (someone was busy doing).' It explains that the subject was occupied with an activity, and as a result, something negative or unintended happened. Use it when you want to excuse or explain why something else could not be done.

rules.title: V-느라고
rules.description: Attach 느라고 directly to the verb stem. This form is the same for all action verbs. It cannot be used with adjectives or with future tense.

examples.english: 친구를 기다리느라고 밥을 못 먹었어요 — Because I was waiting for a friend, I couldn't eat.
일하느라고 연락을 못 했어요 — Because I was working, I couldn't contact you.
공부하느라고 잠을 못 잤어요 — Because I was studying, I couldn't sleep.

notes.title: Same subject only
notes.text: Both clauses must share the same subject. You cannot use -느라고 to explain why someone else could not do something.

notes.title: No positive results
notes.text: -느라고 cannot be used when the result is positive. It is reserved for negative consequences, missed opportunities, or things left undone.

notes.title: Present or past tense only
notes.text: -느라고 describes ongoing or completed actions. You cannot use it with future intentions or hypothetical situations.

exceptions.name: ㄹ ending
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ before ㄴ.

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는 탓에 means 'due to' or 'because of,' blaming a negative result on something. It is stronger and more critical than other 'because' forms. Use it when you want to attribute fault or emphasize that something caused a bad outcome.

rules.title: V-(으)ㄴ 탓에 / A-(으)ㄴ 탓에
rules.description: For past actions or adjectives, attach (으)ㄴ 탓에. 받침 있음 → + 은 탓에; 받침 없음 or ㄹ → + ㄴ 탓에.

examples.english: 어제 너무 많이 먹은 탓에 배가 아파요 — Because I ate too much yesterday, my stomach hurts.
운동을 안 한 탓에 체력이 떨어졌어요 — Because I didn't exercise, my stamina dropped.

rules.title: V-는 탓에
rules.description: For ongoing or habitual actions, attach 는 탓에.

examples.english: 늦게 자는 탓에 아침마다 힘들어요 — Because I sleep late, every morning is hard.
스트레스를 받는 탓에 잠이 안 와요 — Because I'm stressed, I can't sleep.

notes.title: Critical tone
notes.text: 탓에 carries a tone of blame or regret. Avoid using it in polite or sensitive situations where you do not want to sound accusatory.

notes.title: Cross-reference: similar forms
notes.text: See ID 227 (-는 바람에, sudden cause) and ID 228 (-는 통에, disruptive cause) for other negative-cause expressions.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄷ change to ㄹ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄹ ending
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄹ drop the ㄹ before ㄴ.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅅ drop the ㅅ before vowel-initial endings.

explanation: -아/어/여서 그런지 means 'maybe because.' It softens a cause, making it speculative rather than certain. Use it when you suspect something is the reason but you are not entirely sure.

rules.title: V/A-아서 그런지
rules.description: For stems with ㅏ or ㅗ, conjugate with -아서 and add 그런지.

examples.english: 피곤해서 그런지 집중이 안 돼요 — Maybe because I'm tired, I can't focus.

rules.title: V/A-어서 그런지
rules.description: For stems with other vowels, conjugate with -어서 and add 그런지.

examples.english: 배고파서 그런지 머리가 아파요 — Maybe because I'm hungry, my head hurts.
날씨가 추워서 그런지 손이 시려워요 — Maybe because the weather is cold, my hands are freezing.

rules.title: V/A-해서 그런지
rules.description: For 하다 verbs, 하 + 여서 contracts to 해서.

examples.english: 공부해서 그런지 머리가 아파요 — Maybe because I studied, my head hurts.

notes.title: Speculative softening
notes.text: 그런지 adds uncertainty. Without it, -아/어서 states a definite cause. With 그런지, you are guessing or wondering about the connection.

notes.title: Often used with body or feelings
notes.text: This pattern appears frequently when describing physical sensations, moods, and hunches where the cause is unclear.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅂ change to 우 before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㄷ change to ㄹ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: Stems ending in ㅅ drop the ㅅ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㅡ drop
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅡ, the ㅡ drops and the vowel harmonizes.
exceptions.example: 예쁘다 → 예뻐서 그런지, 크다 → 커서 그런지

exceptions.name: 르 irregular
exceptions.description: For 르-ending stems, an extra ㄹ is inserted into the previous syllable before 아/어.

explanation: -(으)로 인해(서) means 'due to' or 'caused by'. It attaches to a noun to explain the reason or cause of a result. It is most commonly used in formal writing, news reports, and academic contexts, and it often describes negative or serious consequences such as accidents, disasters, or problems.

rules.title: Noun with 받침 (except ㄹ) → + 으로 인해(서)
rules.description: When the noun ends in a consonant other than ㄹ, attach 으로 인해(서). 서 is optional and slightly softens the tone.

examples.english: 태풍으로 인해 비행기가 결항되었어요 — Flights were canceled due to the typhoon.
지진으로 인해서 도로가 막혔어요 — The roads were blocked because of the earthquake.

rules.title: Noun with no 받침 or ㄹ ending → + 로 인해(서)
rules.description: When the noun ends in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ, attach 로 인해(서).

examples.english: 홍수로 인해 많은 집이 파괴되었어요 — Many homes were destroyed due to the flood.
사고로 인해서 회사에 늦었어요 — I was late to work because of an accident.

notes.title: Formal and written register
notes.text: -(으)로 인해(서) sounds much more formal than -때문에 (ID 69). Use it in essays, news, and formal speeches rather than casual conversation.

notes.title: Often negative results
notes.text: While not strictly limited to bad outcomes, this grammar strongly collocates with accidents, diseases, natural disasters, and failures.

notes.title: 서 is optional
notes.text: Both -으로 인해 and -으로 인해서 are correct. The version without 서 sounds slightly more concise and written.

explanation: -에 의해(서) means 'by' or 'through'. Its main use is to mark the agent in a passive sentence — the person or thing that performs the action. It can also mean 'by means of' when describing a method or process.

rules.title: Noun + 에 의해(서)
rules.description: Attach 에 의해(서) directly to the noun. There are no 받침-related changes. 서 is optional.

examples.english: 이 그림은 유명한 화가에 의해 그려졌어요 — This painting was drawn by a famous artist.
그 책은 선생님에 의해서 번역되었어요 — That book was translated by the teacher.
이 문제는 법에 의해 해결되어야 해요 — This issue must be resolved by law.

notes.title: Passive voice agent marker
notes.text: In active voice, use -이/가 for the subject. In passive voice, use -에 의해(서) to say who or what did the action.

notes.title: Also means 'by means of'
notes.text: Besides passive agents, -에 의해(서) can describe methods: 과학에 의해 증명되다 (proven by science).

notes.title: Compare with -(으)로 인해(서)
notes.text: Use -(으)로 인해(서) (ID 235) for causes and reasons. Use -에 의해(서) for agents in passive sentences or specific methods.

explanation: A/V-(으)ㄹ까 봐(서) means 'for fear that' or 'in case'. Use it when you do something because you are worried that an undesirable situation might happen. The action in the main clause is taken to prevent or prepare for the worried situation.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ까 봐(서)
rules.description: Attach ㄹ까 봐(서) to stems ending in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ. The result clause explains a preventive action.

examples.english: 늦을까 봐 택시를 탔어요 — I took a taxi for fear of being late.
아플까 봐서 옷을 두껍게 입었어요 — I dressed warmly in case I got sick.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을까 봐(서)
