rules.description: Attach 을까 봐(서) to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 비가 올까 봐 우산을 챙겼어요 — I brought an umbrella in case it rained.
잊을까 봐서 메모를 항상 해요 — I always take notes for fear of forgetting.

notes.title: Only for worries and concerns
notes.text: This grammar is only for situations you want to avoid. Do not use it for positive expectations. For positive intention, use -(으)ㄹ까 해서 (ID 239).

notes.title: 서 is optional
notes.text: Both -ㄹ까 봐 and -ㄹ까 봐서 are correct. Adding 서 makes the causal connection slightly clearer.

notes.title: Present and future only
notes.text: You cannot use -(으)ㄹ까 봐(서) for past worries because the grammar already implies a present concern about a future possibility.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before a vowel.

explanation: V-(으)ㄹ까 하다/싶다 means 'I'm thinking of' or 'I'm considering'. It expresses a tentative intention or plan that has not been fully decided yet. It conveys a softer, more hesitant tone than a firm promise or decision.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ까 하다/싶다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ까 하다 or ㄹ까 싶다 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. 싶다 adds a more emotional or tentative nuance.

examples.english: 주말에 부산에 갈까 해요 — I'm thinking of going to Busan this weekend.
혼자 여행할까 싶어요 — I'm considering traveling alone.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을까 하다/싶다
rules.description: Attach 을까 하다 or 을까 싶다 to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 신발을 살까 해요 — I'm thinking of buying shoes.
한식을 먹을까 싶어요 — I'm considering eating Korean food.

notes.title: Not a firm decision
notes.text: This grammar is much weaker than -(으)ㄹ게요 (ID 20). It means 'I might' rather than 'I will'. Use it when your plans are still flexible.

notes.title: 싶다 is more emotional
notes.text: -ㄹ까 싶다 feels more personal and emotional than -ㄹ까 하다. It is often used when you are daydreaming or half-heartedly considering something.

notes.title: Compare with -(으)ㄹ까 봐(서)
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ까 하다 (ID 239) is about your own intention. -(으)ㄹ까 봐(서) (ID 238) is about worry and prevention. Do not confuse the two.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before a vowel.

explanation: V-(으)ㄹ까 말까 means 'whether or not to'. It expresses internal hesitation about doing something. The speaker is going back and forth in their mind, unable to make a decision. It is subjective and can only describe the speaker's own deliberation.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ까 말까
rules.description: Attach ㄹ까 말까 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. This form is used when the speaker is mentally debating an action.

examples.english: 갈까 말까 고민하고 있어요 — I'm debating whether to go or not.
그 옷을 살까 말까 망설였어요 — I hesitated about whether to buy those clothes.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을까 말까
rules.description: Attach 을까 말까 to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 먹을까 말까 했는데 결국 먹었어요 — I went back and forth about eating it, but I ended up eating it.
사실을 말할까 말까 했어요 — I kept wondering whether to tell the truth or not.

notes.title: Internal hesitation only
notes.text: This grammar describes your own mental debate. You cannot use it to ask someone else what they are thinking. Use -(으)ㄹ까요? (ID 22) for that.

notes.title: Often repeated
notes.text: In casual speech, people repeat the phrase to show deep indecision: 갈까 말까 갈까 말까... (Should I go? Should I not?)

notes.title: Cannot combine with commands or requests
notes.text: The second clause cannot be a command (-세요) or a request (-주세요). It naturally pairs with verbs like 고민하다, 망설이다, and 생각하다.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before a vowel.

explanation: V-(으)ㄹ까 보다 means 'it seems like someone might' or 'I suspect that someone will'. Use it when you observe a situation and speculate about what another person might do. It is based on evidence, context, or observation rather than pure guesswork.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ까 보다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ까 보다 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. The grammar is often conjugated into 보다, 보니, or 보여요.

examples.english: 곧 올까 봐요 — It seems like they might come soon.
지금 출발할까 봐요 — I suspect they might be leaving now.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을까 보다
rules.description: Attach 을까 보다 to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 그 아이가 다 먹을까 보니 걱정돼요 — I'm worried the child might eat it all.
그 사람도 갈까 봐요 — It seems like that person might go too.

notes.title: Observation about others
notes.text: Unlike -(으)ㄹ까 하다 (ID 239), which is about your own intention, -(으)ㄹ까 보다 is about speculating what someone else might do based on what you see.

notes.title: Often used with 보다/보니/보여요
notes.text: Common patterns include -(으)ㄹ까 보다, -(으)ㄹ까 보니, and -(으)ㄹ까 보여요. The conjugation of 보다 matches the sentence context.

notes.title: Compare with -(으)ㄴ가 보다
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ가 보다 (ID 182) is for observing current states: 비가 오는가 봐요 (It seems to be raining). -(으)ㄹ까 보다 is for future possibilities: 비가 올까 봐요 (It looks like it might rain).

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before a vowel.

explanation: V-(으)ㄹ지(도) 모르다 means 'might' or 'may'. It expresses uncertainty about a future event or situation. The speaker is unsure whether something will happen, and 도 can be added for slightly stronger emphasis on the possibility.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ지(도) 모르다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ지(도) 모르다 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. Use this when you are uncertain about a future outcome.

examples.english: 내일 비가 올지도 몰라요 — It might rain tomorrow.
그 사람이 갈지 모르겠어요 — I'm not sure if that person will go.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을지(도) 모르다
rules.description: Attach 을지(도) 모르다 to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 늦을지도 몰라서 일찍 나왔어요 — I left early because I might be late.
중요한 약속을 잊을지 모르겠어요 — I might forget an important appointment.

notes.title: 도 adds emphasis
notes.text: -ㄹ지 모르다 and -ㄹ지도 모르다 are both correct. Adding 도 slightly strengthens the sense of possibility, like saying 'it just might'.

notes.title: Can be used with 알다 in questions
notes.text: In questions, use -(으)ㄹ지 알아요? to ask if someone knows whether something will happen: 내일 갈지 알아요? (Do you know if they will go tomorrow?)

notes.title: Compare with -(으)ㄹ 수도 있다
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 수도 있다 (ID 26) expresses neutral possibility: 'It is possible that...' -(으)ㄹ지(도) 모르다 expresses personal uncertainty: 'I don't know if...'

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before a vowel.

explanation: V-(으)ㄹ 테니(까) means 'I will... so (you should)' or 'I'll... so (please)'. Use it when the speaker states their own intention or plan in the first clause, and then gives a suggestion, request, or advice to the listener in the second clause.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 테니(까)
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 테니(까) to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. 테 comes from the bound noun 터, which marks intention or expectation.

examples.english: 먼저 갈 테니까 천천히 오세요 — I'll go first, so please take your time.
여기서 기다릴 테니까 연락 주세요 — I'll wait here, so please contact me.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 테니(까)
rules.description: Attach 을 테니(까) to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ). 까 is optional but commonly included.

examples.english: 내가 할 테니까 걱정 마세요 — I'll do it, so don't worry.
먹을 테니까 조금만 기다려 주세요 — I'll eat, so please wait a moment.

notes.title: First clause is the speaker's plan
notes.text: The subject of the -ㄹ 테니까 clause must be the speaker. You cannot use it to describe someone else's intention.

notes.title: Second clause is advice or request
notes.text: The main clause is usually an imperative (-세요), a suggestion, or a request directed at the listener. It is not for simple statements of fact.

notes.title: Compare with -(으)ㄹ 텐데(요)
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 테니까 (ID 244) ends with a request. -(으)ㄹ 텐데(요) (ID 245) ends with regret or contrast. Do not confuse the two.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before a vowel.

explanation: V-(으)ㄹ 텐데(요) means 'I would... but' or 'it would be...'. Use it to express regret, concern, or a contrast between what you expected and what actually happened. It often carries a wistful or apologetic tone. 요 can be added for politeness.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 텐데(요)
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 텐데(요) to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. This form is used with action verbs, adjectives, and 이다 to express regret or contrast.

examples.english: 갈 텐데 시간이 없어요 — I would go, but I don't have time.
재미있을 텐데요 못 가서 아쉬워요 — It would be fun, but I'm sad I can't go.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 텐데(요)
rules.description: Attach 을 텐데(요) to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 좋을 텐데 가격이 너무 비싸요 — It would be nice, but the price is too expensive.
도와줄 텐데 내가 바빠서 못 해요 — I would help, but I'm too busy.

notes.title: Expresses regret and contrast
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 텐데(요) is perfect for situations where reality does not match your wishes. It implies 'under different circumstances, this would be true'.

notes.title: Can end a sentence alone
notes.text: You can end a sentence with -(으)ㄹ 텐데(요) without a second clause. The unfinished feeling itself conveys the regret: 갈 텐데... (I would go, but...)

notes.title: Compare with -(으)ㄹ 테니(까)
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 텐데(요) (ID 245) expresses regret and contrast. -(으)ㄹ 테니까 (ID 244) gives advice after stating a plan. The endings determine the meaning.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before a vowel.

explanation: V-(으)려던 참이다 means 'I was just about to' or 'I was on the point of'. Use it when you were literally moments away from starting an action, and something happened — usually someone interrupted you or offered to help at the perfect time.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 려던 참이다
rules.description: Attach 려던 참이다 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. 참 means 'moment' or 'chance', so this grammar emphasizes the perfect timing.

examples.english: 가려던 참이에요 마침 왔네요 — I was just about to leave, and you came at the perfect time.
연락하려던 참이에요 — I was just about to contact you.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 으려던 참이다
rules.description: Attach 으려던 참이다 to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 먹으려던 참이었어요 — I was just about to eat.
자려던 참이에요 — I was just about to sleep.

notes.title: Perfect timing
notes.text: This grammar shines when someone arrives, calls, or offers something right before you were going to act. It creates a natural, friendly reaction.

notes.title: Often used in past or present tense
notes.text: Use 참이에요 for the present moment and 참이었어요 for a past moment. Both convey that the action had not started yet.

notes.title: Compare with -자마자
notes.text: -(으)려던 참이다 (ID 246) means 'just before doing.' -자마자 (ID 198) means 'right after doing.' They are exact opposites in timing.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before a vowel.

explanation: A/V-(으)ㄹ 수밖에 없다 means 'have no choice but to' or 'cannot help but'. It expresses that a certain result is inevitable because of circumstances, logic, or external pressure. There is literally no other option available.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 수밖에 없다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 수밖에 없다 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. Use this for both action verbs and adjectives when the result is unavoidable.

examples.english: 시간이 없어서 택시를 탈 수밖에 없어요 — I have no choice but to take a taxi because there's no time.
증거가 확실해서 믿을 수밖에 없죠 — The evidence is clear, so we can't help but believe it.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 수밖에 없다
rules.description: Attach 을 수밖에 없다 to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 날씨가 좋을 수밖에 없어요 — The weather has no choice but to be good.
너무 슬퍼서 울 수밖에 없었어요 — I was so sad that I couldn't help but cry.

notes.title: Inevitable result
notes.text: This grammar is much stronger than -아/어야 하다 (ID 98). It means the situation forces the outcome, not that someone is enforcing a rule.

notes.title: Works with adjectives too
notes.text: You can use -(으)ㄹ 수밖에 없다 with descriptive verbs: 그 사람은 착할 수밖에 없어요 (That person can't help but be kind).

notes.title: Compare with -(으)ㄹ 수도 있다
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 수밖에 없다 (ID 247) means inevitability. -(으)ㄹ 수도 있다 (ID 26) means possibility. They are opposites in meaning.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before a vowel.

explanation: V-(으)ㄹ 정도(로) means 'to the extent that' or 'so much that'. Use it to describe the degree or level of something by comparing it to another action or state. It sets a benchmark for how intense or extreme something is.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 정도(로)
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 정도(로) to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. 로 is optional but turns the phrase into an adverb that modifies the main clause.

examples.english: 놀랄 정도로 많이 늘었어요 — I've improved so much that it's surprising.
울 정도로 화가 났어요 — I was so angry that I almost cried.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 정도(로)
rules.description: Attach 을 정도(로) to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ). Without 로, 정도 acts as a noun.

examples.english: 피곤할 정도로 일이 많았어요 — I had so much work it was to the point of exhaustion.
먹을 정도로 배고팠어요 — I was hungry enough to eat anything.

notes.title: 로 makes it an adverb
notes.text: With 로, -(으)ㄹ 정도로 modifies the main verb or adjective. Without 로, -(으)ㄹ 정도 is a noun phrase that can be used with 이다: 힘들 정도예요 (It's to the point of being difficult).

notes.title: Comparison benchmark
notes.text: This grammar is perfect for comparisons. It tells the listener exactly how much or how little something is by using a familiar action as a reference.

notes.title: Compare with -(으)ㄹ 지경이다
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 정도(로) (ID 248) is a general extent. -(으)ㄹ 지경이다 (ID 249) is an extreme limit, often negative. Use 지경 when someone is on the verge of breaking down.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before a vowel.

explanation: V-(으)ㄹ 지경이다 means 'on the verge of' or 'almost at the point of'. Use it when someone is so close to a physical or mental limit that they are about to reach a breaking point. It describes extreme situations that are usually negative or overwhelming.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 지경이다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 지경이다 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. 지경 literally means 'boundary' or 'situation', emphasizing the dangerous edge.

examples.english: 배가 너무 아파서 쓰러질 지경이에요 — My stomach hurts so much I'm on the verge of collapsing.
울 지경이에요 — I'm on the verge of tears.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 지경이다
rules.description: Attach 을 지경이다 to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 너무 졸려서 잠들 지경이었어요 — I was so sleepy I was on the verge of dozing off.
배가 불러서 토할 지경이에요 — I'm so full I'm about to throw up.

notes.title: Usually negative or physical
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 지경이다 strongly collocates with pain, exhaustion, anger, and other extreme states. It sounds unnatural with mild or positive situations.

notes.title: Stronger than -(으)ㄹ 정도(로)
notes.text: While -(으)ㄹ 정도(로) (ID 248) simply measures extent, -(으)ㄹ 지경이다 implies danger or loss of control. Choose 지경 for dramatic emphasis.

notes.title: Often used in past tense
notes.text: 지경이었어요 is common when recounting how bad a situation was. Present tense (지경이에요) is used when describing a current crisis.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before a vowel.

explanation: A/V-(으)ㄴ/는/(으)ㄹ지 means 'whether or not'. It turns a statement into an indirect question that can be embedded inside another sentence. Use it with verbs like 알다, 모르다, 궁금하다, and 말하다 to talk about uncertainty regarding a fact.

rules.title: V-는지 / A-(으)ㄴ지 (present)
rules.description: For present tense action verbs, attach 는지. For present tense adjectives, attach (으)ㄴ지. These describe current or general states.

examples.english: 그 사람이 오늘 가는지 몰라요 — I don't know if that person is going today.
방이 작은지 궁금해요 — I'm curious whether the room is small.

rules.title: A/V-았/었/였는지 (past)
rules.description: For past tense, add 았/었/였 to the stem, then attach 는지. This works for both action verbs and adjectives.

examples.english: 밥을 먹었는지 기억이 안 나요 — I can't remember whether I ate or not.
영화가 좋았는지 모르겠어요 — I'm not sure if the movie was good.

rules.title: A/V-(으)ㄹ지 (future)
rules.description: For future tense, attach (으)ㄹ지 to both verbs and adjectives. This expresses uncertainty about what will happen.

examples.english: 내일 올지 알아요? — Do you know if they will come tomorrow?
날씨가 추울지 걱정돼요 — I'm worried whether the weather will be cold.

notes.title: Used with mental verbs
notes.text: The main clause usually contains 알다, 모르다, 궁금하다, or similar verbs that express knowledge or curiosity.

notes.title: Can add 도 for 'whether or not'
notes.text: Use -(으)ㄹ지도 모르다 (ID 242) for 'might'. Use -(으)ㄹ지 (안) -(으)ㄹ지 for explicit 'whether or not': 갈지 안 갈지 몰라요.

notes.title: Compare with -는지 알다/모르다
notes.text: ID 126 teaches the same pattern with 알다/모르다 as the main verb. ID 250 focuses on the indirect question form itself across all tenses.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular (adjective)
exceptions.description: For adjective stems ending in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: 하다 verbs
exceptions.description: 하다 verbs always conjugate regularly.

explanation: A/V-(으)ㄹ 뻔하다 means 'almost' or 'nearly'. Use it when something dangerous, embarrassing, or undesirable almost happened but was avoided at the last moment. It expresses relief that a bad outcome was narrowly prevented.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 뻔하다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 뻔하다 to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. This grammar is almost always used in the past tense: 뻔했다 or 뻔했어요.

examples.english: 버스를 놓쳐서 늦을 뻔했어요 — I almost missed the bus and was nearly late.
더워서 쓰러질 뻔했어요 — It was so hot I almost collapsed.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 뻔하다
rules.description: Attach 을 뻔하다 to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 중요한 약속을 잊을 뻔했어요 — I almost forgot an important appointment.
감동해서 울 뻔했어요 — I was so moved I almost cried.

notes.title: Always implies relief
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 뻔하다 carries a strong feeling of 'whew, that was close.' The listener understands that the bad thing did NOT happen, thanks to luck or quick action.

notes.title: Almost always past tense
notes.text: While technically possible in non-past forms, this grammar is used in the past 99% of the time. Stick to 뻔했어요 for natural speech.

notes.title: Compare with -(으)ㄹ 지경이다
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 뻔하다 (ID 251) means something almost happened but didn't. -(으)ㄹ 지경이다 (ID 249) means you are on the verge of something that is still happening.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before a vowel.

explanation: A/V-았/었/였더라면 means 'if only' or 'had... then'. Use it to talk about a past situation that did NOT happen, and to imagine how things would have been different. It carries strong feelings of regret, longing, or wishful thinking.

rules.title: A/V past tense + 더라면
rules.description: First conjugate the verb or adjective into the past tense (았/었/였), then attach 더라면. This creates a counterfactual past condition.

examples.english: 한국에 갔더라면 좋았을 텐데 — If only I had gone to Korea, it would have been nice.
점심을 먹었더라면 배고프지 않았을 거예요 — If I had eaten lunch, I wouldn't be hungry.

rules.title: Adjective past tense + 더라면
rules.description: Adjectives follow the same pattern. The past tense ending varies depending on the final vowel.

examples.english: 날씨가 좋았더라면 소풍을 갔을 텐데 — If the weather had been nice, we would have gone on a picnic.
꽃이 예뻤더라면 사진을 더 잘 찍었을 거예요 — If the flowers had been pretty, I would have taken better photos.

notes.title: Counterfactual past only
notes.text: This grammar is strictly for things that did NOT happen in the past. You cannot use it for present or future hypotheticals. Use -(으)면 for those.

notes.title: Often paired with -겠다 or -(으)ㄹ 텐데
notes.text: The result clause commonly uses -(으)ㄹ 텐데 (regret), -겠다 (supposition), or -(으)ㄹ 거예요 (prediction) to show the imagined outcome.

notes.title: Compare with -(으)ㄹ 걸 그랬다
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 걸 그랬다 (ID 187) is a shorter, more casual way to say 'I should have.' A/V-았/었/였더라면 is more formal and allows full hypothetical sentences.

exceptions.name: 하다 verbs
exceptions.description: 하다 conjugates to 했더라면.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular (past)
exceptions.description: For adjectives ending in ㅂ, ㅂ becomes 우 in the past tense.

exceptions.name: ㅡ drop (past)
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅡ, the ㅡ drops when adding 어/아.

explanation: V-(으)ㄹ 겸 (해서) means 'partly' or 'as well as'. Use it when you have two reasons or purposes for doing something at the same time. It conveys a practical, efficient attitude of combining two related actions into one trip or effort.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 겸 (해서)
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 겸 (해서) to stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ. The second purpose is often added with another -(으)ㄹ 겸 or a different purpose expression.

examples.english: 운동할 겸 근처 공원에 갔어요 — I went to the nearby park partly to exercise.
산책할 겸 해서 카페에 들렀어요 — I stopped by a cafe while taking a walk.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 겸 (해서)
rules.description: Attach 을 겸 (해서) to stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ). 해서 is optional but commonly included.

examples.english: 친구를 볼 겸 집에 갔어요 — I went home partly to see a friend.
저녁을 먹을 겸 해서 식당에 예약했어요 — I made a restaurant reservation partly to have dinner.

notes.title: Dual purpose
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 겸 implies that the primary action serves two goals at once. The two purposes should be naturally related. It would sound odd to combine completely unrelated activities.

notes.title: 해서 is optional
notes.text: Both -ㄹ 겸 and -ㄹ 겸 해서 are correct. Adding 해서 makes the connection to the main clause smoother and more explicit.

notes.title: Compare with -기 위해서
notes.text: -기 위해서 (ID 113) has one clear purpose. -(으)ㄹ 겸 (해서) (ID 258) has two overlapping purposes. Use 겸 when efficiency and multitasking are the point.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before a vowel.

explanation: Use -기로 하다 when you or someone else makes a conscious decision or plan to do something. It expresses a commitment, promise, or agreement — like saying 'I have decided to...' or 'we agreed to...'

rules.title: V-기로 하다
rules.description: Attach 기로 하다 directly to the verb stem. Use past tense 하다 to show the decision was already made, or present tense for a current decision.

examples.english: 내일 한국에 가기로 했어요 — I've decided to go to Korea tomorrow.
매일 운동하기로 했어요 — I've decided to exercise every day.
커피를 안 마시기로 했어요 — I've decided not to drink coffee.

notes.title: Active decision
notes.text: -기로 하다 is for decisions you make yourself. For decisions made by rules or external circumstances, use -기로 되다 (ID 260).

notes.title: Past tense common
notes.text: Most often you'll see 기로 했어요 or 기로 했다 because the decision was made in the past, even if the action is in the future.

explanation: Use -기로 되다 when something is decided by an external authority, rule, or circumstance rather than personal choice. It has a passive meaning — 'it has been decided that...' or 'it is arranged that...'

rules.title: V-기로 되다
rules.description: Attach 기로 되다 to the verb stem. The subject is often omitted since the decider is external or understood from context.

examples.english: 다음 주에 만나기로 됐어요 — It's been arranged that we'll meet next week.
아침 8시에 출발하기로 됐어요 — It's been decided that we depart at 8 a.m.
내일 문을 열기로 됐어요 — It's been decided that the door will be opened tomorrow.

notes.title: Passive decision
notes.text: Unlike -기로 하다 (ID 259), which is an active personal decision, -기로 되다 implies the decision was made by someone else or by rules.

notes.title: Company and schedule contexts
notes.text: Very common in business Korean for meetings, deadlines, and official schedules.

explanation: Use -(으)ㄴ/는 만큼 to show proportion or degree — 'as much as,' 'to the extent that.' The result in the second clause matches or corresponds to the degree described in the first clause.

rules.title: A-(으)ㄴ 만큼
rules.description: For adjectives: add -은 만큼 if the stem ends in a 받침, or -ㄴ 만큼 if it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 그 사람은 착한 만큼 인기도 많아요 — He's as popular as he is kind.
작은 만큼 휴대하기 편해요 — It's as portable as it is small.

rules.title: V-는 만큼
rules.description: For verbs: add -는 만큼 to the stem.

examples.english: 열심히 노력하는 만큼 실력이 늘어요 — Your skills improve as much as you work hard.

notes.title: Proportional relationship
notes.text: Think of it as 'the more X, the more Y.' The two clauses are directly proportional.

notes.title: Not for exact equality
notes.text: For exact equality ('as much as N'), use N-만큼. -(으)ㄴ/는 만큼 is for proportional situations.

explanation: Use -기만 하면 to express that only one simple condition needs to be met for something to be sufficient or acceptable. It translates to 'all you have to do is...' The 되다 at the end is optional but very common.

rules.title: V-기만 하면
rules.description: Attach 기만 하면 to the verb or adjective stem. Add 되다 at the end to mean 'and that's enough.'

examples.english: 이 버튼을 누르기만 하면 돼요 — All you have to do is press this button.
거기에 가기만 하면 돼요 — You just have to go there.
문을 열기만 하면 돼요 — All you have to do is open the door.

notes.title: 되다 is optional
notes.text: You can say -기만 하면 돼요, -기만 하면 괜찮아요, or just -기만 하면. 되다 is the most common pairing.

notes.title: Simplicity and sufficiency
notes.text: This grammar emphasizes how easy something is — only one action is needed, nothing more.

explanation: Use -을/를 정도(로) to describe the extent, degree, or point of something — 'to the extent that,' 'so much that.' It often sets up a comparison or describes an extreme situation.

rules.title: A/V-(으)ㄹ 정도(로)
rules.description: Add -(으)ㄹ 정도 or -(으)ㄹ 정도로 to the stem. Use 로 when the phrase modifies an adjective or verb; drop 로 when it modifies a noun or ends the clause.

examples.english: 배부를 정도로 많이 먹었어요 — I ate so much that I was full to the point of bursting.
피곤할 정도로 일했어요 — I worked to the point of exhaustion.
놀랄 정도로 많이 늘었어요 — I've improved so much it's surprising.

notes.title: 정도로 vs 정도
notes.text: Use 정도로 when the phrase modifies what comes after. Use 정도 when it stands alone or is followed by 이다.

notes.title: Extreme situations
notes.text: This grammar often describes extreme or notable degrees — more than just a little.

explanation: Use -만 하다 to say something is about the same size, level, or amount as something else. Use -만 못하다 to say it's not even at that level — 'not as much as' or 'not even.'

rules.title: N-만 하다
rules.description: Attach 만 하다 to a noun to express equality in size, amount, or level.

examples.english: 아기가 강아지만 해요 — The baby is about the size of a puppy.
이 책이 사전만 해요 — This book is about as big as a dictionary.

rules.title: N-만 못하다
rules.description: Attach 만 못하다 to express that something falls short of the noun's level.

examples.english: 오늘 날씨가 어제만 못해요 — Today's weather isn't as good as yesterday's.
내 성적이 동생만 못해요 — My grades aren't even as good as my younger sibling's.

notes.title: Size and level
notes.text: -만 하다 commonly refers to physical size, but can also mean skill level or amount.

notes.title: Different from -만큼
notes.text: N-만 하다 compares equality directly. For proportional comparison, use A/V-만큼 or N-만큼.

explanation: Use -(으)ㄹ 뿐만 아니라 to add information — 'not only A but also B.' It emphasizes that there is more than what was first mentioned.

rules.title: A/V-(으)ㄹ 뿐만 아니라
rules.description: Add -(으)ㄹ 뿐만 아니라 to the stem. Use with both adjectives and verbs. The second clause is connected directly.

examples.english: 한국에 갈뿐만 아니라 일본에도 갔어요 — I went not only to Korea but also to Japan.
그 사람은 친절할뿐만 아니라 똑똤해요 — He's not only kind but also smart.
한국어를 공부할뿐만 아니라 일본어도 공부해요 — I study not only Korean but also Japanese.

notes.title: Both clauses must agree
notes.text: The subject should usually be the same in both clauses, or the second clause should clearly continue from the first.

notes.title: Related form
notes.text: See also ID 267 for -(으)ㄹ 뿐이다, which limits meaning to 'only/merely' instead of adding information.

explanation: Use -(으)ㄹ 뿐이다 to limit or minimize something — 'it's just that,' 'merely,' 'only.' It downplays a situation by saying there's only one reason or factor involved.

rules.title: A/V-(으)ㄹ 뿐이다
rules.description: Add -(으)ㄹ 뿐이다 to the stem. Use 이에요/예요 in polite speech. The past tense form is -(으)ㄹ 뿐이었다.

examples.english: 모를 뿐이에요 — I just don't know.
피곤할 뿐이에요 — I'm just tired.
바쁠 뿐이에요 — I'm just busy.

notes.title: Softening effect
notes.text: Using 뿐이다 softens your statement and avoids sounding too blunt or negative.

notes.title: Different from -뿐
notes.text: N-뿐 (ID 270) is for nouns only. A/V-(으)ㄹ 뿐이다 is for verbs and adjectives.

explanation: Use 얼마나 with -(으)ㄴ지 to create exclamatory sentences about degree. It expresses 'how very' or 'I can't tell you how much.' The second clause usually says the feeling or effect is beyond words.

rules.title: 얼마나 A-(으)ㄴ지
rules.description: For adjectives: 얼마나 + stem + -(으)ㄴ지. Use -은지 with 받침, -ㄴ지 with vowel or ㄹ ending.

examples.english: 그 산이 얼마나 아름다운지 몰라요 — You wouldn't believe how beautiful that mountain is.
얼마나 힘든지 말로 표현할 수 없어요 — I can't express in words how hard it was.

rules.title: 얼마나 V-는지
rules.description: For verbs: 얼마나 + stem + -는지.

examples.english: 얼마나 좋아하는지 다 알아요 — Everyone knows how much he likes it.

notes.title: Exclamation pattern
notes.text: This is a classic exclamation structure. The second clause often contains 몰라요, 말로 할 수 없어요, or 다 몰라요.

notes.title: Not a real question
notes.text: Even though it ends in -는지, this is not an actual question. It's an emotional statement about degree.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For adjectives ending in ㅂ, the ㅂ changes to 우 before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㄹ ending
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄹ, the ㄹ drops before ㄴ.

explanation: Use -뿐 after a noun to mean 'only,' 'just,' or 'nothing but.' It limits the subject or object to one single thing. It sounds more emphatic and slightly more formal than -만.

rules.title: N-뿐
rules.description: Attach 뿐 directly to the noun. Usually followed by 이다 (뿐이다) or a negative verb to complete the meaning.

examples.english: 여기 있는 사람은 친구뿐이에요 — The only person here is my friend.
남은 것은 이 한 개뿐이에요 — There's only this one left.
매일 하는 것은 공부뿐이에요 — All I do every day is study.

notes.title: More emphatic than -만
notes.text: -뿐 carries a stronger sense of limitation or exclusivity compared to -만. It can sound more dramatic.

notes.title: Often with 이다
notes.text: N-뿐 is very commonly followed by 이다: N뿐이다 / N뿐이에요.

explanation: Use -조차 after a noun to mean 'even.' It emphasizes that something unexpected or surprising is included. It works in both positive and negative sentences and adds a strong sense of surprise.

rules.title: N-조차
rules.description: Attach 조차 directly to the noun. The sentence often expresses that even this minimum or unexpected thing is true or not true.

examples.english: 한 번조차 안 왔어요 — He hasn't even come once.
물조차 마실 수 없었어요 — I couldn't even drink water.
그것조차 몰라요 — I don't even know that.

notes.title: Stronger than -도
notes.text: -조차 is more emphatic than -도. It emphasizes that even the minimum or least expected thing is involved.

notes.title: Noun only
notes.text: Unlike -조차만, standard -조차 attaches only to nouns. For verbs and adjectives, use other forms like -기조차 하다.

explanation: Use -마저 after a noun to mean 'even' in negative or unfortunate situations. It expresses despair or disappointment that even the last remaining hope or thing has been lost or affected. It carries a sadder, more emotional tone than 조차.

rules.title: N-마저
rules.description: Attach 마저 directly to the noun. Only use in negative, sad, or unfortunate contexts — never in positive sentences.

examples.english: 친구마저 나를 떠났어요 — Even my friend left me.
돈마저 다 잃었어요 — I lost even my money.
집마저 팔아야 했어요 — I even had to sell my house.

notes.title: Negative contexts only
notes.text: Unlike 조차 (ID 271), 마저 cannot be used in positive sentences. It always conveys misfortune.

notes.title: Last remaining thing
notes.text: 마저 often implies that this was the last thing you had left, and now it's gone too.

explanation: Use -(으)ㄴ/는/(으)ㄹ 것 같다 to express supposition, inference, or a gentle guess. 'It seems like,' 'I think,' 'probably.' The tense is shown by the modifier before 것, not by 같다 itself.

rules.title: A-(으)ㄴ 것 같다
rules.description: For adjectives in the present: add -(으)ㄴ 것 같다. Use -은 with 받침, -ㄴ with vowel or ㄹ ending.

examples.english: 날씨가 좋은 것 같아요 — I think the weather is nice.
오늘이 추운 것 같아요 — I think it's cold today.

rules.title: V-는 것 같다
rules.description: For verbs in the present: add -는 것 같다.

examples.english: 지금 비가 오는 것 같아요 — I think it's raining now.

rules.title: A/V-(으)ㄹ 것 같다
rules.description: For future tense or supposition about what will happen: add -(으)ㄹ 것 같다.

examples.breakdown: 내일 비가 오 + 을 것 같다
examples.english: 내일 비가 올 것 같아요 — I think it will rain tomorrow.

notes.title: Most common supposition form
notes.text: This is the most widely used grammar for 'I think' in Korean. It's softer and more natural than directly stating your opinion.

notes.title: First-person intention
notes.text: With first person and future tense, it can express your own intention: 저도 갈 것 같아요 can mean 'I think I'll go too.'

notes.title: Related forms
notes.text: See ID 274 for objective appearance-based inference, ID 276 for colloquial -ㄴ가 보다, and ID 278 for verb-only -나 보다.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For adjectives ending in ㅂ, the ㅂ changes to 우 before a vowel.

explanation: Use -(으)ㄴ/는/(으)ㄹ 모양이다 to make an inference based on external appearance or observable evidence. 'It looks like,' 'it appears.' It is more objective and evidence-based than 것 같다, which can be based on feeling.

rules.title: A-(으)ㄴ 모양이다
rules.description: For adjectives: add -(으)ㄴ 모양이다.

examples.english: 몸이 아픈 모양이에요 — It looks like he's sick.

rules.title: V-는 모양이다
rules.description: For verbs: add -는 모양이다.

examples.english: 아직 자는 모양이에요 — It looks like he's still sleeping.

rules.title: A/V-(으)ㄹ 모양이다
rules.description: For future inference: add -(으)ㄹ 모양이다.

examples.english: 오늘은 늦을 모양이에요 — It looks like he'll be late today.

notes.title: Evidence-based
notes.text: Use 모양이다 when you can see or observe clear evidence. Use 것 같다 (ID 273) when it's more of a feeling or general guess.

notes.title: Visual situations
notes.text: 모양이다 is especially common for describing how someone looks or appears physically.

explanation: Use -(으)ㄴ가 보다 to express a guess or inference based on what you observe. 'It seems like,' 'I guess,' 'apparently.' It is more colloquial and conversational than 것 같다. You have some evidence, but you aren't certain.

rules.title: A/V-(으)ㄴ가 보다
rules.description: Add -(으)ㄴ가 보다 to the stem. Use -은가 보다 with 받침, -ㄴ가 보다 with vowel or ㄹ ending. Conjugate 보다 to 보다, 봐요, or 봤어요.

examples.english: 얼굴이 피곤한가 봐요 — You look tired, I guess.
전화가 안 받는 걸 보니 바쁜가 봐요 — He isn't answering his phone, so I guess he's busy.
매일 듣는 걸 보니 노래를 좋아하는가 봐요 — He listens to it every day, so I guess he likes the song.

notes.title: Colloquial guess
notes.text: This is the most casual and conversational way to say 'it seems like.' Use it in daily speech.

notes.title: Observation required
notes.text: Unlike plain 추측, -ㄴ가 보다 implies you have some observable reason for your guess.

notes.title: Verb form
notes.text: For action verbs, -나 보다 (ID 278) is often preferred, but -는가 보다 is also acceptable in casual speech.

explanation: Use -(으)ㄹ 테니까 to present your assumption or supposition as a reason for a suggestion, request, or command. 'I suppose... so (please)...' The 테 comes from 터 (basis/ground) and expresses the speaker's reasoning about the listener's situation.

rules.title: A/V-(으)ㄹ 테니까
rules.description: Add -(으)ㄹ 테니까 to the stem. The second clause is usually an imperative (-세요), suggestion (-(으)ㅂ시다), or piece of advice.

examples.english: 배고플 테니까 먼저 드세요 — I suppose you're hungry, so please eat first.
추울 테니까 따뜻하게 입으세요 — I suppose it's cold, so dress warmly.
피곤할 테니까 일찍 쉬세요 — I suppose you're tired, so rest early.

notes.title: Speaker's assumption about listener
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 테니까 usually expresses what the speaker supposes about the listener's situation, followed by advice or a request.

notes.title: Not for facts
notes.text: Don't use this for objective facts. It must be a supposition or guess that motivates the following clause.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅂ, the ㅂ changes to 우 before a vowel.

exceptions.name: ㄹ ending
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄹ, the ㄹ drops.

explanation: Use -나 보다 to express an inference or guess based on observation, specifically with action verbs. 'It seems like,' 'apparently,' 'I see that.' It has the same meaning as -(으)ㄴ가 보다 (ID 276) but is the standard form for verbs.

rules.title: V-나 보다
rules.description: Add -나 보다 directly to the verb stem. Conjugate 보다 as needed (보다, 봐요, 봤어요).

examples.english: 집에 안 가나 봐요 — It seems like he's not going home.
음식을 안 먹나 봐요 — It seems like he's not eating the food.
노래를 좋아하나 봐요 — It seems like he likes the song.

notes.title: Verb-only form
notes.text: -나 보다 is used with action verbs. For adjectives, use -(으)ㄴ가 보다 (ID 276).

notes.title: Observable behavior
notes.text: Like -(으)ㄴ가 보다, this form is based on watching someone's actions or behavior and drawing a conclusion.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ걸(요) expresses the speaker's assumption or guess about a present or past situation — like saying 'probably would' or 'must have.' It can also carry a tone of regret when reflecting on the past, similar to 'I should have.'

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ걸(요)
rules.description: Attach ㄹ걸(요) directly to the verb stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 지금쯤 집에 갔을걸요 — They must have gone home by now.
버스가 곧 올걸요 — The bus will probably come soon.
그때 여기서 살걸 — I should have lived here back then.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 을걸(요)
rules.description: Attach 을걸(요) to the verb stem when it ends in a consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 벌써 다 먹었을걸요 — They've probably already eaten it all.
그 책을 읽을걸 — I probably should have read that book.

notes.title: Past-tense anchor
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ걸 often sits inside an past-tense clause (갔을걸, 먹었을걸) to speculate about something that already happened.

notes.title: Regret tone
notes.text: When used with a plain verb stem and no past marker inside, it can sound like wistful regret: 일찍 갈걸 ('I should have left early').

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also ID 285 -(으)ㄹ 리가 없다 for another way to express strong assumptions.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: ㅂ → 우

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄷ → ㄹ

exceptions.name: 르 irregular
exceptions.description: 르 + (으)ㄹ → 르 + ㄹ

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: ㅅ drops

explanation: -나 마나 means 'whether or not' in the sense that the outcome is the same either way. It carries a resigned or dismissive nuance — the action or state barely matters because the result will not change.

rules.title: Verb → + 나 마나
rules.description: Attach 나 마나 directly to the verb stem regardless of the final consonant.

examples.english: 가나 마나 똑같을 거예요 — Whether I go or not, it'll be the same.
먹나 마나 맛이 없어요 — Whether I eat it or not, it tastes bad.
하나 마나 결과는 같아요 — Whether I do it or not, the result is the same.

rules.title: Adjective without 받침 → + 나 마나
rules.description: For adjectives ending in a vowel, attach 나 마나 directly.

examples.english: 크나 마나 다 비싸요 — Whether it's big or not, it's all expensive.

rules.title: Adjective with 받침 → + 으나 마나
rules.description: For adjectives ending in a consonant, attach 으나 마나.

examples.english: 작으나 마나 쓸 만해요 — Whether it's small or not, it's usable.
많으나 마나 필요 없어요 — Whether there's a lot or not, I don't need it.

notes.title: Negative context
notes.text: -나 마나 almost always appears in negative or hopeless contexts. Saying 가나 마나 기쁠 거예요 sounds unnatural because the result is positive.

notes.title: Fixed expression
notes.text: The second half 마나 never changes. Only the first half conjugates to match the verb or adjective.

explanation: -(으)ㄹ 리가 없다 means 'no way' or 'there's no reason to.' It expresses strong disbelief that something is possible. The literal sense is 'there is no logic/reason for that to happen,' making it firmer than a simple guess.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄹ 리가 없다
rules.description: Attach ㄹ 리가 없다 to the verb stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 그 사람이 거기에 갈 리가 없어요 — There's no way that person would go there.
그가 그런 실수를 할 리가 없어요 — There's no way he would make such a mistake.
그 집에 살 리가 없어요 — There's no way they live in that house.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 을 리가 없다
rules.description: Attach 을 리가 없다 to the verb stem when it ends in a consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 그 아이가 이걸 먹을 리가 없어요 — There's no way the child would eat this.
그 책을 읽을 리가 없어요 — There's no way he read that book.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: This grammar pairs naturally with words like 거짓말, 실수, 그런 일 — things that are hard to believe.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also ID 281 -(으)ㄹ걸(요) for supposition, and ID 197 -(으)ㄴ/는 대로 for immediate action.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: ㅂ → 우

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄷ → ㄹ

exceptions.name: 르 irregular
exceptions.description: 르 + (으)ㄹ → 르 + ㄹ

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: ㅅ drops

explanation: -(으)ㄴ 척하다 means 'pretend to have done' something. The past modifier -(으)ㄴ attaches to the verb, and 척하다 means 'to pretend.' Use this when someone acts as if a past or completed action is true — even when it is not.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ 척하다
rules.description: Attach ㄴ 척하다 to the verb stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 친구가 간 척했어요 — My friend pretended to have gone.
저를 모른 척했어요 — He pretended not to know me.
아이가 잔 척했어요 — The child pretended to have slept.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 은 척하다
rules.description: Attach 은 척하다 to the verb stem when it ends in a consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 다 먹은 척했어요 — She pretended to have eaten it all.
못 들은 척했어요 — He pretended not to have heard.

notes.title: Most common usage
notes.text: 모른 척하다 is by far the most frequent collocation you will hear in daily conversation.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 288 for the more formal equivalent -(으)ㄴ 체하다, and ID 289 for pretending an action is currently happening.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄹ drops

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: ㅂ → 우

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄷ → ㄹ

exceptions.name: 르 irregular
exceptions.description: 르 → ㄹ + ㄴ

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: ㅅ drops

explanation: -(으)ㄴ 체하다 means 'pretend to have done' and is functionally identical to -(으)ㄴ 척하다. The difference is tone: 체하다 is more formal, literary, and slightly old-fashioned, while 척하다 is common in everyday speech.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ 체하다
rules.description: Attach ㄴ 체하다 to the verb stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 아무것도 모른 체했어요 — She pretended to know nothing at all.
잔 체하고 조용히 나갔어요 — He pretended to be asleep and quietly went out.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 은 체하다
rules.description: Attach 은 체하다 to the verb stem when it ends in a consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 이미 먹은 체했어요 — He pretended he had already eaten.
못 들은 체하며 고개를 돌렸어요 — She turned her head, pretending not to have heard.

notes.title: Formal and written
notes.text: Use 체하다 in novels, news articles, or formal essays. In spoken Korean, 척하다 sounds far more natural.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 287 -(으)ㄴ 척하다 for the everyday equivalent, and ID 289 for pretending an ongoing action.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄹ drops

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: ㅂ → 우

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄷ → ㄹ

exceptions.name: 르 irregular
exceptions.description: 르 → ㄹ + ㄴ

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: ㅅ drops

explanation: -는 척하다 means 'pretend to be doing.' The present modifier -는 attaches to the verb, so this form describes faking an action that appears to be in progress at that moment.

rules.title: All verbs → + 는 척하다
rules.description: Attach 는 척하다 directly to any verb stem. There is no 받침 variation.

examples.english: 아이가 자는 척했어요 — The child pretended to be sleeping.
공부하는 척했어요 — He pretended to be studying.
선생님 말씀을 듣는 척했어요 — She pretended to be listening to the teacher.

notes.title: Present vs past pretending
notes.text: Use -는 척하다 when the action looks ongoing, and -(으)ㄴ 척하다 (ID 287) when the action looks already completed.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: 자는 척하다, 공부하는 척하다, and 듣는 척하다 are extremely common in daily conversation.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄹ drops before 는
exceptions.example: 살다 → 사는 척하다, 알다 → 아는 척하다

explanation: -(ㄴ/는)다고 하다 is indirect speech for action verbs. It reports what someone said they do, are doing, or did. Think of it as wrapping the plain speech form -(ㄴ/는)다 inside 다고 하다 to say 'they said that they do.'

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + ㄴ다고 하다
rules.description: Action verbs ending in a vowel or ㄹ take ㄴ다고 하다 in the present tense.

examples.english: 친구가 내일 온다고 해요 — My friend says he's coming tomorrow.
엄마가 금방 오신다고 하셨어요 — Mom said she'll be here soon.
그 사람이 여기 산다고 했어요 — He said he lives here.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 는다고 하다
rules.description: Action verbs ending in a consonant other than ㄹ take 는다고 하다 in the present tense.

examples.english: 동생이 밥을 먹는다고 했어요 — My younger sibling said he's eating.
그녀가 책을 읽는다고 해요 — She says she's reading a book.
선생님이 열심히 공부한다고 하셨어요 — The teacher said she studies hard.

notes.title: Tense shifts inside
notes.text: You can embed past or future tense inside: 갔다고 했어요 ('said he went'), 갈 거라고 했어요 ('said he will go').

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 291 for adjectives, ID 292 for nouns, and IDs 293–295 for other speech acts like suggestions and commands.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄹ drops before ㄴ/는
exceptions.example: 살다 → 산다고 하다, 알다 → 안다고 하다

explanation: -다고 하다 is indirect speech for adjectives. It reports what someone said about a state or quality — 'they said that it is [adjective].' All adjectives use the same -다고 attachment with no 받침 variation.

rules.title: All adjectives → + 다고 하다
rules.description: Attach 다고 하다 directly to any adjective stem. There is no 받침 variation for adjectives.

examples.english: 이 식당이 맛있다고 해요 — They say this restaurant is delicious.
오늘 바쁘다고 했어요 — She said she's busy today.
그 집이 예쁘다고 해요 — They say that house is pretty.

notes.title: 아니다 is special
notes.text: Although 아니다 is grammatically an adjective, it does not take 다고. It becomes 아니라고 하다, matching the noun pattern in ID 292.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 290 for action verbs, ID 292 for nouns, and IDs 296–300 for echo and follow-up forms.

explanation: -(이)라고 하다 is indirect speech for nouns. It reports what someone said something is — 'they said it is [noun].' Use 이라고 after a noun with 받침, and 라고 after a noun without 받침.

rules.title: Noun without 받침 → + 라고 하다
rules.description: Attach 라고 하다 to nouns ending in a vowel.

examples.english: 그 사람이 의사라고 해요 — They say that person is a doctor.
한국 학생이라고 했어요 — She said she's a Korean student.

rules.title: Noun with 받침 → + 이라고 하다
rules.description: Attach 이라고 하다 to nouns ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 우리 선생님이라고 했어요 — He said he's our teacher.
큰 회사의 회사원이라고 해요 — They say he works at a big company.

notes.title: 아니다 follows this pattern
notes.text: 아니라고 하다 behaves like a noun: it always uses 라고, never 다고.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 290 for action verbs, ID 291 for adjectives, and IDs 293–295 for commands, suggestions, and questions.

explanation: -자고 하다 reports a suggestion or proposal. It wraps the propositive ending -자 inside 다고 하다 to mean 'someone suggested that we do something.'

rules.title: All verbs → + 자고 하다
rules.description: Attach 자고 하다 directly to any verb stem. There is no 받침 variation.

examples.english: 친구가 영화관에 가자고 해요 — My friend suggests going to the cinema.
저녁을 먹자고 했어요 — He suggested having dinner.
숙제를 내일 하자고 해요 — She suggests doing the homework tomorrow.

notes.title: Softened command
notes.text: -자고 하다 is less forceful than -(으)라고 하다 (ID 294). It always includes the speaker in the proposed action, like saying 'let's.'

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 294 for commands and ID 295 for questions.

explanation: -(으)라고 하다 reports a command, order, or strong request. It wraps the imperative ending -(으)라 inside 다고 하다 to mean 'someone told someone else to do something.'

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 라고 하다
rules.description: Attach 라고 하다 directly to the verb stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 빨리 가라고 했어요 — He told me to go quickly.
여기로 오라고 하셨어요 — She told me to come here.
조용히 살라고 했어요 — They told me to live quietly.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 으라고 하다
rules.description: Attach 으라고 하다 to the verb stem when it ends in a consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 약을 먹으라고 했어요 — The doctor told me to take medicine.
이 책을 읽으라고 하셨어요 — The teacher told me to read this book.

notes.title: Not for self
notes.text: -(으)라고 하다 is for commands given to someone else. If the subject is telling themselves, use -(으)려고 하다 or a different structure.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 293 for suggestions (-자고 하다) and ID 295 for questions (-냐고 하다).

explanation: -냐고 하다 reports a question. It wraps the plain interrogative ending -냐 inside 다고 하다 to mean 'someone asked if...' Use this for yes/no questions and wh-questions alike.

rules.title: All verbs → + 냐고 하다
rules.description: Attach 냐고 하다 directly to any verb stem. There is no 받침 variation.

examples.english: 어디 가냐고 물어봤어요 — He asked where I was going.
밥 먹었냐고 했어요 — He asked if I had eaten.
뭐 하냐고 해요 — He asks what I'm doing.

notes.title: Replace the question mark
notes.text: Take the original question, drop the question mark, and attach 냐고: 갔어? → 갔냐고, 뭐 해? → 뭐 하냐고.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See IDs 290–294 for statements, suggestions, and commands.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄹ drops before 냐
exceptions.example: 살다 → 사냐고 하다, 알다 → 아냐고 하다

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는다고(요)? is an echo question. It repeats what someone just said to seek confirmation or express mild surprise — like saying 'You mean...?' or 'So you're saying...?' Adding 요 softens the tone.

rules.title: Verb (vowel or ㄹ ending) → + ㄴ다고(요)?
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a vowel or ㄹ, attach ㄴ다고(요)?.

examples.english: 내일 간다고요? — You mean you're going tomorrow?
혼자 온다고? — You're coming alone?

rules.title: Verb (consonant ending) → + 는다고(요)?
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a consonant other than ㄹ, attach 는다고(요)?.

examples.english: 그걸 먹는다고요? — You're going to eat that?
혼자 한다고요? — You mean you're doing it alone?

rules.title: Adjective → + 다고(요)?
rules.description: Adjectives always attach 다고(요)? with no variation.

examples.english: 맛있다고요? — You mean it's delicious?
오늘 바쁘다고? — You're busy today?

notes.title: Intonation matters
notes.text: With rising intonation, it seeks confirmation. With flat or falling intonation, it can show disbelief.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 297 -(으)ㄴ/는다면서(요)? for 'I heard that...' and ID 298 -(으)ㄴ/는다니(요)? for expressing stronger disbelief.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄹ drops before ㄴ/는

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는다면서(요)? is used when you want to confirm something you heard from someone else. It translates to 'I heard that...' or 'Didn't you say...?' and carries a tone of seeking verification.

rules.title: Verb (vowel or ㄹ ending) → + ㄴ다면서(요)?
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a vowel or ㄹ, attach ㄴ다면서(요)?.

examples.english: 한국에 간다면서요? — I heard you're going to Korea?
내일 온다면서? — Didn't you say you're coming tomorrow?

rules.title: Verb (consonant ending) → + 는다면서(요)?
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a consonant other than ㄹ, attach 는다면서(요)?.

examples.english: 다이어트한다면서요? — Didn't you say you're on a diet?

rules.title: Adjective → + 다면서(요)?
rules.description: Adjectives always attach 다면서(요)? with no variation.

examples.english: 그 집이 싸다면서요? — I heard that house is cheap?
바쁘다면서요? — Didn't you say you were busy?

notes.title: Confrontational nuance
notes.text: If the facts don't match what you heard, this can sound slightly confrontational. Use 요 to soften.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 296 -(으)ㄴ/는다고(요)? for echo questions and ID 298 -(으)ㄴ/는다니(요)? for expressing disbelief.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄹ drops before ㄴ/는

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는다니(요)? expresses surprise or disbelief at what someone just said. It translates to 'You mean...?' or 'I can't believe...' The emotion is stronger than -(으)ㄴ/는다고(요)? and often shows genuine shock.

rules.title: Verb (vowel or ㄹ ending) → + ㄴ다니(요)?
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a vowel or ㄹ, attach ㄴ다니(요)?.

examples.english: 혼자 간다니? — You mean you're going alone? I can't believe it.
그 사람이 온다니요? — He's really coming?

rules.title: Verb (consonant ending) → + 는다니(요)?
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a consonant other than ㄹ, attach 는다니(요)?.

examples.english: 그걸 먹는다니! — I can't believe you're eating that!

rules.title: Adjective → + 다니(요)?
rules.description: Adjectives always attach 다니(요)? with no variation.

examples.english: 이렇게 크다니! — I can't believe it's this big!
시험이 어렵다니요? — You mean the exam is hard?

notes.title: Emotional reaction
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ/는다니 is not neutral. It always carries emotion — surprise, shock, or disbelief. Use it carefully in formal settings.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 296 -(으)ㄴ/는다고(요)? for milder confirmation and ID 297 -(으)ㄴ/는다면서(요)? for checking hearsay.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄹ drops before ㄴ/는

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는다거나 is indirect speech for listing alternatives. It reports that someone mentioned one or more options — 'they said they do this or that.' It is the quoted form of the casual listing connector -(으)ㄴ/는다거나.

rules.title: Verb (vowel or ㄹ ending) → + ㄴ다거나
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a vowel or ㄹ, attach ㄴ다거나.

examples.english: 한국에 간다거나 일본에 간다거나 해요 — They say they might go to Korea or Japan.

rules.title: Verb (consonant ending) → + 는다거나
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a consonant other than ㄹ, attach 는다거나.

examples.english: 피자를 먹는다거나 치킨을 시킨다거나 — They said they eat pizza or order chicken.

rules.title: Adjective → + 다거나
rules.description: Adjectives always attach 다거나 with no variation.

examples.english: 집이 크다거나 마당이 있다거나 — They said the house is big or it has a yard.
날씨가 좋다거나 바다가 있다거나 — They said the weather is nice or there's a beach.

notes.title: Listing pattern
notes.text: This grammar typically lists two or more options, often ending with a vague verb like 하다 to show uncertainty.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See IDs 290–295 for basic indirect speech forms.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄹ drops before ㄴ/는

explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는다기에 / 다길래 means 'because they said' or 'since I heard that.' It explains why the speaker took action based on what they heard from someone else. -다길래 is more casual and colloquial; -다기에 is slightly more formal.

rules.title: Verb (vowel or ㄹ ending) → + ㄴ다기에 / ㄴ다길래
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a vowel or ㄹ, attach ㄴ다기에 or ㄴ다길래.

examples.english: 친구가 온다기에 많이 준비했어요 — Since I heard my friend was coming, I prepared a lot.
손님이 온다길래 방을 청소했어요 — Since I heard guests were coming, I cleaned the room.

rules.title: Verb (consonant ending) → + 는다기에 / 는다길래
rules.description: For action verbs ending in a consonant other than ㄹ, attach 는다기에 or 는다길래.

examples.english: 아이가 안 먹는다길래 다른 걸 만들었어요 — Since I heard the child wasn't eating, I made something else.

rules.title: Adjective → + 다기에 / 다길래
rules.description: Adjectives always attach 다기에 or 다길래 with no variation.

examples.english: 친구가 아프다기에 병원에 같이 갔어요 — Since I heard my friend was sick, I went to the hospital with her.
날씨가 춥다길래 코트를 입었어요 — Since I heard it was cold, I wore a coat.

notes.title: Causal link
notes.text: Both forms strongly imply that the speaker acted because of what they heard. The result clause is almost always about the speaker's own action.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See IDs 290–295 for basic indirect speech forms, and ID 299 -(으)ㄴ/는다거나 for listing alternatives.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: ㄹ drops before ㄴ/는

explanation: This grammar is used when someone quoted or said something, but the actual result turned out different or contrasting. It combines indirect quotation of a present-tense statement with 더니, which shows a turn of events. Use it when you want to say "They said X, but (in reality) Y."

rules.title: Action verbs with vowel or ㄹ ending → V-ㄴ다더니
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다더니 to the stem. This form is used when quoting an action that was said to happen.

examples.english: 친구가 온다더니 안 왔어요 — They said a friend was coming, but they didn't come.
케이크를 만든다더니 사 온 거예요 — They said they'd make a cake, but they bought one instead.

rules.title: Action verbs with consonant ending → V-는다더니
rules.description: Attach 는다더니 to the stem. Use this for action verbs that end in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 다 먹는다더니 남겼더라고요 — They said they'd eat it all, but they left some.
책을 읽는다더니 자꾸 전화만 해요 — They said they'd read, but they keep making phone calls.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs → A-다더니
rules.description: Attach 다더니 directly to the adjective stem. There is no 받침 distinction for adjectives.

examples.english: 그 집이 작다더니 생각보다 컸어요 — They said that house was small, but it was bigger than I thought.
맛있다더니 먹어 보니 별로였어요 — They said it was delicious, but when I tried it, it was nothing special.

rules.title: Noun + 이다 → 이라더니 / 아니라더니
rules.description: For nouns with 이다, use 이라더니. For 아니다, use 아니라더니.

examples.english: 학생이라더니 벌써 졸업했대요 — They said (he) was a student, but I heard he already graduated.

notes.title: Contrast is key
notes.text: The second clause usually describes a result that contrasts with the quoted statement. However, it can also confirm what was said, though this is less common.

notes.title: Speaker's direct observation
notes.text: The result in the second clause must be something the speaker directly observed or learned about firsthand.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also 302 (A/V-(으)ㄴ/는다니까) for giving reasons based on hearsay, and 303 for observed results.

explanation: Use this when giving a reason or cause based on what someone else said. It combines indirect quotation of a present statement with 니까 ("because"). It translates to "They say... so..." or "Because they say..." Use it to pass along someone's statement as justification for an action or conclusion.

rules.title: Action verbs with vowel or ㄹ ending → V-ㄴ다니까
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다니까 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 친구가 온다니까 기다리세요 — They say a friend is coming, so please wait.
곧 시작한다니까 서둘러요 — They say it'll start soon, so hurry.

rules.title: Action verbs with consonant ending → V-는다니까
rules.description: Attach 는다니까 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 끝난다니까 이제 쉬어요 — They say it's ending, so rest now.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs → A-다니까
rules.description: Attach 다니까 directly to the adjective stem.

examples.english: 시험이 어렵다니까 열심히 공부하세요 — They say the exam is hard, so study hard.
날씨가 춥다니까 따뜻하게 입으세요 — They say the weather is cold, so dress warmly.

notes.title: Relaying instructions
notes.text: This is commonly used to pass along instructions, warnings, or advice from a third party.

notes.title: Quoted information as reason
notes.text: The quoted statement is treated as the direct cause for the command, suggestion, or statement in the second clause.

explanation: Use these endings when you heard someone say something and you either observed it to be true (다더라) or recall hearing it (다던데). 다더라 emphasizes that you observed the result yourself, while 다던데 is softer and more retrospective — it sets up background information or seeks mild agreement.

rules.title: Action verbs with vowel or ㄹ ending → V-ㄴ다더라 / V-ㄴ다던데
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다더라 or ㄴ다던데 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 그 사람이 내일 온다더라 — They said that person is coming tomorrow (and I observed the situation).
비가 온다던데 우산을 챙기세요 — They said it's going to rain, so take an umbrella.

rules.title: Action verbs with consonant ending → V-는다더라 / V-는다던데
rules.description: Attach 는다더라 or 는다던데 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 수업이 곧 시작한다더라 — They said class is starting soon (and I confirmed it).

rules.title: Descriptive verbs → A-다더라 / A-다던데
rules.description: Attach 다더라 or 다던데 directly to the adjective stem.

examples.english: 그 집이 맛있다더라 — They said that place is delicious (and I found it to be true).
요즘 바쁘다던데 연락해 보세요 — They said they're busy these days, so try contacting them.

notes.title: Direct realization vs. soft recall
notes.text: 다더라 carries a sense of personal realization — you heard it and then saw it with your own eyes. 다던데 is softer, used to pass on what you heard as background.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also 301 (A/V-(으)ㄴ/는다더니) for contrasting results, and 315 (V-던데) for retrospective observation.

explanation: Use this when you want to express surprise, disbelief, or a strong reaction to something you heard. It combines quoted speech with 다니, which adds an emotional punch. It translates to "They say... (can you believe it?)" or "(I heard that)... (surprising!)."

rules.title: Action verbs with vowel or ㄹ ending → V-ㄴ다니
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다니 to the verb stem. Use this when reacting with surprise to news about an action.

examples.english: 그 사람이 한국에 간다니 믿을 수 없어요 — I can't believe they say that person is going to Korea.
회사를 그만둔다니 정말이에요? — They say (he's) quitting the company — is it true?

rules.title: Action verbs with consonant ending → V-는다니
rules.description: Attach 는다니 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 시험에 떨어진다니 너무 슬프다 — They say (he) failed the exam; how sad.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs → A-다니
rules.description: Attach 다니 directly to the adjective stem.

examples.english: 그녀가 예쁘다니 처음 들었어요 — I heard for the first time that they say she's pretty.
그 문제가 쉽다니 정말요? — They say that problem is easy — really?

notes.title: Emotionally charged
notes.text: 다니 is emotionally loaded — use it for surprise, joy, sadness, or disbelief. It is not neutral.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Often used with 믿을 수 없다, 놀랍다, 슬프다, or 처음 들었다 to reinforce the emotional reaction.

explanation: This means "even if they say..." or "no matter how much they claim...". It dismisses or downplays what someone says, emphasizing that the result or the speaker's stance won't change. Use it when you want to express skepticism or firmness despite someone's words.

rules.title: Action verbs with vowel or ㄹ ending → V-ㄴ다고 해도
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다고 해도 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 늦는다고 해도 기다릴 거예요 — Even if they say they'll be late, I'll wait.

rules.title: Action verbs with consonant ending → V-는다고 해도
rules.description: Attach 는다고 해도 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 그렇게 말한다고 해도 안 믿어요 — Even if they say that, I don't believe it.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs → A-다고 해도
rules.description: Attach 다고 해도 directly to the adjective stem.

examples.english: 시험이 어렵다고 해도 열심히 봐야 해요 — Even if they say the exam is hard, I have to try my best.
괜찮다고 해도 병원에 가 보세요 — Even if they say it's fine, go to the hospital.

notes.title: Unchanged stance
notes.text: This grammar emphasizes that the speaker's decision or the situation remains unchanged regardless of what is claimed.

notes.title: Firm tone
notes.text: It can sound firm or even stubborn depending on intonation and context. Use carefully in sensitive conversations.

explanation: This is a formal, written-style connective meaning "therefore," "because," or "as." Use it in official documents, essays, presentations, and formal speeches to show a logical cause-and-effect relationship between two clauses.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 므로
rules.description: Attach 므로 directly to the stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 시간이 없으므로 서둘러야 합니다 — There is no time, therefore we must hurry.
이 길이 길므로 차를 타고 가세요 — As this road is long, please go by car.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 으므로
rules.description: Attach 으므로 to stems ending in a consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 그 말이 맞으므로 따르겠습니다 — As those words are correct, I will comply.
늦으므로 미리 출발하겠습니다 — As we are late, we will depart early.

notes.title: Formal register only
notes.text: This is much more formal than -아/어서 or -(으)니까. Avoid it in casual conversation.

notes.title: Second clause type
notes.text: The second clause is usually a statement, plan, or formal request — not a raw command ending in -아/어.

explanation: Use this sentence ending when giving an explanation, reason, or background for what you just said or did. It translates to "you see" or "the thing is." It softens statements and invites the listener to understand your perspective. The 요 form (거든요) is common in polite conversation.

rules.title: V/A-거든(요)
rules.description: Attach 거든(요) directly to the verb or adjective stem. There is no 받침 distinction.

examples.english: 내일 가거든요 — You see, I'm going tomorrow.
피곤하거든요 — You see, I'm tired.
바쁘거든요 — You see, I'm busy.

notes.title: Proactive explanation
notes.text: 거든(요) cannot be used to answer a direct question. It proactively provides a reason before being asked.

notes.title: Conversational softness
notes.text: It is softer and more conversational than -아/어서 or -(으)니까 when ending a sentence.

explanation: This mid-sentence connective has two main uses. First, it sets up a condition: "if [something happens], then..." Second, it gives a reason or cause for what follows. In both cases, the second clause is typically a command, suggestion, or question. It is more common in written or slightly formal contexts than casual speech.

rules.title: V-거든 (conditional "if")
rules.description: Use 거든 in the first clause when the second clause is a command, suggestion, or question. It means "if."

examples.english: 한국에 가거든 부산도 들러 봐 — If you go to Korea, visit Busan too.
그 영화를 보거든 후기 좀 알려 줘 — If you watch that movie, let me know what you think.

rules.title: V-거든 (reason "because")
rules.description: Use 거든 to give a reason that leads to a command or suggestion in the second clause.

examples.english: 늦거든 미리 연락해 — Because we're late, contact them in advance.
바쁘거든 나중에 해 — Because it's busy, do it later.

notes.title: Second clause restriction
notes.text: When used as "if," the second clause is almost always an imperative, propositive, or question. A plain statement in the second clause is unnatural.

notes.title: Not a sentence ending
notes.text: 거든 without 요 is strictly a mid-sentence connective. Do not end a sentence with 거든 alone in standard Korean.

explanation: Use this casual connective when you did something because you observed or noticed a situation. It often implies a spontaneous reaction based on what you saw or felt. It can carry a slight sense of complaint or simple explanation. The subject of the main clause is usually the speaker.

rules.title: V/A-길래
rules.description: Attach 길래 directly to the verb or adjective stem. There is no 받침 distinction.

examples.english: 친구가 가길래 따라갔어요 — My friend was going, so I followed.
비가 오길래 우산을 가져갔어요 — It was raining, so I took an umbrella.
말이 없길래 무슨 일이 있는 줄 알았어요 — He was so quiet, I thought something was wrong.

notes.title: Very casual
notes.text: 길래 is extremely casual. Do not use it in formal writing or with superiors.

notes.title: Speaker's action
notes.text: The main clause usually describes the speaker's own action or judgment. Using it for someone else's action sounds odd.

explanation: This formal connective means "since" or "as," and it emphasizes that the result matches the degree of the cause. It combines reason (니) with extent (만큼). Use it when you want to say that because A is true, B should follow to the same degree.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 니만큼
rules.description: Attach 니만큼 directly to the stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 열심히 공부하니만큼 좋은 성적을 받을 거예요 — Since you study hard, you will get good grades.
오래 기다리니만큼 볞음이 있을 거예요 — Since you've waited so long, it will be rewarding.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 으니만큼
rules.description: Attach 으니만큼 to stems ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 잘 먹으니만큼 건강해요 — Since you eat well, you're healthy.
당신 말이 맞으니만큼 따르겠습니다 — As your words are correct, I will follow.

notes.title: Proportionality
notes.text: 니만큼 is more formal than 니까 and emphasizes that the result should match the effort or degree of the cause.

notes.title: Common contexts
notes.text: It is common in speeches, essays, and formal requests where cause and effect should be proportional.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before ㄴ.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄹ, drop ㄹ before ㄴ.

explanation: This literary and formal connective means "even if." It is used to concede a point while emphasizing that the result remains unchanged. It carries a slightly resigned or rhetorical tone, often used in proverbs, formal writing, or dramatic speech. It is more formal and less common in everyday conversation than -아/어도.

rules.title: Action verbs → V-는들
rules.description: Attach 는들 to the verb stem. There is no 받침 distinction for action verbs.

examples.english: 지금 가는들 늦지 않을까요? — Even if we go now, won't we be late?
열심히 하는들 결과가 똑같아요 — Even if I work hard, the result is the same.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs with consonant ending → A-(으)ㄴ들
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ들 to the adjective stem. Use -은들 after a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 돈이 많은들 다 행복합니까? — Even if one has a lot of money, is one happy?
날씨가 좋은들 갈 수가 없어요 — Even if the weather is good, I can't go.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs with vowel or ㄹ ending → A-ㄴ들
rules.description: Attach -ㄴ들 after a vowel or ㄹ-ending adjective stem.

examples.english: 집이 큰들 혼자서 다 쓸 수 있어요? — Even if the house is big, can you use it all alone?
길이 긴들 걸어갈 수 있어요 — Even if the road is long, we can walk.

notes.title: Rhetorical tone
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ/는들 is often used in rhetorical questions or philosophical statements. It sounds more dramatic than -아/어도.

notes.title: Unchanged outcome
notes.text: The second clause frequently contains a negative or unchanged outcome to contrast with the concession.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄹ, drop ㄹ before ㄴ.

explanation: Use this connective when you observed something in the past and want to describe what happened next — usually a change, contrast, or result. It connects a past observation (retrospective) to a present situation. The subject of both clauses is typically the same when showing personal change, or different when showing cause and effect.

rules.title: V-더니 (same subject — change over time)
rules.description: Attach 더니 to the verb or adjective stem. Use this when the same person or thing shows a change from past to present.

examples.english: 어제는 춥더니 오늘은 따뜻하네요 — Yesterday it was cold, but today it's warm.
그때는 그렇게 말하더니 이제 와서 후회해요 — Back then you said that, but now you regret it.

rules.title: V-더니 (different subjects — cause and result)
rules.description: Use this when someone else's observed action leads to a result or new situation for the speaker or another subject.

examples.english: 친구가 전화하더니 기분이 좋아졌어요 — My friend called, and I felt better.
버스가 늦더니 지각했어요 — The bus was late, so I was tardy.

notes.title: Factual second clause
notes.text: 더니 cannot be used when the second clause is a command, suggestion, or intention. It must be a factual statement.

notes.title: Retrospective observation
notes.text: The first clause must be something the speaker personally observed in the past. You cannot use it for things you only heard about.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also 315 (V-던데) for sharing background observations, and 316 (V-더라) for direct observation endings.

explanation: This means "even if" and is used for hypothetical or imagined situations. It is more hypothetical and less certain than -아/어도, which often describes actual attempts. Use it to talk about things that haven't happened yet or are unlikely, emphasizing that the result would still hold.

rules.title: V/A-더라도
rules.description: Attach 더라도 directly to the verb or adjective stem. There is no 받침 distinction.

examples.english: 비가 오더라도 가야 해요 — Even if it rains, I have to go.
힘이 들더라도 포기하지 마세요 — Even if it's hard, don't give up.
실패하더라도 다시 시도하세요 — Even if you fail, try again.

notes.title: Hypothetical nuance
notes.text: 더라도 is softer and more hypothetical than -아/어도. It imagines a scenario rather than testing it through actual experience.

notes.title: Determination and advice
notes.text: It often appears with expressions of determination, reassurance, or advice to show unwavering stance.

explanation: Use this when recalling something you observed in the past and using it as background for what you are saying now. It can appear mid-sentence (던데) to set up context, or at the end (던데요) to share an observation and invite agreement. It carries a warm, reflective tone.

rules.title: V-던데(요)
rules.description: Attach 던데(요) directly to the verb or adjective stem. There is no 받침 distinction.

examples.english: 그가 김치를 먹던데 맛있나 봐요 — He was eating kimchi; it must be delicious.
아기가 자던데 깨우지 마세요 — The baby was sleeping, so don't wake him.
그녀가 노래하던데 목소리가 참 좋아요 — She was singing, and her voice is really good.

notes.title: Retrospective observation
notes.text: 던데(요) is retrospective: you must have seen or experienced the action yourself. It cannot be used for secondhand information.

notes.title: Mid-sentence vs. ending
notes.text: Mid-sentence 던데 sets up background for your next point. Sentence-ending 던데요 softly shares an observation and invites agreement.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also 316 (V-더라) for direct observation endings, and 317 (V-던가) for uncertain recall.

explanation: Use these sentence endings when reporting something you directly observed or experienced. 더라 is a plain, matter-of-fact observation. 더군요 adds a sense of realization, mild surprise, or new understanding. Both are only used when the speaker personally witnessed the situation.

rules.title: V-더라
rules.description: Attach 더라 directly to the verb or adjective stem. Use this for neutral, factual observations.

examples.english: 그 사람이 학교에 가더라 — I noticed that person going to school.
사람이 참 많더라 — There were so many people (I saw).

rules.title: V-더군(요)
rules.description: Attach 더군(요) to the stem. Use this when the observation brings a sense of realization or mild surprise.

examples.english: 그 집이 참 예쁘더군요 — That house was really pretty, I noticed.
그가 많이 먹더군요 — He ate a lot (I observed).

notes.title: First-person only
notes.text: These endings are strictly first-person. You cannot use them to report what the listener observed.

notes.title: Storytelling and diaries
notes.text: They are common in storytelling, diary entries, and sharing personal experiences with friends.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also 315 (V-던데) for sharing background observations with context.

explanation: Use these when you are trying to recall a past observation but feel uncertain or vague about the details. 던가(요)? is question-like: "I wonder if..." or "Was it...?" 던데(요) is statement-like but soft and uncertain: "I seem to recall that..." Both express the speaker's fuzzy memory about something they think they saw or heard.

rules.title: V-던가(요)?
rules.description: Attach 던가(요)? to the stem. Use this when wondering aloud about a past observation.

examples.english: 그 사람이 거기 가던가요? — I wonder if that person was going there?
아기가 벌써 자던가? — I wonder if the baby was already asleep?

rules.title: V-던데(요)
rules.description: Attach 던데(요) to the stem. Use this when making a soft, uncertain statement of recollection.

examples.english: 그가 김치를 먹던데 — I seem to recall he was eating kimchi.
거기에 있던데 — I think it was there.

notes.title: Soft and tentative
notes.text: These forms are softer and more tentative than direct questions or statements. They are useful when you want to mention something without claiming certainty.

notes.title: Seeking confirmation
notes.text: Use 던가(요)? when you want the listener to confirm or correct your vague memory.

explanation: Use this when an action was repeated or continued over time, and as a natural result, something new was realized or happened. It translates to "after doing something repeatedly..." or "as I kept doing...". The (가) is optional — adding it slightly emphasizes the ongoing process.

rules.title: V-다(가) 보니(까)
rules.description: Attach 다(가) 보니(까) directly to the verb stem. There is no 받침 distinction.
