examples.english: 한국에 자주 가다 보니 한국어가 늘었어요 — I went to Korea often, and my Korean improved.
그 노래를 많이 듣다 보니 좋아지더라고요 — I listened to that song a lot, and I came to like it.
서울에서 살다가 보니 교통이 익숙해졌어요 — Living in Seoul, I got used to the traffic.

notes.title: Same subject required
notes.text: The subject of both clauses must be the same — it describes the speaker's own gradual realization or change.

notes.title: Unintentional result
notes.text: The result is usually something unintentional or naturally discovered, not a planned outcome.

notes.title: Optional 가
notes.text: -다 보니 and -다가 보니 are both correct. Adding 가 puts slightly more emphasis on the ongoing process.

explanation: Use -다(가) 보면 to say that if you keep doing an action, a certain result or realization will naturally follow. It means 'if you keep doing...' and is often used to give encouragement or describe an inevitable discovery that comes from repetition.

rules.title: V-다(가) 보면
rules.description: Attach -다(가) 보면 directly to the verb stem. The 다가 can be shortened to 다 in casual speech. This form emphasizes persistence leading to an outcome.

examples.english: 열심히 공부하다(가) 보면 반드시 좋은 결과가 있을 거예요 — If you keep studying hard, you will surely see good results.
이 음식을 먹다(가) 보면 익숙해질 거예요 — If you keep eating this food, you'll get used to it.
매일 연습하다(가) 보면 실력이 늘 거예요 — If you keep practicing every day, your skills will improve.

notes.title: Encouragement tone
notes.text: This grammar is often used to comfort or encourage someone that persistence leads to results.

notes.title: 다가 can be shortened
notes.text: Both 다(가) 보면 and 다가 보면 are acceptable; 다 보면 is more common in spoken Korean.

explanation: Use -다고 치다 to set a hypothetical premise. It translates to 'assuming that,' 'suppose that,' or 'let's say that...' and is used when you want to reason from a starting point that may or may not be true.

rules.title: V-(ㄴ/는)다고 치다 / A-다고 치다
rules.description: For action verbs, use the plain form: -ㄴ다고 치다 after vowels or ㄹ, and -는다고 치다 after other consonants. For descriptive verbs, attach -다고 치다 directly. 치다 itself conjugates normally.

examples.english: 내일 간다고 치고 계획을 세우자 — Let's assume you're going tomorrow and make a plan.
다 먹는다고 쳐도 남을 거예요 — Even assuming you eat it all, there will be leftovers.
좋다고 치자. 하지만 문제가 있어요 — Let's assume it's good. But there's a problem.

notes.title: Conjugation of 치다
notes.text: Common forms include 다고 쳐도 (even assuming), 다고 치자 (let's assume), and 다고 치고 (assuming).

notes.title: Hypothetical only
notes.text: This form explicitly marks the premise as hypothetical. Don't use it for real conditions.

explanation: Use -든지 to express free choice among alternatives or to say 'whatever' or 'no matter what.' It indicates that any option is acceptable or that the result is the same regardless of the choice.

rules.title: V-든지
rules.description: Attach -든지 directly to the verb stem. There is no 받침 distinction. Often used in the two-part structure 든지... 든지.

examples.english: 밥을 먹든지 국수를 먹든지 마음대로 해요 — Whether you eat rice or noodles, it's up to you.
집에 가든지 여기 있든지 네가 결정해 — Whether you go home or stay here, you decide.
어떻게 하든지 결과는 같을 거예요 — Whatever you do, the result will be the same.

notes.title: Two-part structure
notes.text: Often used as 든지... 든지 to present two alternatives. Single use also works: 뭐든지 (whatever).

notes.title: Common pairings
notes.text: Frequently followed by expressions like 좋다, 마음대로, 상관없다, or 괜찮다.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See 324 (A/V-(이)나 -(이)나 할 것 없이) for a more formal 'regardless of' meaning.

explanation: Use -듯이 to say that someone does something 'as if' or 'like' something else. It describes an action that resembles another action or state, often when the resemblance is intentional or noticeable.

rules.title: V-듯이
rules.description: Attach -듯이 directly to the verb stem. For past states or completed actions, use -(으)ㄴ 듯이.

examples.english: 아는 듯이 말했지만 사실 몰랐어요 — He spoke as if he knew, but actually didn't.
자는 듯이 눈을 감고 가만히 있었어요 — He sat still with his eyes closed as if sleeping.
운 듯이 눈이 빨갛게 되었어요 — Her eyes turned red as if she had cried.

notes.title: Past state
notes.text: Use -(으)ㄴ 듯이 for completed actions or states: 본 듯이, 들은 듯이.

notes.title: Not for guesses
notes.text: Don't confuse with -나 보다 or -는 모양이다, which are for speculation. 듯이 is about observable resemblance.

explanation: Use -(이)나 -(이)나 할 것 없이 to say 'regardless of' or 'whether... or not.' It emphasizes that something applies universally without exception, no matter what condition is named.

rules.title: A/V-(으)나... (으)나 할 것 없이
rules.description: Attach (으)나 to the verb or adjective stem: 나 after a vowel, 으나 after a consonant. Repeat with the same or a contrasting stem, then add 할 것 없이.

examples.english: 비싸나 싸나 할 것 없이 다 잘 팔려요 — Regardless of whether it's expensive or cheap, everything sells well.
오나 마나 할 것 없이 자리를 미리 예약해 둘게요 — Regardless of whether he comes or not, I'll reserve a seat in advance.
좋으나 나쁘나 할 것 없이 모두 받아들여야 해요 — Regardless of whether it's good or bad, you have to accept it all.

notes.title: Fixed pattern
notes.text: The structure requires two alternatives followed by 할 것 없이. Don't omit 할 것 없이.

notes.title: Formal and literary
notes.text: This form is more common in writing or formal speech. In casual conversation, consider -(이)나 마나 or -든지.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See 322 (V-든지) for a more casual 'whatever' meaning.

explanation: Use -고(요) to add information gently or to list items with a softening effect. It functions as 'and' but carries a milder, more polite tone than -고 alone. It is commonly used in explanations, presentations, and polite conversation.

rules.title: A/V-고(요)
rules.description: Attach -고(요) directly to the verb or adjective stem. The 요 can be dropped for casual speech.

examples.english: 저는 김치찌개를 먹었고요, 친구는 비빔밥을 먹었어요 — I had kimchi stew, and my friend had bibimbap.
이 가방은 예쁘고요, 가격도 괜찮아요 — This bag is pretty, and the price is good too.
서울에 살고요, 회사는 부산에 있어요 — I live in Seoul, and my company is in Busan.

notes.title: Softening effect
notes.text: The 요 adds politeness and creates a gentle pause. Common in customer service, presentations, and formal introductions.

notes.title: Can drop 요
notes.text: In casual speech among friends, just use -고.

notes.title: Not a question softener
notes.text: Unlike -ㄴ/는데요, -고(요) is for adding information, not softening questions.

explanation: Use -고 말다 to indicate that an action ended up happening, often unintentionally or inevitably. It can express completion, regret, or a result that was bound to occur despite efforts to prevent it.

rules.title: V-고 말다
rules.description: Attach -고 말다 to the verb stem. 말다 conjugates normally for tense: 고 말았다, 고 말아, etc.

examples.english: 중요한 약속을 까먹고 말았어요 — I ended up forgetting an important appointment.
핸드폰을 떨어뜨리고 말았어요 — I ended up dropping my phone.
결국 참지 못하고 울고 말았어요 — In the end I couldn't hold back and ended up crying.

notes.title: Regret or inevitability
notes.text: Often carries a sense that the result was unavoidable or unfortunate.

notes.title: Past tense is most common
notes.text: The form 고 말았다 is most common; present 고 말아요 is rare. This grammar is mostly used for past results.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See 329 (V-고 들다) for a more narrative or literary 'end up doing' form.

explanation: Use -고 들다 to say that someone 'ended up doing' something, often unexpectedly or against their initial intention. It has a more narrative or literary flavor than -고 말다 and is commonly found in storytelling.

rules.title: V-고 들다
rules.description: Attach -고 들다 directly to the verb stem. 들다 conjugates for tense. Common patterns include V-고 들었다 and V-고 들면.

examples.english: 화가 나서 결국 그 말을 하고 들었다 — Angered, he ended up saying it after all.
피곤해서 늦잠을 자고 들었다 — I was tired and ended up oversleeping.
참으려 했는데 화내고 들었다 — I tried to hold it in, but ended up getting angry.

notes.title: Narrative tone
notes.text: This form is more common in written narratives, novels, and dramatic storytelling than in daily conversation.

notes.title: Conditional use
notes.text: V-고 들면 means 'if one ends up V-ing,' which is a common pattern for warnings or hypothetical outcomes.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See 328 (V-고 말다) for the more common spoken equivalent.

explanation: Use -고 말고(요) to express emphatic agreement. It translates to 'of course,' 'definitely,' or 'you bet' and is used when you strongly agree with a statement or answer a question in the affirmative.

rules.title: A/V-고 말고(요)
rules.description: Attach -고 말고(요) directly to the verb or adjective stem. The 요 is optional and adds politeness.

examples.english: 내일 파티에 가고 말고요 — Of course I'll go to the party tomorrow.
이 선물 받고 싶어요? 좋고 말고요 — Do you want this gift? Of course I'd love it.
그 일 내가 하고 말고요 — Of course I'll do that job.

notes.title: Strong agreement
notes.text: This is very emphatic. Only use it when you want to show enthusiastic or definite agreement.

notes.title: Casual contexts
notes.text: In very formal settings, this can sound too casual or strong. Use 네, 그렇습니다 instead.

notes.title: Not for negative agreement
notes.text: To strongly disagree, use other forms. -고 말고 is strictly for positive agreement.

explanation: Use -기 십상이다 to say that something is very likely to happen or prone to occur. It translates to 'very likely to,' 'prone to,' or 'tends to' and often implies that the outcome is almost expected, especially when it is negative.

rules.title: A/V-기 십상이다
rules.description: Attach -기 십상이다 directly to the verb or adjective stem. There is no 받침 distinction.

examples.english: 초보자는 실수하기 십상이에요 — Beginners are very likely to make mistakes.
날씨가 추우면 감기에 걸리기 십상이에요 — When the weather is cold, you're very likely to catch a cold.
혼자 있으면 지루하기 십상이에요 — If you're alone, you're very likely to get bored.

notes.title: Negative tendency
notes.text: This grammar often describes undesirable outcomes. 십상 literally means 'ten times out of ten.'

notes.title: Not for positive predictions
notes.text: While grammatically possible with positive events, it sounds most natural with negative or challenging outcomes. Use -(으)ㄹ 거예요 for neutral positive predictions.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See 332 (A/V-기 마련이다) for 'bound to' in a more neutral, universal sense.

explanation: Use -기 마련이다 to say that something is bound to happen or is only natural. It translates to 'bound to,' 'is bound to,' or 'is only natural' and is used for universal truths, natural consequences, or things that happen as a matter of course.

rules.title: A/V-기 마련이다
rules.description: Attach -기 마련이다 directly to the verb or adjective stem. There is no 받침 distinction.

examples.english: 세상은 변하기 마련이에요 — The world is bound to change.
처음에는 힘들기 마련이에요 — It's bound to be hard at first.
바쁘면 가끔 잊기 마련이에요 — When you're busy, you're bound to forget sometimes.

notes.title: Universal or natural
notes.text: 마련 means 'as a rule' or 'naturally.' Use it for things that are expected by common sense.

notes.title: Not for single events
notes.text: Don't use this for a specific one-time prediction. It describes general patterns or inevitable tendencies.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See 331 (A/V-기 십상이다) for a similar meaning with a negative tendency.

explanation: Use -(으)ㄴ/는 걸(요) to explain, justify, or emphasize a reason in a soft, friendly way. It translates to 'you know...' or 'the thing is...' and is very common in spoken Korean when giving excuses or expressing mild emotion.

rules.title: V-(으)ㄴ/는 걸(요) / A-(으)ㄴ 걸(요)
rules.description: For present action verbs, use -는 걸(요). For past action verbs and descriptive verbs, use -(으)ㄴ 걸(요): -ㄴ 걸 after vowels, -은 걸 after consonants.

examples.english: 오늘은 늦은 걸요 — You know, I'm late today.
어제 잘 못 자서 피곤한 걸요 — You know, I'm tired because I didn't sleep well.
우산을 안 가져왔는데 비가 오는 걸요 — You know, I didn't bring an umbrella and it's raining.

notes.title: Very conversational
notes.text: This is almost exclusively spoken. It softens complaints, excuses, and explanations.

notes.title: Emotional nuance
notes.text: Can express mild disappointment, surprise, or realization depending on intonation.

notes.title: Not for formal writing
notes.text: Avoid this form in essays, reports, or formal presentations. Use -기 때문입니다 instead.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before vowel-initial endings.

explanation: Use -(는)군(요) or -(는)구나 to express a sudden realization or new discovery. It translates to 'Oh, I see,' 'So that's how it is,' or 'I didn't know that' and is used when you just learned something.

rules.title: V-는군(요) / A-군(요)
rules.description: For action verbs in the present, attach -는군(요). For descriptive verbs, attach -군(요) directly. For past action verbs, use -었군(요).

examples.english: 아이가 벌써 자는군요 — Oh, the child is already asleep.
열심히 공부하는군요 — Oh, you study hard.
여기가 참 좋군요 — Oh, this place is really nice.

notes.title: Politeness level
notes.text: 군요 is polite and can be used with 요. 구나 is casual and intimate.

notes.title: First-person realization
notes.text: When used about oneself, it can sound like you're reflecting on your own situation: 피곤하구나.

notes.title: Past tense for action verbs
notes.text: Action verbs in the past use -었군요, not *ㄴ군요. For example, 왔군요 (not 왔는군요).

explanation: Use -(으)ㄴ가(요)? to express curiosity, doubt, or wonder. It translates to 'I wonder...' or 'is it...?' and is used for self-reflection, rhetorical questions, or polite speculation. It carries a more thoughtful and literary tone than -(느)냐.

rules.title: V-는가(요)? / A-(으)ㄴ가(요)?
rules.description: For present action verbs, use -는가(요)?. For past action verbs, use -았/었는가(요)?. For descriptive verbs, use -(으)ㄴ가(요)?: -ㄴ가 after vowels, -은가 after consonants.

examples.english: 저 사람이 오는가요? — I wonder if that person is coming.
내 생각이 맞는가? — I wonder if my thinking is correct.
이 가방이 비싼가요? — I wonder if this bag is expensive.

notes.title: Reflective tone
notes.text: More literary and contemplative than 냐. Often used in inner monologue, essays, or poetry.

notes.title: With 요
notes.text: Adding 요 makes it polite but still thoughtful. Without 요, it can sound like you're talking to yourself.

notes.title: Not for direct questions
notes.text: In casual conversation, use -아/어요? -(으)ㄴ가요 is acceptable but slightly formal or reflective.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before vowel-initial endings.

explanation: Use -잖아(요) to remind the listener of something they should already know. It translates to 'you know...,' 'after all,' or 'as you know' and carries the nuance that the information is obvious or shared.

rules.title: A/V-잖아(요)
rules.description: Attach -잖아(요) directly to the verb or adjective stem. For past tense, attach to the past stem: -았/었잖아(요).

examples.english: 일이 많아서 힘들잖아요 — You know, work is hard because there's a lot of it.
우리 내일 만나기로 약속했잖아요 — You know, we promised to meet tomorrow.
제 말이 맞잖아요 — You know, I'm right.

notes.title: Common in speech
notes.text: This is one of the most frequently used endings in casual Korean conversation.

notes.title: Can sound defensive
notes.text: Depending on tone, it can come across as pushy or argumentative. Use 요 to soften.

notes.title: Past tense
notes.text: Simply add 잖아요 to the past form: 갔잖아요, 먹었잖아요, 좋았잖아요.

explanation: Use -(ㄴ/는)다 to make plain declarative statements. It is the 해라체 speech level used in newspapers, novels, diaries, impersonal writing, and when talking to oneself. For adjectives, simply use -다.

rules.title: V-(ㄴ/는)다
rules.description: For action verbs: use -ㄴ다 after a vowel or ㄹ-ending stem, and -는다 after other consonants. Adjectives do not take ㄴ/는 and simply use -다.

examples.english: 내일 학교에 간다 — I go to school tomorrow.
아침을 먹는다 — I eat breakfast.
숙제를 한다 — I do homework.

notes.title: Adjectives don't get ㄴ/는
notes.text: Adjectives and 이다 simply take -다: 크다, 작다, 이다. Only action verbs use ㄴ/는다.

notes.title: Self-talk
notes.text: Koreans often use this form when muttering to themselves or stating facts plainly.

notes.title: Not for polite conversation
notes.text: Using this with strangers or superiors can sound blunt or rude. Use -아/어요 instead.

exceptions.name: ㄹ ending
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㄹ, drop ㄹ and add -ㄴ다.

explanation: Use -아/어라 to give commands or orders in the 해라체 speech level. It is the casual imperative form used between close friends, by seniors to juniors, in written instructions, or in urgent situations.

rules.title: V-아/어라
rules.description: After ㅏ or ㅗ vowels, use -아라. After other vowels, use -어라. 하다 verbs become 해라.

examples.english: 빨리 가라 — Go quickly.
다 먹어라 — Eat it all.
열심히 공부해라 — Study hard.

notes.title: 해라체 command
notes.text: This is stronger and more direct than -(으)세요. Appropriate for clear instructions.

notes.title: Context matters
notes.text: Between strangers or in polite settings, this can sound aggressive. Use -(으)세요 for general politeness.

notes.title: Written usage
notes.text: Commonly found in signs, manuals, recipes, and test instructions: 여기에 이름을 써라.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: 으 drop
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅡ, drop ㅡ and add -어라.

explanation: V-아/어야지 expresses the speaker's self-reminder, determination, or intention to do something. It is like telling yourself 'I should...' or 'I'd better...' Use it when making a personal decision, motivating yourself, or acknowledging that something needs to be done. It often appears as a sentence ending.

rules.title: ㅏ/ㅗ ending → + 아야지
rules.description: For verb stems ending in ㅏ or ㅗ, attach 아야지.

examples.english: 이제 집에 가야지 — I should go home now.
생일 선물을 사야지 — I should buy a birthday gift.

rules.title: Other vowels → + 어야지
rules.description: For all other verb stems, attach 어야지.

examples.english: 점심을 먹어야지 — I should eat lunch.
내일 일찍 일어나야지 — I should wake up early tomorrow.

rules.title: 하다 → + 해야지
rules.description: 하다 verbs combine to form 해야지.

examples.english: 내일 시험이니까 공부해야지 — I have an exam tomorrow, so I should study.

notes.title: Self-directed only
notes.text: This ending is almost always used when talking to yourself. It sounds odd when directed at others.

notes.title: Related form
notes.text: See ID 341 (V-아/어야) for the same stem combined with a different ending that creates a necessary condition.

explanation: V-아/어야 and N(이)야 express a necessary condition — 'only if' or 'have to.' For verbs, it means an action must happen for something else to be possible. For nouns, it means something must be the case. It often pairs with 하다 or 돼요 to strengthen the sense of obligation.

rules.title: Verb → stem + 아/어야
rules.description: Attach 아/어야 to the verb stem using regular 아/어 conjugation.

examples.english: 열심히 공부해야 합격할 수 있어요 — You can pass only if you study hard.
약은 식후에 먹어야 돼요 — You have to take medicine after eating.

rules.title: Noun + (이)야
rules.description: Attach 이야 to nouns ending in a consonant; 야 to nouns ending in a vowel.

examples.english: 학생이야 할인을 받을 수 있어요 — Only if you're a student can you get a discount.
친구야 이해할 수 있어 — Only if you're a friend can you understand.

notes.title: Ambiguity with obligation
notes.text: 아/어야 can mean both 'only if' (conditional) and 'have to' (obligation). Context usually makes the meaning clear.

notes.title: Do not confuse
notes.text: 아/어야지 (ID 340) is self-motivation. 아/어야 is a general condition or requirement that can apply to anyone.

explanation: V-나(요)?, 니?, and 냐? are casual or plain-style question endings. They are used in informal settings, among close friends, or in written plain speech. 나(요) softens the question slightly; 니 and 냐 are blunt 반말.

rules.title: V-나(요)
rules.description: Attach 나 to the verb stem. Add 요 for slightly more politeness in otherwise casual speech.

examples.english: 언제 가나요? — When are you going?
뭐 하나요? — What are you doing?

rules.title: V-니
rules.description: Attach 니 to the verb stem. A common 반말 question ending.

examples.english: 뭐 먹니? — What are you eating?
언제 오니? — When are you coming?

rules.title: V-냐
rules.description: Attach 냐 to the verb stem. The most direct and blunt casual question form.

examples.english: 어떻게 하냐? — What are you going to do?
그걸 좋아하냐? — You like that?

notes.title: Politeness level
notes.text: Avoid 니 and 냐 with strangers, elders, or in formal situations. 나(요) is acceptable in some semi-formal writing.

notes.title: Intonation matters
notes.text: In spoken Korean, 니 and 냐 often sound more confrontational or surprised than curious. Use them carefully.

explanation: V-기에(는) is a formal or written-style connector meaning 'because' or 'since.' It gives a reason for the following clause. It is more formal than 아/어서 and tends to appear in essays, reports, news, or formal speech. The optional 는 adds emphasis.

rules.title: Verb stem + 기에(는)
rules.description: Attach 기에 directly to the verb stem. Add 는 for extra emphasis on the reason.

examples.english: 버스가 늦기에 택시를 탔어요 — The bus was late, so I took a taxi.
비가 오기에 우산을 가져갔어요 — It was raining, so I took an umbrella.
집이 멀기에 일찍 출발했어요 — Home is far, so I left early.

notes.title: Formal register
notes.text: 기에 is rarely used in casual conversation. In daily speech, replace it with 아/어서 or -(으)니까.

notes.title: Tense restriction
notes.text: 기에 is usually attached to the verb stem directly. Do not add past or future tense before 기에.

explanation: V-기는(요) is used to contradict, deny, or downplay a previous statement. It is similar to saying 'Not really' or 'On the contrary.' It is commonly used when someone compliments you and you want to be humble, or when correcting an assumption.

rules.title: Verb stem + 기는(요)
rules.description: Attach 기는 to the verb stem. Add 요 for a polite but modest tone. Without 요, it is blunt 반말.

examples.english: 한국어를 잘하기는요, 아직 어려워요 — Not really good at Korean, it's still hard.
그 사람을 알기는요, 한 번 본 적만 있어요 — I don't really know him, I've only seen him once.
예쁘기는요, 그냥 그래요 — Not really pretty, it's just okay.

notes.title: Humble response pattern
notes.text: This is the go-to pattern for politely rejecting compliments in Korean. It is considered graceful, not rude.

notes.title: Tone matters
notes.text: Without 요, 기는 can sound dismissive. In 반말, use it only with close friends.

explanation: V-아/어 가다 and V-아/어 오다 are directional auxiliary patterns. They describe doing an action while moving in a specific direction. 가다 indicates motion away from the speaker; 오다 indicates motion toward the speaker. They create vivid, dynamic descriptions of movement.

rules.title: V-아/어 가다 (motion away)
rules.description: Do an action while going away from the speaker or current location.

examples.english: 학교까지 걸어 갔어요 — I walked to school.
버스를 향해 달려 갔어요 — Ran toward the bus.

rules.title: V-아/어 오다 (motion toward)
rules.description: Do an action while coming toward the speaker or current location.

examples.english: 친구가 뛰어 왔어요 — My friend came running.
커피를 들어 오세요 — Please bring in the coffee.

notes.title: Spatial awareness
notes.text: The direction is always relative to the speaker at the time of speaking. Use 가다 even when the listener is going with you, as long as it is away from where you are now.

notes.title: Related pattern
notes.text: See ID 347 for movement outward (나가다/내다), which adds the nuance of exiting or extracting.

explanation: V-아/어 나가다 and V-아/어 내다 describe doing an action while moving outward or extracting something. 나가다 focuses on physically exiting a space while performing the action. 내다 can mean bringing something out or completing an action thoroughly from beginning to end.

rules.title: V-아/어 나가다 (exit while doing)
rules.description: Perform an action while moving out of a place.

examples.english: 소방관이 뛰어 나갔어요 — The firefighter ran out.
상자를 들어 나갔어요 — Carried the box and went out.

rules.title: V-아/어 내다 (bring out / do completely)
rules.description: Do an action and bring the result out, or carry an action through to completion.

examples.english: 물을 한 번에 마셔 냈어요 — Drank all the water in one go.
수건을 꽉 짜 냈어요 — Squeezed the towel out tightly.

notes.title: Subtle difference
notes.text: 나가다 is always about physical exit. 내다 as an auxiliary can also mean completion, not just outward movement.

notes.title: Related pattern
notes.text: See ID 346 for basic directional auxiliaries (가다/오다).

explanation: V-아/어 두다 and V-아/어 놓다 mean to do something in advance and leave it in that state for later use. They express preparation, reservation, or keeping something ready. The two are largely interchangeable as auxiliaries, though 두다 feels slightly more planned.

rules.title: V-아/어 두다
rules.description: Do an action and keep the result for future use.

examples.english: 미리 사 두세요 — Buy it in advance.
준비해 둘게요 — I'll prepare it in advance.

rules.title: V-아/어 놓다
rules.description: Do an action and leave the result in place.

examples.english: 메모를 써 놓았어요 — I wrote a memo and left it there.
창문을 열어 놓았어요 — I left the window open.

notes.title: Interchangeable auxiliaries
notes.text: In modern Korean, 아/어 두다 and 아/어 놓다 are nearly identical when used as auxiliaries. 두다 is slightly more common in formal contexts.

notes.title: Related pattern
notes.text: See ID 350 (V-아/어 있다) for a state that exists without the planning nuance.

explanation: V-아/어 있다 describes a state that remains as a result of a completed action. It is different from V-고 있다, which describes an ongoing action. Use 아/어 있다 when something was done and the resulting condition still exists.

rules.title: Intransitive verbs + 아/어 있다
rules.description: Used with intransitive verbs to show a maintained physical state.

examples.english: 친구가 벤치에 앉아 있어요 — My friend is sitting on the bench.
버스 정류장에 사람들이 서 있어요 — People are standing at the bus stop.
고양이가 소파에 누워 있어요 — The cat is lying on the sofa.

rules.title: Passive / result state
rules.description: Used with passive or change-of-state verbs to show a lasting result.

examples.english: 음식이 조금 남아 있어요 — Some food remains.
벌써 깨어 있었어요 — Was already awake.

notes.title: Not the same as -고 있다
notes.text: 앉고 있다 means 'in the process of sitting down.' 앉아 있다 means 'is seated.' The first is an action; the second is a state.

notes.title: Related pattern
notes.text: See ID 351 (V-아/어 지다) for the change that leads to a state, and ID 349 (V-아/어 두다) for intentionally maintaining a state.

exceptions.name: 눕다
exceptions.description: ㅂ irregular: ㅂ drops and 와 is added.

explanation: V-아/어 지다 has two major functions. First, it creates a passive verb from a transitive verb. Second, it expresses a change of state, like 'become' or 'get.' It turns adjectives or descriptive ideas into dynamic processes.

rules.title: Change of state (become)
rules.description: Attach 아/어 지다 to adjectives or intransitive descriptions to show becoming.

examples.english: 한국어가 점점 쉬워지고 있어요 — Korean is gradually becoming easier.
친구와 멀어졌어요 — I grew apart from my friend.

rules.title: Passive voice
rules.description: Attach 아/어 지다 to transitive verbs to form a passive.

examples.english: 이 컵은 한국에서 만들어졌어요 — This cup was made in Korea.

notes.title: Many lexicalized passives
notes.text: Some verbs already have lexicalized passive forms (e.g., 먹다 → 먹히다, 팔다 → 팔리다). When those exist, they are preferred over 아/어 지다.

notes.title: Related pattern
notes.text: See ID 350 (V-아/어 있다) for the resultant state after a change has occurred.

exceptions.name: 쉽다
exceptions.description: ㅂ irregular: ㅂ drops and 와 is added.

explanation: V-아/어 대다 means to keep doing something repeatedly or continuously. It often carries a nuance of excess, annoyance, or doing something without restraint. It is commonly used to describe sounds, speech, or emotional outbursts that go on and on.

rules.title: Verb stem + 아/어 대다
rules.description: Attach 아/어 대다 to the verb stem. The action is repeated or prolonged.

examples.english: 강아지가 밤새 울어댔어요 — The puppy kept crying all night.
수업 시간에 떠들어대지 마세요 — Don't keep chattering during class.
혼자서 웃어대고 있었어요 — Was just laughing to himself.

notes.title: Often negative
notes.text: While neutral in some contexts, 아/어 대다 frequently implies that the repeated action is excessive or bothersome.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: 울어대다, 떠들어대다, 소리쳐대다, 먹어대다 are all very common collocations.

explanation: V-아/어 먹다 is a highly colloquial auxiliary that means to do something thoroughly, completely, or to a strong degree. It is rough, casual, and sometimes slangy. Despite using 먹다, it has nothing to do with eating. It is mostly used among friends or in informal media.

rules.title: Verb stem + 아/어 먹다
rules.description: Attach 아/어 먹다 to the verb stem. The action is done completely or intensely.

examples.english: 시험을 망쳐 먹었어 — I completely messed up the exam.
약속을 잊어 먹었어 — I totally forgot the promise.
열쇠를 까먹었어 — I completely forgot the keys.

notes.title: Register warning
notes.text: This form is very casual and can sound crude. Never use it in formal writing or with elders.

notes.title: Semantic intensifier
notes.text: 아/어 먹다 does not change the core meaning. It simply intensifies it, like adding 'the hell out of' in English.

explanation: V-아/어 죽다 is a hyperbolic expression meaning you are overwhelmed by something to the point of feeling like you might die. It is almost never literal. It is commonly used to describe extreme hunger, laughter, heat, cold, or fatigue.

rules.title: Verb stem + 아/어 죽겠다
rules.description: Attach 아/어 죽겠다 to the verb stem. 죽다 is almost always conjugated with the future intent 겠다 in this pattern.

examples.english: 배고파 죽겠어 — I'm starving.
이 영화 재미있어 죽겠다 — This movie is hilariously funny.
여름에 더워 죽겠어 — It's unbearably hot in summer.

notes.title: Hyperbole is key
notes.text: This is pure exaggeration. No one actually thinks they will die. It is a friendly, vivid way to express intensity.

notes.title: Related pattern
notes.text: See ID 353 (V-아/어 먹다) for another casual auxiliary that intensifies meaning.

exceptions.name: 배고프다
exceptions.description: ㅡ drop: ㅡ drops and 아 is added.

exceptions.name: 덥다
exceptions.description: ㅂ irregular: ㅂ drops and 와 is added.

explanation: V-아/어 달라(고) 하다 is the standard pattern for asking someone to do something for you. It is less direct than 아/어 주세요 and functions as an indirect request. When shortened in casual speech, it becomes V-아/어 달라. It is an essential pattern for making polite or reported requests.

rules.title: V-아/어 달라(고) 하다
rules.description: Attach 아/어 달라고 하다 to the verb stem. Use this to report a request or make an indirect appeal.

examples.english: 동생에게 숙제를 도와 달라고 했어요 — I asked my younger sibling to help with homework.
책을 읽어 달라고 했어요 — I asked them to read the book.
한국어를 가르쳐 달라고 했어요 — I asked them to teach me Korean.

notes.title: Reported speech
notes.text: 달라고 하다 is the most natural way to report a request. '저는 선생님께 설명해 달라고 했어요' means 'I asked the teacher to explain.'

notes.title: Not an auxiliary verb
notes.text: 달라 comes from 주다 (to give) in the request form. Unlike 가다 or 오다, it is not a standalone auxiliary verb, but the pattern is fixed and productive.

exceptions.name: 돕다
exceptions.description: ㅂ irregular: ㅂ drops and 와 is added.

explanation: V-아/여 보이다 means 'to look' or 'to seem' from the observer's perspective. It describes how something appears to others. It is formed by adding 보이다 to an adjective or descriptive verb stem. This is different from the passive 보이다 (to be seen).

rules.title: Adjective stem + 아/어 보이다
rules.description: Attach 아/어 보이다 to descriptive verbs. 하다 becomes 해 보이다.

examples.english: 그 옷이 정말 예뻐 보여요 — That dress looks really pretty.
이 문제는 어려워 보여요 — This problem looks difficult.
어제 잠을 못 잤나 봐요, 피곤해 보여요 — You look tired, maybe you couldn't sleep yesterday.

notes.title: Observer's perspective
notes.text: 아/어 보이다 is always about appearance to an outside observer. You cannot use it to describe your own internal state as you experience it.

notes.title: Not the passive 보이다
notes.text: 산 보이다 means 'the mountain is visible.' 산이 멋있어 보이다 means 'the mountain looks cool.' These are different grammars.

exceptions.name: 어렵다
exceptions.description: ㅂ irregular: ㅂ drops and 와 is added.

explanation: V-아/어 가지고 is a casual connector with two main uses. First, it shows sequence: doing one thing and then another. Second, it shows cause and effect, like 'so' or 'because.' It is very common in spoken Korean but too casual for formal writing.

rules.title: Sequence (do A, then B)
rules.description: Attach 아/어 가지고 to the first verb to show that its action precedes the next.

examples.english: 책을 사 가지고 왔어요 — I bought a book and brought it.
옷을 입어 가지고 나갔어요 — Got dressed and went out.

rules.title: Cause and effect
rules.description: Attach 아/어 가지고 to the cause clause. The result is usually negative or consequential.

examples.english: 아침을 안 먹어 가지고 배가 고파요 — I didn't eat breakfast, so I'm hungry.
밤새 공부해 가지고 잠이 와요 — I studied all night, so I'm sleepy.

notes.title: Too casual for formal use
notes.text: In essays, reports, or polite speeches, replace 가지고 with 아/어서 or -(으)니까.

notes.title: Often shortened to 갖고
notes.text: In rapid speech, 가지고 is frequently pronounced and even written as 갖고.

explanation: N-에 따라(서) means 'according to,' 'depending on,' or 'in accordance with.' It shows that a result, opinion, or situation changes based on the noun. It is extremely common in both speech and writing.

rules.title: Noun + 에 따라(서)
rules.description: Attach 에 따라(서) directly to the noun. The 서 is optional and does not change the meaning.

examples.english: 날씨에 따라서 계획이 달라요 — Plans change depending on the weather.
취향은 사람에 따라서 달라요 — Tastes vary from person to person.
상황에 따라서 대처 방법이 달라요 — The way to handle it differs depending on the situation.

notes.title: Common set phrases
notes.text: 사람에 따라서, 상황에 따라서, 시간에 따라서, 장소에 따라서 are among the most frequent collocations.

notes.title: Formality tip
notes.text: 에 따라(서) is completely neutral. It is safe to use in any register, from casual chats to academic papers.

explanation: N-을/를 비롯해(서) means 'including' or 'starting with.' It is used to introduce a group by naming its most prominent member first, then implying that others follow. Use it when you want to highlight one person, place, or thing as the first in a series.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 비롯해(서)
rules.description: Attach 을 비롯해(서) to nouns ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 김 교수를 비롯해 많은 연구원들이 참석했어요 — Professor Kim and many researchers attended.
서울을 비롯해서 전국에 비가 왔어요 — It rained across the whole country, starting with Seoul.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 를 비롯해(서)
rules.description: Attach 를 비롯해(서) to nouns ending in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 이 교수를 비롯해 다른 교수님들도 동의하셨어요 — This professor and other professors also agreed.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: ~을/를 비롯한 + noun is a common modifier form. Also frequently paired with 많은, 여러, or 다양한.

notes.title: 서 is optional
notes.text: 비롯해 and 비롯해서 have the same meaning. The longer form feels slightly more connected in a sentence.

explanation: N-을/를 통해(서) means 'through' or 'by means of.' It is used to indicate a medium, channel, experience, or person that enables an action or result. Use it when describing how information, influence, or change passes from one point to another.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 을 통해(서)
rules.description: Attach 을 통해(서) to nouns ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 이번 프로젝트를 통해 많은 것을 배웠어요 — I learned a lot through this project.
선생님을 통해 그 소식을 들었어요 — I heard that news through the teacher.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 를 통해(서)
rules.description: Attach 를 통해(서) to nouns ending in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 인터넷을 통해서 세계 소식을 빨리 알 수 있어요 — We can quickly learn world news through the internet.

notes.title: Multiple meanings of 'through'
notes.text: Can describe physical channels (door), media (TV, internet), experiences (경험을 통해), or people (친구를 통해).

notes.title: Not for gratitude
notes.text: Do not confuse with N- 덕분에, which expresses gratitude. 통해(서) is neutral and simply describes a pathway.

explanation: 아무 -(이)나 means 'any' or 'no matter which.' It is used when all options are acceptable and the choice does not matter. Use it with a positive verb to express indifference or universal applicability.

rules.title: Consonant ending → 아무 N + 이나
rules.description: When the noun ends in a consonant, attach 이나 to 아무 + noun.

examples.english: 아무 책이나 골라도 좋아요 — Any book you pick is fine.
아무 문제나 풀 수 있어요 — I can solve any problem.

rules.title: Vowel ending → 아무 N + 나
rules.description: When the noun ends in a vowel, attach 나 directly to 아무 + noun.

examples.english: 아무 때나 연락해도 돼요 — You can contact me anytime.

notes.title: Must use a positive verb
notes.text: 아무(이)나 requires a positive verb. For negative sentences, use 아무 -도 (ID 368).

notes.title: Common contractions
notes.text: 아무거나 (anything), 아묘나 (anyone), 아무 데나 (anywhere) are extremely common contracted forms.

explanation: 아무 -도 means 'any... at all' and is always paired with a negative verb. It is used to strongly negate a whole category, meaning 'not any X' or 'no X whatsoever.' Use it when you want to emphasize complete absence or denial.

rules.title: 아무 + Noun + 도
rules.description: Attach 도 directly to 아무 + noun. There is no 이/가 alternation; 도 replaces the subject or object particle.

examples.english: 아무 일도 없었어요 — Nothing happened at all.
아무 말도 하지 마세요 — Don't say anything at all.
아무것도 못 먹었어요 — I couldn't eat anything at all.

notes.title: Must be negative
notes.text: 아무 -도 MUST be followed by a negative expression: 안, 못, -지 않다, -지 못하다, 없다, 모른다, etc.

notes.title: Direct pair with ID 367
notes.text: See ID 367 (아무 -(이)나) for the positive 'any' counterpart. They form a natural positive-negative pair.

explanation: V-는 수(가) 있다 means 'there is a way to' or 'it is possible to.' It expresses that a method or possibility exists for doing something. Use it when talking about feasibility, options, or solutions.

rules.title: All verbs → V stem + 는 수(가) 있다 / 없다
rules.description: Attach 는 수(가) 있다 or 없다 to any verb stem. The 가 is optional.

examples.english: 문제를 해결하는 수가 있어요 — There is a way to solve the problem.
그런 일은 피할 수가 없어요 — There's no way to avoid such a thing.
이렇게 하는 수밖에 없어요 — There's no choice but to do it this way.

notes.title: Common fixed expression
notes.text: -는 수가 없다 is a very common way to say 'impossible' or 'no way.' 수밖에 없다 means 'have no choice but to.'

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See ID 369 for 'a way exists.' For general ability, -(으)ㄹ 수 있다 is more common in daily speech.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㄹ, drop ㄹ before ㄴ.
exceptions.example: 만들다 → 만드는 수가 있다, 살다 → 사는 수가 있다, 알다 → 아는 수가 있다

explanation: V-는 한 means 'as long as' or 'provided that.' It sets a condition that guarantees a certain result. Use it when the result clause is absolute, certain, or holds true under the stated condition.

rules.title: All verbs → V stem + 는 한
rules.description: Attach 는 한 to the verb stem. The second clause often contains an absolute expression.

examples.english: 약속을 지키는 한 문제없어요 — As long as you keep your promise, there's no problem.
내가 아는 한 그 사람은 착해요 — As far as I know, that person is kind.
여기서 일하는 한 규칙을 지켜야 해요 — As long as you work here, you must follow the rules.

notes.title: Absolute result clause
notes.text: The second clause often uses strong or absolute expressions like 괜찮다, 문제없다, 상관없다, 안 되다, or -아/어도 된다.

notes.title: As far as I know
notes.text: With 알다 or 기억하다, 는 한 means 'as far as I know/remember,' a slightly different but very common usage.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㄹ, drop ㄹ before ㄴ.
exceptions.example: 알다 → 아는 한, 살다 → 사는 한

explanation: V-는 한편 means 'on one hand' or 'while also.' It is used to describe two actions, states, or roles that coexist, often contrasting or complementing each other. Use it when someone does two things simultaneously or when you want to present two sides of a situation.

rules.title: All verbs → V stem + 는 한편
rules.description: Attach 는 한편 to the verb stem. The clause can describe simultaneous actions or contrasting qualities.

examples.english: 그는 회사를 다니는 한편 학교에서도 공부해요 — He works at a company while also studying at school.
한국어를 배우는 한편 한국 문화도 관심 있어요 — While learning Korean, I'm also interested in Korean culture.
그는 울고 싶은 한편 웃고 싶기도 했어요 — On one hand he wanted to cry, and on the other he wanted to laugh.

notes.title: Also used with adjectives
notes.text: Adjectives use A-ㄴ/은 한편: 비싼 한편 품질이 좋아요 — It's expensive, but on the other hand, the quality is good.

notes.title: More formal than -(으)면서
notes.text: In casual speech, -(으)면서 is more common for simultaneous actions. 한편 feels more written or formal.

explanation: V-는 대신(에) means 'instead of' or 'in exchange for.' It is used when one action replaces another or when compensation is given for something. Use it to describe substitution or trade-offs between two actions.

rules.title: All verbs → V stem + 는 대신(에)
rules.description: Attach 는 대신(에) to the verb stem. 에 is optional.

examples.english: 택시를 타는 대신에 버스를 탔어요 — I took the bus instead of a taxi.
주말에 쉬는 대신에 평일에 쉴 거예요 — I'll rest on weekdays instead of the weekend.
돈을 많이 내는 대신에 좋은 자리를 받았어요 — I paid a lot of money in exchange for a good seat.

notes.title: Two meanings
notes.text: Simple substitution ('instead of doing X, do Y') and compensation ('in exchange for X, Y'). Context makes the meaning clear.

notes.title: Same or different subjects
notes.text: The two clauses can share the same subject or have different subjects. Both are grammatically natural.

explanation: V-는 데 (좋다/나쁘다) means 'good/bad for doing X.' It is used to evaluate a place, tool, method, or situation in terms of how well it serves a particular purpose. Use it with evaluative adjectives like 좋다, 나쁘다, 편하다, or 불편하다.

rules.title: All verbs → V stem + 는 데
rules.description: Attach 는 데 to the verb stem, then follow with an evaluative adjective.

examples.english: 이 호텔은 쉬는 데 좋아요 — This hotel is good for resting.
이 책은 한국어를 배우는 데 도움이 돼요 — This book is helpful for learning Korean.
이 프로그램은 일하는 데 편해요 — This program is convenient for working.

notes.title: Common adjective pairings
notes.text: Frequently used with 좋다/나쁘다, 편하다/불편하다, 적합하다/부적합하다, and 도움이 되다.

notes.title: Evaluation grammar
notes.text: Similar to -기에는 but more commonly used for general suitability of places, tools, or environments.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㄹ, drop ㄹ before ㄴ.
exceptions.example: 살다 → 사는 데, 알다 → 아는 데, 만들다 → 만드는 데

explanation: V-는 셈치다 means 'consider it as' or 'think of it as.' It is used to reframe a situation so that someone feels less pressure or sees things from a different perspective. Use it to encourage, comfort, or give permission to treat something casually.

rules.title: All verbs → V stem + 는 셈치다
rules.description: Attach 는 셈치다 to the verb stem. Common forms include 는 셈치고 and the imperative 는 셈쳐라.

examples.english: 배우는 셈치고 한번 해 보세요 — Think of it as learning and give it a try.
공짜로 먹는 셈치고 마음 편하게 드세요 — Consider it a free meal and eat comfortably.
실수하는 셈치고 다시 시작합시다 — Let's consider it a mistake and start over.

notes.title: Most common form
notes.text: 는 셈치고 is far more common in everyday speech than 는 셈치다. Use it to put someone at ease.

notes.title: Imperative is rare
notes.text: The imperative form 셈쳐라 is very rare in modern casual speech. Stick to 는 셈치고 or 는 셈치고요.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㄹ, drop ㄹ before ㄴ.
exceptions.example: 알다 → 아는 셈치다, 살다 → 사는 셈치다

explanation: V-는 편이다 and A-은 편이다 mean 'tend to' or 'relatively.' They are used to soften a statement, making it less absolute. Use them when you want to express a general tendency without sounding too forceful.

rules.title: Action verb → V stem + 는 편이다
rules.description: Attach 는 편이다 to action verb stems.

examples.english: 저는 매운 음식을 잘 먹는 편이에요 — I tend to eat spicy food well.
그 사람은 한국 역사를 잘 아는 편이에요 — He tends to know Korean history well.

rules.title: Descriptive verb → A stem + 은 편이다
rules.description: Attach 은 편이다 to descriptive verb (adjective) stems.

examples.english: 이 식당은 가격이 싼 편이에요 — This restaurant is relatively cheap.
한국어는 발음이 어려운 편이에요 — Korean tends to have difficult pronunciation.
여기서 학교까지 긴 편이에요 — It's relatively far from here to school.

notes.title: Adverb pairings
notes.text: Frequently used with 꽤, 상당히, 그렇게, or 별로 to strengthen or weaken the statement.

notes.title: Not for one-time events
notes.text: Cannot be used with one-time event verbs like 죽다, 태어나다, or 결혼하다. It describes general tendencies only.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㄹ, drop ㄹ before ㄴ.
exceptions.example: 알다 → 아는 편이다, 살다 → 사는 편이다, 길다 → 긴 편이다, 멀다 → 먼 편이다

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: When the adjective stem ends in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before vowel-initial endings.
exceptions.example: 춥다 → 추운 편이다, 덥다 → 더운 편이다, 어렵다 → 어려운 편이다

exceptions.name: 르 irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in 르, change 르 to ㄹㄹ.
exceptions.example: 고르다 → 고른 편이다, 빠르다 → 빠른 편이다

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before vowel-initial endings.

exceptions.name: 으 drop
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅡ, drop ㅡ and add the appropriate vowel.
exceptions.example: 예쁘다 → 예쁜 편이다, 나쁘다 → 나쁜 편이다

explanation: A/V-았/었/였던 것 같다 means 'I think it was' or 'it seems like it was.' It is used when reflecting on past events, states, or experiences based on memory or indirect evidence. Use it to express a tentative recollection or guess about the past.

rules.title: Past form + 던 것 같다
rules.description: Form the past tense of the verb or adjective, then attach 던 것 같다. There is no 받침 distinction at the 던 attachment point because the past form already ends in 았/었/였.

examples.english: 어제 비가 왔던 것 같아요 — I think it rained yesterday.
그 사람은 학생이었던 것 같아요 — I think that person was a student.
옛날에 여기에 공원이 있었던 것 같아요 — I think there used to be a park here.

notes.title: Memory-based reflection
notes.text: This form is based on memory or reflection, not current observation. Use it when you are not entirely sure about a past fact.

notes.title: Different from -나 보다
notes.text: -았/었나 보다 is based on current evidence and observation. -았/었던 것 같다 is based on memory or indirect recollection.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before vowel-initial endings in the past form.
exceptions.example: 걷다 → 걸었던 것 같다, 듣다 → 들었던 것 같다

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before vowel-initial endings in the past form.
exceptions.example: 춥다 → 추웠던 것 같다, 덥다 → 더웠던 것 같다

exceptions.name: 르 irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in 르, add an extra ㄹ and conjugate with 라/러.
exceptions.example: 고르다 → 골랐던 것 같다, 빠르다 → 빨랐던 것 같다

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before vowel-initial endings in the past form.

exceptions.name: 으 drop
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅡ, drop ㅡ and add the appropriate past tense vowel.
exceptions.example: 예쁘다 → 예뻤던 것 같다, 나쁘다 → 나빴던 것 같다

explanation: V-았/었으면 하다 means 'I wish that' or 'I hope that.' It is used to express a desire about someone else's action or about a situation the speaker cannot directly control. Use it when making a polite wish or expressing hope for a future outcome.

rules.title: All verbs → Past form + 으면 하다
rules.description: Form the past tense of the verb, then attach 으면 하다. Despite the past-tense shape, the meaning is about a present or future wish.

examples.english: 빨리 나았으면 해요 — I hope you get well soon.
내일 비가 안 왔으면 해요 — I hope it doesn't rain tomorrow.
모두가 행복했으면 해요 — I hope everyone is happy.

notes.title: Wish about others or situations
notes.text: This form is almost always used for wishes about other people or external situations. For your own intentions, use -(으)ㄹ게요 or -(으)려고 해요.

notes.title: Common endings
notes.text: -았/었으면 해요 and -았/었으면 좋겠어요 are the most natural everyday forms. They are gentle and indirect.

exceptions.name: ㄷ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㄷ, change ㄷ to ㄹ before vowel-initial endings in the past form.
exceptions.example: 걷다 → 걸었으면 하다, 듣다 → 들었으면 하다

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before vowel-initial endings in the past form.
exceptions.example: 춥다 → 추웠으면 하다, 덥다 → 더웠으면 하다

exceptions.name: 르 irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in 르, add an extra ㄹ and conjugate with 라/러.
exceptions.example: 고르다 → 골랐으면 하다, 빠르다 → 빨랐으면 하다

exceptions.name: ㅅ irregular
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅅ, drop ㅅ before vowel-initial endings in the past form.

exceptions.name: 으 drop
exceptions.description: When the stem ends in ㅡ, drop ㅡ and add the appropriate past tense vowel.
exceptions.example: 예쁘다 → 예뻤으면 하다, 나쁘다 → 나빴으면 하다

explanation: A formal written expression that minimizes or belittles the significance of a noun, translating to 'nothing more than' or 'merely.' It is frequently used in academic essays, editorials, and critical analysis to downplay the importance of a subject.

rules.title: Attaching to Nouns
rules.description: Attach -에 지나지 않다 directly to a noun to indicate that something is nothing more than what the noun describes. The verb 지나다 is conjugated in the negative.

examples.breakdown: 이것은 (this) 하나의 예 (one example) 에 지나지 않는다 (is nothing more than)
examples.english: This is nothing more than one example.

examples.korean: 그의 성공은 단지 운에 지나지 않는다.
examples.breakdown: 그의 성공은 (his success) 단지 (merely) 운 (luck) 에 지나지 않는다 (is nothing more than)
examples.english: His success is merely nothing more than luck.

rules.title: Nuanced Negation in Writing
rules.description: Unlike blunt negations, this structure softens the dismissal while maintaining a formal, analytical tone. It often appears alongside 싶다 or 생각하다 in more subjective statements.

examples.korean: 이번 결과가 우연이 아니라 필연에 지나지 않는다.
examples.breakdown: 이번 결과가 (this result) 우연이 아니라 (not coincidence) 필연 (inevitability) 에 지나지 않는다 (is nothing more than)
examples.english: This result is nothing more than inevitability, not coincidence.

notes.title: Register
notes.text: This grammar is almost exclusively used in formal written Korean. It is rare in casual conversation.

notes.title: Similar Expression
notes.text: -에 불과하다 carries a nearly identical meaning but is even more common in news articles.

notes.title: Noun Restriction
notes.text: This ending attaches only to nouns. For verbs or adjectives, nominalize them first using -는 것 or similar forms.

explanation: A formal, cautious negation used in written and academic contexts to state that one cannot reasonably claim something to be true. It is softer and more analytical than direct negation.

rules.title: Basic Structure
rules.description: Attach -다고 할 수 없다 to the stem of an adjective or verb. The structure literally means 'one cannot say that...' and is used to refute or cast doubt on a proposition.

examples.breakdown: 그것이 (that) 옳다고 (to be right) 할 수 없다 (cannot say)
examples.english: One cannot say that is right.

examples.korean: 이 문제가 간단하다고 할 수 없다.
examples.breakdown: 이 문제가 (this problem) 간단하다고 (to be simple) 할 수 없다 (cannot say)
examples.english: This problem cannot be said to be simple.

rules.title: Strengthened Negation
rules.description: Adverbs like 완전히 (completely), 절대 (absolutely), or 쉽게 (easily) can precede the clause to add intensity while retaining the formal tone.

examples.korean: 이理論이 완전히 틀렸다고 할 수 없다.
examples.breakdown: 이 이론이 (this theory) 완전히 (completely) 틀렸다고 (to be wrong) 할 수 없다 (cannot say)
examples.english: One cannot say that this theory is completely wrong.

notes.title: Academic Tone
notes.text: This structure is essential for argumentative essays and critiques where direct contradiction might seem too strong.

notes.title: Positive Counterpart
notes.text: The positive form A/V-다고 할 수 있다 means 'one can say that,' used to assert a claim cautiously.

notes.title: Tense Variation
notes.text: Past tense uses -았/었다고 할 수 없다, e.g., 그가 잘못했다고 할 수 없다.

explanation: A formal analytical expression used to propose an interpretation or perspective on a situation without asserting absolute truth. It translates to 'can be seen as' or 'can be viewed as.'

rules.title: Interpretative Statements
rules.description: Attach -다고 볼 수 있다 to an adjective or verb stem to present a viewpoint or analysis. This is common in academic papers, reports, and formal evaluations.

examples.korean: 이 현상은 긍정적이라고 볼 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 이 현상은 (this phenomenon) 긍정적이라고 (to be positive) 볼 수 있다 (can be seen as)
examples.english: This phenomenon can be seen as positive.

examples.korean: 그의 행동은 일종의 도전이라고 볼 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 그의 행동은 (his action) 일종의 (a kind of) 도전이라고 (as a challenge) 볼 수 있다 (can be seen as)
examples.english: His actions can be seen as a kind of challenge.

rules.title: Objective Framing
rules.description: Often preceded by phrases like ~는 것으로 or ~ㄹ/을 것으로 to frame the analysis in an objective, third-person perspective.

examples.korean: 이 결과는 성공적인 것으로 볼 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 이 결과는 (this result) 성공적인 것으로 (as a successful thing) 볼 수 있다 (can be seen)
examples.english: This result can be seen as successful.

notes.title: Academic Essential
notes.text: This grammar is indispensable for literature reviews, art criticism, and sociological analysis in Korean.

notes.title: Alternative Forms
notes.text: More formal variants include -다고 여길 수 있다 and -다고 간주될 수 있다.

notes.title: Noun Adaptation
notes.text: When following a noun, use -이라고/라고 볼 수 있다, e.g., 이것은 전환점이라고 볼 수 있다.

explanation: Expresses objective possibility or capability in formal and written contexts. At the advanced level, it appears in complex sentences with conditional or hypothetical clauses, indicating that a certain outcome is within the realm of possibility.

rules.title: Formal Possibility
rules.description: Attach -ㄹ/을 수 있다 to the adjective or verb stem. For stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ, use -ㄹ 수 있다; for stems ending in a consonant, use -을 수 있다.

examples.korean: 이런 일이 다시 일어날 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 이런 일이 (such an event) 다시 (again) 일어날 (to occur) 수 있다 (is possible)
examples.english: It is possible that such an event will occur again.

examples.korean: 이 이론은 실용적으로 적용될 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 이 이론은 (this theory) 실용적으로 (practically) 적용될 (to be applied) 수 있다 (is possible)
examples.english: This theory can be practically applied.

rules.title: Hypothetical Contexts
rules.description: In advanced writing, this form frequently follows conditional clauses marked by -(으)면 or -다면 to discuss potential outcomes.

examples.breakdown: 노력한다면 (if one tries) 성공할 (to succeed) 수 있다 (it is possible)
examples.english: If one tries, it is possible to succeed.

notes.title: Advanced Usage
notes.text: At Level 5-6, this grammar is rarely isolated; it is usually embedded in multi-clause formal sentences.

notes.title: Emphatic Counterpart
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 수가 있다 adds emphasis and is common in spoken Korean, but less so in formal writing.

notes.title: Negative Form
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 수 없다 expresses impossibility. Note that 수없이 (countlessly) is a separate word and should not be confused.

explanation: Expresses a conditional guarantee: as long as the condition in the first clause is maintained, the result in the second clause will hold. It carries a formal, definitive tone and often appears in written promises, policies, or logical statements.

rules.title: Conditional Guarantee
rules.description: Attach -는 한 to a verb stem. The main clause usually states a guaranteed or inevitable consequence. This form is definitive and leaves little room for exceptions.

examples.breakdown: 노력하는 (as long as one tries) 한 (condition) 실패하지 않을 (will not fail) 것이다 (it is certain)
examples.english: As long as one tries, one will not fail.

examples.breakdown: 살아 있는 (as long as one lives) 한 (condition) 희망은 (hope) 있다 (exists)
examples.english: As long as one is alive, there is hope.

rules.title: Definitive Consequences
rules.description: The main clause often contains a strong declarative, sometimes negative, outcome such as a prohibition, punishment, or unchangeable result.

examples.korean: 규칙을 어기는 한 참가할 수 없다.
examples.breakdown: 규칙을 어기는 (as long as one breaks the rules) 한 (condition) 참가할 (to participate) 수 없다 (cannot)
examples.english: As long as you break the rules, you cannot participate.

notes.title: Logical Certainty
notes.text: This grammar implies that the condition is sufficient to guarantee the result, making it stronger than -(으)면.

notes.title: Negative Results
notes.text: It is very common for the main clause to describe a limitation or prohibition rather than a positive outcome.

notes.title: Verb Focus
notes.text: While labeled A/V, this form is most natural with verbs and descriptive verbs ending in -있다/없다.

explanation: A formal additive connective meaning 'on top of' or 'in addition to.' It stacks two facts or qualities about the same subject, emphasizing that both apply simultaneously. Both clauses should share the same polarity (both positive or both negative).

rules.title: Additive Connection
rules.description: For adjectives, use -(으)ㄴ 데다가; for verbs, use -는 데다가. The first clause provides an initial quality, and the second clause adds another layer.

examples.breakdown: 그는 (he) 똑똑한 (smart) 데다가 (on top of) 성실하다 (is diligent)
examples.english: On top of being smart, he is diligent.

examples.korean: 날씨가 추운 데다가 바람도 많이 분다.
examples.breakdown: 날씨가 (weather) 추운 (cold) 데다가 (on top of) 바람도 (wind also) 많이 분다 (blows a lot)
examples.english: On top of being cold, the wind is also blowing hard.

rules.title: Consistency Requirement
rules.description: The two clauses connected by -(으)ㄴ/는 데다가 should align in terms of positivity or negativity. Mixing a positive trait with a negative result creates awkwardness unless intentional contrast is desired.

examples.korean: 그 영화는 지루한 데다가 길기까지 하다.
examples.breakdown: 그 영화는 (that movie) 지루한 (boring) 데다가 (on top of) 길기까지 하다 (is even long)
examples.english: That movie is boring, and on top of that, it is even long.

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: While used in speech, this form is particularly common in formal written descriptions and reviews.

notes.title: Synonymous Form
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 뿐만 아니라 is functionally similar but places slightly more emphasis on the second clause.

notes.title: Polarity Match
notes.text: Avoid mixing positive and negative clauses unless you intend to create irony or contrast.

notes.title: Shortened Form
notes.text: In very casual speech, 데다가 may be shortened to 데다, but this is inappropriate for formal writing.

explanation: Indicates a cause or reason that leads to a disruptive, inconvenient, or negative result. It is more formal and written in tone than -아/어서, and it specifically highlights the problematic nature of the consequence.

rules.title: Disruptive Causation
rules.description: For adjectives, use -(으)ㄴ 통에; for verbs, use -는 통에. The main clause must describe a negative or undesirable outcome caused by the preceding situation.

examples.korean: 비가 오는 통에 야외 공연이 취소되었다.
examples.breakdown: 비가 오는 (because it was raining) 통에 (cause) 야외 공연이 (outdoor performance) 취소되었다 (was canceled)
examples.english: Because of the rain, the outdoor performance was canceled.

examples.breakdown: 교통이 복잡한 (because traffic was heavy) 통에 (cause) 약속에 (to the appointment) 늦었다 (was late)
examples.english: Because of the heavy traffic, I was late for the appointment.

rules.title: Emotional Disruption
rules.description: This form can also describe internal states that cause disruption, such as surprise, confusion, or anger.

examples.korean: 갑작스러운 소식에 놀란 통에 말을 잇지 못했다.
examples.breakdown: 갑작스러운 소식에 (at the sudden news) 놀란 (surprised) 통에 (because) 말을 잇지 못했다 (could not continue speaking)
examples.english: Surprised by the sudden news, I could not continue speaking.

notes.title: Negative Outcome Required
notes.text: The result clause must be negative or problematic. Using this grammar for positive outcomes is grammatically awkward.

notes.title: News and Reports
notes.text: This structure is frequently found in news reports and formal explanations of accidents or delays.

notes.title: Colloquial Alternative
notes.text: In spoken Korean, N 때문에 or A/V-아/어서 are more common for expressing similar causation.

explanation: Expresses that something is natural, expected, or understandable given the circumstances. It translates to 'it is natural that' or 'it stands to reason that,' and is often used to justify reactions or outcomes.

rules.title: Expressing Naturalness
rules.description: For adjectives, use -(으)ㄴ 법이다; for verbs, use -는 법이다. This form states that the described situation is naturally expected.

examples.breakdown: 사람인지라 (since we are human) 실수하는 (to make mistakes) 법이다 (it is natural)
examples.english: Since we are human, it is natural to make mistakes.

examples.breakdown: 처음에는 (at first) 서툰 (to be clumsy) 법이다 (it is natural)
examples.english: It is natural to be clumsy at first.

rules.title: Justifying Reactions
rules.description: Often used to explain why someone reacted a certain way or why an outcome was inevitable. It can be paired with -아/어서 or -니까 clauses.

examples.breakdown: 어려운 문제였으니 (since it was a difficult problem) 틀린 (to be wrong) 법이다 (it is natural)
examples.english: It was a difficult problem, so it is natural to get it wrong.

notes.title: Frequent Collocation
notes.text: This grammar frequently appears with -인지라 to provide reasoning, e.g., 학생인지라 모르는 법이다.

notes.title: Synonymous Expression
notes.text: -는 것이 당연하다 is a more direct way to say the same thing, but -(으)ㄴ/는 법이다 is more idiomatic and natural.

notes.title: Register
notes.text: While used in writing, this form also appears frequently in formal speech and presentations.

explanation: Indicates that a situation amounts to, is equivalent to, or can be considered as something else. It is used to reinterpret or summarize a situation in different terms, often to clarify its true nature.

rules.title: Equivalency and Reinterpretation
rules.description: For adjectives, use -(으)ㄴ 셈이다; for verbs, use -는 셈이다. This form reframes the preceding situation as being equal to the following noun or state.

examples.breakdown: 이번 달은 (this month) 쉬는 (resting) 셈이다 (amounts to)
examples.english: This month amounts to taking a rest.

examples.breakdown: 그 말은 (those words) 사실상 (in effect) 거절한 (refusal) 셈이다 (amount to)
examples.english: Those words amount to a refusal in effect.

rules.title: Summarizing Situations
rules.description: Often used with adverbs like 사실상 (in effect), 이제 (now), or 거의 (almost) to emphasize that the current state is equivalent to another.

examples.korean: 열 번 중 여덟 번을 맞추면 거의 합격한 셈이다.
examples.breakdown: 열 번 중 여덟 번을 (eight out of ten times) 맞추면 (if correct) 거의 (almost) 합격한 (passing) 셈이다 (amounts to)
examples.english: Getting eight out of ten right amounts to almost passing.

notes.title: Analytical Usage
notes.text: This grammar is very useful for summarizing complex situations into simpler terms in both writing and speech.

notes.title: Related Form
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ/는 것과 마찬가지다 means 'is the same as,' while 셈이다 focuses on numerical or logical equivalency.

notes.title: Written Nuance
notes.text: In formal writing, it often appears alongside ~ㄹ/을 것으로 본다 to present an analytical conclusion.

explanation: Expresses the act of pretending or feigning a certain state or action. It is a highly versatile form used in both spoken and written Korean to describe deliberate deception or play-acting.

rules.title: Pretending to Be or Do
rules.description: For adjectives, use -(으)ㄴ 척하다; for verbs, use -는 척하다. The subject intentionally acts as if the described state or action were true.

examples.english: Pretended not to know.

examples.breakdown: 아픈 (sick) 척하며 (pretending) 학교에 (to school) 안 갔다 (did not go)
examples.english: Pretended to be sick and did not go to school.

rules.title: Strong Negation Variant
rules.description: When combined with negative auxiliary verbs, it creates emphatic denial: -(으)ㄴ/는 척도 하지 않다 means 'does not even pretend to.'

examples.korean: 그는 내 말을 들은 척도 하지 않았다.
examples.breakdown: 그는 (he) 내 말을 (my words) 들은 (listening) 척도 하지 않았다 (did not even pretend)
examples.english: He did not even pretend to listen to me.

notes.title: High Frequency
notes.text: This is one of the most common advanced grammar points and appears in novels, dramas, and essays alike.

notes.title: Literary Variant
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ/는 체하다 is an older, more literary variant of the same meaning, found in classical texts.

notes.title: Bound Noun
notes.text: 척 is a bound noun and cannot be used independently without a preceding modifier.

explanation: A comprehensive conditional form meaning 'as long as.' It attaches to both adjectives and verbs to establish a condition that, if maintained, guarantees a specific result. It is definitive and formal in tone.

rules.title: Full Adjective and Verb Conjugation
rules.description: For adjectives ending in a consonant, use -(으)ㄴ 한; for adjectives ending in a vowel or ㄹ, use -ㄴ 한. For verbs, use -는 한. The main clause states the inevitable consequence.

examples.korean: 시간이 있는 한 계속 공부할 것이다.
examples.breakdown: 시간이 있는 (as long as there is time) 한 (condition) 계속 (continuously) 공부할 (will study) 것이다 (it is certain)
examples.english: As long as I have time, I will continue studying.

examples.korean: 건강이 좋은 한 걱정할 필요가 없다.
examples.breakdown: 건강이 좋은 (as long as health is good) 한 (condition) 걱정할 (to worry) 필요가 없다 (there is no need)
examples.english: As long as your health is good, there is no need to worry.

rules.title: Negative and Restrictive Outcomes
rules.description: The main clause frequently contains prohibitions, limitations, or unavoidable negative consequences, making this grammar suitable for rules and formal declarations.

examples.korean: 증거가 확실한 한 유죄로 볼 수밖에 없다.
examples.breakdown: 증거가 확실한 (as long as evidence is certain) 한 (condition) 유죄로 (as guilty) 볼 수밖에 없다 (cannot help but see)
examples.english: As long as the evidence is certain, one cannot help but see it as guilty.

notes.title: Comprehensive Form
notes.text: Unlike the shorthand A/V-는 한, this notation explicitly includes the adjective stem variants with -(으)ㄴ.

notes.title: Avoid Confusion
notes.text: Do not confuse this with -(으)ㄴ/는 한 meaning 'a kind of,' as in 그런 한 사람 (that kind of person). Context determines the meaning.

notes.title: Equivalent Expression
notes.text: -기만 하면 carries a similar meaning but is less formal and slightly more spoken in register.

explanation: A formal expression meaning 'lie in' or 'consist in.' It is used to pinpoint the exact essence, core, or critical factor of a subject. This grammar is almost exclusively used in formal definitions, philosophical statements, and analytical writing.

rules.title: Locating the Essence
rules.description: Attach -는 데 있다 to a verb stem. The pattern typically follows a noun phrase like N의 핵심은, N의 의미는, or N의 가치는, pointing to where the true nature of something resides.

examples.korean: 문제는 서로 신뢰하지 않는 데 있다.
examples.breakdown: 문제는 (the problem) 서로 (mutually) 신뢰하지 않는 (not trusting) 데 있다 (lies in)
examples.english: The problem lies in not trusting each other.

examples.korean: 성공의 비결은 포기하지 않는 데 있다.
examples.breakdown: 성공의 비결은 (the secret of success) 포기하지 않는 (not giving up) 데 있다 (lies in)
examples.english: The secret to success lies in not giving up.

rules.title: Abstract Subject Focus
rules.description: The subject is almost always an abstract noun such as 비결, 핵심, 의미, 가치, or 중요성. Concrete subjects sound unnatural with this grammar.

examples.korean: 이 법의 취지는 국민을 보호하는 데 있다.
examples.breakdown: 이 법의 취지는 (the spirit of this law) 국민을 보호하는 (protecting the people) 데 있다 (lies in)
examples.english: The spirit of this law lies in protecting the people.

notes.title: Philosophical Tone
notes.text: This structure is essential for writing thesis statements, policy definitions, and philosophical arguments.

notes.title: Related Expression
notes.text: -에 달려 있다 means 'depends on,' focusing on dependence rather than essence.

notes.title: Negative Forms
notes.text: The negative form -지 않는 데 있다 is extremely common because the essence often lies in what one avoids doing.

explanation: A highly formal expression indicating that a conclusion, belief, or result is drawn from a preceding fact. It translates to 'from the fact that' and is almost exclusively used in academic, legal, and analytical writing.

rules.title: Basis of Reasoning
rules.description: Attach -는 데서 to a verb stem. The first clause provides the factual basis, and the second clause presents the conclusion or inference drawn from it.

examples.korean: 그가 직접 확인하는 데서 그 정보는 믿을 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 그가 직접 확인하는 (from the fact that he checks directly) 데서 (basis) 그 정보는 (that information) 믿을 수 있다 (can be trusted)
examples.english: From the fact that he checks it personally, that information can be trusted.

examples.korean: 국민이 참여하는 데서 민주주의의 가치가 있다.
examples.breakdown: 국민이 참여하는 (from the fact that citizens participate) 데서 (basis) 민주주의의 (democracy's) 가치가 (value) 있다 (exists)
examples.english: From the fact that citizens participate, the value of democracy exists.

rules.title: Conclusion Patterns
rules.description: The main clause frequently uses deduction verbs like -ㄹ/을 것이다, -ㄴ/은다고 생각하다, or -ㄹ/을 수 있다 to show that the conclusion is derived from the basis.

examples.korean: 학생들이 열심히 배우는 데서 교육의 목적이 달성된다.
examples.breakdown: 학생들이 열심히 배우는 (from students studying hard) 데서 (basis) 교육의 (education's) 목적이 (goal) 달성된다 (is achieved)
examples.english: The goal of education is achieved from the fact that students study hard.

notes.title: Academic Register
notes.text: This grammar is rarely used outside of formal written discourse. It signals a high level of analytical rigor.

notes.title: Distinguish from Location
notes.text: Do not confuse this with -는 데서 meaning 'at the place where,' e.g., 공부하는 데서 (at the place where one studies). Context and the main clause verb reveal the meaning.

notes.title: Verb Restriction
notes.text: While labeled for verbs, adjectives can be nominalized or used in relative clauses preceding 데서, but the direct attachment follows the standard A/V-는 rule.

explanation: A formal causative expression translating to 'due to' or 'owing to.' It attaches to nouns and is primarily used in news reports, official documents, and formal announcements to explain serious or significant causes.

rules.title: Formal Causation
rules.description: Attach -으로 인해(서) to a noun. For nouns ending in a consonant, use -으로; for nouns ending in a vowel, use -로. The optional 서 adds a slight increase in formality.

examples.breakdown: 홍수로 (due to the flood) 인해 (causation) 많은 주민이 (many residents) 대피했다 (evacuated)
examples.english: Due to the flood, many residents evacuated.

examples.breakdown: 그의 부주의로 (due to his carelessness) 인해 (causation) 사고가 (an accident) 발생했다 (occurred)
examples.english: Due to his carelessness, an accident occurred.

rules.title: Adnominal Form
rules.description: The form -으로 인한 can modify a following noun, creating adnominal phrases such as 'damage due to X' or 'loss caused by Y.'

examples.breakdown: 지진으로 인한 (damage due to the earthquake) 피해가 (damage) 막대하다 (is enormous)
examples.english: The damage due to the earthquake is enormous.

notes.title: Negative or Serious Events
notes.text: This grammar is most natural with negative, serious, or newsworthy events. Using it for trivial matters sounds overly dramatic.

notes.title: Optional 서
notes.text: -으로 인해 and -으로 인해서 are both correct. The longer form feels slightly more formal and deliberate.

notes.title: Casual Alternative
notes.text: N 때문에 is the standard casual causative, while -으로 인해(서) belongs to formal registers.

notes.title: Noun Requirement
notes.text: Verbs and adjectives must be nominalized with -기, -음, or -는 것 before attaching -으로 인해(서).

explanation: A highly formal Sino-Korean expression meaning 'is attributable to,' 'is due to,' or 'owes to.' It represents the highest register among causative expressions and is almost exclusively found in academic papers, technical reports, and formal analyses.

rules.title: Attribution to a Cause
rules.description: Attach -에 기인하다 to a noun to formally attribute a result to a specific cause. The verb 기인하다 is of Sino-Korean origin and carries an elevated, scholarly tone.

examples.breakdown: 이 현상은 (this phenomenon) 기후 변화에 (to climate change) 기인한다 (is attributable)
examples.english: This phenomenon is attributable to climate change.

examples.breakdown: 그의 성공은 (his success) 부단한 노력에 (to unceasing effort) 기인한다 (is attributable)
examples.english: His success is attributable to unceasing effort.

rules.title: Academic Conclusions
rules.description: This form frequently concludes analytical sections by summarizing the primary cause of a phenomenon. It is often paired with other formal structures.

examples.korean: 이러한 결과는 실험 설계의 오류에 기인한다고 볼 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 이러한 결과는 (such results) 실험 설계의 오류에 (to experimental design errors) 기인한다고 볼 수 있다 (can be seen as attributable)
examples.english: Such results can be seen as attributable to experimental design errors.

notes.title: Highest Register
notes.text: Among all causative expressions, -에 기인하다 is the most formal and scholarly. Use it in dissertations, white papers, and formal reports.

notes.title: Frequent Pairings
notes.text: Often combined with -ㄴ/은다고 볼 수 있다 or -ㄹ/을 것으로 추정된다 to maintain analytical distance.

notes.title: Noun Only
notes.text: This grammar attaches directly to nouns only. To use it with verbs, nominalize with -기 or -음 first.

notes.title: Sino-Korean Origin
notes.text: 기인 (起因) means 'origin' or 'cause.' Understanding the Hanja helps distinguish it from casual alternatives.

explanation: Attach 을/를 원인으로 to a noun to formally state that something is the cause or reason behind a subsequent situation. This expression is predominantly used in written, academic, and news contexts. It adds an objective tone, attributing causation to the preceding noun rather than a personal claim. The second clause typically describes a consequence, reaction, or phenomenon that arose from that cause.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 원인으로
rules.description: Use 을 with nouns ending in a consonant and 를 with nouns ending in a vowel. Followed by a statement explaining what resulted from that cause.

examples.korean: 기후 변화를 원인으로 여러 생태계가 파괴되고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 기후 변화(Climate change) + 를 원인으로 + 여러 생태계가 파괴되고 있다.
examples.english: With climate change as the cause, various ecosystems are being destroyed.

examples.breakdown: 경제 위기(Economic crisis) + 를 원인으로 + 실업률이 급등했다.
examples.english: With the economic crisis as the cause, the unemployment rate surged.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 원인으로 한 + Noun
rules.description: When used attributively before a noun, add 한 to modify the following noun, creating phrases like 'a war caused by territorial disputes'.

examples.korean: 영토 분쟁을 원인으로 한 전쟁이 발발했다.
examples.breakdown: 영토 분쟁(Territorial dispute) + 을 원인으로 한 + 전쟁이 발발했다.
examples.english: A war caused by a territorial dispute broke out.

notes.title: Written/Formal Register
notes.text: This pattern is almost exclusively used in news reports, academic papers, and formal analysis. In spoken Korean, ~때문에 or ~덕분에 are far more common.

notes.title: Objective Causation
notes.text: Using 을/를 원인으로 frames the cause as an observable fact rather than a subjective opinion, which is why it is preferred in objective writing.

notes.title: Often Followed by Passive Verbs
notes.text: The result clause frequently uses passive constructions (피해를 입다, 발생하다, 초래되다) to maintain an objective tone.

explanation: Attach 을/를 결과로 to a noun to indicate that something occurred as a consequence or outcome. This structure formally introduces a result and is common in analytical writing, research papers, and reports. It emphasizes that the following situation is a direct consequence of the preceding noun.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 결과로
rules.description: Use 을 after consonant-ending nouns and 를 after vowel-ending nouns. The following clause describes what happened as a consequence.

examples.korean: 협상 결렬을 결과로 양국 관계가 악화되었다.
examples.breakdown: 협상 결렬(Negotiation breakdown) + 을 결과로 + 양국 관계가 악화되었다.
examples.english: With the breakdown of negotiations as the result, bilateral relations deteriorated.

examples.korean: 긴 침체를 결과로 많은 기업이 도산했다.
examples.breakdown: 긴 침체(Long stagnation) + 를 결과로 + 많은 기업이 도산했다.
examples.english: With a long stagnation as the result, many companies went bankrupt.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 결과로 한 + Noun
rules.description: Add 한 before a noun to modify it attributively, describing something 'resulting from' the noun.

examples.korean: 정책 실패를 결과로 한 사회적 혼란이 가중되고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 정책 실패(Policy failure) + 를 결과로 한 + 사회적 혼란이 가중되고 있다.
examples.english: Social chaos resulting from a policy failure is intensifying.

notes.title: Formal Register Only
notes.text: This is a highly formal pattern found in news, academic writing, and official reports. Do not use it in casual conversation.

notes.title: Distinction from ~때문에
notes.text: While ~때문에 focuses on the cause itself, ~을/를 결과로 focuses on framing the consequence, often presenting it as an outcome that requires further analysis.

notes.title: Frequently with Negative Outcomes
notes.text: This pattern often collocates with negative consequences such as 혼란, 파국, 도산, 악화, but can also describe positive results in objective reports.

explanation: Attach 을/를 대상으로 to a noun to indicate that an action is directed toward a specific target, group, or object. It is commonly used in official announcements, surveys, policies, and academic studies to specify who or what is being targeted by the subsequent action.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 대상으로
rules.description: Use 을 after consonant-ending nouns and 를 after vowel-ending nouns. The following clause states what action is performed on that target.

examples.korean: 청년층을 대상으로 취업 지원 프로그램을 운영한다.
examples.breakdown: 청년층(Youth demographic) + 을 대상으로 + 취업 지원 프로그램을 운영한다.
examples.english: They operate an employment support program targeting the youth demographic.

examples.breakdown: 외국인(Foreigners) + 을 대상으로 + 한국어 강좌를 개설했다.
examples.english: They opened a Korean language course targeting foreigners.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 대상으로 한 + Noun
rules.description: Add 한 before a noun to create attributive expressions such as 'a study targeting students'.

examples.korean: 대학생을 대상으로 한 설문 조사 결과를 발표했다.
examples.breakdown: 대학생(University students) + 을 대상으로 한 + 설문 조사 결과를 발표했다.
examples.english: They presented the results of a survey conducted on university students.

notes.title: Formal and Official Usage
notes.text: This pattern is standard in government documents, academic research, and institutional announcements. In casual speech, ~한테 or ~에게 are more natural.

notes.title: Versatile Target Reference
notes.text: The noun can refer to people, regions, objects, or abstract concepts. The action can be anything from surveys and education to medical treatment and marketing.

notes.title: Often with 수/연구/조사/정책
notes.text: Common collocations include 대상으로 조사하다, 대상으로 연구하다, 대상으로 정책을 시행하다.

explanation: Attach 을/를 대상자로 to a noun to designate someone or something as the specific target or recipient of an action, benefit, or policy.
