This expression is more specific than 대상으로 and is often used in administrative, legal, and institutional contexts where precise classification is necessary.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 대상자로
rules.description: Use 을 after consonant-ending nouns and 를 after vowel-ending nouns. The following clause identifies the action or classification applied to the designated target.

examples.breakdown: 저소득 가정(Low-income household) + 을 대상자로 + 지원금을 지급한다.
examples.english: They provide subsidies designating low-income households as the targets.

examples.korean: 장애인을 대상자로 복지 서비스를 확대하고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 장애인(People with disabilities) + 을 대상자로 + 복지 서비스를 확대하고 있다.
examples.english: They are expanding welfare services with people with disabilities as the designated targets.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 대상자로 삼다/정하다/선정하다
rules.description: This pattern frequently pairs with verbs like 삼다, 정하다, or 선정하다 to explicitly state that someone has been chosen or designated as the target.

examples.korean: 지역 주민을 대상자로 삼아 건강 검진을 실시했다.
examples.breakdown: 지역 주민(Local residents) + 을 대상자로 삼아 + 건강 검진을 실시했다.
examples.english: They conducted health screenings with local residents as the designated targets.

notes.title: Administrative Precision
notes.text: 대상자로 carries a sense of official designation, making it more appropriate than 대상으로 in legal documents and eligibility criteria.

notes.title: Distinction from 대상으로
notes.text: While 대상으로 broadly states that something is a target, 대상자로 emphasizes formal selection or classification. 대상자 literally means 'target person/entity'.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Frequently used with 삼다, 정하다, 선정하다, 포함하다, 제외하다, and similar classification verbs.

explanation: Attach 을/를 주제로 to a noun to indicate that something is the central theme or topic of a discussion, work, event, or artistic creation. This pattern is widely used in academic, cultural, and media contexts to introduce the main subject matter.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 주제로
rules.description: Use 을 after consonant-ending nouns and 를 after vowel-ending nouns. The following clause describes an activity organized around that theme.

examples.korean: 환경 보호를 주제로 국제 포럼이 열린다.
examples.breakdown: 환경 보호(Environmental protection) + 를 주제로 + 국제 포럼이 열린다.
examples.english: An international forum is being held with environmental protection as the theme.

examples.breakdown: 전쟁(War) + 을 주제로 한 + 영화가 개봉되었다.
examples.english: A film with war as its theme has been released.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 주제로 삼다/다루다/삼아
rules.description: When the theme is actively chosen, verbs like 삼다 or 다루다 are used. 삼아 is the adverbial form connecting to another clause.

examples.korean: 사랑과 이별을 주제로 삼아 소설을 썼다.
examples.breakdown: 사랑과 이별(Love and parting) + 을 주제로 삼아 + 소설을 썼다.
examples.english: I wrote a novel taking love and parting as the theme.

notes.title: Versatile Usage
notes.text: This pattern applies to academic papers, conferences, films, novels, art exhibitions, debates, and any event or creation with a central topic.

notes.title: Common Verbs
notes.text: Frequent verb pairings include 다루다, 삼다, 열리다, 개최되다, 출품하다, 연출하다, 발표하다.

notes.title: Written and Cultural Register
notes.text: While acceptable in spoken Korean when discussing events or media, it is most commonly encountered in written descriptions of cultural and academic activities.

explanation: Attach 을/를 소재로 to a noun to indicate that a creative work, story, or project is based on or derived from a particular material, source, or real-life event. This pattern is predominantly used when discussing literature, film, art, and media that draw inspiration from existing subjects.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 소재로
rules.description: Use 을 after consonant-ending nouns and 를 after vowel-ending nouns. The following clause describes the creative work or project based on that material.

examples.breakdown: 실제 사건(Real incident) + 을 소재로 + 영화를 제작했다.
examples.english: They produced a film based on a real incident as the material.

examples.korean: 민화를 소재로 한 전시회가 열리고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 민화(Folk painting) + 를 소재로 한 + 전시회가 열리고 있다.
examples.english: An exhibition based on folk paintings as its material is being held.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 소재로 삼다/다루다/삼아
rules.description: Use 삼다 or 다루다 to express the active choice of material. 삼아 connects adverbially to the creative action.

examples.korean: 고려 시대를 소재로 삼아 드라마를 연출했다.
examples.breakdown: 고려 시대(Goryeo dynasty) + 를 소재로 삼아 + 드라마를 연출했다.
examples.english: They directed a drama taking the Goryeo dynasty as its material.

notes.title: Creative and Media Context
notes.text: 소재 specifically refers to creative material or subject matter. It is almost exclusively used in artistic, literary, and media contexts rather than academic ones (where 주제로 is preferred).

notes.title: Distinction from 주제로
notes.text: 주제로 refers to the central theme or message, whereas 소재로 refers to the raw material, source, or inspiration from which the work is derived.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Frequently used with 창작하다, 제작하다, 연출하다, 그리다, 쓰다, and similar creative production verbs.

explanation: Attach 을/를 배경으로 to a noun to indicate that an event, story, or situation unfolds against the backdrop of a particular setting, era, or circumstance. This pattern is essential in historical narratives, literary analysis, and film descriptions to establish the contextual environment.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 배경으로
rules.description: Use 을 after consonant-ending nouns and 를 after vowel-ending nouns. The following clause describes what occurs within that setting or context.

examples.korean: 1980년대를 배경으로 한 영화가 큰 인기를 끌었다.
examples.breakdown: 1980년대(The 1980s) + 를 배경으로 한 + 영화가 큰 인기를 끌었다.
examples.english: A film set against the backdrop of the 1980s gained great popularity.
The narrative unfolds with war as the backdrop.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 배경으로 삼다/하다
rules.description: Use 삼다 or 하다 to actively choose or describe a backdrop. This emphasizes intentional setting selection in creative works.

examples.korean: 소설은 조선 시대를 배경으로 삼고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 소설(Novel) + 은 + 조선 시대(Joseon dynasty) + 를 배경으로 삼고 있다.
examples.english: The novel takes the Joseon dynasty as its backdrop.

notes.title: Temporal and Spatial Settings
notes.text: The noun can refer to historical periods, locations, social conditions, political climates, or any contextual environment that frames the main action.

notes.title: Common in Reviews and Analysis
notes.text: This pattern is standard in book reviews, film critiques, and historical writing when describing the setting of a narrative or event.

notes.title: Can Describe Real Events Too
notes.text: Beyond fiction, it can describe real-world developments unfolding against economic or political backdrops, e.g., 금융 위기를 배경으로 한 기업 구조조정.

explanation: Attach 을/를 물론 to a noun to mean 'not to mention' or 'let alone,' emphasizing that the noun represents the most obvious or basic example, and the following clause extends that inclusion to other items or people. It often carries a nuance of surprise or emphasis regarding the broader scope.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 물론
rules.description: Use 을 after consonant-ending nouns and 를 after vowel-ending nouns. The following clause includes additional items beyond the stated noun.

examples.english: Not to mention the students, even the teachers participated.
The food was excellent, not to mention the service.

rules.title: Followed by 도/까지/조차
rules.description: The extended item in the following clause is typically marked with 도, 까지, or 조차 to reinforce the inclusiveness.

examples.breakdown: 대통령(President) + 을 물론 + 각료들까지 비판에 직면했다.
examples.english: Not to mention the president, even the ministers faced criticism.

notes.title: Emphatic Inclusion
notes.text: This pattern strongly emphasizes that if the most obvious example is true, then lesser or related examples are even more certainly included.

notes.title: Fixed Colloquial Variant
notes.text: In casual speech, (는)은 물론이고 is commonly used for the same meaning with a slightly more conversational tone.

notes.title: Particle Alignment
notes.text: When the extended noun is the subject of the following clause, use 이/가. When it is an object, use 을/를. Use 도 for general inclusiveness.

explanation: Attach 은/는 물론 to a noun to mean 'not to mention' or 'of course,' with a slightly stronger topic-marking nuance than 을/를 물론. It presents the noun as a given fact before extending the scope to additional elements. This is one of the most common advanced inclusive expressions in formal Korean.

rules.title: Noun + 은/는 물론
rules.description: Use 은 after consonant-ending nouns and 는 after vowel-ending nouns. The following clause adds further examples or details that go beyond the stated noun.

examples.english: Not to mention the price, the quality is also satisfactory.
Not to mention Korean, he is also fluent in English.

rules.title: Extended Form: N + 은/는 물론이고
rules.description: Adding 이고 makes the expression more colloquial and emphatic. It is widely used in both spoken and written Korean.

examples.english: Not to mention appearance, even personality is excellent.

notes.title: Most Common Form
notes.text: 은/는 물론 is more frequently encountered in TOPIK reading and listening materials than 을/를 물론. Master this form first.

notes.title: Interchangeable with ~은/는 물론이고
notes.text: The two forms are largely interchangeable, but 물론이고 sounds slightly softer and more conversational, while plain 물론 is more compact and formal.

notes.title: Scope Expansion
notes.text: The second element is usually marked with 도, 까지, or 마저 to show the widening scope of the statement.

explanation: Attach 도 마찬가지다 to a noun to assert that the same situation, condition, or truth applies to that noun as well. It translates to 'the same goes for' or 'it is the same with.' This expression is used when extending a previously stated observation to another subject.

rules.title: Noun + 도 마찬가지다
rules.description: 도 is the additive particle attached directly to the noun. 마찬가지다 is the predicative form meaning 'it is the same.' The noun receives the same treatment or shares the same condition as something previously mentioned.

examples.english: The same goes for me.
This problem is no different.

rules.title: Sentence Ending and Connective Forms
rules.description: 마찬가지다 can be conjugated normally: 마찬가지입니다 (formal), 마찬가지예요 (polite), 마찬가지인 (attributive), 마찬가지로 (adverbial).

examples.breakdown: 다음 주에도(Next week too) + 마찬가지로 + 진행될 예정이다.
examples.english: It is scheduled to proceed in the same way next week as well.

notes.title: Agreement and Extension
notes.text: This pattern is a concise way to express agreement or note that a condition extends to another party. It is common in discussions, debates, and comparative analysis.

notes.title: Requires Prior Context
notes.text: Because 마찬가지 means 'the same,' there must be a preceding statement that establishes what the noun is being compared to. It cannot stand alone without context.

notes.title: With Verbs: V-는 것도 마찬가지다
notes.text: You can nominalize a verb with 는 것 and then add 도 마찬가지다: 늦는 것도 마찬가지다 (Being late is the same).

explanation: Attach 이/가 마찬가지다 to a noun to assert that the noun is the same in condition, situation, or nature as something previously mentioned. Unlike 도 마찬가지다, which uses the additive particle, 이/가 마찬가지다 uses the subject marker, making the noun the grammatical subject of the sentence. The nuance is nearly identical in practice.

rules.title: Noun + 이/가 마찬가지다
rules.description: Use 이 after consonant-ending nouns and 가 after vowel-ending nouns. The noun becomes the subject, and the predicate states that it is the same as the previously established reference.

examples.english: The result is the same.

examples.breakdown: 그 사람의 생각(His thought) + 이 마찬가지다.
examples.english: His thinking is the same.

rules.title: Negation: 이/가 마찬가지가 아니다
rules.description: To state that something is not the same, negate 마찬가지다 in the standard manner.

examples.english: The situation is not the same.

notes.title: Subtle Distinction from 도
notes.text: In everyday usage, 도 마찬가지다 and 이/가 마찬가지다 are often interchangeable. However, 이/가 marks the noun as the grammatical subject, which can feel slightly more analytical or explanatory.

notes.title: Standalone Usage Possible
notes.text: Because 이/가 makes the noun the subject, this form can occasionally appear without explicit prior context if the shared condition is understood situationally.

notes.title: Common in Explanatory Contexts
notes.text: This pattern often appears in explanatory or analytical discourse where the speaker is comparing subjects directly.

explanation: Attach (이)나 다름없다 to a noun to express that a situation or thing is virtually, practically, or essentially the same as the noun, even if not identical in name or form. It conveys a strong sense of equivalence and is common in both spoken and written Korean at advanced levels.

rules.title: Noun + (이)나 다름없다
rules.description: Use 이나 after consonant-ending nouns and 나 after vowel-ending nouns. 다름없다 is the contracted form of 다르지 않다, meaning 'not different.' The entire expression means 'no different from.'

examples.english: It was a result no different from failure.

examples.breakdown: 그의 말(His words) + 은 + 거짓말(Lie) + 이나 다름없었다.
examples.english: His words were no different from a lie.

rules.title: Past Tense and Attributive Usage
rules.description: 다름없다 conjugates normally. Use 다름없었다 for past tense and 다름없는 for modifying nouns.

examples.breakdown: 그때의 기분(The feeling then) + 은 + 꿈(Dream) + 이나 다름없었다.
examples.english: The feeling at that time was no different from a dream.

notes.title: Strong Equivalence
notes.text: This expression implies near-total equivalence. It is stronger than 비슷하다 (similar) and should be used when the speaker wants to equate two things in practical terms.

notes.title: Often Used for Hyperbole
notes.text: In casual speech, it can be used hyperbolically: 죽으나 다름없는 고통 (pain no different from death). Context determines whether it is literal or exaggerated.

notes.title: Formal Written Form
notes.text: In very formal writing, you may encounter 다름이 없다, which is the expanded form with the same meaning.

explanation: Attach 과/와 마찬가지다 to a noun to state that something is exactly the same as the noun in nature, condition, or behavior. This pattern uses the comitative particle 과/와 (with/and) to establish a direct comparison, making it particularly useful for analogies and parallel reasoning.

rules.title: Noun + 과/와 마찬가지다
rules.description: Use 과 after consonant-ending nouns and 와 after vowel-ending nouns. 마찬가지다 functions as the predicate stating that the subject is the same as the compared noun.

examples.breakdown: 물(Water) + 은 + 생명체(Living thing) + 와 마찬가지다.
examples.english: Water is the same as a living thing.

examples.breakdown: 그의 태도(His attitude) + 는 + 예전(Past) + 과 마찬가지다.
examples.english: His attitude is the same as before.

rules.title: Adverbial Form: 과/와 마찬가지로
rules.description: Change 마찬가지다 to 마찬가지로 to use it adverbially, describing how an action is performed in the same way.

examples.breakdown: 어른(Adults) + 과 마찬가지로 + 아이들도 존중받아야 한다.
examples.english: Children should be respected, just like adults.

notes.title: Direct Comparison
notes.text: 과/와 마찬가지다 sets up an explicit A = B comparison. It is more direct than 도 마찬가지다, which extends a prior statement without naming the comparator.

notes.title: Common in Analogies
notes.text: This pattern is frequently used in essays, speeches, and logical arguments to draw analogies between two concepts.

notes.title: Nearly Interchangeable with ~처럼/같이
notes.text: In many contexts, ~과/와 마찬가지다 and ~처럼/같이 are interchangeable. However, 마찬가지다 carries a slightly more formal and analytical nuance.

explanation: Attach 과/와는 달리 to a noun to express that something is different from or unlike the noun in a specific way. This is a fundamental contrastive pattern used to highlight differences between two subjects, situations, or conditions. It is essential for comparative analysis in advanced Korean.

rules.title: Noun + 과/와는 달리
rules.description: Use 과 after consonant-ending nouns and 와 after vowel-ending nouns. Add 는 for topic/contrast emphasis. The following clause describes how the subject differs from the noun.

examples.breakdown: 동생(Younger sibling) + 과는 달리 + 형(Older brother) + 은 + 조용하다.
examples.english: Unlike his younger sibling, the older brother is quiet.
Unlike expectations, the exam was easy.

rules.title: Adverbial Usage in Mid-Sentence
rules.description: The pattern can appear in the middle of a sentence to set up a contrast between two clauses.

examples.breakdown: 작년(Last year) + 과는 달리 + 올해는 매출이 증가했다.
examples.english: Unlike last year, sales increased this year.

notes.title: Core Contrastive Pattern
notes.text: 과/와는 달리 is one of the most important contrastive expressions at the advanced level. It appears frequently in TOPIK reading passages, essays, and news articles.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Frequently paired with 예상, 계획, 과거, 다른 나라, 전통, and other reference points used for comparison.

notes.title: 는 is Optional but Preferred
notes.text: While 과/와 달리 is grammatically acceptable, adding 는 strengthens the contrastive nuance and is more natural in formal writing.

explanation: Attach 과/와는 반대로 to a noun to state that something is the exact opposite of the noun in nature, direction, or result. This is a stronger form of contrast than 과/와는 달리, emphasizing polar opposition rather than mere difference. It is widely used in argumentative writing, analysis, and logical comparison.

rules.title: Noun + 과/와는 반대로
rules.description: Use 과 after consonant-ending nouns and 와 after vowel-ending nouns. The following clause presents a situation that is diametrically opposed to the noun.

examples.breakdown: 이론(Theory) + 과는 반대로 + 실제 결과가 나타났다.
examples.english: Contrary to theory, actual results appeared.
Contrary to the claim, evidence was insufficient.

rules.title: With Verbs: V-는 것과는 반대로
rules.description: Nominalize a verb with 는 것 to contrast an entire action or situation rather than a simple noun.

examples.breakdown: 예상하는 것(What was expected) + 과는 반대로 + 경기가 회복되었다.
examples.english: Contrary to what was expected, the economy recovered.

notes.title: Stronger than 달리
notes.text: While 달리 implies general difference, 반대로 implies direct opposition. Use 반대로 only when the contrast is fundamental and polar.

notes.title: Academic and Logical Usage
notes.text: This pattern is essential in essays, research papers, and debates where the writer must highlight contradictions between theory and practice, claims and evidence, or expectations and reality.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Frequently paired with 이론, 주장, 예상, 생각, 방향, 경향, and similar nouns representing positions or predictions.

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: This is a formal expression suitable for advanced writing. In casual speech, ~아니라 or ~는 게 아니라 may be used for simpler oppositions.

explanation: Used in formal writing and speeches to highlight a sharp difference between two subjects. Attaches to a noun with the particle 과/와 plus 는 for topical contrast, followed by 대조적으로 (contrastively). It carries a deliberate, analytical tone suitable for essays, reports, and academic discussion.

rules.title: Noun + 과/와는 대조적으로
rules.description: Attach 과/와는 대조적으로 to a noun to present it as the contrasting counterpart. Use 과 after consonant-ending nouns, 와 after vowel-ending nouns.

examples.korean: 도시의 삶과는 대조적으로 시골 생활은 한적하다.
examples.breakdown: 도시의 삶(city life) + 과는 대조적으로 (in contrast to) 시골 생활(rural life) + 는(topic) 한적하다 (is tranquil)
examples.english: In contrast to city life, rural life is tranquil.

examples.korean: 작년의 부진과는 대조적으로 올해는 매출이 급증했다.
examples.breakdown: 작년의 부진(last year's sluggishness) + 과는 대조적으로 (in contrast to) 올해는(this year) 매출이(sales) 급증했다 (surged)
examples.english: In contrast to last year's sluggishness, sales surged this year.

rules.title: Placement in a sentence
rules.description: Typically placed at the beginning of a sentence or clause, setting up the contrast before the main statement. It is almost exclusively used in written Korean.

examples.korean: 이理論과는 대조적으로 새로운 연구 결과가 나왔다.
examples.breakdown: 이理論(this theory) + 과는 대조적으로 (in contrast to) 새로운 연구 결과(new research results) + 가(subject) 나왔다 (came out)
examples.english: In contrast to this theory, new research results have emerged.

notes.title: Written register
notes.text: 과/와는 대조적으로 is formal and bookish. In spoken Korean, use ~와/과 달리 or 반대로 instead.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Often paired with 이론(theory), 예상(expectation), 전통(tradition), 과거(past), 경향(tendency).

notes.title: Interchangeability
notes.text: Can often replace 과/와는 반대로 in written contexts, but 대조적으로 sounds more academic and analytical.

explanation: A versatile contrast marker used in both writing and formal speech. Attaches to a noun with 에 반해(서) to indicate that the following clause diverges from the noun in an unexpected or noteworthy way. It is common in news, essays, and reports.

rules.title: Noun + 에 반해(서)
rules.description: Attach 에 반해 or 에 반해서 to a noun. Both forms are acceptable; 반해 is slightly more written, 반해서 more neutral.

examples.korean: 남편은 조용한 성격에 반해 아내는 활발하다.
examples.breakdown: 남편은(husband, topic) 조용한 성격(quiet personality) + 에 반해 (in contrast to) 아내는(wife, topic) 활발하다 (is outgoing)
examples.english: In contrast to her husband's quiet personality, his wife is outgoing.

examples.korean: 주변의 우려에 반해 프로젝트는 성공적으로 마무리되었다.
examples.breakdown: 주변의 우려(surrounding concerns) + 에 반해 (in contrast to) 프로젝트는(project, topic) 성공적으로(successfully) 마무리되었다 (was concluded)
examples.english: In contrast to surrounding concerns, the project was successfully concluded.

rules.title: Clause-initial or mid-sentence
rules.description: Can appear at the beginning of a sentence or embedded mid-sentence to create a direct contrast between two parallel elements.

examples.korean: 경제학과 학생들에 반해 문학과 학생들은 취업이 어렵다는 인식이 있다.
examples.breakdown: 경제학과 학생들(economics students) + 에 반해 (in contrast to) 문학과 학생들은(literature students, topic) 취업이(employment) 어렵다는 인식이(perception of being difficult) 있다 (exists)
examples.english: In contrast to economics students, there is a perception that literature students struggle to find jobs.

notes.title: 반해 vs 반해서
notes.text: 에 반해 sounds more formal and written. 에 반해서 is acceptable in both writing and formal speech.

notes.title: Nuanced contrast
notes.text: Implies that the result is notable precisely because it goes against the noun. It suggests a reversal or contradiction.

notes.title: Frequent pairings
notes.text: Commonly used with 예상(expectation), 걱정(worry), 우려(concern), 추세(trend), 전망(outlook), 성향(personality).

notes.title: Not for simple comparison
notes.text: Unlike 에 비해, 에 반해 implies an opposing or divergent relationship, not merely a difference in degree.

explanation: Used to compare the degree, amount, or quality of something against a reference point. Attaches to a noun with 에 비해(서) and is standard in both written and spoken Korean, though it sounds most natural in analytical or descriptive contexts.

rules.title: Noun + 에 비해(서)
rules.description: Attach 에 비해 or 에 비해서 to a noun. The following clause describes how the subject differs in degree from the reference noun.

examples.breakdown: 한국은(Korea, topic) 면적(area) + 에 비해 (compared to) 인구가(population) 많다 (is large)
examples.english: Korea has a large population compared to its area.

examples.breakdown: 예산(budget) + 에 비해서 (compared to) 성과가(results) 미미했다 (were meager)
examples.english: The results were meager compared to the budget.

rules.title: Comparative degree expressions
rules.description: Often used with adjectives expressing degree such as 크다, 작다, 많다, 적다, 높다, 낮다, 빠르다, 느리다 to highlight proportional differences.

examples.breakdown: 나이(age) + 에 비해 (compared to) 생각이(thought) 깊다 (is deep/mature)
examples.english: His thinking is mature for his age.

rules.title: Implied contrast without explicit subject
rules.description: When the subject is clear from context, it may be omitted, leaving only 에 비해 + predicate.

examples.breakdown: 작년(last year) + 에 비해 (compared to) 매출이(sales) 20% 늘었다 (increased by 20%)
examples.english: Sales increased by 20% compared to last year.

notes.title: 비해 vs 비해서
notes.text: Both forms are fully acceptable. 에 비해 is slightly more common in writing; 에 비해서 is neutral.

notes.title: Objective comparison
notes.text: Unlike 에 반해, 에 비해 is neutral and used for measurable or observable differences, not contradictions.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Frequently used with 과거(past), 예상(expectation), 다른 나라(other countries), 평균(average), 작년(last year).

explanation: A formal modifier that transforms a noun into an attributive phrase meaning 'regarding,' 'concerning,' or 'about.' It is ubiquitous in official documents, academic writing, news headlines, and formal speech. 에 대한 always modifies the noun that follows it.

rules.title: Noun + 에 대한 + Noun
rules.description: 에 대한 attaches to a noun and modifies the following noun. The structure is [topic noun] + 에 대한 + [modified noun].

examples.breakdown: 이 문제(this problem) + 에 대한 (regarding) 논의가(discussion) 필요하다 (is necessary)
examples.english: Discussion regarding this problem is necessary.

examples.korean: 학생들에 대한 교육 정책을 검토하고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 학생들에 대한 (regarding students) 교육 정책(education policy) + 를(object) 검토하고 있다 (are reviewing)
examples.english: They are reviewing education policies regarding students.

rules.title: 에 대한 as a nominalized phrase
rules.description: 에 대한 can modify abstract nouns like 견해(view), 입장(position), 조사(investigation), 보고(report), 결정(decision), and 의견(opinion).

examples.korean: 환경 보호에 대한 정부의 입장을 발표했다.
examples.breakdown: 환경 보호(environmental protection) + 에 대한 (regarding) 정부의(the government's) 입장을(position) 발표했다 (announced)
examples.english: The government announced its position regarding environmental protection.

notes.title: Must modify a noun
notes.text: 에 대한 cannot end a sentence. It must always precede and modify a noun. To end a sentence, use 에 대해(서) instead.

notes.title: Formal register
notes.text: This is the standard formal way to express 'about' or 'regarding' in writing and official contexts.

notes.title: Frequent modified nouns
notes.text: Commonly modifies 보고(report), 연구(research), 조사(investigation), 결정(decision), 입장(position), 의견(opinion), 관심(interest).

explanation: Similar in meaning to 에 대한, but 에 관한 is slightly more literary and often used when discussing abstract topics, theories, or areas of knowledge. It turns a noun into an attributive modifier meaning 'about,' 'concerning,' or 'on the subject of.' It appears frequently in academic titles, book names, and formal articles.

rules.title: Noun + 에 관한 + Noun
rules.description: 에 관한 attaches to a noun and modifies the following noun, creating an attributive phrase about the topic.

examples.breakdown: 역사(history) + 에 관한 (about) 책을(book) 읽고 있다 (am reading)
examples.english: I am reading a book about history.

examples.breakdown: 이 현상(this phenomenon) + 에 관한 (about) 연구가(research) 부족하다 (is insufficient)
examples.english: Research about this phenomenon is insufficient.

rules.title: 에 관한 in titles and headings
rules.description: Often used in the titles of papers, books, lectures, and presentations due to its elevated, academic tone.

examples.korean: 한국 문화에 관한 세미나가 열릴 예정이다.
examples.breakdown: 한국 문화(Korean culture) + 에 관한 (about) 세미나가(seminar) 열릴 예정이다 (is scheduled to be held)
examples.english: A seminar about Korean culture is scheduled to be held.

notes.title: 에 관한 vs 에 대한
notes.text: Both mean 'about,' but 에 관한 is more literary and academic, while 에 대한 is broader and more common in everyday formal contexts.

notes.title: Sentence-ending restriction
notes.text: Like 에 대한, 에 관한 cannot end a sentence. It must modify a following noun. Use 에 관해(서) for sentence endings.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Frequently modifies 연구(research), 논문(paper), 책(book), 강의(lecture), 세미나(seminar), 논의(discussion), 보고서(report).

explanation: A formal attributive expression meaning 'related to' or 'connected with.' It attaches to a noun with 에 관련된 and modifies a following noun. It is standard in academic, official, and journalistic writing to specify the domain or subject matter of the modified noun.

rules.title: Noun + 에 관련된 + Noun
rules.description: 에 관련 attaches to a noun, and 된 is the past participle of 되다, forming an adjectival modifier. The entire phrase modifies the noun that follows.

examples.korean: 이 사건에 관련된 사람들을 조사해야 한다.
examples.breakdown: 이 사건(this incident) + 에 관련된 (related to) 사람들을(people) 조사해야 한다 (must investigate)
examples.english: We must investigate the people related to this incident.

examples.breakdown: 환경(environment) + 에 관련된 (related to) 법률이(laws) 개정되었다 (were revised)
examples.english: Laws related to the environment have been revised.

rules.title: 에 관련된 with abstract topics
rules.description: Commonly used with policy, academic, and technical topics to specify what field or subject something belongs to.

examples.korean: 데이터 보안에 관련된 문제가 점점 심각해지고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 데이터 보안(data security) + 에 관련된 (related to) 문제가(problem) 점점 심각해지고 있다 (is becoming increasingly serious)
examples.english: Problems related to data security are becoming increasingly serious.

notes.title: Synonym: 와/과 관련된
notes.text: 와/과 관련된 is also common and often interchangeable, but 에 관련된 is preferred when emphasizing the domain or subject area.

notes.title: Attributive only
notes.text: 에 관련된 must modify a noun. For predicate usage, use 에 관련되다 or 에 관련하여.

notes.title: Frequent modified nouns
notes.text: Commonly modifies 문제(issue), 사건(incident), 법률(law), 정책(policy), 연구(research), 자료(data), 내용(content).

explanation: A formal modifier meaning 'corresponding to,' 'equivalent to,' or 'falling under.' It attaches to a noun with 에 해당하는 and modifies a following noun. It is used in legal, bureaucratic, academic, and formal written contexts to categorize or define membership in a group.

rules.title: Noun + 에 해당하는 + Noun
rules.description: 에 해당하다 means 'to correspond to' or 'to fall under.' The modifier form 에 해당하는 turns this into an attributive phrase.

examples.korean: 이 직위에 해당하는 자격을 갖추어야 한다.
examples.breakdown: 이 직위(this position) + 에 해당하는 (corresponding to) 자격을(qualifications) 갖추어야 한다 (must possess)
examples.english: You must possess the qualifications corresponding to this position.

examples.breakdown: 청년층(youth bracket) + 에 해당하는 (corresponding to) 연령대는(age range) 19세에서 34세이다 (is 19 to 34)
examples.english: The age range corresponding to the youth bracket is 19 to 34.

rules.title: Bureaucratic and legal usage
rules.description: Frequently appears in regulations, contracts, and official documents when defining who or what qualifies under a certain category.

examples.korean: 범죄에 해당하는 행위는 처벌받을 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 범죄(crime) + 에 해당하는 (corresponding to) 행위는(acts) 처벌받을 수 있다 (can be punished)
examples.english: Acts corresponding to crimes can be punished.

notes.title: Formal categorization
notes.text: 에 해당하는 is the standard formal way to express 'falling under' a category in legal and bureaucratic Korean.

notes.title: Predicate form
notes.text: As a predicate, use 에 해당하다 (e.g., 이것은 범죄에 해당한다 = This falls under a crime).

notes.title: Not for simple similarity
notes.text: Implies official or categorical correspondence, not just vague similarity. Use 비슷한 for simple resemblance.

explanation: A formal modifier meaning 'belonging to' or 'included in.' It attaches to a noun with 에 속하는 and modifies a following noun. It is used to express membership, classification, or inclusion within a group, organization, or category.

rules.title: Noun + 에 속하는 + Noun
rules.description: 에 속하다 means 'to belong to.' The modifier form 에 속하는 turns it into an attributive phrase indicating membership or classification.

examples.breakdown: 한국(Korea) + 에 속하는 (belonging to) 섬들이(islands) 많다 (are many)
examples.english: There are many islands belonging to Korea.

examples.breakdown: 그는(he, topic) 우리 팀(our team) + 에 속하는 (belonging to) 연구원이다 (is a researcher)
examples.english: He is a researcher belonging to our team.

rules.title: Classification and category
rules.description: Used to indicate that something falls within a larger class, type, or group, often in academic or descriptive writing.

examples.breakdown: 포유류(mammals) + 에 속하는 (belonging to) 동물은(animals) 젖을(milk) 먹인다 (feed)
examples.english: Animals belonging to mammals feed milk.

notes.title: Attributive only
notes.text: 에 속하는 must modify a noun. For predicate usage, use 에 속하다 (e.g., 이 섬은 한국에 속한다).

notes.title: Vs 에 포함되는
notes.text: Both indicate inclusion, but 에 속하는 implies membership in a defined group, while 에 포함되는 emphasizes being contained within a set.

notes.title: Common contexts
notes.text: Used with 국가(country), 팀(team), 조직(organization), 카테고리(category), 계(classification), 과목(subject).

explanation: A formal modifier meaning 'included in' or 'being part of.' It attaches to a noun with 에 포함되는 and modifies a following noun. It emphasizes that something is contained within or counted as part of a larger set, list, or group. Common in statistical, administrative, and descriptive contexts.

rules.title: Noun + 에 포함되는 + Noun
rules.description: 에 포함되다 is the passive of 포함하다 (to include). The modifier form 에 포함되는 indicates something that is passively included within a larger whole.

examples.breakdown: 이 목록(this list) + 에 포함되는 (included in) 항목을(items) 확인하세요 (please check)
examples.english: Please check the items included in this list.

examples.breakdown: 통계(statistics) + 에 포함되는 (included in) 인구는(population) 도시 거주자뿐이다 (is only city residents)
examples.english: The population included in the statistics consists only of city residents.

rules.title: Set membership and enumeration
rules.description: Used when defining what is counted within a set, survey, budget, plan, or category.

examples.breakdown: 예산(budget) + 에 포함되는 (included in) 비용을(costs) 다시(again) 검토했다 (reviewed)
examples.english: We reviewed again the costs included in the budget.

notes.title: Vs 에 속하는
notes.text: 에 포함되는 focuses on being counted or contained within a set, while 에 속하는 emphasizes group membership or affiliation.

notes.title: Passive construction
notes.text: Because it is the passive form of 포함하다, the included item is the subject or topic, not the agent doing the including.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Frequently used with 목록(list), 통계(statistics), 예산(budget), 계획(plan), 범위(range), 항목(item), 자료(data).

explanation: An advanced expression meaning 'beyond,' 'going past,' or 'transcending.' It attaches to a noun with 을/를 넘어서 and indicates that an action, state, or concept exceeds the original scope of the noun. It is used in formal writing, speeches, and essays to convey expansion, growth, or breaking of boundaries.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 넘어서
rules.description: Attach 를 넘어서 after vowel-ending nouns, 을 넘어서 after consonant-ending nouns. The phrase means going beyond the noun in scope, degree, or meaning.

examples.korean: 국경을 넘어서 전 세계적으로 문제가 되고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 국경(borders) + 을 넘어서 (beyond) 전 세계적으로(globally) 문제가(issue) 되고 있다 (is becoming)
examples.english: It is becoming an issue globally, beyond borders.

examples.korean: 개인의 이익을 넘어서 사회적 가치를 추구해야 한다.
examples.breakdown: 개인의 이익(personal gain) + 을 넘어서 (beyond) 사회적 가치(social value) + 를(object) 추구해야 한다 (must pursue)
examples.english: We must pursue social value beyond personal gain.

rules.title: 을/를 넘어서 as a sentence adverbial
rules.description: Often placed at the beginning of the main clause, the phrase sets up the idea of transcending a limit before stating the resulting action or state.

examples.korean: 언어의 장벽을 넘어서 서로 소통할 수 있었다.
examples.breakdown: 언어의 장벽(language barrier) + 을 넘어서 (beyond) 서로(each other) 소통할 수 있었다 (were able to communicate)
examples.english: We were able to communicate with each other beyond the language barrier.

notes.title: Written and rhetorical tone
notes.text: 을/를 넘어서 is common in editorials, speeches, and formal essays. It carries a sense of aspiration or overcoming limits.

notes.title: Vs 을/를 넘어
notes.text: 넘어서 emphasizes the act of going beyond and continuing forward. 넘어 is similar but slightly less emphatic about the onward progression.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Frequently used with 국경(border), 한계(limit), 장벽(barrier), 수준(level), 개인(personal), 시대(era), 지역(region).

notes.title: Not for physical crossing only
notes.text: While it can describe physical crossing, at Level 5-6 it is tested most often in abstract, metaphorical contexts.

explanation: A versatile and widely used expression meaning 'through' or 'by means of.' It attaches to a noun with 을/를 통해 to indicate a medium, channel, process, or means by which something is accomplished. It is standard in both formal and informal contexts.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 통해
rules.description: Attach 를 통해 after vowel-ending nouns, 을 통해 after consonant-ending nouns. The phrase introduces the means or medium through which an action occurs.

examples.korean: 인터넷을 통해 많은 정보를 얻을 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 인터넷(internet) + 을 통해 (through) 많은 정보를(much information) 얻을 수 있다 (can obtain)
examples.english: You can obtain a lot of information through the internet.

examples.korean: 그는 친구를 통해 그 소식을 들었다.
examples.breakdown: 그는(he, topic) 친구(friend) + 를 통해 (through) 그 소식을(that news) 들었다 (heard)
examples.english: He heard that news through a friend.

rules.title: Process or experience as a medium
rules.description: Can describe going through an experience, process, or period that brings about change or results.

examples.korean: 이번 프로젝트를 통해 많은 것을 배웠다.
examples.breakdown: 이번 프로젝트(this project) + 를 통해 (through) 많은 것을(many things) 배웠다 (learned)
examples.english: I learned a lot through this project.

notes.title: Neutral register
notes.text: 을/를 통해 is acceptable in formal writing, business emails, and everyday conversation. It is one of the most common medium markers.

notes.title: Vs 을/를 통하여
notes.text: 통해 and 통하여 are interchangeable in meaning. 통해 is slightly more common in modern Korean; 통하여 sounds more formal and literary.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Used with 매체(media), 인터넷(internet), 친구(friend), 경험(experience), 교육(education), 협력(cooperation), 대화(conversation).

explanation: A formal variant of 을/를 통해 with an identical core meaning of 'through' or 'by means of.' The ending 여서 adds a slightly more literary, deliberate, and elevated tone. It is common in official documents, formal speeches, news reports, and academic prose.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 통하여
rules.description: Attach 를 통하여 after vowel-ending nouns, 을 통하여 after consonant-ending nouns. Functions identically to 을/를 통해 but with a more elevated register.

examples.breakdown: 정부는(government, topic) 법률(laws) + 을 통하여 (through) 시장을(market) 규제한다 (regulates)
examples.english: The government regulates the market through laws.

examples.korean: 이 프로그램을 통하여 청년들의 취업률이 높아졌다.
examples.breakdown: 이 프로그램(this program) + 을 통하여 (through) 청년들의(youth) 취업률이(employment rate) 높아졌다 (rose)
examples.english: The youth employment rate rose through this program.

rules.title: Formal documents and announcements
rules.description: Because of its elevated tone, 통하여 appears frequently in public announcements, policy papers, and written reports.

examples.breakdown: 공식 채널(official channel) + 을 통하여 (through) 입장을(position) 밝혔다 (clarified)
examples.english: They clarified their position through an official channel.

notes.title: Formal register
notes.text: 통하여 is slightly more formal and literary than 통해. Choose 통하여 for official writing, policy documents, and academic essays.

notes.title: Fully interchangeable
notes.text: In terms of grammar and meaning, 통하여 and 통해 are fully interchangeable. The choice is stylistic, not semantic.

notes.title: Common contexts
notes.text: Frequently used in 정부(government), 법률(law), 정책(policy), 교육(education), 공식(official), 국제(international) contexts.

explanation: A formal expression meaning 'via,' 'passing through,' or 'by way of.' It attaches to a noun with 을/를 거쳐서 and indicates an intermediate location, stage, or process that something passes through before reaching a final state. It is standard in formal writing and detailed descriptions.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 거쳐서
rules.description: Attach 를 거쳐서 after vowel-ending nouns, 을 거쳐서 after consonant-ending nouns. Indicates an intermediate step, place, or process.

examples.breakdown: 그는(he, topic) 프랑스(France) + 를 거쳐서 (via) 영국에(Britain) 도착했다 (arrived)
examples.english: He arrived in Britain via France.

examples.korean: 이 문제는 여러 단계를 거쳐서 해결되었다.
examples.breakdown: 이 문제(this problem) + 는(topic) 여러 단계(various stages) + 를 거쳐서 (through) 해결되었다 (was resolved)
examples.english: This problem was resolved through various stages.

rules.title: Process and progression
rules.description: Used to describe sequential processes, bureaucratic procedures, or developmental stages that an entity must pass through.

examples.korean: 제안은 검토 위원회를 거쳐서 최종 승인을 받았다.
examples.breakdown: 제안(proposal) + 는(topic) 검토 위원회(review committee) + 를 거쳐서 (through) 최종 승인을(final approval) 받았다 (received)
examples.english: The proposal received final approval through the review committee.

notes.title: Vs 을/를 통해
notes.text: 거쳐서 emphasizes passing through an intermediate point or stage. 통해 emphasizes the medium or means itself without the sense of sequential progression.

notes.title: Formal register
notes.text: 거쳐서 is formal and common in written descriptions of processes, travel routes, and administrative procedures.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Used with 도시(city), 국가(country), 단계(stage), 과정(process), 위원회(committee), 검토(review), 협상(negotiation).

explanation: A slightly more concise variant of 을/를 거쳐서, meaning 'passing through' or 'via.' The removal of 서 gives it a lighter, more direct feel while retaining the same core meaning of an intermediate point or process. It is common in written and formal spoken Korean.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 거쳐
rules.description: Attach 를 거쳐 after vowel-ending nouns, 을 거쳐 after consonant-ending nouns. Functions identically to 거쳐서 but with a slightly smoother, less explicit sequential tone.

examples.korean: 우리 마을은 산을 거쳐 들어가야 한다.
examples.breakdown: 우리 마을(our village) + 는(topic) 산(mountain) + 을 거쳐 (passing through) 들어가야 한다 (must enter)
examples.english: You must enter our village passing through the mountain.

examples.korean: 그는 작은 회사를 거쳐 대기업에 취업했다.
examples.breakdown: 그는(he, topic) 작은 회사(small company) + 를 거쳐 (via) 대기업에(large corporation) 취업했다 (got a job)
examples.english: He got a job at a large corporation via a small company.

rules.title: Spatial and procedural passing
rules.description: Used for both physical routes and abstract procedures, often implying that the intermediate noun is a necessary or formative stage.

examples.korean: 이 길을 거쳐 정상에 오를 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 이 길(this path) + 을 거쳐 (via) 정상에(summit) 오를 수 있다 (can climb)
examples.english: You can reach the summit via this path.

notes.title: 거쳐 vs 거쳐서
notes.text: 거쳐서 emphasizes the sequential nature of passing through. 거쳐 is more neutral and slightly more common in spatial descriptions.

notes.title: Same register
notes.text: Both forms are formal and appropriate for written Korean. The choice depends on whether you want to highlight the step-by-step process (거쳐서) or the path itself (거쳐).

notes.title: Common contexts
notes.text: Used with 여행(travel), 이사(relocation), 경력(career), 교육(education), 협력(cooperation), 중계(relay).

explanation: A formal expression meaning 'instead of' or 'in place of.' It attaches to a noun with 을/를 대신하여 and indicates substitution or replacement. The subject of the main clause performs an action on behalf of or in place of the noun. It is common in official writing, formal speeches, and polite requests.

rules.title: Noun + 을/를 대신하여
rules.description: Attach 를 대신하여 after vowel-ending nouns, 을 대신하여 after consonant-ending nouns. The main clause describes what is done in place of the noun.

examples.breakdown: 부장님(boss) + 을 대신하여 (instead of) 회의에(meeting) 참석했다 (attended)
examples.english: I attended the meeting instead of the boss.

examples.breakdown: 현금(cash) + 을 대신하여 (instead of) 카드로(by card) 결제했습니다 (paid)
examples.english: I paid by card instead of cash.

rules.title: Substitution of method or agent
rules.description: Can indicate replacing a person, object, method, or approach with another. Often used in official contexts to express delegation or alternative means.

examples.breakdown: 전통 방식(traditional method) + 을 대신하여 (instead of) 신기술을(new technology) 도입했다 (introduced)
examples.english: They introduced new technology instead of the traditional method.

notes.title: Formal register
notes.text: 대신하여 is formal and common in business writing, official documents, and speeches. In casual speech, use 대신에 or 대신.

notes.title: Vs 대신에
notes.text: 대신에 is neutral and used in everyday conversation. 대신하여 is elevated and appropriate for formal contexts.

notes.title: Subject must be explicit
notes.text: When using 대신하여 to indicate substitution of a person, it is clearest when the subject doing the replacement is explicitly stated.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Used with 대표(representative), 부장(manager), 동료(colleague), 현금(cash), 전통(tradition), 구식 방식(old method), 직접(directly).

explanation: A formal expression indicating substitution or replacement. N-을/를 대신해 states that someone or something acts in place of another. It is commonly used in written and formal contexts to describe delegation, proxy, or alternative action. The form derives from 대신 (代身, 'substitute') combined with the adverbial 하다 conjugation -해.

rules.title: Basic substitution structure
rules.description: Attach 을/를 to the noun representing the original entity, then add 대신해 to indicate replacement.

examples.korean: 회의에 참석하지 못한 동료를 대신해 발표를 맡았다.
examples.breakdown: 회의(meeting)에 참석(attend)하지 못한(unable) 동료(colleague)를 대신해(in place of) 발표(presentation)를 맡았다(took charge).
examples.english: I took charge of the presentation in place of a colleague who could not attend the meeting.
I used the bus instead of the train.

rules.title: Emotional or abstract replacement
rules.description: Used when substituting abstract entities such as feelings, words, or concepts in literary or rhetorical contexts.

examples.english: I showed through action instead of words.

notes.title: Written nuance
notes.text: 대신해 is the adverbial form (대신하다 → 대신하여/대신해). In highly formal documents, 대신하여 is preferred; 대신에 is more colloquial.

notes.title: Contrast with -는 대신에
notes.text: The clausal connector -는 대신에 (doing A instead of B) applies to verbs, whereas N-을/를 대신해 applies strictly to nouns.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Frequently appears with verbs like 맡다, 대표하다, 출석하다, 수행하다, and 이용하다.

explanation: A formal noun-modifying expression meaning 'for the purpose of' or 'intended for.' N-을/를 위한 attaches directly before another noun to qualify it. It is ubiquitous in official titles, product descriptions, and formal rhetoric. The root noun 위해 (爲) carries a classical Sino-Korean register.

rules.title: Noun modification
rules.description: Place 을/를 위한 directly before the noun it modifies to specify the intended recipient or purpose.

examples.english: A scholarship for students has been prepared.

examples.breakdown: 환자(patients)를 위한(for) 특별(special) 식단(diet)이 제공된다(is provided).
examples.english: A special diet is provided for patients.

rules.title: Abstract and institutional targets
rules.description: Used with abstract beneficiaries such as 국가, 민족, 미래, 평화 to elevate register in speeches and policy documents.

examples.breakdown: 국가(nation)를 위한(for) 헌신(devotion)은 국민의 의무이다(the citizen's duty).
examples.english: Devotion for the nation is the duty of the citizen.

notes.title: Register and tone
notes.text: Compared to -에게/한테, 위한 is more formal and objective. It is the standard form in public signage, legal texts, and official announcements.

notes.title: Position in the sentence
notes.text: As a noun modifier, it must appear directly before the modified noun and cannot stand alone as a predicate.

notes.title: Not for verbs
notes.text: To express 'for' with a verbal clause, use the clausal form -기 위해 or -도록. N-을/를 위한 requires a preceding noun.

explanation: The adverbial form of 위하다, used to indicate purpose or beneficiary when followed by a verb clause. N-을/를 위해서 expresses 'for the sake of' and sets up the subsequent action as being motivated by the noun. It is essential in formal expository and persuasive prose.

rules.title: Purpose-driven action
rules.description: Attach 을/를 위해서 before a clause to show that the action is carried out for the noun's benefit.

examples.breakdown: 아이들(children)를 위해서(for the sake of) 희생(sacrifice)를 감수했다(endured).
examples.english: I endured sacrifice for the sake of the children.

examples.breakdown: 건강(health)를 위해서(for the sake of) 매일(every day) 운동한다(exercise).
examples.english: I exercise every day for my health.

rules.title: Formal and abstract beneficiaries
rules.description: Used with abstract nouns to construct elevated, persuasive sentences in essays, speeches, and editorials.

examples.korean: 민주주의를 위해서 싸웠던 선열들을 기억해야 한다.
examples.breakdown: 민주주의(democracy)를 위해서(for the sake of) 싸웠던(who fought) 선열들(forefathers)를 기억해야 한다(must remember).
examples.english: We must remember the forefathers who fought for democracy.

notes.title: -서 contraction
notes.text: 위해서 is the full adverbial form. In rapid speech it may shorten, but in formal writing the full form is expected. Do not confuse with 위해, which modifies nouns when followed by a noun.

notes.title: Clause-level usage
notes.text: Unlike 위한, which modifies a noun, 위해서 introduces an entire predicate clause. It answers the adverbial question 'for what purpose?'

notes.title: Synonym: -을/를 위해
notes.text: In modern usage, -을/를 위해 (without 서) is also common and functionally identical in most contexts. The addition of -서 is slightly more formal and explicit.

explanation: A formal noun-modifying expression stating that something is directed toward a specific goal or target. N-을/를 목적으로 한 derives from 목적 (目的, 'goal/purpose') combined with the adnominal form of 하다. It is standard in news reports, academic writing, and policy descriptions to frame intentions objectively.

rules.title: Objective description of purpose
rules.description: Place before a noun to describe a plan, project, or action as being aimed at the stated goal.

examples.korean: 외국인을 목적으로 한 한국어 교육 프로그램이 개발되었다.
examples.breakdown: 외국인(foreigners)를 목적으로 한(aimed at) 한국어 교육(Korean language education) 프로그램(program)이 개발되었다(was developed).
examples.english: A Korean language education program aimed at foreigners was developed.

examples.korean: 청소년을 목적으로 한 예산은 증가 추세이다.
examples.breakdown: 청소년(youth)를 목적으로 한(aimed at) 예산(budget)은 증가(increase) 추세이다(trend).
examples.english: The budget aimed at youth is on an increasing trend.

rules.title: Framing policy and research goals
rules.description: Common in institutional writing to introduce the target demographic or objective of an initiative without subjective evaluation.

examples.korean: 지역 균형 발전을 목적으로 한 정책이 시행 중이다.
examples.breakdown: 지역 균형 발전(regional balanced development)를 목적으로 한(aimed at) 정책(policy)이 시행 중이다(is being implemented).
examples.english: A policy aimed at regional balanced development is being implemented.

notes.title: Register elevation
notes.text: 목적으로 한 elevates register significantly compared to -려고 하는 or -기 위한. It removes the speaker's subjectivity, making it ideal for journalism and reports.

notes.title: Noun-modifier only
notes.text: This form must directly precede a noun. To use it as a predicate, rephrase with -을/를 목적으로 하다 or -을/를 목적으로 한다.

notes.title: Related forms
notes.text: -을/를 목적으로 (adverbial), -을/를 목적으로 하는 (present adnominal), and -을/를 목적으로 한 (past/perfect adnominal) all share the same root but differ in grammatical role.

explanation: A formal expression denoting that something is constructed, analyzed, or developed upon a certain foundation or source material. N-을/를 바탕으로 한 derives from 바탕 (base/foundation). It is prevalent in academic, artistic, and scientific writing to acknowledge underlying principles or empirical data.

rules.title: Foundational basis
rules.description: Indicates that the modified noun rests upon or derives from the referenced base. It implies depth, legitimacy, and methodological grounding.

examples.korean: 실제 사례를 바탕으로 한 논문이 발표되었다.
examples.breakdown: 실제(actual) 사례(cases)를 바탕으로 한(based on) 논문(thesis)이 발표되었다(was presented).
examples.english: A thesis based on actual cases was presented.

examples.korean: 민속 신화를 바탕으로 한 뮤지컬이 큰 인기를 끌었다.
examples.breakdown: 민속 신화(folk mythology)를 바탕으로 한(based on) 뮤지컬(musical)이 큰 인기를 끌었다(gained great popularity).
examples.english: A musical based on folk mythology gained great popularity.

rules.title: Methodological grounding in research
rules.description: Used to state that a study, theory, or framework is built upon specific data, theories, or observations.

examples.korean: 설문 조사를 바탕으로 한 통계 분석이 수행되었다.
examples.breakdown: 설문 조사(survey)를 바탕으로 한(based on) 통계(statistical) 분석(analysis)이 수행되었다(was conducted).
examples.english: A statistical analysis based on a survey was conducted.

notes.title: 바탕 vs. 기초
notes.text: 바탕 implies a broad, often qualitative foundation such as culture, experience, or observation. 기초 is more technical and structural. Both are advanced but carry slightly different connotations.

notes.title: Adverbial counterpart
notes.text: The clausal form -을/를 바탕으로 (without 한) functions adverbially: 이理論을 바탕으로 새로운 모델을 제안한다.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Frequently paired with 사례, 경험, 데이터, 이론, 신화, 전통, 현실, and 사실.

explanation: A formal expression indicating that something is founded upon a specific principle, data set, or structural element. N-을/를 기초로 한 derives from 기초 (基礎, 'basis/foundation'). It is heavily favored in scientific, engineering, and theoretical discourse where precision and structural dependency are emphasized.

rules.title: Structural or theoretical foundation
rules.description: Used when the modified entity is constructed upon a defined framework, dataset, or theoretical model.

examples.korean: 최신 연구를 기초로 한 임상 지침서가 발간되었다.
examples.breakdown: 최신(latest) 연구(research)를 기초로 한(based on) 임상(clinical) 지침서(guideline)가 발간되었다(was published).
examples.english: A clinical guideline based on the latest research was published.

examples.korean: 통계 모델을 기초로 한 예측 시스템을 개발했다.
examples.breakdown: 통계(statistical) 모델(model)을 기초로 한(based on) 예측(prediction) 시스템(system)를 개발했다(developed).
examples.english: We developed a prediction system based on a statistical model.

rules.title: Policy and institutional frameworks
rules.description: Frequently appears in legal and administrative texts to anchor decisions in codified principles or agreements.

examples.korean: 헌법을 기초로 한 법률 개정안이 제출되었다.
examples.breakdown: 헌법(constitution)를 기초로 한(based on) 법률(law) 개정안(amendment)이 제출되었다(was submitted).
examples.english: A legal amendment based on the constitution was submitted.

notes.title: 기초 vs. 바탕 nuance
notes.text: 기초 is more technical and implies a defined starting point or structural groundwork. 바탕 is broader and more organic. In scientific writing, 기초 is generally preferred.

notes.title: Conjugation stability
notes.text: 기초 ends in a vowel, so 로 is used regardless of the noun's batchim. Compare 사례를 바탕으로 한 where 으로 appears because 바탕 has batchim.

notes.title: Related academic collocations
notes.text: 기초 연구 (basic research), 기초 자료 (basic data), 기초 통계 (basic statistics), and 기초 이론 (basic theory) frequently precede this form.

explanation: A highly formal expression meaning 'on the premise of' or 'predicated upon.' N-을/를 전제로 한 derives from 전제 (前提, 'premise/assumption'). It is used in logic, law, diplomacy, and advanced academic writing to indicate that a proposition or action logically depends on a stated condition being true.

rules.title: Logical and conditional dependency
rules.description: Indicates that the modified noun is valid only if the stated premise holds. It signals a strict conditional relationship.

examples.korean: 상호 신뢰를 전제로 한 협상이 시작되었다.
examples.breakdown: 상호(mutual) 신뢰(trust)를 전제로 한(on the premise of) 협상(negotiation)이 시작되었다(began).
examples.english: Negotiations on the premise of mutual trust have begun.

examples.korean: 경제 성장을 전제로 한 정책은 위험할 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 경제 성장(economic growth)를 전제로 한(predicated on) 정책(policy)은 위험할 수 있다(can be dangerous).
examples.english: A policy predicated on economic growth can be dangerous.

rules.title: Academic and logical argumentation
rules.description: Used in philosophy and theory to ground an argument in an accepted axiom or hypothesis.

examples.korean: 인간의 이성을 전제로 한 윤리 체계를 검토하겠다.
examples.breakdown: 인간의(human) 이성(reason)를 전제로 한(on the premise of) 윤리(ethics) 체계(system)를 검토하겠다(will examine).
examples.english: I will examine an ethical system on the premise of human reason.

notes.title: Strong conditional force
notes.text: 전제로 한 implies that if the premise fails, the entire dependent proposition collapses. It is stronger than 단순히 조건으로 한.

notes.title: Academic register
notes.text: This form is almost exclusively found in formal essays, legal contracts, diplomatic statements, and logical treatises. It is rarely used in conversation.

notes.title: Conjugation
notes.text: 전제 has batchim (ㅈ), so 으로 is required: 전제로. The adnominal 한 derives from 하다.

explanation: A formal expression indicating that something is established contingent upon a specific requirement being met. N-을/를 조건으로 한 derives from 조건 (條件, 'condition'). It is standard in legal agreements, contracts, policy documents, and formal proposals to enumerate prerequisites.

rules.title: Contingent arrangements
rules.description: Describes a noun as being valid, offered, or executed only under the stated condition.

examples.korean: 성과급 지급을 조건으로 한 근무 계약을 체결했다.
examples.breakdown: 성과급(performance pay) 지급(payment)를 조건으로 한(on the condition of) 근무(work) 계약(contract)를 체결했다(concluded).
examples.english: We concluded a work contract on the condition of performance pay.

examples.breakdown: 익명(anonymity)를 조건으로 한(on the condition of) 인터뷰(interview)가 진행되었다(was conducted).
examples.english: An interview on the condition of anonymity was conducted.

rules.title: Policy and diplomatic conditions
rules.description: Used in international relations and corporate governance to frame conditional support, aid, or agreements.

examples.korean: 비핵화를 조건으로 한 경제 지원안이 논의되고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 비핵화(denuclearization)를 조건으로 한(on the condition of) 경제(economic) 지원안(aid plan)이 논의되고 있다(is being discussed).
examples.english: An economic aid plan on the condition of denuclearization is being discussed.

notes.title: Legal precision
notes.text: 조건으로 한 is more precise and contractual than 단순 조건. It explicitly frames the modified noun as contingent.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Frequently paired with 동의, 승인, 합의, 비밀 유지, 실명 공개, 규정 준수, and similar institutional terms.

notes.title: Predicate form
notes.text: As a main predicate, use -을/를 조건으로 하다. As an adverbial modifier within a clause, use -을/를 조건으로.

explanation: A formal expression meaning 'by means of' or 'using as a method.' N-을/를 수단으로 한 derives from 수단 (手段, 'means/method'). It is used in critical analysis, policy evaluation, and academic writing to describe the instrumentality of an approach—often with a tone of scrutiny or instrumentalization.

rules.title: Instrumental method or approach
rules.description: Frames the modified noun as an action or strategy carried out through the stated means.

examples.korean: 언어를 수단으로 한 문화 교류가 활발해지고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 언어(language)를 수단으로 한(by means of) 문화(cultural) 교류(exchange)가 활발해지고 있다(is becoming active).
examples.english: Cultural exchange by means of language is becoming more active.

examples.korean: 기술을 수단으로 한 감시 체계에 대한 우려가 커지고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 기술(technology)를 수단으로 한(by means of) 감시(surveillance) 체계(system)에 대한(concerning) 우려(concerns)가 커지고 있다(are growing).
examples.english: Concerns about a surveillance system by means of technology are growing.

rules.title: Critical and evaluative usage
rules.description: Often appears in critical discourse when analyzing how power, control, or influence is exercised through specific channels.

examples.korean: 미디어를 수단으로 한 정치 선전이 확산되고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 미디어(media)를 수단으로 한(by means of) 정치(political) 선전(propaganda)이 확산되고 있다(is spreading).
examples.english: Political propaganda by means of media is spreading.

notes.title: Connotation of instrumentalization
notes.text: 수단으로 한 can carry a critical nuance, especially when the means is seen as reducing something to a tool. Context determines whether the tone is neutral or evaluative.

notes.title: Academic and journalistic register
notes.text: This form is standard in sociology, media studies, political science, and investigative journalism. It is rarely used in everyday speech.

notes.title: Conjugation
notes.text: 수단 has batchim (ㄴ), so 으로 is required. The full form is 수단으로 한.

notes.title: Related expression
notes.text: Compare with -을/를 통해, which is more neutral and common in general contexts. 수단으로 한 is more formal and emphasizes instrumental agency.

explanation: A formal written connective that negates the preceding noun and presents an alternative. Common in academic Korean, editorials, and rhetorical speech to reframe a concept by explicit contrast.

rules.title: Basic Contrastive Structure
rules.description: Attach the subject particle 이/가 to the noun being negated, followed by 아닌, then state the alternative noun or clause.

examples.breakdown: 이것이 실패가 아닌 (not a failure) 성공의 시작이다 (the beginning of success).
examples.english: This is not a failure but the beginning of success.

rules.title: Reframing with Nominalized Clauses
rules.description: Can be used with 것 or other nominalizers to contrast entire situations rather than single nouns.

examples.breakdown: 해결이 아닌 (not a solution) 새로운 도전의 출발점이다 (a starting point for a new challenge).
examples.english: It is not a solution but a starting point for a new challenge.

rules.title: Identifying Role or Nature
rules.description: Often used to clarify that someone or something is not what was assumed, but something else.

examples.breakdown: 그는 친구가 아닌 (not as a friend) 동료로서 (as a colleague) 왔다 (came).
examples.english: He came not as a friend but as a colleague.

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: This structure is far more common in written Korean and formal speeches than in casual conversation.

notes.title: Particle Choice
notes.text: Use 이 after consonants and 가 after vowels, following standard subject particle rules.

notes.title: Spoken Alternative
notes.text: In spoken Korean, N-이/가 아니라 is more common for the same meaning.

notes.title: Common Mistake
notes.text: Do not omit the subject particle before 아닌; 이/가 is grammatically required.

explanation: A formal expression meaning 'including' or 'not excluding.' Used to indicate that the preceding noun is part of a larger group, total, or set.

rules.title: Including in a Total
rules.description: Attach 을/를 to the noun, followed by 포함하여 to indicate inclusion in a count or group.

examples.breakdown: 학생들을 포함하여 (including students) 100명이 참석했다 (100 people attended).
examples.english: One hundred people attended, including the students.

rules.title: Listing Multiple Items
rules.description: Often used with totals or comprehensive lists in formal contexts.

examples.breakdown: 서울을 포함하여 (including Seoul) 5개 도시를 (5 cities) 방문할 예정이다 (plan to visit).
examples.english: We plan to visit five cities, including Seoul.

rules.title: People, Places, and Objects
rules.description: Can be used flexibly with people, locations, or abstract items.

examples.breakdown: 원고를 포함하여 (including the manuscript) 모두 10명의 연구자가 (all 10 researchers) 참여했다 (participated).
examples.english: All ten researchers participated, including the author of the manuscript.

notes.title: Written Style
notes.text: This form is characteristic of formal documents, reports, and official announcements.

notes.title: Particle Selection
notes.text: Use 을 after consonants and 를 after vowels.

notes.title: Adnominal Form
notes.text: N-을/를 포함한 modifies nouns directly: 학생들을 포함한 그룹 (a group including students).

explanation: Expresses possibility or uncertainty about a future event or state. It suggests something 'might' happen without strong commitment, and is common in both spoken and written Korean.

rules.title: Expressing Uncertainty
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ지도 모르다 to adjectives or verbs to indicate that something is possible but not certain.

examples.english: It might rain.

rules.title: Warning or Precaution
rules.description: Often used to express concern about a possible negative outcome, especially in instructions.

examples.breakdown: 늦게 도착할지도 모르니 (since I might arrive late) 미리 연락하세요 (please contact in advance).
examples.english: I might arrive late, so please contact me in advance.

rules.title: Casual Contraction
rules.description: In spoken Korean, this often contracts to -(으)ㄹ지 몰라요 with the same meaning.

examples.breakdown: 그가 거짓말을 하고 있을지도 (might be lying) 모른다.
examples.english: He might be lying.

notes.title: Probability Level
notes.text: This form implies roughly 30-50% probability—more tentative than -(으)ㄹ 것 같다.

notes.title: Conjugation
notes.text: 모르다 conjugates normally: 모른다, 몰라요, 몰랐다.

notes.title: Common Error
notes.text: Do not confuse with -(으)ㄹ지 모르겠다, which expresses the speaker's own uncertainty about a decision.

explanation: Expresses a strong assumption or expectation about a present or future situation that contrasts with reality. Carries a tone of regret, disappointment, or surprise.

rules.title: Unmet Present Expectation
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ 터인데 to express what should logically be happening now, contrasted with what is actually happening.

examples.korean: 그는 곧 도착할 터인데 연락이 없다.
examples.breakdown: 그는 곧 도착할 터인데 (he should be arriving soon, but) 연락이 없다 (there is no contact).
examples.english: He should be arriving soon, but there is no contact.

rules.title: Regret or Disappointment
rules.description: Often used when the speaker feels something should be different from how it actually is.

examples.korean: 지금쯤 집에 있을 터인데 아무도 대답이 없다.
examples.breakdown: 지금쯤 집에 있을 터인데 (he should be home by now, but) 아무도 대답이 없다 (no one answers).
examples.english: He should be home by now, but no one is answering.

rules.title: Future-Oriented Assumption
rules.description: Can project an expected future outcome that is already showing signs of deviation.

examples.korean: 이번 달 말에는 프로젝트가 끝날 터인데 일정이 밀릴 것 같다.
examples.breakdown: 이번 달 말에는 (by the end of this month) 프로젝트가 끝날 터인데 (the project should finish, but) 일정이 밀릴 것 같다 (the schedule seems delayed).
examples.english: The project should be finished by the end of this month, but the schedule seems to be slipping.

notes.title: Etymology
notes.text: 터 comes from 의지나 방향의 근거 (basis/direction), giving the sense of 'bound to' or 'supposed to.'

notes.title: Contraction
notes.text: This is a contraction of -(으)ㄹ 터이다 + ㄴ/는데, where 터이다 expresses strong assumption.

notes.title: Nuanced Tone
notes.text: The second clause always contrasts with the expectation, often implying disappointment or concern.

notes.title: Register
notes.text: Common in both spoken and written Korean, but carries emotional weight.

explanation: Expresses the speaker's assumption, intention, or guess, followed by a contrasting clause. Used to soften statements, express reservations, or politely explain limitations.

rules.title: Speaker Intention with Contrast
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ 테지만 to express what the speaker intends or assumes, followed by a caveat.

examples.breakdown: 내일 갈 테지만 (I would go tomorrow, but) 날씨가 걱정된다 (I'm worried about the weather).
examples.english: I would go tomorrow, but I'm worried about the weather.

rules.title: Polite Softening
rules.description: Frequently used to politely soften a refusal or to mention a limitation.

examples.korean: 제가 직접 하겠지만 시간이 꽤 걸릴 것 같습니다.
examples.breakdown: 제가 직접 하겠지만 (I will do it myself, but) 시간이 꽤 걸릴 것 같습니다 (it will take quite a while).
examples.english: I will do it myself, but I think it will take quite a while.

rules.title: Assumption About Others
rules.description: Can also be used when making assumptions about other people's knowledge or situations.

examples.korean: 그 사람도 알고 있을 테지만 일단 말해두겠다.
examples.breakdown: 그 사람도 알고 있을 테지만 (he probably knows too, but) 일단 말해두겠다 (I'll mention it just in case).
examples.english: He probably knows too, but I'll mention it just in case.

notes.title: Softening Device
notes.text: 테 is a highly versatile ending that softens assertive statements, making them less direct.

notes.title: Contraction
notes.text: This is a contraction of -(으)ㄹ 터이다 + 지만.

notes.title: Related Forms
notes.text: Related forms include -(으)ㄹ 텐데 and -(으)ㄹ 테니(까).

notes.title: Subject Limitation
notes.text: When expressing intention, the subject is usually the speaker. For others, it expresses conjecture.

explanation: Expresses a counterfactual assumption about a past situation. Strongly implies that the expected outcome did not occur, often carrying regret, sympathy, or criticism.

rules.title: Past Unmet Expectation
rules.description: Attach 았/었/였을 텐데 to verbs or adjectives to express a past assumption that was contradicted by reality.

examples.korean: 일찍 출발했을 텐데 교통 체증 때문에 늦었다.
examples.breakdown: 일찍 출발했을 텐데 (he must have left early, but) 교통 체증 때문에 (because of traffic) 늦었다 (was late).
examples.english: He must have left early, but he was late because of traffic.

rules.title: Regret About Others
rules.description: Frequently used to express regret or criticism about someone else's past actions or inaction.

examples.korean: 미리 알았을 텐데 왜 말하지 않았어?
examples.breakdown: 미리 알았을 텐데 (you must have known in advance, but) 왜 말하지 않았어 (why didn't you say anything?).
examples.english: You must have known in advance, so why didn't you say anything?

rules.title: Past State Assumption
rules.description: Can be used with adjectives to describe a past state that is being reflected upon.

examples.korean: 그때는 힘들었을 텐데 잘 견뎌 줘서 고맙다.
examples.breakdown: 그때는 힘들었을 텐데 (it must have been hard then, but) 잘 견뎌 줘서 고맙다 (thank you for enduring it well).
examples.english: It must have been hard back then, so thank you for enduring it well.

notes.title: Past Counterfactual
notes.text: This is the past-tense counterpart of -(으)ㄹ 터인데, used exclusively for retrospection.

notes.title: Tense Selection
notes.text: Use 았 for ㅏ/ㅗ stems, 었 for other vowels, and 였 for 하다 verbs.

notes.title: Emotional Weight
notes.text: Often carries strong connotation of regret, sympathy, or criticism depending on context.

notes.title: Formal Variant
notes.text: In very formal writing, 았/었/였을 터인데 is sometimes preferred over 았/었/였을 텐데.

explanation: Indicates the degree or extent of an action or state. It can express intensity, comparison, or a standard against which something is measured.

rules.title: Intensity of Action
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ 정도(로) to describe how far an action or state progresses.

examples.breakdown: 배가 아플 정도로 (to the extent that my stomach hurt) 웃었다 (I laughed).
examples.english: I laughed so hard my stomach hurt.

rules.title: Comparison of States
rules.description: Used to compare one situation with another, often to emphasize how extreme the first situation is.

examples.breakdown: 잠이 올 정도로 (to the extent of falling asleep) 지루했다 (it was boring).
examples.english: It was so boring I could have fallen asleep.

rules.title: Hyperbole and Vivid Description
rules.description: 정도(로) can be shortened to 정도 when the modifying context is clear, and is often used for hyperbole.

examples.breakdown: 목이 쉴 정도로 (to the extent of losing my voice) 외쳤다 (I shouted).
examples.english: I shouted until I was hoarse.

notes.title: Particle Option
notes.text: Add 로 when the phrase modifies an adjective or verb. Without 로, it modifies a noun: 그 정도 (that degree).

notes.title: Intensity Marker
notes.text: This grammar is frequently used for hyperbole and vivid description in both speech and writing.

notes.title: Related Forms
notes.text: N-정도 means 'approximately' with numbers: 한 시간 정도 (about one hour).

notes.title: Common Error
notes.text: Do not use this form for positive desire; use -(으)ㄹ 정도로 only for describing actual extent.

explanation: Describes an extreme situation where someone is on the verge of a physical or emotional limit. Usually implies a negative or overwhelming state.

rules.title: Physical Extremity
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ 지경이다 to express being on the verge of a physical breakdown.

examples.breakdown: 배고파서 (because I'm so hungry) 쓰러질 지경이다 (I'm on the verge of collapsing).
examples.english: I'm so hungry I'm on the verge of collapsing.

rules.title: Emotional Overwhelm
rules.description: Often used with emotional states like anger or sadness to express reaching a breaking point.

examples.breakdown: 화가 나서 (because I was so angry) 울 지경이었다 (I was on the verge of tears).
examples.english: I was so angry I was on the verge of tears.

rules.title: Cognitive or Sensory Limits
rules.description: Can be used with cognitive or sensory states indicating disorientation.

examples.breakdown: 너무 졸려서 (because I was so sleepy) 눈앞이 깜깜해질 지경이었다 (my vision was going dark).
examples.english: I was so sleepy my vision was going dark.

notes.title: Negative Bias
notes.text: This grammar strongly favors negative contexts. Avoid using it for positive situations.

notes.title: Extreme Degree
notes.text: 지경 literally means 'boundary' or 'border,' emphasizing a critical threshold.

notes.title: Tense Flexibility
notes.text: Can be used in present (지경이다) or past (지경이었다) depending on context.

notes.title: Similar Grammar
notes.text: Compare with -(으)ㄹ 뻔하다, which means 'almost/nearly' and focuses on narrowly avoided outcomes.

explanation: Expresses a conjecture or inference based on external observation. Indicates that the speaker has deduced something from visible or observable evidence.

rules.title: Adjective and Past State Inference
rules.description: Use -(으)ㄴ가 보다 with adjectives and past-tense verbs to indicate a deduced state.

examples.breakdown: 그 사람은 바쁜가 보다 (that person seems busy).
examples.english: That person seems busy.

rules.title: Present Action Inference
rules.description: Use -나 보다 with present-tense verbs to indicate a deduced ongoing action.

examples.breakdown: 비가 오나 보다 (it seems to be raining).
examples.english: It seems to be raining.

rules.title: Past Action Inference
rules.description: Use -았/었나 보다 to infer that a past action has already occurred.

examples.breakdown: 그는 이미 떠났나 보다 (he seems to have already left).
examples.english: He seems to have already left.

notes.title: Observation-Based
notes.text: Unlike -(으)ㄹ 것 같다, this grammar emphasizes deduction from observable evidence.

notes.title: Adjective Form
notes.text: For adjectives and past verbs, use -(으)ㄴ가 보다. For present verbs, use -나 보다.

notes.title: Subject Limitation
notes.text: When the first-person is the subject, -(으)ㄴ가 보다 can only be used in questions: 내가 틀렌가 보다.

notes.title: Formal Variant
notes.text: In formal writing, -(으)ㄴ가 본데 and -나 본데 appear in narrative contexts.

explanation: Draws a conclusion based on visible evidence or observed facts. The speaker infers something from what they have seen or noticed.

rules.title: Inference from Observable States
rules.description: Use -(으)ㄴ 걸 보니 for adjectives and completed actions visible to the speaker.

examples.korean: 얼굴이 붉은 걸 보니 술을 마셨나 보다.
examples.breakdown: 얼굴이 붉은 걸 보니 (seeing that his face is red) 술을 마셨나 보다 (he seems to have been drinking).
examples.english: Judging from his red face, he seems to have been drinking.

rules.title: Inference from Ongoing Actions
rules.description: Use -는 걸 보니 for ongoing actions that provide evidence for a conclusion.

examples.korean: 늦게까지 일하는 걸 보니 정말 바쁜 모양이다.
examples.breakdown: 늦게까지 일하는 걸 보니 (seeing him working late) 정말 바쁜 모양이다 (he really seems busy).
examples.english: Judging from him working late, he really seems busy.

rules.title: Abbreviated and Extended Forms
rules.description: 보니 is the standard abbreviation of 보니까. Both are acceptable, with 보니까 adding slight emphasis.

examples.korean: 아무 말도 안 하는 걸 보니 아직 마음이 상한 것 같다.
examples.breakdown: 아무 말도 안 하는 걸 보니 (judging from him not saying anything) 아직 마음이 상한 것 같다 (I think he's still upset).
examples.english: Judging from him not saying anything, I think he's still upset.

notes.title: Visual Evidence
notes.text: This grammar specifically requires observable evidence; do not use it for abstract guesses.

notes.title: Abbreviation
notes.text: 보니 is the standard shortened form of 보니까 in everyday speech.

notes.title: Past Tense
notes.text: For completed actions, both -은/ㄴ 걸 보니 and -았/었는 걸 보니 are acceptable, but the former is more natural.

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: In academic writing, prefer -(으)ㄴ/는 것으로 보아 or -음을 미루어 보아.

explanation: Describes the act of feigning or pretending to be in a certain state or to perform an action. 체하다 is a more formal and literary variant of 척하다.

rules.title: Feigning a State
rules.description: Use -(으)ㄴ 척하다 with adjectives and past states to pretend to be in a certain condition.

examples.breakdown: 그는 바쁜 척하며 (pretending to be busy) 자리를 피했다 (avoided the situation).
examples.english: He pretended to be busy and avoided the situation.

rules.title: Feigning an Action
rules.description: Use -는 척하다 with ongoing actions to pretend to perform something.

examples.breakdown: 아는 척하지 마세요 (don't pretend to know).
examples.english: Don't pretend to know.

rules.title: Formal Variant: 체하다
rules.description: 체하다 is used in formal or written contexts with identical grammar but elevated register.

examples.breakdown: 잠든 체하며 (pretending to be asleep) 엿들었다 (eavesdropped).
examples.english: He eavesdropped while pretending to be asleep.

notes.title: Formality Distinction
notes.text: 척하다 is standard in spoken Korean. 체하다 is formal and literary, rarely used in casual conversation.

notes.title: Adjective Form
notes.text: For adjectives, always use -(으)ㄴ 척하다: 바쁜 척하다. For verbs, use -는 척하다.

notes.title: Common Error
notes.text: Do not say *모르는 척하다 is wrong; 모르다 is a verb, so 모르는 척하다 is correct.

notes.title: Related Form
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ 체하다 is identical in meaning but stylistically elevated.

explanation: Indicates that an action is performed while maintaining a particular state, often one that should have been changed. Common in written Korean and descriptive prose.

rules.title: Persistent Physical State
rules.description: Use -(으)ㄴ 채(로) with states resulting from completed actions that persist during another action.

examples.breakdown: 신발을 신은 채(로) (with shoes on) 방에 들어갔다 (entered the room).
examples.english: He entered the room while still wearing his shoes.

rules.title: Neglect or Failure to Change
rules.description: Often implies that a state should have been altered but was left unchanged.

examples.breakdown: 불을 켜 놓은 채 (with the light left on) 잠들었다 (fell asleep).
examples.english: He fell asleep with the light left on.

rules.title: Posture and Mental State
rules.description: Can be used with physical postures or mental states maintained during an activity.

examples.breakdown: 눈을 감은 채 (with eyes closed) 명상에 집중했다 (focused on meditation).
examples.english: He focused on meditation with his eyes closed.

notes.title: Written Preference
notes.text: This form is especially common in descriptive writing, literature, and formal reports.

notes.title: Particle 로
notes.text: 로 is optional but recommended when the phrase modifies the manner of the following verb.
