notes.title: State Requirement
notes.text: The preceding action must represent a persistent state, not a momentary action.

notes.title: Contrast with -면서
notes.text: Unlike -면서, which implies simultaneous actions, -(으)ㄴ 채(로) emphasizes an unchanged state.

explanation: Expresses a subjective impression or feeling that something appears to be a certain way. 듯싶다 is slightly more tentative and colloquial than 듯하다.

rules.title: Adjective and Past State Impression
rules.description: Use -(으)ㄴ 듯하다 with adjectives and past states to express a subjective impression.

examples.english: He seemed sad.

rules.title: Present Action Impression
rules.description: Use -는 듯하다 with present-tense verbs to describe an ongoing appearance.

examples.breakdown: 비가 올 듯하다 (it seems like it will rain).
examples.english: It seems like it will rain.

rules.title: Tentative Variant: 듯싶다
rules.description: 듯싶다 can replace 듯하다 for a softer, more uncertain tone, often with -게 for adverbial usage.

examples.korean: 그녀는 알고 있는 듯싶게 고개를 끄덕였다.
examples.breakdown: 그녀는 알고 있는 듯싶게 (as if she knew) 고개를 끄덕였다 (she nodded).
examples.english: She nodded as if she knew.

notes.title: Subjective Impression
notes.text: This grammar emphasizes the speaker's subjective feeling rather than objective evidence.

notes.title: Conjugation
notes.text: 듯하다 conjugates as a regular 하다 verb: 듯하다, 듯해요, 듯했습니다.

notes.title: Adverbial Form
notes.text: Add -게 to modify verbs: 듯하게 웃었다 (smiled as if...).

notes.title: Formal Writing
notes.text: In academic prose, avoid 듯싶다; use 듯하다 or objective constructions like -(으)ㄴ 것으로 보인다.

explanation: An objective, formal expression used to state that something appears to be the case based on observation or evidence. Common in news, reports, and academic writing.

rules.title: Objective Observation
rules.description: Attach -는 것으로 보이다 to verbs for objective observation based on evidence.

examples.breakdown: 경제가 회복되고 있는 것으로 (as the economy is recovering) 보인다 (it appears).
examples.english: The economy appears to be recovering.

rules.title: Evidence-Based Assessment
rules.description: Often used with statistical or observable evidence to draw a measured conclusion.

examples.korean: 그는 이 사실을 전혀 모르는 것으로 보인다.
examples.breakdown: 그는 이 사실을 전혀 (he completely) 모르는 것으로 보인다 (appears not to know this fact).
examples.english: He appears to be completely unaware of this fact.

rules.title: Retrospective Observation
rules.description: Can be conjugated to past tense for observations about prior situations.

examples.korean: 당시 그들은 준비가 부족했던 것으로 보인다.
examples.breakdown: 당시 그들은 준비가 (at that time, their preparation) 부족했던 것으로 보인다 (appears to have been insufficient).
examples.english: At that time, their preparation appears to have been insufficient.

notes.title: Objective Register
notes.text: This form is strongly objective and preferred in journalism, research papers, and official reports.

notes.title: Verb-Only
notes.text: This grammar attaches only to verbs. For nouns, use N-인 것으로 보인다.

notes.title: Avoid Subjectivity
notes.text: Do not use this form for personal feelings; use -(으)ㄹ 것 같다 instead.

notes.title: Past Tense
notes.text: Use -는 것으로 보였다 or -던 것으로 보인다 for past observations.

explanation: A formal passive construction stating that something is widely known, recognized, or established as a fact. Common in academic and encyclopedic writing.

rules.title: Widely Known Facts
rules.description: Attach -는 것으로 알려져 있다 to state facts that are publicly or academically recognized.

examples.korean: 이 약은 부작용이 거의 없는 것으로 알려져 있다.
examples.breakdown: 이 약은 부작용이 (this medicine's side effects) 거의 없는 것으로 (of being almost nonexistent) 알려져 있다 (is known).
examples.english: This medicine is known to have almost no side effects.

rules.title: Historical or Biographical Facts
rules.description: Can be used with historical or established information about people, places, or events.

examples.breakdown: 그는 1970년에 (he, in 1970) 태어난 것으로 (of being born) 알려져 있다 (is known).
examples.english: He is known to have been born in 1970.

rules.title: Passive Tense Flexibility
rules.description: The passive form 알려지다 can be conjugated for tense and politeness to match context.

examples.korean: 이 지역은 예로부터 무역의 중심지였던 것으로 알려져 있다.
examples.breakdown: 이 지역은 예로부터 (this area, since old times) 무역의 중심지였던 것으로 (of having been a trade center) 알려져 있다 (is known).
examples.english: This area is known to have been a center of trade since ancient times.

notes.title: Encyclopedic Tone
notes.text: This construction carries an authoritative, encyclopedic tone suitable for definitive statements.

notes.title: Passive Form
notes.text: 알려져 있다 is the passive state 'is known.' Use 알려져 있었다 for past known facts.

notes.title: Similar Forms
notes.text: Related constructions include -는 것으로 전해지다, -는 것으로 밝혀지다, and -는 것으로 여겨지다.

notes.title: Factuality
notes.text: Only use this form for facts that are widely established, not for personal knowledge.

explanation: A formal passive construction used to express that an action or state is widely believed or considered to be the case. Commonly found in news reports, academic papers, and formal written discourse, this grammar softens assertions by attributing them to collective opinion rather than personal judgment.

rules.title: Basic Formation
rules.description: Attach 는 것으로 생각된다 to the stem of an action verb. This structure is the passive equivalent of 사람들은 ~라고 생각한다, and it is almost exclusively used in the third-person or impersonal register.
examples: 이번 정책이 효과가 있는 것으로 생각된다. → 이번 정책이 (this policy) + 효과가 있다 (to be effective) + 는 것으로 생각된다 (is thought to) → It is thought that this policy is effective.
그의 연구가 학계에서 높이 평가받는 것으로 생각된다. → 그의 연구 (his research) + 높이 평가받다 (to be highly regarded) + 는 것으로 생각된다 → His research is thought to be highly regarded in academic circles.

rules.title: Contextual Usage
rules.description: This grammar is typically used with general subjects like 사람들은, 일반적으로, or 학계에서는. It softens assertions by attributing the opinion to others, making it ideal for formal hypothesis and consensus statements.
examples: 일반적으로 어린아이들이 빠르게 언어를 습득하는 것으로 생각된다. → 일반적으로 (generally) + 어린아이들이 (children) + 빠르게 언어를 습득하다 (to acquire language quickly) + 는 것으로 생각된다 → It is generally thought that children acquire language quickly.

notes.title: Written Register
notes.text: This grammar is almost exclusively used in written and formal contexts. In spoken Korean, use ~(으)ㄴ/는 것 같다 or ~다고 생각하다 instead.

notes.title: Impersonal Subject
notes.text: The subject is often omitted or generic (e.g., 사람들은, 많은 이들은). The construction itself implies a collective belief rather than a personal one.

notes.title: Nominalization Required
notes.text: The key to this grammar is the nominalization of the preceding clause with 는 것. Without it, the sentence becomes ungrammatical.

explanation: A formal passive expression indicating that something is estimated, presumed, or inferred based on available evidence, data, or circumstances. Common in statistical reports, investigations, and scientific writing, it conveys reasoned estimation rather than stated fact.

rules.title: Basic Formation
rules.description: Attach 는 것으로 추정된다 to the stem of an action verb. It conveys that the speaker is making a reasoned estimation rather than stating a fact, often based on data or circumstantial evidence.
examples: 사고 원인이 기계 결함에 있는 것으로 추정된다. → 사고 원인 (cause of the accident) + 기계 결함에 있다 (to be in mechanical defect) + 는 것으로 추정된다 → The cause of the accident is estimated to be a mechanical defect.
실종자가 이미 해외로 빠져나간 것으로 추정된다. → 실종자 (missing person) + 이미 해외로 빠져나가다 (to have already escaped abroad) + 는 것으로 추정된다 → The missing person is presumed to have already fled abroad.

rules.title: Formal Register Only
rules.description: This construction is strictly formal and is rarely used in casual conversation. It pairs well with nouns like 통계적으로, 현재로서는, or 근거로 to strengthen the evidentiary tone.
examples: 통계적으로 이번 분기 매출이 전년 대비 감소한 것으로 추정된다. → 통계적으로 (statistically) + 이번 분기 매출 (this quarter's sales) + 전년 대비 감소하다 (to decrease compared to the previous year) + 는 것으로 추정된다 → Statistically, this quarter's sales are estimated to have decreased compared to the previous year.

notes.title: Scientific Nuance
notes.text: 추정되다 carries a stronger sense of logical inference than 생각되다. Use it when referring to data-driven conclusions or investigative hypotheses.

notes.title: Not for Certainty
notes.text: Avoid using 추정되다 when stating facts or certainties. It inherently acknowledges room for error or revision.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Frequently appears with phrases like ~에 근거하여 (based on), ~으로 보아 (judging from), and 현재까지는 (as of now).

explanation: A formal passive construction meaning 'is understood to' or 'is comprehended to.' Note: 이할된다 appears to be a typographical variant of 이핵된다 (the standard passive form of 이해하다). This pattern is used in legal, academic, and official documents to state how a clause or situation is interpreted.

rules.title: Basic Formation
rules.description: Attach 는 것으로 이핵된다 to the stem of an action verb. It expresses that a certain interpretation or understanding has been established, especially in contexts requiring precise meaning.
examples: 이 조항은 모든 회원에게 적용되는 것으로 이핵된다. → 이 조항 (this clause) + 모든 회원에게 적용되다 (to be applied to all members) + 는 것으로 이핵된다 → This clause is understood to apply to all members.
그의 발언은 우리 회사의 입장을 대변하는 것으로 이핵된다. → 그의 발언 (his remarks) + 우리 회사의 입장을 대변하다 (to speak on behalf of our company's position) + 는 것으로 이핵된다 → His remarks are understood to represent our company's position.

rules.title: Legal and Official Contexts
rules.description: This grammar frequently appears in contracts, laws, and official statements where precise interpretation is required. It establishes a shared understanding that may have legal or procedural consequences.
examples: 계약서의 해당 내용은 양측의 합의가 완료된 것으로 이핵된다. → 해당 내용 (the relevant content) + 양측의 합의가 완료되다 (for both parties' agreement to be completed) + 는 것으로 이핵된다 → The relevant content of the contract is understood to mean that both parties' agreement has been completed.

notes.title: Typographical Note
notes.text: The form 이할된다 in the original source is a typographical error for 이핵된다. Always use the standard spelling 이핵된다 in actual writing and exams.

notes.title: Interpretation vs. Belief
notes.text: Use 이핵되다 when discussing how a text, statement, or action is interpreted—not when expressing personal belief or statistical inference.

notes.title: Passive Form
notes.text: 이핵되다 is the passive of 이해하다. The full pattern requires 는 것으로 to nominalize the preceding action before attaching the passive verb.

explanation: An advanced conditional-concessive pattern meaning 'just because (someone does/says/is)...' The following clause presents a contrary outcome or rejects a logical assumption. It is a staple of argumentative and analytical writing at the advanced level.

rules.title: Verb and Adjective Formation
rules.description: For action verbs, use -(으)ㄴ/는다고 해서. For descriptive verbs (adjectives), use -다고 해서. For nouns, use -(이)라고 해서. The pattern negates the expectation that the first clause would naturally lead to the second.
examples: 돈이 많다고 해서 행복한 것은 아니다. → 돈이 많다 (to have a lot of money) + 다고 해서 → Just because one has a lot of money doesn't mean they are happy.
그가 유명하다고 해서 무조건 옳은 것은 아니다. → 유명하다 (to be famous) + 다고 해서 → Just because he is famous doesn't mean he is unconditionally right.
학생이라고 해서 공부만 해야 하는 것은 아니다. → 학생이다 (to be a student) + 이라고 해서 → Just because someone is a student doesn't mean they only have to study.

rules.title: Negative or Contrasting Main Clause
rules.description: The main clause almost always contains a negation, a limitation, or a contrasting statement introduced by (그렇다고) ~는/은 아니다, ~기는 어렵다, or ~ㄹ/을 수 없다.
examples: 비싸다고 해서 무조건 좋은 물건은 아니다. → 비싸다 (to be expensive) + 다고 해서 → Just because something is expensive doesn't mean it is unconditionally a good product.
경험이 많다고 해서 모든 일을 잘할 수 있는 것은 아니다. → 경험이 많다 (to have a lot of experience) + 다고 해서 → Just because someone has a lot of experience doesn't mean they can do everything well.

rules.title: Extended to Reported Speech
rules.description: This pattern can also follow quoted statements, creating a complex structure that rejects an argument attributed to someone else.
examples: 그렇게 하면 성공할 수 있다고 해서 실제로 성공하는 것은 아니다. → 성공할 수 있다 (can succeed) + 고 해서 → Just because people say you can succeed by doing so doesn't mean you will actually succeed.

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: While usable in speech, this pattern is especially common in essays, editorials, and debate contexts where logical rebuttal is required.

notes.title: Mandatory Negation
notes.text: The main clause must contain a negative or limiting element. A positive assertion following -(으)ㄴ/는다고 해서 is ungrammatical or changes the meaning entirely.

notes.title: Synonymous Patterns
notes.text: Closely related to ~(으)ㄴ/는다고 해서 ~ㄹ/을 것이다 and ~(이)라고 해서 ~ㄹ/을 수 없다. The nuance is always one of rejecting a simplistic cause-effect relationship.

notes.title: Adjective vs. Verb Conjugation
notes.text: Be careful: action verbs take -(으)ㄴ/는다고 (가다 → 간다고, 먹다 → 먹는다고), while descriptive verbs take -다고 (예쁘다 → 예쁘다고, 크다 → 크다고).

explanation: A cautious assertion pattern meaning 'it can be said that...' or 'one may say that...' It softens a claim by presenting it as a reasonable characterization rather than an absolute truth. Essential for academic and formal writing where intellectual humility is expected.

rules.title: Verb and Adjective Formation
rules.description: For action verbs, attach -(으)ㄴ/는다고 할 수 있다. For descriptive verbs, attach -다고 할 수 있다. This structure frames the preceding clause as a defensible statement rather than a fact.
examples: 그는 매우 성실하다고 할 수 있다. → 성실하다 (to be sincere) + 다고 할 수 있다 → He can be said to be very sincere.
그녀는 끊임없이 노력한다고 할 수 있다. → 노력하다 (to strive) + 는다고 할 수 있다 → She can be said to constantly strive.
이 문제는 매우 복잡하다고 할 수 있다. → 복잡하다 (to be complex) + 다고 할 수 있다 → This problem can be said to be very complex.

rules.title: Moderating Strong Claims
rules.description: Use this grammar to avoid sounding overly dogmatic. It signals that you are offering an interpretation that is open to discussion but well-supported.
examples: 그의 작품은 현대 미술의 전환점이라고 할 수 있다. → 현대 미술의 전환점이다 (to be a turning point in modern art) + 라고 할 수 있다 → His work can be said to be a turning point in modern art.

rules.title: Past and Future Tense
rules.description: The pattern can be extended to past and future tenses by modifying the verb form before the quotative marker.
examples: 그의 업적은 당시로서는 혁명적이었다고 할 수 있다. → 혁명적이었다 (was revolutionary) + 고 할 수 있다 → His achievement can be said to have been revolutionary at the time.

notes.title: Academic Hedging
notes.text: This is a key 'hedging' device in Korean academic writing. It allows the writer to make substantive claims while maintaining intellectual humility.

notes.title: Nominal Compatibility
notes.text: When used with nouns, the pattern becomes N-(이)라고 할 수 있다. Always ensure the copula 이다 is properly conjugated before attaching the quotative.

notes.title: Alternatives
notes.text: Similar expressions include ~(으)ㄴ/는다고 볼 수 있다 and ~(으)ㄴ/는다고 여겨진다, though 할 수 있다 emphasizes the speaker's reasoned judgment.

notes.title: Subjectivity Required
notes.text: This grammar should not be used for objective facts (e.g., 'The Earth is round'). Use it for interpretations, evaluations, and characterizations.

explanation: A comparative-reframing pattern meaning 'rather than saying...' It rejects an initial description and replaces it with a more accurate one. Common in analytical essays and nuanced argumentation where polite correction or reinterpretation is needed.

rules.title: Verb and Adjective Formation
rules.description: For action verbs, use -(으)ㄴ/는다기보다는. For descriptive verbs, use -다기보다는. The second clause provides the preferred characterization, often using -(으)ㄴ/는다고 하는 것이 맞다 or a similar expression.
examples: 그가 게으르다기보다는 일의 우선순위를 다르게 생각하는 것이다. → 게으르다 (to be lazy) + 다기보다는 → Rather than saying he is lazy, he thinks about work priorities differently.
이것이 실패라기보다는 새로운 시작이라고 할 수 있다. → 실패이다 (to be a failure) + 라기보다는 → Rather than calling this a failure, it can be called a new beginning.
그가 포기한 것이라기보다는 현실을 인정한 것이라고 본다. → 포기한 것이다 (to be giving up) + 라기보다는 → Rather than saying he gave up, I see it as acknowledging reality.

rules.title: Pairing with Alternative Descriptions
rules.description: The pattern is most effective when the second half offers a clear alternative, often introduced by ~ㄴ/은/는 것이다, ~ㄴ/은/는 편이다, or ~라고 본다.
examples: 이 도시가 조용하다기보다는 활기가 없다고 하는 것이 더 정확하다. → 조용하다 (to be quiet) + 다기보다는 → Rather than saying this city is quiet, it is more accurate to say it lacks vitality.
그의 말이 거짓말이라기보다는 과장된 표현이라고 보는 것이 옳다. → 거짓말이다 (to be a lie) + 라기보다는 → Rather than calling his words a lie, it is right to see them as an exaggerated expression.

rules.title: Noun Form: -(이)라기보다는
rules.description: When used with nouns, the pattern becomes -(이)라기보다는, making it highly versatile across parts of speech.
examples: 이것이 문제라기보다는 도전 과제라고 생각해야 한다. → 문제이다 (to be a problem) + 라기보다는 → Rather than considering this a problem, we should think of it as a challenge.

notes.title: Nuanced Correction
notes.text: This pattern is not a blunt contradiction. It politely reframes a description, making it ideal for academic writing and diplomatic communication.

notes.title: Common Second-Half Patterns
notes.text: Frequently paired with ~라고 할 수 있다, ~라고 보는 것이 낫다, and ~에 가깝다 to introduce the revised interpretation.

notes.title: Not for Direct Denial
notes.text: Avoid using this grammar for direct factual disputes (e.g., 'Rather than saying 2+2=5...'). It is designed for subjective reinterpretation, not objective correction.

notes.title: Verb Stem Rule
notes.text: Action verbs with 받침 take -는다기보다는 (먹는다기보다는), while those without take -ㄴ다기보다는 (간다기보다는).

explanation: A formal additive connector meaning 'not only... but also...' It elevates the register compared to the more common ~(으)ㄹ 뿐만 아니라 and is a hallmark of advanced formal writing, speeches, and official documents.

rules.title: Basic Formation
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ 뿐더러 to the verb or adjective stem. The second clause must add information that is parallel to or intensifies the first clause.
examples: 그는 학문에 뛰어날 뿐더러 인품도 훌륭하다. → 뛰어나다 (to excel) + 을 뿐더러 → He excels not only in academics but also in character.
이 정책은 경제적 효과가 클 뿐더러 사회적 안정에도 기여한다. → 크다 (to be big) + 을 뿐더러 → This policy not only has great economic effects but also contributes to social stability.
그녀는 한국어를 유창하게 말할 뿐더러 중국어와 일본어도 할 줄 안다. → 말하다 (to speak) + 을 뿐더러 → She not only speaks Korean fluently but also knows Chinese and Japanese.

rules.title: Formal Register Requirement
rules.description: 뿐더러 is considered more formal and literary than 뿐만 아니라. It is preferred in essays, official documents, and speeches where elevated diction is expected.
examples: 이 프로젝트는 시간이 오래 걸릴 뿐더러 비용도 많이 든다. → 오래 걸리다 (to take a long time) + 을 뿐더러 → This project not only takes a long time but also costs a lot.
그의 책은 내용이 깊을 뿐더러 문장력도 뛰어나다. → 깊다 (to be deep) + 을 뿐더러 → His book is not only deep in content but also outstanding in writing.

notes.title: Written Preference
notes.text: 뿐더러 appears far more frequently in written Korean than in spoken Korean. In casual speech, always use 뿐만 아니라.

notes.title: No Negative First Clause
notes.text: While 뿐만 아니라 can sometimes follow negative clauses, 뿐더러 works most naturally with positive assertions. For negative addition, consider ~기는커녕 or ~은/는 물론.

notes.title: Consonant Stem Rule
notes.text: Stems ending in a consonant (except ㄹ) take -을 뿐더러. Stems ending in a vowel or ㄹ take -ㄹ 뿐더러.

notes.title: Intensifying Second Clause
notes.text: The second clause often contains an intensifying element (e.g., 도, 또한, 게다가) or a higher-degree description to justify the use of this additive pattern.

explanation: A formal nominalization pattern meaning 'in that...,' 'from the fact that...,' or 'in the aspect of...' It establishes the basis for a judgment, conclusion, or observation by highlighting a particular circumstance as the logical foundation.

rules.title: Verb Formation
rules.description: For action verbs in the present tense, use -(으)ㄴ/는 데서. For past actions, use -(으)ㄴ 데서. This indicates that the basis for the main clause lies in the stated circumstance.
examples: 그가 끝까지 포기하지 않는 데서 그의 진심을 느낄 수 있다. → 포기하지 않다 (to not give up) + 는 데서 → In the fact that he doesn't give up until the end, one can feel his sincerity.
이 책이 많은 독자에게 사랑받는 데서 그 가치를 알 수 있다. → 사랑받다 (to be loved) + 는 데서 → From the fact that this book is loved by many readers, its value can be known.
그가 과거에 실패한 데서 중요한 교훈을 얻었다. → 실패하다 (to fail) + 한 데서 → From the fact that he failed in the past, I gained an important lesson.

rules.title: Adjective Formation
rules.description: For descriptive verbs, use -(으)ㄴ 데서. The noun 데 (thing/fact/point) is modified by the adjective, creating a nominalized basis for evaluation.
examples: 이 지역이 환경적으로 취약한 데서 보호 대책이 필요하다. → 취약하다 (to be vulnerable) + 한 데서 → In that this region is environmentally vulnerable, protective measures are needed.
그의 태도가 매우 진지한 데서 이 프로젝트에 대한 그의 열정을 알 수 있다. → 진지하다 (to be serious) + 한 데서 → In that his attitude is very serious, one can know his passion for this project.

rules.title: Establishing Logical Basis
rules.description: This pattern is frequently used in essays and analyses to connect an observation to a conclusion, often with verbs like 알 수 있다, 문제라고 할 수 있다, or 의의가 있다.
examples: 두 나라가 오랜 역사를 공유하는 데서 문화적 유사성이 생겨났다. → 공유하다 (to share) + 는 데서 → In that the two countries share a long history, cultural similarities have emerged.

notes.title: Formal Analysis
notes.text: This grammar is a cornerstone of formal expository writing. It allows the writer to move smoothly from observation to inference.

notes.title: Common Main Clause Verbs
notes.text: The main clause frequently contains 알 수 있다 (can know), 문제라고 할 수 있다 (can be said to be a problem), 의의가 있다 (has significance), or 필요하다 (is needed).

notes.title: Not a Physical Location
notes.text: Do not confuse this with the locative -에서. While 데 can mean 'place,' here it is an abstract nominalizer referring to a fact or circumstance.

notes.title: Past Tense Marking
notes.text: For past tense verbs, use -(으)ㄴ 데서 for simple past (간 데서) and -던 데서 for retrospective past (가던 데서). Be precise with aspect.

explanation: A formal pattern using the plain-form ending -다는 to modify 점. In practice, this most commonly attaches to descriptive verbs (A-다는 점에서) and action verbs (V-ㄴ/는다는 점에서) to mean 'from the point that...' While listed as N- in the source, standard modern Korean primarily uses this structure with predicates ending in -다, whereas nouns with the copula 이다 typically use -(이)라는 점에서 (see ID 449).

rules.title: Adjective and Verb Formation
rules.description: For descriptive verbs, use -다는 점에서. For action verbs, use -(으)ㄴ/는다는 점에서. The pattern isolates a specific fact as the basis for an evaluative statement.
examples: 이 문제가 매우 어렵다는 점에서 학생들의 충분한 이해가 필요하다. → 어렵다 (to be difficult) + 다는 점에서 → In that this problem is very difficult, students' thorough understanding is needed.
그가 꾸준히 노력한다는 점에서 높이 평가해야 한다. → 노력하다 (to strive) + 는다는 점에서 → From the point that he consistently strives, we must evaluate him highly.
이 지역이 교통이 불편하다는 점에서 개선이 시급하다. → 불편하다 (to be inconvenient) + 다는 점에서 → In that this area has poor traffic, improvement is urgent.

rules.title: Evaluative Function
rules.description: The main clause almost always contains an evaluative statement—positive, negative, or analytical—based on the significance of the quoted fact.
examples: 그의 태도가 성실하다는 점에서 그를 신뢰할 수 있다. → 성실하다 (to be sincere) + 하다는 점에서 → In that his attitude is sincere, we can trust him.

rules.title: Comparison with N-(이)라는 점에서
rules.description: While functionally similar to N-(이)라는 점에서, the -다는 form is used with predicates that end in the plain-form -다. Nouns with the copula 이다 almost always take -(이)라는 점에서 instead.
examples: 그의 연구가 혁신적이라는 점에서 주목할 만하다. → 혁신적이다 (to be innovative) + 이라는 점에서 → In that his research is innovative, it is noteworthy. (Note: 혁신적이다 uses the copula, so 이라는 is preferred.)

notes.title: Point of Evaluation
notes.text: 점 literally means 'point.' Use this grammar when you want to isolate a single, specific fact as the basis for your argument.

notes.title: Predicate-Focused Pattern
notes.text: This pattern works most naturally with adjectives and verbs in their plain form. For nouns with 이다, prefer N-(이)라는 점에서 (ID 449).

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: 다는 점에서 is a high-register pattern. In casual speech, use ~라서 or ~니까 instead, though they lack the analytical precision of 점에서.

notes.title: Spacing
notes.text: Be careful with spacing: write 다는 점에서 as three separate words (다는 점에서), not as a single compound.

explanation: A formal pattern meaning 'in that it is N' or 'from the point that it is N.' It is functionally similar to N-다는 점에서 but places slightly more emphasis on the identity or naming of the noun itself. It is a staple of comparative and evaluative writing at the advanced level.

rules.title: Basic Formation
rules.description: For nouns ending in a consonant (except ㄹ), attach 이라는 점에서. For nouns ending in a vowel or ㄹ, attach 라는 점에서. It turns a nominal identity into an evaluative basis.
examples: 이것이 예술작품이라는 점에서 다양한 해석이 가능하다. → 예술작품이다 (to be a work of art) + 이라는 점에서 → In that this is a work of art, diverse interpretations are possible.
그가 외국인이라는 점에서 언어 장벽이 있을 수 있다. → 외국인이다 (to be a foreigner) + 이라는 점에서 → In that he is a foreigner, there may be a language barrier.
이 사건이 역사적인 사건이라는 점에서 많은 연구가 이루어지고 있다. → 역사적인 사건이다 (to be a historical event) + 이라는 점에서 → In that this incident is a historical event, much research is being conducted.

rules.title: Identity-Focused Evaluation
rules.description: Unlike 다는 점에서, which can follow adjectives directly, (이)라는 점에서 emphasizes the classification or identity of the noun. It answers the question 'Because it is what kind of thing?'
examples: 이 프로그램이 교육용 프로그램이라는 점에서 학생들에게 매우 유용하다. → 교육용 프로그램이다 (to be an educational program) + 이라는 점에서 → In that this program is an educational program, it is very useful for students.
그의 연구가 이례적인 연구라는 점에서 주목할 가치가 있다. → 이례적인 연구이다 (to be an unusual study) + 라는 점에서 → In that his research is an unusual study, it is worth paying attention to.

rules.title: Comparison with N-다는 점에서
rules.description: In practice, N-다는 점에서 and N-(이)라는 점에서 are often interchangeable. However, (이)라는 점에서 is preferred when emphasizing a definition, title, or category.
examples: 이 문제가 복합적인 문제라는 점에서 단순한 해결책은 없다. → 복합적인 문제이다 (to be a complex problem) + 라는 점에서 → In that this problem is a complex problem, there is no simple solution.

notes.title: Emphasis on Classification
notes.text: Use (이)라는 점에서 when you want to stress what something is called or how it is classified, rather than just its general characteristics.

notes.title: Phonetic Rule
notes.text: Nouns ending in ㄹ take 라는 점에서 (e.g., 모델이라는 점에서). Nouns ending in other consonants take 이라는 점에서 (e.g., 학생이라는 점에서).

notes.title: Evaluative Main Clauses
notes.text: Like other 점에서 patterns, the main clause typically evaluates significance (의미가 있다), acknowledges limitations (문제가 있다), or draws conclusions (~기 때문이다).

notes.title: Not for Actions
notes.text: This pattern is strictly for nouns. For actions, use V-ㄴ/는다는 점에서. For adjectives, use A-다는 점에서.

explanation: A formal analytical pattern meaning 'from the aspect that it is N' or 'in terms of being N.' It examines a subject through a specific facet, dimension, or characteristic. Essential for analytical essays, reports, and multi-dimensional evaluations.

rules.title: Basic Formation
rules.description: Attach 이라는 측면에서 to a noun. It treats the noun as an analytical lens through which the subject is evaluated. The noun usually represents a field, dimension, or specific characteristic.
examples: 이 도시는 관광 도시라는 측면에서 매우 발달해 있다. → 관광 도시이다 (to be a tourist city) + 라는 측면에서 → From the aspect that this city is a tourist city, it is very developed.
이 제품은 가격이라는 측면에서 경쟁력이 있다. → 가격이다 (to be price) + 이라는 측면에서 → From the aspect of price, this product has competitiveness.
그의 연구는 방법론이라는 측면에서 혁신적이다. → 방법론이다 (to be methodology) + 이라는 측면에서 → His research is innovative from the aspect of methodology.

rules.title: Multi-Dimensional Analysis
rules.description: This pattern is frequently used to compare or evaluate a single subject across multiple dimensions by listing different nouns with 측면에서.
examples: 이 정책은 경제적 측면에서 본다면 긍정적이지만 환경적 측면에서는 문제가 있다. → 경제적 측면 (economic aspect) + 에서 본다면 → If viewed from the economic aspect, this policy is positive, but from the environmental aspect, there are problems.
이 문제는 사회적 측면에서뿐만 아니라 법적 측면에서도 검토해야 한다. → 사회적 측면 (social aspect) + 에서도 → This issue must be examined not only from the social aspect but also from the legal aspect.

rules.title: Noun Modification with Copula
rules.description: The noun preceding 이라는 측면에서 often includes a modifying clause or another noun that clarifies which aspect is being discussed.
examples: 이 지역이 개발 지역이라는 측면에서 인프라 투자가 시급하다. → 개발 지역이다 (to be a development area) + 라는 측면에서 → From the aspect that this region is a development area, infrastructure investment is urgent.

notes.title: Facet Selection
notes.text: 측면 means 'aspect' or 'facet.' Use this grammar when you want to isolate one specific dimension of a complex issue for focused discussion.

notes.title: Compatibility with Multiple Aspects
notes.text: You can list multiple aspects using N-적 측면에서 (e.g., 경제적 측면에서, 사회적 측면에서) to structure a balanced, multi-dimensional analysis.

notes.title: Common Verbs in Main Clause
notes.text: The main clause often contains 평가되다 (to be evaluated), 검토되다 (to be examined), 고려되어야 하다 (must be considered), or 의미가 있다 (has meaning).

notes.title: Not for Personal Opinions
notes.text: While you can express opinions, 측면에서 frames them as analytical observations. For purely emotional reactions, use ~에 대해 or ~를 보고 instead.

explanation: A formal pattern meaning 'from the perspective that it is N' or 'through the lens of N.' It indicates that an observation or judgment is made using a particular theoretical, conceptual, or personal framework. Common in academic and philosophical writing.

rules.title: Basic Formation
rules.description: Attach 이라는 관점에서 to a noun representing a perspective, theory, ideology, or standpoint. It frames the following statement as the product of that specific viewpoint.
examples: 이 문제는 교육자라는 관점에서 본다면 매우 심각하다. → 교육자이다 (to be an educator) + 라는 관점에서 본다면 → From the perspective that one is an educator, this problem is very serious.
이 이론은 역사적 관점에서 본다면 획기적인 것이다. → 역사적 관점 (historical perspective) + 에서 본다면 → From the historical perspective, this theory is groundbreaking.
그의 주장은 실용주의라는 관점에서 이핵될 수 있다. → 실용주의이다 (to be pragmatism) + 라는 관점에서 → His claim can be understood from the perspective of pragmatism.

rules.title: Theoretical and Conceptual Lenses
rules.description: This pattern excels when applying established theories, disciplines, or worldviews to a subject. The noun is often an academic field or ideology.
examples: 이 현상은 사회학적 관점에서 설명할 수 있다. → 사회학적 관점 (sociological perspective) + 에서 → This phenomenon can be explained from a sociological perspective.
그의 작품은 심리학이라는 관점에서 분석하면 흥미롭다. → 심리학이다 (to be psychology) + 이라는 관점에서 → His work is interesting when analyzed from the perspective of psychology.

rules.title: Comparison of Perspectives
rules.description: Multiple 관점에서 clauses can be juxtaposed to show how different frameworks yield different conclusions.
examples: 경제적 관점에서는 긍정적이지만 윤리적 관점에서는 부정적이다. → 경제적 관점 (economic perspective) + 에서는 → From the economic perspective it is positive, but from the ethical perspective it is negative.

notes.title: Framework Indicator
notes.text: 관점 means 'perspective' or 'viewpoint.' Use this grammar to signal that you are adopting a specific intellectual framework rather than making an objective universal claim.

notes.title: Academic Disciplines
notes.text: Frequently paired with academic adjectives: 경제적 관점에서 (from the economic perspective), 문화적 관점에서 (from the cultural perspective), 비판적 관점에서 (from a critical perspective).

notes.title: Verb Pairing
notes.text: Common verbs include 바라보다 (to look at), 분석하다 (to analyze), 접근하다 (to approach), 평가하다 (to evaluate), and 이해하다 (to understand).

notes.title: Person-Specific Perspectives
notes.text: When using a person's identity (e.g., 교육자라는 관점에서), you are speaking as if from their position. Be careful not to conflate this with their actual stated opinions.

explanation: A highly formal causative pattern meaning 'due to N,' 'owing to N,' or 'caused by N.' It is the advanced, written-register equivalent of -때문에 and -로 인해, and it is indispensable for official documents, news reports, and formal essays.

rules.title: Basic Formation
rules.description: For nouns ending in a consonant (except ㄹ), attach 으로 인하여. For nouns ending in a vowel or ㄹ, attach 로 인하여. The pattern indicates that the noun is the direct cause of the following result.
examples: 화재로 인하여 많은 재산 피해가 발생했다. → 화재 (fire) + 로 인하여 → Due to the fire, much property damage occurred.
경제 위기로 인하여 실업률이 급등했다. → 경제 위기 (economic crisis) + 로 인하여 → Due to the economic crisis, the unemployment rate surged sharply.
갑작스러운 기상 변화로 인하여 비행기가 지연되었다. → 갑작스러운 기상 변화 (sudden weather change) + 로 인하여 → Due to sudden weather changes, the airplane was delayed.

rules.title: Negative and Neutral Contexts
rules.description: While 으로 인하여 can be used for positive outcomes, it is most commonly associated with negative consequences such as accidents, losses, delays, and problems.
examples: 교통 사고로 인하여 도로가 완전히 봉쇄되었다. → 교통 사고 (traffic accident) + 로 인하여 → Due to a traffic accident, the road was completely blocked.
병으로 인하여 그는 일을 그만두어야 했다. → 병 (illness) + 으로 인하여 → Due to illness, he had to quit his job.
오해로 인하여 두 사람 사이에 갈등이 생겼다. → 오해 (misunderstanding) + 로 인하여 → Due to a misunderstanding, a conflict arose between the two people.

rules.title: Integration with Complex Nouns
rules.description: The noun can be expanded into a complex nominal phrase to specify the exact nature of the cause.
examples: 기업의 부실 경영으로 인하여 수많은 직원이 해고되었다. → 부실 경영 (poor management) + 으로 인하여 → Due to the company's poor management, numerous employees were dismissed.
화력 발전소의 매연 배출로 인하여 대기 오염이 심각해지고 있다. → 매연 배출 (smoke emission) + 로 인하여 → Due to smoke emissions from thermal power plants, air pollution is becoming serious.

notes.title: Highest Formality
notes.text: 으로 인하여 is one of the most formal causative expressions in Korean. In speech, use -때문에 or -로 인해. In writing, this form signals seriousness and authority.

notes.title: Phonetic Rule
notes.text: Follows standard 은/는 particle rules: consonant-final nouns (except ㄹ) take 으로 (감기으로), vowel-final nouns take 로 (비로), and ㄹ-final nouns take 로 (걸물로).

notes.title: Subject-Object Ambiguity
notes.text: When the causing noun is an action, it may be clearer to use the nominalized form (e.g., 화재 발생으로 인하여 rather than just 화재로 인하여).

notes.title: Result Clause Characteristics
notes.text: The result clause frequently uses passive verbs (발생하다, 야기되다, 초래되다) or formal Sino-Korean vocabulary to match the high register of the cause clause.

explanation: An experiential-result pattern meaning 'having done [repeatedly/continuously], (I came to realize/result happened).' It expresses a gradual realization, habituation, or unexpected consequence that emerged from sustained experience. Common in both spoken and written Korean at the advanced level.

rules.title: Action Verb Formation
rules.description: Attach -다 보니 to the plain form of an action verb. It indicates that a result or realization naturally emerged after repeating the action over time.
examples: 한국 드라마를 자주 보다 보니 한국어가 늘었다. → 보다 (to watch) + 다 보니 → Having watched Korean dramas frequently, my Korean improved.
매일 운 동을 하다 보니 체 력이 좋아졌다. → 하다 (to do) + 다 보니 → Having exercised every day, my physical strength improved.
혼자 살다 보니 요리 실력이 늘었다. → 살다 (to live) + 다 보니 → Having lived alone, my cooking skills improved.

rules.title: Adjective Formation
rules.description: Attach -다 보니 to the plain form of a descriptive verb. It indicates that a realization or result emerged from continuously being in a certain state.
examples: 바쁘다 보니 가족과의 시간이 줄었다. → 바쁘다 (to be busy) + 다 보니 → Being so busy, time with family decreased.
친구와 멀리 있다 보니 연락이 뜸해졌다. → 멀리 있다 (to be far away) + 다 보니 → Being far from my friend, contact became infrequent.

rules.title: Unexpected or Gradual Results
rules.description: The result clause often contains something unexpected, a new discovery, or a changed state. Common result patterns include -게 되었다, -았/었다, and -(으)ㄴ/는 것을 알게 되었다.
examples: 열심히 공부하다 보니 시험에서 좋은 성적을 받게 되었다. → 공부하다 (to study) + 다 보니 → Having studied hard, I ended up getting good grades on the exam.
다른 나라에 오래 살다 보니 그 나라 문화에 익숙해졌다. → 살다 (to live) + 다 보니 → Having lived in another country for a long time, I became accustomed to its culture.

notes.title: Experiential Nuance
notes.text: 다 보니 emphasizes that the result was not planned but emerged organically from repeated experience. It adds a reflective, almost autobiographical quality.

notes.title: No Intentional Result
notes.text: Avoid using 다 보니 when the result was deliberately sought. For intentional results, use -기 위해 or -(으)려고.

notes.title: Common Result Patterns
notes.text: Results frequently include -게 되었다 (came to), -았/었다 (ended up), -(으)ㄴ/는 줄 몰랐다 (didn't realize), and -ㄴ/은 것 같다 (seems that).

notes.title: Continuous Aspect
notes.text: The action or state in the first clause must be durative or repetitive. Momentary verbs (e.g., 죽다, 도착하다) do not work well with 다 보니 unless used iteratively.

explanation: A conditional-experiential pattern meaning 'if you keep doing...' or 'if one is continuously...' It predicts a likely result based on sustained action or state. Unlike 다 보니, which states a realized result, 다 본다면 presents a conditional prediction. Note: 볼면 in the original source is a typographical error for 본다면.

rules.title: Action Verb Formation
rules.description: Attach -다 본다면 to the plain form of an action verb. It forms a conditional clause predicting what will happen if the action is continued.
examples: 꾸준히 노력하다 본다면 언젠가 성공할 것이다. → 노력하다 (to make an effort) + 다 본다면 → If you keep making steady efforts, you will succeed someday.
매일 연습하다 본다면 실력이 늘게 될 것이다. → 연습하다 (to practice) + 다 본다면 → If you practice every day, your skills will improve.
다양한 사람을 만나다 본다면 세상을 넓게 볼 수 있다. → 만나다 (to meet) + 다 본다면 → If you meet various people, you can see the world broadly.

rules.title: Adjective Formation
rules.description: Attach -다 본다면 to the plain form of a descriptive verb. It predicts a consequence of remaining in a certain state.
examples: 건강을 소중히 여기다 본다면 생활 습관도 자연스럽게 바뀔 것이다. → 여기다 (to consider) + 다 본다면 → If you keep valuing your health, your lifestyle habits will naturally change.
어려운 문제를 계속 생각하다 본다면 해결 방법이 떠오를 수도 있다. → 생각하다 (to think) + 다 본다면 → If you keep thinking about a difficult problem, a solution may come to mind.

rules.title: Advice and Predictions
rules.description: This pattern is frequently used to give advice, warnings, or confident predictions based on accumulated experience or common wisdom.
examples: 시간이 지나다 본다면 모든 것이 해결될 것이다. → 지나다 (to pass) + 다 본다면 → If time keeps passing, everything will be resolved.
책을 많이 읽다 본다면 생각의 깊이가 달라진다. → 읽다 (to read) + 다 본다면 → If you read a lot of books, the depth of your thinking changes.

notes.title: Typographical Note
notes.text: The original source lists 볼면, which is a typographical error for 본다면 (the conditional form of 보다). Always use 본다면 in actual usage.

notes.title: Conditional vs. Realized
notes.text: 다 본다면 is conditional (will happen), while 다 보니 is factual (did happen). Do not confuse the two: 다 보니 looks back on experience, 다 본다면 looks forward to consequences.

notes.title: Verb-Focused Pattern
notes.text: While labeled A/V, 다 본다면 is most natural with action verbs. Adjective usage is limited and often sounds more natural when rephrased with a verbal expression.

notes.title: Advice Context
notes.text: This pattern is a staple of wisdom-giving and mentoring contexts. It frames advice as an inevitable outcome of persistence rather than a command.

explanation: Expresses that something is bound to happen or turn out a certain way as a natural, inevitable consequence. Often used for general truths or predictable outcomes. Common in formal and written Korean.

rules.title: Basic Form
rules.description: Attach 기 마련이다 to the verb or adjective stem. No tense or honorific changes occur within the grammar itself.

examples.english: Anyone is bound to make mistakes.
If you work hard, you are bound to succeed.

rules.title: With Adjectives
rules.description: Used with descriptive verbs to describe inevitable states or feelings that arise naturally.

examples.english: It's only natural to be happy to meet after a long time.

rules.title: Negative Contexts
rules.description: Often used to describe unavoidable negative situations that naturally accompany a condition.

examples.english: When there's a lot of work, it is bound to be difficult.

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: This grammar is commonly found in essays, editorials, and formal speech. It carries a slightly literary and authoritative tone.

notes.title: No Tense Variation
notes.text: Unlike many grammar points, 기 마련이다 does not change for past or future tense. The inevitability it expresses is timeless.

notes.title: General Truths
notes.text: It is most natural when stating universal truths, widely accepted observations about people, society, or nature.

explanation: Indicates that a result or situation changes according to or depending on something else. The outcome varies in direct relation to the preceding condition or process.

rules.title: Verb Form
rules.description: Attach 는 데 따라(서) to action verb stems to show that results vary depending on the action.

examples.breakdown: 노력하는 데 따라서 = 노력하 (stem) + 는 데 따라(서)
examples.english: The results vary depending on the effort.

examples.korean: 시간이 지나는 데 따라 점점 익숙해졌다.
examples.breakdown: 지나는 데 따라 = 지나 (stem) + 는 데 따라
examples.english: As time passed, I gradually got used to it.

rules.title: Adjective Form
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 데 따라(서) to descriptive verb stems. The outcome changes in accordance with the described state.

examples.korean: 날씨가 추운 데 따라 매출이 떨어졌다.
examples.breakdown: 추운 데 따라 = 춥 (stem) + 은 데 따라
examples.english: Sales dropped in accordance with the cold weather.

rules.title: Process Emphasis
rules.description: The verbal form emphasizes the ongoing process or changing state, unlike the simpler noun form 에 따라(서).

examples.korean: 상황이 변하는 데 따라 대응책을 수정해야 한다.
examples.breakdown: 변하는 데 따라 = 변하 (stem) + 는 데 따라
examples.english: We must revise our response plans as the situation changes.

notes.title: Versatile Usage
notes.text: While 데 따라(서) attaches to verbs and adjectives, the simpler 에 따라(서) is used with nouns to express the same dependency.

notes.title: Omitting 서
notes.text: The 서 in 따라서 can be omitted without changing the meaning. 따라 is slightly more concise and common in writing.

notes.title: Result Variation Required
notes.text: This grammar always implies that the outcome changes. It is not used when the result is fixed or constant regardless of the condition.

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: Frequently appears in academic writing, reports, and formal presentations to show conditional or proportional relationships.

explanation: Means 'since' or 'now that' a certain situation has occurred or state exists, one might as well take a particular action or accept a consequence. It implies making the best of a given circumstance.

rules.title: Verb Form
rules.description: Attach 는 바에(는) to action verb stems. The following clause usually contains a decision or recommendation.

examples.english: Since you're going, you might as well go early.
Now that we've started, we must see it through to the end.

rules.title: Adjective Form
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 바에(는) to descriptive verb stems. Used when a state prompts a particular course of action.

examples.korean: 멀리 온 바에 구경도 하고 가세요.
examples.english: Since you've come a long way, please look around before you go.

rules.title: Decision or Acceptance
rules.description: The second clause usually contains a decision, recommendation, or resigned acceptance of the situation.

examples.english: Since you're spending money, you might as well spend it properly.

notes.title: Resigned Acceptance
notes.text: This grammar often carries a nuance of resignation or pragmatic acceptance of an unavoidable situation.

notes.title: Directional Aspect
notes.text: Many common examples involve movement verbs like 가다 or 오다 because the grammar originally carried a directional sense.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Frequently used with 이미, 벌써, or 차라리 in the second clause to strengthen the 'might as well' nuance.

notes.title: Not Simple Causation
notes.text: Do not confuse this with 니까 or 아/어서. 바에는 implies a deliberate decision made in light of an existing situation, not just cause and effect.

explanation: Indicates doing something else while one is already engaged in a particular action or in a particular state. Equivalent to 'while I'm at it' or 'since I'm already...'

rules.title: Verb Form
rules.description: Attach 는 김에 to action verb stems. The main clause describes the primary activity, and the 김에 clause introduces the additional action.

examples.korean: 마트에 가는 김에 우유도 사 오세요.
examples.english: Since you're going to the mart, please buy some milk too.

examples.korean: 한국에 온 김에 한복도 입어 봤다.
examples.english: Since I came to Korea, I tried on a hanbok as well.

rules.title: Adjective Form
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 김에 to descriptive verb stems, though this is less common than with action verbs.

examples.english: Since I'm hungry already, let's eat before we go.

rules.title: Seizing the Opportunity
rules.description: Emphasizes taking advantage of an existing situation to accomplish an additional task efficiently.

examples.english: While I was at it exercising, I met a friend too.

notes.title: Convenience Nuance
notes.text: This grammar emphasizes doing something extra for convenience because the opportunity or situation already exists.

notes.title: Common in Spoken Korean
notes.text: Unlike many Level 5-6 grammar points, 는 김에 is widely used in everyday conversation and is essential for natural fluency.

notes.title: Time Proximity
notes.text: The two actions must happen around the same time or in close connection. You cannot use it for actions separated by days or weeks.

notes.title: Intent Required
notes.text: Usually the subject intends to do both actions, or at least takes advantage of the first situation to accomplish the second.

explanation: Means to consider, assume, or treat a situation as if it were a certain way. It downplays or recontextualizes the reality of a situation, often used as an act of reframing.

rules.title: Verb Form
rules.description: Attach 는 셈치다 to action verb stems. Often appears as 는 셈치고 when connecting to another clause.

examples.english: Please overlook it as if you don't know.
Let's try it once as if we're dead.

rules.title: Adjective Form
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ 셈치다 to descriptive verb stems to treat a state as if it were true.

examples.english: Don't worry about it as if it doesn't exist.

rules.title: With Connective Endings
rules.description: More commonly appears as 는 셈치고 to connect to another clause rather than as a standalone sentence ending.

examples.english: You can start again as if you failed.

notes.title: Counterfactual Nuance
notes.text: This grammar often treats a real situation counterfactually to change one's attitude or approach toward it.

notes.title: 셈이다 vs 셈치다
notes.text: 는 셈이다 means 'it is considered that...' while 는 셈치다 means 'to consider or treat as if...' The former is a statement; the latter is an act of reframing.

notes.title: Formal and Literary
notes.text: While understandable in speech, 셈치다 is more common in written contexts, dramas, and slightly formal dialogue.

notes.title: Speaker-Oriented
notes.text: Primarily used from the speaker's own perspective or in direct imperatives. For reporting others' assumptions, alternative expressions are preferred.

explanation: Expresses regret about a past action or inaction. The speaker laments that they did not do something they now realize they should have done. It is a retrospective expression of remorse.

rules.title: Basic Form
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ 걸 그랬다 to the verb or adjective stem. The grammar itself ends in 그랬다 to mark retrospective reflection.

examples.breakdown: 잘 걸 그랬어요 = 자 (stem) + ㄹ 걸 그랬다
examples.english: I should have gone to bed early.

examples.breakdown: 조용할 걸 그랬다 = 조용하 (stem) + ㄹ 걸 그랬다
examples.english: I should have been quieter back then.

rules.title: Expressing Reverse Regret
rules.description: When used with a negative verb or opposite meaning, it expresses regret about something one actually did.

examples.korean: 안 먹을 걸 그랬어. 배가 아파.
examples.breakdown: 안 먹을 걸 그랬어 = 먹 (stem) + 을 걸 그랬다
examples.english: I shouldn't have eaten. My stomach hurts.

rules.title: Common Time References
rules.description: Often preceded by time expressions like 그때, 어제, or 일찍 to anchor the regret in a specific past moment.

examples.korean: 어제 그 영화를 안 볼 걸 그랬어.
examples.breakdown: 안 볼 걸 그랬어 = 보 (stem) + ㄹ 걸 그랬다
examples.english: I shouldn't have watched that movie yesterday.

notes.title: Colloquial Expression
notes.text: This grammar is primarily used in spoken Korean and informal writing. It conveys emotion directly and vividly.

notes.title: Past-Only Reference
notes.text: Although the grammar itself ends in 그랬다, it is often preceded by explicit time expressions to pinpoint the missed opportunity.

notes.title: Cannot Be Used for Future
notes.text: This grammar strictly refers to past regrets. For present or future wishes, use 았/었으면 좋겠다 or (으)면 좋겠다 instead.

notes.title: Common in Daily Life
notes.text: It is one of the most frequently used advanced grammar points in everyday conversation among Korean speakers.

explanation: Expresses hesitation or indecision about whether to do something or not. It captures the internal conflict of weighing options without yet reaching a conclusion.

rules.title: Basic Form
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ까 말까 to the verb or adjective stem. It creates a binary hesitation between doing and not doing.

examples.english: I'm wondering whether to go or not.

examples.korean: 이 옷을 살까 말까 했는데 샀어요.
examples.english: I hesitated whether to buy these clothes or not, but I bought them.

rules.title: Expanded Form with 하다
rules.description: Can be expanded to (으)ㄹ까 말까 하다 when used as a predicate describing the ongoing state of hesitation.

examples.breakdown: 말할까 말까 해요 = 말하 (stem) + ㄹ까 말까 하다
examples.english: I'm torn between telling that person or not.

rules.title: With Adjectives
rules.description: Less common but possible with certain adjectives describing states or conditions one is considering.

examples.korean: 이 집이 좋을까 말까 생각해 봤어요.
examples.english: I wondered whether this house would be good or not.

notes.title: Binary Choice
notes.text: This grammar is specifically for binary indecision: to do or not to do. It does not work for choosing between two different actions.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: It frequently appears with 고민하다, 생각하다, or 했다 to explicitly state that the subject was deliberating.

notes.title: Not for Past Completed Actions
notes.text: While the main clause can be past tense, the 까 말까 itself refers to a contemplated but unresolved action at that time.

notes.title: Expressive and Colloquial
notes.text: Like (으)ㄹ 걸 그랬다, this grammar is common in spoken Korean and adds a relatable, human nuance to dialogue.

explanation: Expresses a tentative plan, thought, or intention to do something. It is less definite than (으)려고 하다 and implies the speaker is still considering or has not fully committed.

rules.title: Basic Form
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄹ까 하다 to the verb or adjective stem. It softens the statement by showing the idea is not yet final.

examples.english: I'm thinking of resting at home this weekend.
I am considering going to Korea next month.

rules.title: Expressing Hesitant Plans
rules.description: The speaker has an idea but hasn't fully committed. It serves as a polite way to announce an unconfirmed intention.

examples.english: I'm thinking of having salad for lunch.

rules.title: Tentative Suggestion
rules.description: Can be used to softly propose something without imposing on the listener.

examples.korean: 이 문제는 나중에 다시 논의할까 합니다.
examples.english: I propose we discuss this issue again later.

notes.title: Softer than 려고 하다
notes.text: (으)ㄹ까 하다 is more tentative and polite than (으)려고 하다. It leaves room for change or external input.

notes.title: First-Person Preference
notes.text: While technically usable with third-person subjects, it is overwhelmingly used with first-person subjects to express one's own tentative plans.

notes.title: Not for Strong Intentions
notes.text: Avoid this grammar if you want to express firm determination. Use (으)ㄹ 것이다 or 기로 했다 instead.

notes.title: Honorific Usage
notes.text: In formal settings, use (으)ㄹ까 합니다 or (으)ㄹ까 싶습니다. The latter is even more humble and common in business emails.

explanation: Expresses a wish or hope that something were different, typically regarding someone else's actions or an external situation. It often carries a sense of longing or earnest desire.

rules.title: Basic Form
rules.description: Attach 았/었/였으면 하다 to the verb or adjective stem. The past tense base is required even when wishing for future events.

examples.english: I hope the weather is nice tomorrow.
I wish my friend would come quickly.

rules.title: Third-Person Focus
rules.description: Most naturally used for wishes about others' behavior or external circumstances, not one's own direct intentional actions.

examples.english: I wish my parents were healthy.

rules.title: With Negative Verbs
rules.description: Can express a hope that something does NOT happen, using the negative form before the past base.

examples.breakdown: 상처받지 않았으면 해요 = 상처받 (stem) + 지 않았으면 해요
examples.english: I hope nobody gets hurt.

notes.title: First-Person Restriction
notes.text: This grammar is awkward when used for the speaker's own intentional actions. Saying '저는 갔으면 해요' is unnatural because it implies a lack of control over one's own actions.

notes.title: Past Base, Future Meaning
notes.text: Despite using 았/었/였, the wish often refers to future events. The past form creates a hypothetical distance that softens the wish.

notes.title: Emotional Register
notes.text: This grammar carries warmth and sincerity. It is common in letters, conversations with close friends, and personal reflections.

notes.title: Alternative: 좋겠다
notes.text: A simpler alternative is (으)면 좋겠다. 았/었/였으면 하다 is slightly more emotional and emphatic in tone.

explanation: Expresses uncertainty or lack of knowledge about a fact, state, or future event. It corresponds to 'I don't know whether...' and requires matching the tense marker to the time of the embedded clause.

rules.title: Present Tense
rules.description: Use 는지 with action verbs and (으)ㄴ지 with descriptive verbs for present states.

examples.english: I don't know if that person is coming.
I don't know if this food is spicy.

rules.title: Past Tense
rules.description: Use 았/었는지 for both action and descriptive verbs to express uncertainty about past events.

examples.english: I don't know if he came yesterday.
I don't know if the exam was difficult.

rules.title: Future Tense
rules.description: Use (으)ㄹ지 for uncertainty about future events or hypothetical situations.

examples.english: I don't know if it will rain tomorrow.
I don't know if this plan will succeed.

notes.title: Action vs. Descriptive Present
notes.text: A common mistake is using 는지 with descriptive verbs. Remember: descriptive verbs in the present use (으)ㄴ지, not 는지.

notes.title: Tense Agreement
notes.text: The tense marker inside the 지 clause must match the time you are uncertain about. Do not mix past markers with future contexts.

notes.title: 强化形式: 지도 모르다
notes.text: Adding 도 to make (으)ㄴ/는/(으)ㄹ지도 모르다 slightly strengthens the uncertainty. It can also mean 'might possibly' when used positively.

notes.title: Frequent in Speculation
notes.text: This grammar is essential for expressing doubt, speculation, and admission of ignorance in both formal and informal contexts.

explanation: The plain-style quotation marker used to directly quote or report statements, thoughts, or labels. It is the foundational form of the Korean indirect and direct speech system.

rules.title: Action Verbs (Present)
rules.description: Attach 는다고 to action verb stems for present tense statements in plain style.

examples.english: The student said, 'I study.'
My friend said he is running late.

rules.title: Descriptive Verbs (Present)
rules.description: Attach 다고 to descriptive verb stems for present tense statements.

examples.english: Mom said the weather is good.
The teacher said this book is difficult.

rules.title: Nouns (Identification)
rules.description: Attach (이)라고 to nouns for naming, identifying, or labeling.

examples.english: They said that person is a doctor.
This is called Hanbo.

notes.title: Action vs. Descriptive Distinction
notes.text: The most critical distinction is between action verbs (는다고) and descriptive verbs (다고). Mixing these is a common advanced-level error.

notes.title: Past Tense Quotation
notes.text: For past tense, both verb types take 았/었다고: 갔다고 (action), 예뻤다고 (descriptive). The 는/는 distinction disappears in the past.

notes.title: (이)라고 vs 라고
notes.text: Use 이라고 after consonant-ending nouns and 라고 after vowel-ending nouns. However, many native speakers colloquially use 라고 for both.

notes.title: Foundation of Reported Speech
notes.text: Mastering this plain form is essential because all other reported speech modifiers (는다는, 다는, 는다고 하다, etc.) are built upon it.

explanation: A modifier form derived from the plain quotation marker. It turns quoted content into an adjective that modifies a following noun, equivalent to 'the fact/statement that...'

rules.title: Modifying with Action Verbs
rules.description: Attach 는다는 to action verb stems to create a modifier for nouns.

examples.english: I heard the news that he is coming.
I heard words saying that I do well.

rules.title: Modifying with Descriptive Verbs
rules.description: Attach 다는 to descriptive verb stems to modify nouns with quoted states.

examples.english: There was a forecast that the weather would be good.
There are many opinions that this is difficult.

rules.title: Modifying with Nouns
rules.description: Attach (이)라는 to nouns to create modifiers that identify or label.

examples.english: I heard a story that that person is a doctor.
There is no evidence that this is real.

notes.title: Attributive Function
notes.text: The key difference from (으)ㄴ/는다고 is that (으)ㄴ/는다는 functions as an attributive modifier. It must be followed by a noun.

notes.title: Common Noun Collocations
notes.text: Frequently modifies nouns like 말 (words), 소식 (news), 사실 (fact), 이야기 (story), and 예보 (forecast).

notes.title: Cannot End a Sentence
notes.text: Unlike (으)ㄴ/는다고 하다, (으)ㄴ/는다는 cannot stand alone as a predicate. It requires a noun to modify.

notes.title: Embedded Information
notes.text: This grammar is crucial for embedding quoted information into noun phrases, a common feature in advanced Korean writing and journalism.

explanation: The standard reported speech construction. It reports what someone else said, using the plain quotation form plus the verb 하다 (to say/do).

rules.title: Reporting Statements (Action Verbs)
rules.description: Use 는다고 하다 for reporting action verb statements in the present tense.

examples.english: The student says he will come tomorrow.
My friend says he finished his homework.

rules.title: Reporting Statements (Descriptive Verbs)
rules.description: Use 다고 하다 for reporting descriptive verb statements in the present tense.

examples.english: My mother said the food is delicious.
The teacher said the exam is easy.

rules.title: Tense in the Main Clause
rules.description: The tense of 하다 reflects when the reporting happened, not when the original statement was made.

examples.korean: 그는 어제 내일 비가 온다고 했다.
examples.english: He said yesterday that it would rain tomorrow.
He is now saying that it is raining.

notes.title: Indirect Speech Foundation
notes.text: This is the most fundamental form of Korean indirect speech. Mastering it unlocks more complex forms like (으)ㄴ/는다고 들었다 and (으)ㄴ/는다고 생각하다.

notes.title: Honorific Conversion
notes.text: When reporting what an elder said, use 하시다 or 하셨다 in the main clause: 어머니께서 좋다고 하셨어요.

notes.title: Quotations vs. Reported Speech
notes.text: Direct quotations use exact words with quotation marks. Indirect speech with (으)ㄴ/는다고 하다 adapts pronouns and time expressions to the reporter's frame.

notes.title: Alternative Reporting Verbs
notes.text: Instead of 하다, you can use 묻다 (ask), 대답하다 (answer), 외치다 (shout), or 생각하다 (think) to add nuance.

explanation: Nominalizes quoted speech to present a fact, definition, or explanation. It literally means 'the thing that is said/known is...' and is used to clarify or emphasize an underlying truth.

rules.title: Explaining Facts with Action Verbs
rules.description: Use 는다는 것이다 to explain what an action means or represents.

examples.korean: 꾸준히 노력하는 것이 바로 성공한다는 것이다.
examples.english: Making steady effort is exactly what succeeding means.
His action means he is lying.

rules.title: Defining with Descriptive Verbs
rules.description: Use 다는 것이다 to define or explain states and conditions.

examples.english: It means this problem is difficult.
It means that person is kind.

rules.title: Used in Explanations and Conclusions
rules.description: Often appears at the end of explanations to summarize the core meaning or essence of a matter.

examples.korean: 이렇게 하는 게 바로 성공한다는 것이다.
examples.english: Doing it this way is exactly what it means to succeed.

notes.title: Explanatory Force
notes.text: This grammar adds weight and finality to an explanation. It is common in teaching, essays, and formal speeches where the speaker drives a point home.

notes.title: 것이다 Contraction
notes.text: In colloquial speech, 것이다 often contracts to 거다 or 거이다. In writing, it should remain as 것이다.

notes.title: Not for Simple Reporting
notes.text: Unlike (으)ㄴ/는다고 하다, this form is not for reporting what someone said. It is for explaining the meaning or essence of a situation.

notes.title: Academic and Formal Tone
notes.text: Due to its explanatory nature, this grammar appears frequently in textbooks, academic papers, and formal definitions.

explanation: Highlights a specific aspect, point, or observation about a situation. It is often used to draw attention to a noteworthy detail in formal analysis or discussion.

rules.title: Pointing Out with Action Verbs
rules.description: Use 는다는 점 to highlight an action-related observation or characteristic.

examples.korean: 그가 항상 시간을 지킨다는 점이 좋아요.
examples.english: I like the point that he is always punctual.
I confirmed the point that the students are participating hard.

rules.title: Pointing Out with Descriptive Verbs
rules.description: Use 다는 점 to highlight a state, condition, or quality.

examples.english: The advantage is the point that this method is effective.
We need to consider the point that the price is cheap.

rules.title: In Formal Evaluation
rules.description: Commonly used in assessments, reviews, and academic writing to enumerate strengths, weaknesses, or notable features.

examples.english: I acknowledge the point that this thesis is logical, but...

notes.title: Formal and Analytical
notes.text: This grammar is a staple of formal analysis, reports, and academic Korean. It frames observations as discrete points worthy of attention.

notes.title: Common with Evaluation Verbs
notes.text: Often paired with 고려하다 (consider), 인정하다 (acknowledge), 문제 삼다 (take issue with), and 강조하다 (emphasize).

notes.title: Not for Emotional Statements
notes.text: Because 점 implies a logical, countable point, this grammar feels out of place in highly emotional or intimate contexts.

notes.title: Synonym: 바
notes.text: In some contexts, (으)ㄴ/는다는 바 can replace 점 with a slightly more abstract nuance, though 점 is far more common in modern Korean.

explanation: Used to discuss, analyze, or evaluate a specific aspect or dimension of an action or state. This pattern is formed by combining the plain-form quotation of a verb/adjective with the abstract noun 측면. It is characteristic of formal essays, academic papers, and analytical writing.

rules.title: Forming the quoted modifier
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄴ/는다는 to the verb or adjective stem. Action verbs use -(으)ㄴ/는다는 (가다 → 간다는, 먹다 → 먹는다는, 하다 → 한다는). Adjectives use -다는 (어렵다 → 어렵다는, 크다 → 크다는).

examples.english: From the aspect that the economy is recovering

examples.korean: 이 문제가 중요하다는 측면을 간과해서는 안 된다
examples.breakdown: 이 문제(가) + 중요하 + 다는 + 측면(을) + 간과해서는 안 된다
examples.english: We must not overlook the aspect that this problem is important

rules.title: Common collocating nouns
rules.description: While 측면 is the focus here, this quoted modifier can also modify other abstract analytical nouns such as 관점, 의미, 이유, 가정, 전제. The structure remains the same.

examples.breakdown: 학생들(이) + 참여하 + 는다는 + 측면 + 에서 + 긍정적이다
examples.english: It is positive from the aspect that the students are participating

notes.title: Formal and written register
notes.text: This construction is rarely used in casual conversation. It belongs to the written, expository register expected at TOPIK Level 5–6.

notes.title: Difference from -는 것
notes.text: -는다는 측면 adds a layer of reported or quoted framing, treating the clause as a proposition under analysis, whereas -는 것 is more neutral.

notes.title: Common set phrases
notes.text: Frequently appears in set phrases like ~는 측면에서 볼 때 (when viewed from the aspect that...), ~는 측면이 있다 (there is an aspect that...).

explanation: Used to present or examine a particular perspective, viewpoint, or way of seeing a situation. The quoted clause modifies 관점, indicating the lens through which something is viewed. Common in analytical and argumentative writing.

rules.title: Quoted clause + 관점
rules.description: The pattern attaches -(으)ㄴ/는다는 to the predicate, creating a quoted modifier for the noun 관점. It frames the following statement as originating from a specific viewpoint.

examples.breakdown: 이것(이) + 발전하 + 는다는 + 관점 + 에서(는) + 옳은 결정이다
examples.english: From the perspective that this develops, it is the right decision

examples.korean: 그렇게 판단한다는 관점 자체가 문제라고 본다
examples.breakdown: 그렇게 + 판단하 + 는다는 + 관점 + 자체(가) + 문제(라고) + 본다
examples.english: I see the perspective of judging it that way itself as the problem

rules.title: Expanding the viewpoint
rules.description: You can qualify 관점 further with determiners or additional modifiers to specify whose perspective or what kind of perspective is being discussed.

examples.korean: 정부가 개입해야 한다는 관점도 일리가 있다
examples.breakdown: 정부(가) + 개입해야 한다는 + 관점 + 도 + 일리(가) 있다
examples.english: The perspective that the government should intervene also has merit

notes.title: Analytical register
notes.text: Like other -는다는 + abstract noun patterns, this is a hallmark of advanced expository Korean and signals objective analysis of differing viewpoints.

notes.title: Synonymous structures
notes.text: It overlaps in meaning with -(으)ㄴ/는다는 시각, -(으)ㄴ/는다는 견해, and -(으)ㄴ/는다는 입장, though 관점 emphasizes the analytical lens rather than a personal opinion.

notes.title: Avoid overuse in speech
notes.text: In spoken Korean, natives are more likely to say ~라고 생각하면 or ~는 관점으로 볼 때. Reserve -(으)ㄴ/는다는 관점 for essays and presentations.

explanation: Used to clarify, define, or discuss the significance or semantic content of an action or state. The quoted clause specifies what something 'means' in a given context. Found in formal analysis, criticism, and academic prose.

rules.title: Defining meaning or implication
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄴ/는다는 to the predicate and modify 의미. This frames the meaning of the preceding clause as something to be interpreted or explained.

examples.breakdown: 그의 말(은) + 포기하 + 겠다는 + 의미(로) + 들렸다
examples.english: His words sounded like the meaning that he would give up

examples.breakdown: 변화(가) + 필요하 + 다는 + 의미(를) + 전달하고 싶었다
examples.english: I wanted to convey the meaning that change is necessary

rules.title: Interpretive nuance
rules.description: This pattern often appears when interpreting symbols, texts, behavior, or policy, where the speaker assigns a specific meaning to something.

examples.korean: 이 조치는 개혁을 추진한다는 의미를 담고 있다
examples.breakdown: 이 조치(는) + 개혁(을) + 추진하 + 는다는 + 의미(를) + 담고 있다
examples.english: This measure contains the meaning that it is pushing ahead with reform

notes.title: Distinguished from 뜻
notes.text: 의미 is more abstract and analytical, whereas 뜻 is more literal. -(으)ㄴ/는다는 의미 fits philosophical, literary, and critical analysis.

notes.title: Frequent collocations
notes.text: Common pairings include 의미를 담다, 의미를 지니다, 의미를 부여하다, and 의미를 해석하다.

notes.title: Expansion with further clauses
notes.text: You can expand with ~의미에서 (in the meaning that...) or ~의미로 (with the meaning that...).

explanation: Used to present, explain, or question the reason or justification behind an action or state. The quoted clause specifies the content of the reason. This is a formal, written pattern common in argumentation and logical analysis.

rules.title: Stating a quoted reason
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄴ/는다는 to the predicate to create a quoted reason. The resulting noun phrase functions as the subject, object, or complement of a larger sentence.

examples.english: The reason that (you) are postponing it is hard to accept

examples.korean: 그가 떠난다는 이유를 설명해 달라고 했다
examples.breakdown: 그(가) + 떠나 + ㄴ다는 + 이유(를) + 설명해 달라고 했다
examples.english: I asked him to explain the reason that he is leaving

rules.title: Evaluating or questioning reasons
rules.description: Because 이유 carries an explanatory burden, this pattern frequently appears with evaluating predicates such as 타당하다, 부족하다, 명확하다, or 궁금하다.

examples.korean: 비용이 많이 든다는 이유만으로 포기할 수는 없다
examples.breakdown: 비용(이) + 많이 + 드 + 는다는 + 이유만으로 + 포기할 수는 없다
examples.english: You cannot give up solely for the reason that it costs a lot

notes.title: Difference from -기 때문에
notes.text: -기 때문에 states a causal relationship within a clause. -(으)ㄴ/는다는 이유 turns the entire causal proposition into a noun phrase that can be questioned or evaluated.

notes.title: Formal register marker
notes.text: In speech, people more naturally say 왜 그러냐고 or 왜 그런지. The -는다는 이유 construction elevates the register to formal exposition.

notes.title: Spacing with 이유
notes.text: 이유 is a noun; be careful not to write 이유에 or 이유를 incorrectly when the particle is required by the outer sentence grammar.

explanation: Used to set up a hypothetical scenario or assumption for the purpose of argument, analysis, or logical deduction. The quoted clause specifies the content of the assumption. This is a staple of formal logic and academic writing.

rules.title: Framing a hypothesis
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄴ/는다는 to the predicate and modify 가정. The resulting phrase introduces a premise that may or may not be true but is accepted for the sake of discussion.

examples.korean: 이 이론이 옳다는 가정 하에 연구를 진행했다
examples.breakdown: 이 이론(이) + 옳 + 다는 + 가정 + 하에 + 연구(를) + 진행했다
examples.english: We proceeded with the research under the assumption that this theory is correct

examples.breakdown: 실패하 + 는다는 + 가정 + 조차 + 하기 싫었다
examples.english: I didn't even want to make the assumption that I would fail

rules.title: Common set expressions
rules.description: 가정 often appears in set phrases such as 가정 하에, 가정을 세우다, and 가정이 성립하다. Mastering these collocations is essential for natural usage.

examples.korean: 모든 조건이 만족된다는 가정 하에서만 이 결과가 유효하다
examples.breakdown: 모든 조건(이) + 만족되 + 는다는 + 가정 + 하에서만 + 이 결과(가) + 유효하다
examples.english: This result is valid only under the assumption that all conditions are satisfied

notes.title: Logical and academic register
notes.text: This pattern is ubiquitous in mathematics, science, law, and philosophy papers where hypotheses must be explicitly stated before reasoning.

notes.title: Related nouns
notes.text: Similar structures include -(으)ㄴ/는다는 전제 (premise), -(으)ㄴ/는다는 조건 (condition), and -(으)ㄴ/는다는 명제 (proposition).

notes.title: Conditional connectives
notes.text: After introducing a 가정, the main clause often uses -(으)면, -다면, or -(으)ㄹ 때 to draw out implications.

explanation: Used to establish a foundational premise or precondition upon which a conclusion, argument, or action depends. The quoted clause specifies the content of the premise. More formal than 가정, 전제 emphasizes logical or contractual groundwork.

rules.title: Stating a foundational premise
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄴ/는다는 to the predicate and modify 전제. The phrase establishes something that must be accepted or true before the main assertion follows.

examples.breakdown: 양측(이) + 동의하 + 는다는 + 전제(로) + 협상(을) + 시작했다
examples.english: We began negotiations on the premise that both sides agree

examples.korean: 그가 참석한다는 전제가 깨지면 계획이 무산된다
examples.breakdown: 그(가) + 참석하 + 는다는 + 전제(가) + 깨지면 + 계획(이) + 무산된다
examples.english: If the premise that he attends collapses, the plan falls through

rules.title: Premise vs. assumption
rules.description: While 가정 is a working hypothesis, 전제 is a foundational condition. 전제 often appears in legal, logical, and policy contexts where conditions must be explicitly met.

examples.korean: 시장이 자유롭다는 전제 하에서 경제 모델을 분석했다
examples.breakdown: 시장(이) + 자유롭 + 다는 + 전제 + 하에서 + 경제 모델(을) + 분석했다
examples.english: We analyzed the economic model under the premise that the market is free

notes.title: Formal and contractual nuance
notes.text: 전제 carries a weight of obligation and foundation. It is frequently used in contracts, treaties, and formal agreements where clauses depend on stated conditions.

notes.title: Set phrase: 전제 하에
notes.text: The postpositional phrase -(으)ㄴ/는다는 전제 하에 / 전제 하에서 is a fixed, high-register expression meaning 'on the premise that...'

notes.title: Premise validity
notes.text: Sentences with 전제 often evaluate whether the premise is valid (성립하다), violated (위반되다), or broken (깨지다).

explanation: Expresses a strong concession meaning 'even if' or 'even though.' It indicates that the result in the main clause remains unchanged regardless of the condition. This is a formal, written pattern common in TOPIK Level 5–6 passages.

rules.title: Conjugation
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄹ지라도 to the verb or adjective stem. Use -을지라도 after a consonant (except ㄹ), -ㄹ지라도 after a vowel or ㄹ, and -일지라도 for 이다.

examples.english: Even if it rains, I will keep the promise
Even if it is difficult, I will not give up

examples.breakdown: 그(가) + 반대하 + ㄹ지라도 + 내 의견(을) + 말하겠다
examples.english: Even if he opposes it, I will say my opinion

rules.title: Nuance: unwavering result
rules.description: -(으)ㄹ지라도 implies that the speaker's will, judgment, or the objective result is firm and unaffected by the hypothetical condition. It is stronger and more literary than -아/어도.

examples.korean: 실패할지라도 도전하는 것 자체가 의미 있다
examples.breakdown: 실패하 + ㄹ지라도 + 도전하는 것 + 자체(가) + 의미(가) 있다
examples.english: Even if I fail, the act of challenging itself is meaningful

notes.title: Interchangeable with -아/어도?
notes.text: In casual speech, -아/어도 is preferred. -(으)ㄹ지라도 elevates the register and adds a tone of resolution or rhetorical conviction.

notes.title: Frequently tested in reading
notes.text: TOPIK reading passages often use -(으)ㄹ지라도 in opinion editorials and persuasive essays to show unwavering stance.

notes.title: Do not confuse with -(으)ㄹ지도 모르다
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ지라도 is a conditional connector. -(으)ㄹ지도 모르다 expresses possibility ('might'). Keep the structures separate.

explanation: A highly formal, literary, and forceful expression meaning 'even if,' 'even though,' or 'rather.' It often conveys a firm choice between two alternatives, accepting a lesser hardship to avoid a greater one. Characteristic of classical rhetoric and solemn declarations.

rules.title: Conjugation and core meaning
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄹ지언정 to the stem. Use -을지언정 after a consonant (except ㄹ) and -ㄹ지언정 after a vowel or ㄹ. It expresses a strong concession with an implied preference.

examples.english: Even if I die, I will not surrender
Even if I am poor, I want to live honestly

examples.korean: 혼자일지언정 그들과 함께 있고 싶지 않다
examples.breakdown: 혼자 + 이 + ㄹ지언정 + 그들과 함께 있고 싶지 않다
examples.english: Even if I am alone, I do not want to be with them

rules.title: Choosing the lesser of two evils
rules.description: Often used when the speaker accepts an undesirable outcome to avoid something worse. The second clause usually contains a negative or a firm resolution.

examples.breakdown: 늦 + 을지언정 + 서두르다가 실수(는) + 하지 않겠다
examples.english: Even if I am late, I will not rush and make a mistake

notes.title: Classical and solemn tone
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ지언정 carries an archaic, resolute, and even defiant tone. It is rarely used in everyday conversation but appears in literature, speeches, and formal rhetoric.

notes.title: Comparison with -(으)ㄹ지라도
notes.text: Both mean 'even if,' but -(으)ㄹ지언정 is more dramatic and choice-oriented, whereas -(으)ㄹ지라do is a neutral concession.

notes.title: Not for casual contexts
notes.text: Using -(으)ㄹ지언정 in casual speech can sound theatrical or archaic. Reserve it for written essays, literary analysis, and formal oratory.

explanation: Means 'even if (one) might...' or 'although it may...' It expresses a concession while acknowledging the possibility of the stated condition. Slightly softer than -(으)ㄹ지라도, this pattern blends possibility with concession and is typical of formal, reflective prose.

rules.title: Concession + possibility
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄹ지 to the stem, followed by 몰라도 (literally 'not knowing'). The whole construction means 'even though it might be the case that...'

examples.korean: 그가 올지 몰라도 일단 준비해 둬라
examples.breakdown: 그(가) + 오 + ㄹ지 + 몰라도 + 일단 + 준비해 둬라
examples.english: Even if he might come, be prepared just in case

examples.breakdown: 힘들 + 지 + 몰라도 + 끝까지 + 항상 합시다
examples.english: Even if it may be hard, let's try until the end

rules.title: Softened contrast
rules.description: Unlike the firmer -(으)ㄹ지라도, this pattern leaves room for uncertainty. It is often used when giving advice, making contingency plans, or reflecting on possibilities.

examples.korean: 비용이 많이 들지 몰라도 투자할 가치가 있다
examples.breakdown: 비용(이) + 많이 + 들 + 지 + 몰라도 + 투자할 가치(가) 있다
examples.english: Even if the cost may be high, it is worth investing

notes.title: Difference from -(으)ㄹ지도 모르다
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ지도 모르다 means 'might, possibly.' -(으)ㄹ지 몰라도 uses the same possibility but frames it as a concessive clause ('even if it might...').

notes.title: Written and reflective tone
notes.text: This pattern is more common in written essays and editorials than in spoken Korean, where people tend to say 혹시 ~일지도 모르니까.

notes.title: Common follow-up
notes.text: The main clause often contains a suggestion, command, or evaluative statement that holds regardless of the uncertain condition.

explanation: Expresses 'even if' or 'even though' with a hypothetical or retrospective nuance. It attaches to the verb or adjective stem to present a condition under which the main clause still holds. Common in formal speech and writing, as well as thoughtful reflection.

rules.title: Retrospective conditional form
