rules.description: Attach -더라도 directly to the verb or adjective stem. This combines the retrospective marker 더 with the conditional 라도. It presents a condition as something recalled or hypothetically experienced.

examples.breakdown: 내(가) + 가 + 더라도 + 소용(이) 없을 것이다
examples.english: Even if I go, it will be of no use

examples.breakdown: 그(가) + 알 + 더라도 + 놀라지는 않을 거야
examples.english: Even if he finds out, he probably won't be surprised

examples.breakdown: 어렵 + 더라도 + 한 번 + 시도해 보자
examples.english: Even if it's difficult, let's try it once

rules.title: Hypothetical past form
rules.description: In some contexts, 더라도 can also attach to the past tense stem (-았/었-) to mean 'even if (something) had happened.' This strengthens the hypothetical nuance.

examples.breakdown: 그때 + 가 + 았더라도 + 결과(는) + 같았을 것이다
examples.english: Even if I had gone then, the result would have been the same

notes.title: Retrospective nuance
notes.text: The 더 in -더라도 carries a retrospective or hypothetical feel, as if the speaker is imagining or recalling the situation before stating the outcome.

notes.title: Comparison with -(으)ㄹ지라도
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ지라도 is more common in essays. -더라도 feels slightly more personal or situational and is acceptable in both formal speech and writing.

notes.title: Do not confuse with -더라고요
notes.text: -더라고요 is an experiential evidential ending ('I noticed that...'). -더라도 is a conditional ('even if'). Context and intonation make the distinction clear.

explanation: Means 'not only... but also.' It is an additive connective that expands the scope of a statement by adding a second, usually stronger or complementary, element. It is one of the most important advanced additive patterns for TOPIK Level 5–6.

rules.title: Conjugation and basic usage
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄹ뿐만 아니라 to the verb or adjective stem. Use -을뿐만 아니라 after a consonant (except ㄹ) and -ㄹ뿐만 아니라 after a vowel or ㄹ. The second clause is usually introduced by 도, 또한, or 게다가.

examples.korean: 그는 공부를 잘할뿐만 아니라 울동도 출중하다
examples.breakdown: 그(는) + 공부(를) + 잘하 + ㄹ뿐만 아니라 + 울동(도) + 출중하다
examples.english: Not only does he study well, but he is also outstanding at sports

examples.korean: 이 제품은 저렴할뿐만 아니라 품질도 우수하다
examples.breakdown: 이 제품(은) + 저렴하 + ㄹ뿐만 아니라 + 품질(도) + 우수하다
examples.english: This product is not only inexpensive, but its quality is also excellent

rules.title: Expanding to clauses and nouns
rules.description: -(으)ㄹ뿐만 아니라 can connect two full clauses or two parallel noun phrases. When used with nouns, 이다 becomes -(이)ㄸ뿐만 아니라 or 더 나아가서.

examples.breakdown: 회사원 + 이 + ㄹ뿐만 아니라 + 작가 + 이기도 하다
examples.english: Not only is he an office worker, but he is also a writer

examples.korean: 서울에서 살았을뿐만 아니라 부산에서도 살아 봤다
examples.breakdown: 서울 + 에서 + 살 + 았을뿐만 아니라 + 부산 + 에서도 + 살아 봤다
examples.english: Not only did I live in Seoul, but I also lived in Busan

notes.title: Most common advanced additive
notes.text: This is the standard advanced replacement for -고도 and the most frequently tested additive pattern at Level 5–6.

notes.title: Spacing note
notes.text: 뿐만 아니라 is typically written as one unit with the preceding ending attached. There is no space between -(으)ㄹ and 뿐만 아니라.

notes.title: Frequent pairing in the second clause
notes.text: The second clause often uses 도, 또한, 게다가, 나아가, or 오히려 to reinforce the additive or contrastive expansion.

explanation: Means 'all you have to do is...' or 'as long as you...' It expresses a sufficient condition: if the stated action is performed, the desired result will follow. This pattern is direct and practical, often used in instructions, advice, and logical explanation.

rules.title: Sufficient condition
rules.description: Attach -기만 하면 to the verb or adjective stem. The pattern breaks down as -기 (nominalizer) + -만 (only) + 하면 (if one does). It states the single necessary action for a result.

examples.korean: 열심히 하기만 하면 성공할 수 있다
examples.breakdown: 열심히 + 하 + 기만 하면 + 성공할 수 있다
examples.english: All you have to do is work hard, and you can succeed

examples.breakdown: 버튼(을) + 누르 + 기만 하면 + 문(이) + 열린다
examples.english: All you have to do is press the button, and the door opens

examples.korean: 건강하기만 하면 다른 것은 바라지 않는다
examples.breakdown: 건강하 + 기만 하면 + 다른 것(은) + 바라지 않는다
examples.english: As long as I am healthy, I don't wish for anything else

rules.title: Emphasis on simplicity
rules.description: Often used to reassure or simplify a process. It implies that no other conditions or complications are necessary.

examples.english: All you have to do is submit the application

notes.title: Difference from -(으)면
notes.text: -(으)면 is a general conditional. -기만 하면 adds the nuance of 'only' or 'simply,' emphasizing that the condition is minimal and sufficient.

notes.title: Common in instructions
notes.text: This pattern is very common in manuals, recipes, and advice columns where the goal is to show how easy a process is.

notes.title: Negative nuance with critical context
notes.text: When used with negative or extreme verbs, it can sound dismissive: 죽기만 하면 된다 ('All you have to do is die')—be careful with tone.

explanation: Expresses possibility or uncertainty, meaning 'might,' 'may,' or 'possibly.' The optional 도 adds slight emphasis. It is a fundamental advanced pattern for conveying epistemic uncertainty and appears frequently in both spoken and written Korean at Level 5–6.

rules.title: Basic possibility
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄹ지 to the stem, followed by 모르다. Use -(으)ㄹ지도 모르다 for slightly stronger emphasis. The negation of knowledge (모르다) metaphorically conveys uncertainty about the outcome.

examples.english: It might rain tomorrow
He might have already left

rules.title: With and without 도
rules.description: Without 도, the expression is neutral. With 도, it adds the nuance of 'even' or 'also'—as in 'not knowing even whether.' Both are grammatically correct and common.

examples.breakdown: 회의(가) + 연기되 + ㄹ지 + 모르니 + 확인해 봐라
examples.english: The meeting might be postponed, so check it

examples.breakdown: 그 방법(이) + 효과(가) + 있 + 을지도 + 모르겠다
examples.english: That method might be effective

notes.title: Most common possibility expression
notes.text: This is the standard advanced replacement for -겠- and -나 보다 when expressing objective uncertainty.

notes.title: Past and future application
notes.text: You can attach -(으)ㄹ지(도) 모르다 to past stems (-았/었을지) to mean 'might have,' or to future/positive stems for 'might.'

notes.title: Common endings
notes.text: Frequently appears with sentence endings like -겠다, -(으)니까, or -아/어서 to soften statements or give precautionary advice.

explanation: Means 'might not' or 'may not.' It is the negative counterpart to -(으)ㄹ지 모르다, expressing negative possibility or uncertainty about a non-occurrence. This pattern is formal, literary, and less common in everyday speech, making it a hallmark of advanced written Korean.

rules.title: Negative possibility
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄹ지 to the stem, followed by 않다. The structure literally means 'it is not the case that it will (happen),' functioning as a formal expression of negative possibility.

examples.english: He might not come
The situation might not improve

rules.title: Written and formal nuance
rules.description: Unlike -지 않을지도 모르다, which is longer but more natural in speech, -(으)ㄹ지 않다 is compact and literary. It appears in editorials, literature, and formal documents.

examples.korean: 이 정책이 성공할지 않다는 우려가 있다
examples.breakdown: 이 정책(이) + 성공하 + ㄹ지 + 않다는 + 우려(가) 있다
examples.english: There is concern that this policy might not succeed

notes.title: Not for casual speech
notes.text: In spoken Korean, natives overwhelmingly prefer 안 올지도 모른다 or 올지 않을지도 몰라. -(으)ㄹ지 않다 sounds archaic or highly formal.

notes.title: Relationship to -(으)ㄹ지 모르다
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ지 모르다 = 'might.' -(으)ㄹ지 않다 = 'might not.' They form a positive-negative pair of epistemic possibility.

notes.title: Often nominalized
notes.text: In advanced writing, this pattern frequently appears nominalized as -(으)ㄹ지 않다는 (것) or -(으)ㄹ지 않음, serving as the subject or object of evaluative predicates.

explanation: Means 'know whether' or 'don't know whether.' It is used to express knowledge or ignorance about a possibility, outcome, or fact. The quoted clause functions as an embedded indirect question, and the main clause uses 알다 or 모르다 to indicate the epistemic stance.

rules.title: Embedded indirect question
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄹ지 to the stem to form an embedded question about possibility. The main clause then states whether the subject knows or does not know the answer to that question.

examples.english: I don't know when he will come

examples.breakdown: 이 방법(이) + 맞 + 을지 + 잘 + 모르겠어요
examples.english: I am not sure whether this method is right

examples.korean: 그녀가 사실을 알고 있을지 알 수 없다
examples.breakdown: 그녀(가) + 사실(을) + 알고 있 + 을지 + 알 수 없다
examples.english: It is impossible to know whether she knows the truth

rules.title: With 알다 and 모르다
rules.description: 모르다 is far more common and appears in a wide range of contexts. 알다 is rarer and usually appears in negative or potential forms like 알 수 있다/없다 or 알다가 몰라도, because direct knowledge of an uncertain future is semantically limited.

examples.english: No one knows who will take responsibility

rules.title: Expanding with question words
rules.description: The embedded clause can include interrogative words such as 누가, 무엇을, 어디서, 언제, 왜, or 얼마나 to create more specific indirect questions.

examples.breakdown: 회의(가) + 몇 시에 + 끝나 + ㄹ지 + 모르겠다
examples.english: I don't know what time the meeting will end

notes.title: Core indirect question pattern
notes.text: This is one of the two primary advanced indirect question patterns (along with -는지). -(으)ㄹ지 specifically questions future possibility or uncertainty.

notes.title: Frequent in reported speech
notes.text: Often used in reported or quoted thought: ~일지 모른다고 생각하다, ~일지 모르겠다고 말하다.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Frequently paired with 잘 모르겠다, 전혀 모른다, 알 수 없다, and 알 듯 모르다.

explanation: A high-level, often literary construction used to emphatically negate the first clause while simultaneously negating (or asserting the impossibility of) a second clause. It translates to 'far from X, (one can't even) Y.' The second clause is always negative or opposite in polarity, creating a strong rhetorical contrast.

rules.title: Basic Structure: V-기는커녕 + Negative Clause
rules.description: Attach -기는커녕 to a verb stem to set up the first action as something that didn't happen, then follow with a clause stating that something even more basic also didn't happen.

examples.breakdown: 밥(food) + 을(object particle) + 먹(eat) + 기는커녕(far from) + 물(water) + 도(even) + 마시(drink) + 지 못했다(could not)
examples.english: Far from eating, I couldn't even drink water.

examples.korean: 잠을 자기는커녕 눈을 붙일 수도 없었다.
examples.breakdown: 잠(sleep) + 을(object particle) + 자(sleep) + 기는커녕(far from) + 눈(eyes) + 을(object particle) + 붙일(close) + 수도 없었다(could not even)
examples.english: Far from sleeping, I couldn't even close my eyes.

rules.title: Noun Equivalents: N-은/는커녕
rules.description: With nouns, use -(이)는커녕 (often contracted or appearing as -은커녕). The pattern intensifies the negation by contrasting two things on a scale.

examples.breakdown: 돈(money) + 은커녕(let alone) + 시간(time) + 도(even) + 없었다(did not have)
examples.english: Let alone money, I didn't even have time.

notes.title: Strong Negation Required
notes.text: The clause following -기는커녕 MUST be negative. Using a positive clause is grammatically unacceptable. This is a fixed-polarity construction.

notes.title: Written / Rhetorical Register
notes.text: While understood in speech, this grammar carries a formal, emphatic nuance. It appears frequently in editorials, essays, and literary prose on TOPIK II reading sections.

notes.title: Interchangeable with -기는커녕
notes.text: -기는커녕 and -은/는커녕 are often taught together. The key is that the following clause must be lower on a hierarchy of expectation and must be negated.

explanation: An advanced, formal expression marking the culmination or final stage of a process. It indicates that a situation has reached the point of doing something, often after a gradual progression. It is highly literary and common in historical narratives, academic writing, and formal essays.

rules.title: V-기에 이르다: Reaching a Point
rules.description: Attach -기에 이르다 to a verb stem to express that events progressed to the stage where the action occurs. The subject is usually a situation or process, not a person.

examples.korean: 두 나라 간 분쟁이 전면전으로 번지기에 이륙했다.
examples.breakdown: 두(two) + 나라(countries) + 간(between) + 분쟁(conflict) + 이(subject particle) + 전면전(full-scale war) + 로(into) + 번지(spread) + 기에 이륙했다(reached the point of)
examples.english: The conflict between the two countries reached the point of escalating into full-scale war.

examples.korean: 그런 결론에 이르기까지 수많은 논의가 있었다.
examples.breakdown: 그런(such) + 결론(conclusion) + 에(to) + 이르(reach) + 기까지(until) + 수많은(countless) + 논의(discussions) + 가(subject particle) + 있었다(there were)
examples.english: Countless discussions took place before reaching such a conclusion.

rules.title: Noun Form: -에 이르다
rules.description: When used with a noun, 에 이르다 means 'to reach/extend to.' Combined with -기, it creates a verbal noun construction.

examples.korean: 그의 연구는 학계에 큰 반향을 일으키기에 이륙했다.
examples.breakdown: 그의(his) + 연구(research) + 는(topic particle) + 학계(academic circles) + 에(in) + 큰(great) + 반향(repercussion) + 을(object particle) + 일으키(raise) + 기에 이륙했다(reached the point of)
examples.english: His research reached the point of causing a great stir in academic circles.

notes.title: Subject Restriction
notes.text: The subject is typically an impersonal situation, event, or condition. Saying '내가 가기에 이륙했다' is unnatural; use 개인이 ~하게 되다 instead.

notes.title: Formal Register Marker
notes.text: -기에 이르다 is almost exclusively found in formal written Korean. It elevates the tone significantly and signals a formal progression in narrative or argument.

notes.title: Past Tense Forms
notes.text: The past tense conjugation is 이륙했다. The non-past is 이른다. These must be conjugated accurately on the verb 이르다.

notes.title: Not Interchangeable with -게 되다
notes.text: While similar, -기에 이르다 implies a longer, more consequential buildup. -게 되다 is neutral and used in all registers. Do not substitute them carelessly in formal writing.

explanation: A formal construction indicating that the outcome of a situation is entirely contingent on a specific factor. It translates to 'depend on,' 'hinge on,' or 'rest upon.' It is a staple of expository writing and formal speech, particularly in policy, academic, and business contexts.

rules.title: V-기에 달려 있다 / N-에 달려 있다
rules.description: Use -기에 달려 있다 after a verb stem, or -에 달려 있다 after a noun, to state that a result depends on that factor. The nuance is absolute contingency.

examples.korean: 프로젝트의 성공이 팀원들의 협력에 달려 있다.
examples.breakdown: 프로젝트의(project's) + 성공(success) + 이(subject particle) + 팀원들의(team members') + 협력(cooperation) + 에 달려 있다(depends on)
examples.english: The success of the project depends on the team members' cooperation.

examples.korean: 문제 해결이 정부의 의지에 달려 있다.
examples.breakdown: 문제(problem) + 해결(resolution) + 이(subject particle) + 정부의(government's) + 의지(will) + 에 달려 있다(depends on)
examples.english: Solving the problem hinges on the government's will.

rules.title: Expanded Form: V-느냐에 달려 있다
rules.description: A common variant uses -느냐에 달려 있다 to frame the dependent factor as a choice or conditional action, meaning 'whether or not one does X depends on...'

examples.breakdown: 시장의(market's) + 반응(response) + 이(subject particle) + 좋(be good) + 으냐에 달려 있다(depends on whether)
examples.english: It depends on whether the market's reaction is good.

notes.title: Fixed Expression
notes.text: -에 달려 있다 is a highly fixed idiom. The verb 달리다 here is not the standard 'run' meaning; it is an archaic/literary usage meaning 'to hang upon' or 'be suspended from.'

notes.title: Formal Context Only
notes.text: This grammar is standard in formal essays, news reports, and official speeches. In casual conversation, use ~에 따라 달라요 or ~에 따라서예요.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Frequently collocates with nouns like 성공(success), 결과(result), 운命(fate), 미래(future), and 의지(will).

explanation: A descriptive grammar pattern meaning 'prone to,' 'liable to,' or 'tends to.' It attaches to process verbs or negative states to indicate a natural disposition toward a certain outcome. Common in written explanations of characteristics, risks, and tendencies.

rules.title: V-기 쉽다: Natural Tendency
rules.description: Attach -기 쉽다 to a verb to express that the action or state occurs easily or readily. Often used with negative or warning contexts.

examples.breakdown: 긴장(be nervous) + 하면(if) + 실수(mistake) + 하(do) + 기 쉽다(prone to)
examples.english: When nervous, one is prone to making mistakes.

examples.breakdown: 이(this) + 약(medicine) + 는(topic particle) + 부작용(side effects) + 가(subject particle) + 나타나(appear) + 기 쉽다(prone to)
examples.english: This medicine is prone to causing side effects.

rules.title: Adjective Stem Usage
rules.description: While primarily used with verbs, -기 쉽다 can follow adjective stems nominalized with -아/어지기 to indicate a tendency to become a certain way.

examples.breakdown: 이(this) + 지역(area) + 는(topic particle) + 오염(pollution) + 되(become) + 기 쉽다(prone to)
examples.english: This area is prone to becoming polluted.

notes.title: Negative Connotation
notes.text: Although neutral in form, -기 쉽다 is overwhelmingly used for undesirable or risky tendencies in formal discourse. Using it for positive traits (e.g., 행복하기 쉽다) is rare and awkward.

notes.title: Comparison with -는 경향이 있다
notes.text: -는 경향이 있다 means 'tends to' and is neutral. -기 쉽다 implies an inherent susceptibility or ease of occurrence, making it slightly stronger and more objective.

notes.title: Antonym: -기 어렵다
notes.text: The direct antonym is -기 어렵다 (hard to). Both share the same structural logic but convey opposite semantic values.

explanation: A descriptive pattern indicating difficulty, resistance, or improbability in performing an action. It is the direct antonym of -기 쉽다 and is widely used in both formal analysis and general description to characterize something as hard to achieve or unlikely to occur.

rules.title: V-기 어렵다: General Difficulty
rules.description: Attach -기 어렵다 to a verb stem to state that the action is difficult to perform. The subject is often the action itself or a sentence topic describing conditions.

examples.breakdown: 그의(his) + 말(words) + 는(topic particle) + 믿(believe) + 기 어렵다(hard to)
examples.english: His words are hard to believe.

examples.breakdown: 이론(theory) + 을(object particle) + 현실(reality) + 에(to) + 적용(apply) + 하(do) + 기 어렵다(hard to)
examples.english: It is difficult to apply the theory to reality.

rules.title: A-기 어렵다 via -아/어지기
rules.description: To express that becoming a certain state is difficult, attach -아/어지기 어렵다 to an adjective stem.

examples.breakdown: 상황(situation) + 이(subject particle) + 개선(improvement) + 되(become) + 기 어렵다(hard to)
examples.english: The situation is difficult to improve.

notes.title: Synonyms: -기 힘들다
notes.text: -기 힘들다 is semantically identical in most contexts but slightly more colloquial. -기 어렵다 is preferred in formal, academic, and official writing.

notes.title: Impersonal Subject
notes.text: The construction is often used impersonally: 이해하기 어렵다 (it is hard to understand). When a person is the subject, the dative particle -에게 is often used: 나에게는 이해하기 어렵다.

notes.title: Conjugation Note
notes.text: 어렵다 is an ㅂ-irregular adjective: 어려워요, 어렵습니다, 어려웠습니다. Ensure correct conjugation when transforming the sentence.

explanation: An advanced idiom meaning 'it depends on how one does it' or 'it's up to the way something is done.' It attaches to a verb stem and relativizes the quality or outcome of an action entirely to the manner of its execution. It is common in evaluative, editorial, and advisory contexts.

rules.title: V-기 나름이다: Contingent on Method
rules.description: Attach -기 나름이다 to a verb stem. The sentence subject is typically the gerund (the -기 phrase), and the overall meaning is that the result varies according to approach.

examples.breakdown: 어떤(any) + 책(book) + 이든(even if) + 읽(read) + 기 나름이다(depends on how)
examples.english: Any book is good or bad depending on how you read it.

examples.breakdown: 성공(success) + 는(topic particle) + 노력(effort) + 하(do) + 기 나름이다(depends on how)
examples.english: Success depends on how you make the effort.

rules.title: Noun Modifier: V-기 나름인
rules.description: When modifying a noun, use -기 나름인 to create an attributive phrase meaning 'which depends on how one does X.'

examples.breakdown: 해석(interpretation) + 하(do) + 기 나름인(depends on how) + 문제(problem) + 다(is)
examples.english: It is a problem whose answer depends on how you interpret it.

notes.title: Fixed Noun: 나름
notes.text: 나름 is a dependent noun meaning 'one's own way' or 'method.' It cannot stand alone without a modifier. Always preceded by -기 or similar attributive forms.

notes.title: Neutral / Evaluative Tone
notes.text: Unlike -기 쉽다 or -기 어렵다, this grammar is neutral. It shifts responsibility or variability to the agent's method, not to inherent difficulty.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Frequently used with 노력하다, 해석하다, 사용하다, 받아들이다, and 활용하다 to emphasize that the value of an action is method-dependent.

explanation: A highly literary, idiomatic expression meaning 'there is no match for' or 'extremely.' It is used with adjectives (or nominalized states) to express an extreme degree of emotion or quality. It is classic, formal, and somewhat archaic, appearing in literary criticism, essays, and stylized speech.

rules.title: A-기 짝이 없다: Extreme Degree
rules.description: Attach -기 짝이 없다 to an adjective stem (or state verb nominalized with -기) to express that the quality is unmatched or extreme. The literal meaning is 'there is no pair/match for X.'

examples.korean: 그 소식을 듣고 황당하기 짝이 없었다.
examples.breakdown: 그(that) + 소식(news) + 를(object particle) + 듣(listen) + 고(and) + 황당(absurd) + 하(be) + 기 짝이 없었다(there was no match/extremely)
examples.english: Hearing the news, I was extremely dumbfounded.

examples.breakdown: 가을(autumn) + 하늘(sky) + 는(topic particle) + 맑(clear) + 기 짝이 없다(there is no match/extremely)
examples.english: The autumn sky is extremely clear.

rules.title: Emotional Adjectives
rules.description: The pattern strongly collocates with psychological and emotional adjectives such as 기쁘다, 슬프다, 황당하다, 쓸쓸하다, and 고맙다.

examples.breakdown: 고독(loneliness) + 하(be) + 기 짝이 없는(without match/extremely) + 밤(night) + 이었다(was)
examples.english: It was an extremely lonely night.

notes.title: Literary Register
notes.text: This is a highly literary expression. Using it in casual conversation sounds archaic or theatrical. Reserve it for formal writing, literature, or stylized speech.

notes.title: Noun 짝 Meaning
notes.text: 짝 originally means 'pair,' 'match,' or 'mate.' 짝이 없다 literally means 'there is no pair,' which idiomatically signals an extreme, incomparable degree.

notes.title: Cannot Use with Action Verbs
notes.text: -기 짝이 없다 is almost exclusively used with descriptive verbs (adjectives) or state nouns. Using it with pure action verbs (e.g., 뛰기 짝이 없다) is ungrammatical.

explanation: Another highly literary idiom synonymous with -기 짝이 없다, meaning 'extremely' or 'there is no limit to.' It conveys an extreme degree of a quality or emotion and is characterized by its classical, elegant tone. The noun 그지 is an archaic form meaning 'limit' or 'boundary.'

rules.title: A-기 그지없다: Extreme State
rules.description: Attach -기 그지없다 to an adjective stem to express an extreme or boundless degree. The literal sense is 'there is no limit/boundary to X.'

examples.breakdown: 그의(his) + 도움(help) + 에(to) + 고맙(grateful) + 기 그지없다(there is no limit/extremely)
examples.english: I am extremely grateful for his help.

examples.breakdown: 겨울(winter) + 바닷가(beach) + 는(topic particle) + 쓸쓸(lonely) + 하(be) + 기 그지없다(there is no limit/extremely)
examples.english: The winter beach is extremely desolate.

rules.title: Prenominal Form: -기 그지없는
rules.description: Use -기 그지없는 to modify a noun directly, attributing an extreme quality to it.

examples.breakdown: 기쁘(joyful) + 기 그지없는(without limit/extremely) + 소식(news) + 이었다(was)
examples.english: It was extremely joyful news.

notes.title: Classical / Archaic Tone
notes.text: -기 그지없다 carries an elegant, classical flavor. It is ideal for literature, formal essays, and ceremonial speech, but entirely out of place in casual conversation.

notes.title: 그지 Etymology
notes.text: 그지 comes from the archaic noun meaning 'limit' or 'end.' 그지없다 = 'without limit.' This etymology explains why the expression conveys boundlessness.

notes.title: Interchangeable with -기 짝이 없다
notes.text: In most literary contexts, -기 그지없다 and -기 짝이 없다 are interchangeable. -기 그지없다 sounds slightly softer or more lyrical; -기 짝이 없다 sounds more emphatic.

explanation: A formal, humble construction meaning 'nothing more than,' 'just,' or 'merely.' It is used to downplay an action, state, or expectation by presenting it as the only possible or remaining option. It is ubiquitous in formal apologies, modest statements, and official declarations.

rules.title: A/V-(으)ㄹ 따름이다: The Only Thing
rules.description: Attach -(으)ㄹ 따름이다 to an adjective or verb stem. Use 을 after a consonant (e.g., 읽을 따름이다) and ㄹ after a vowel (e.g., 할 따름이다). It presents the action as the sole or mere reality.

examples.breakdown: 저는(I) + 맡은(assigned) + 일(work) + 를(object particle) + 하(do) + 었을(past attributive) + 따름입니다(is merely)
examples.english: I merely did the work I was assigned.
Now there is nothing to do but wait.

rules.title: Downplaying with Humble Nuance
rules.description: When used with verbs of speaking or acting, -(으)ㄹ 따름이다 adds a layer of humility, suggesting the speaker has no special authority or intention beyond the stated action.

examples.breakdown: 제(my) + 개인적인(personal) + 의견(opinion) + 일(be) + 따름입니다(is merely)
examples.english: This is merely my personal opinion.

notes.title: Formal Register Only
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 따름이다 is standard in formal and official language. It would sound stilted or pretentious in everyday conversation.

notes.title: Comparison with -(으)ㄹ 뿐이다
notes.text: -(으)ㄹ 뿐이다 also means 'only/just,' but 따름이다 carries a stronger sense of limitation, resignation, or humble acceptance of the sole available option.

notes.title: Common Set Phrases
notes.text: Common collocations include 기다릴 따름이다, 기대할 따름이다, 알릴 따름이다, and 노력할 따름이다.

notes.title: No Imperative or Propositive Use
notes.text: Because 따름이다 implies a resigned or humble statement of fact, it cannot be used with imperative or propositive endings. Saying 가라 따름이다 is ungrammatical.

explanation: A literary and formal nominalizer meaning 'called,' 'named,' or 'what is called.' It is a contraction of -(이)라고 하는 and is used when defining, quoting, or philosophizing about a noun. It is common in essay titles, definitions, and introspective writing.

rules.title: N-(이)란: Defining a Concept
rules.description: Attach -이란 to nouns ending in a consonant and -란 to nouns ending in a vowel. It sets up a definition or meditation on the nature of the noun.

examples.breakdown: 사랑(love) + 이란(what is called) + 무엇인가(what is it)
examples.english: What is this thing called love?

examples.breakdown: 인간(human) + 이란(called) + 존재(being) + 는(topic particle) + 끊임없이(constantly) + 배운다(learns)
examples.english: The being called human constantly learns.

rules.title: N-란 as Title / Topic Marker
rules.description: -란 is frequently used in essay and book titles, as well as in opening sentences that introduce a subject for philosophical or analytical discussion.

examples.english: On Happiness / What is Called Happiness

notes.title: Contraction of -(이)라고 하는
notes.text: -(이)란 is a literary contraction of -(이)라고 하는. It retains the full semantic weight of the longer form but with greater elegance and brevity.

notes.title: Formal / Literary Context
notes.text: This form is standard in essays, literature, and formal speeches. In casual conversation, use -(이)라는 거 or -(이)라는 것.

notes.title: Particle Compatibility
notes.text: After -(이)란, particles like 은/는, 을/를, and 이/가 can follow. Example: 자유란은(what is called freedom as a topic) or 자유란 것(what is called freedom as a thing).

explanation: The standard attributive form of quotation and naming, meaning 'called' or 'what is called.' It is the full, non-contracted form of -(이)라고 하는 and is used to modify nouns by specifying their name, title, or conceptual label. It appears across all registers but is especially common in formal and expository writing.

rules.title: N-(이)라는 + Noun: Modification
rules.description: Attach -이라는 to a noun ending in a consonant and -라는 to a noun ending in a vowel, then place it before the noun it modifies. It functions as a relative clause meaning 'called/named X.'

examples.breakdown: 한국인(Korean person) + 이라는(called) + 정체성(identity) + 는(topic particle) + 소중하다(is precious)
examples.english: The identity of being called a Korean is precious.

examples.breakdown: 친구(friend) + 라는(called) + 이름(name) + 으로(as) + 그를(him) + 불렀다(called)
examples.english: I called him by the name of friend.

rules.title: N-(이)라는 것: Nominalization
rules.description: When followed by 것, the phrase becomes a nominalized clause meaning 'the thing/fact that is called X.' This is one of the most common abstract constructions in Korean.

examples.breakdown: 공평(fairness) + 이라는(called) + 것(thing) + 은(topic particle) + 상대적이다(is relative)
examples.english: What is called fairness is relative.

notes.title: All-Purpose Attributive
notes.text: -(이)라는 is the standard, all-purpose attributive form for naming and quoting. It is grammatically productive and appears at every proficiency level, though its abstract nominalizations are advanced.

notes.title: Difference from -(이)란
notes.text: -(이)란 is a literary contraction used for titles, topics, and definitions. -(이)라는 is the full attributive form used for modifying nouns in running text. They are contextually distinct but semantically related.

notes.title: Frequent in Abstract Discourse
notes.text: In TOPIK II essays, -(이)라는 것은/것은 is a key structure for introducing abstract concepts for analysis, comparison, and definition.

explanation: A formal, evaluative relative clause meaning 'what can be called' or 'that which may be termed.' It is used to introduce a noun with cautious qualification, suggesting that the label is appropriate or defensible. Common in academic writing, reviews, and analytical essays.

rules.title: N-(이)라 할 수 있는 + Noun
rules.description: Attach -이라 할 수 있는 to a noun ending in a consonant and -라 할 수 있는 to a noun ending in a vowel. The structure literally means 'a noun that one can call X.' It modifies the following noun with a qualified label.

examples.korean: 그것은 세계 최고라 할 수 있는 작품이다.
examples.breakdown: 그것은(that) + 세계(world) + 최고(best) + 라 할 수 있는(what can be called) + 작품(work) + 이다(is)
examples.english: That is a work that can be called the best in the world.

examples.korean: 이 사건은 대표적인 예라 할 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 이(this) + 사건(incident) + 는(topic particle) + 대표적인(representative) + 예(example) + 라 할 수 있다(can be called)
examples.english: This incident can be called a representative example.

rules.title: Standalone Usage with 있다
rules.description: The phrase can also end a sentence with 이/가 있다, meaning 'there is something that can be called X.'

examples.korean: 완벽한 해결책이라 할 수 있는 것은 없다.
examples.breakdown: 완벽한(perfect) + 해결책(solution) + 이라 할 수 있는(what can be called) + 것(thing) + 은(topic particle) + 없다(does not exist)
examples.english: There is nothing that can be called a perfect solution.

notes.title: Epistemic Hedge
notes.text: -라 할 수 있는 functions as an epistemic hedge. It softens the assertion by framing the label as a reasoned judgment rather than an absolute fact.

notes.title: Academic Register
notes.text: This construction is highly characteristic of academic, critical, and journalistic writing where authors must qualify their claims precisely.

notes.title: Antonym: -라 할 수 없는
notes.text: The direct negative counterpart is N-(이)라 할 수 없는 (what cannot be called). Both forms are often taught together as a polarity pair.

explanation: The negative counterpart to -(이)라 할 수 있는, meaning 'what cannot be called' or 'that which may not be termed.' It is used to disqualify a label or to state that something fails to meet the criteria for a particular designation. Essential for critical analysis and refutation in formal writing.

rules.title: N-(이)라 할 수 없는 + Noun
rules.description: Attach -이라 할 수 없는 to a noun ending in a consonant and -라 할 수 없는 to a noun ending in a vowel. 할 is the contracted form of 할. This modifies the following noun with a negated, disqualified label.

examples.korean: 그것은 정답이라 할 수 없는 해석이다.
examples.breakdown: 그것은(that) + 정답(correct answer) + 이라 할 수 없는(what cannot be called) + 해석(interpretation) + 이다(is)
examples.english: That is an interpretation that cannot be called the correct answer.

examples.breakdown: 이(this) + 를(object particle) + 우연(coincidence) + 이라 할 수 없다(cannot be called)
examples.english: This cannot be called a coincidence.

rules.title: Pronunciation Note: 할
rules.description: 할 is the contracted pronunciation/spelling of 할 in this specific idiom. It is not standard in other contexts. When reading aloud, it is pronounced [hal] just like 할.

examples.breakdown: 완벽하다(perfect) + 라 할 수 없는(what cannot be called) + 결과물(result) + 이다(is)
examples.english: It is a result that cannot be called perfect.

notes.title: 할 is a Contraction
notes.text: Note the spelling: 할 (with ㅐ) is the contracted form of 할. It appears exclusively in this idiom and a few related set phrases. Do not use it as a general substitute for 할.

notes.title: Critical / Refutative Function
notes.text: This grammar is a key tool for critique, refutation, and negative evaluation in essays and argumentation. It allows the writer to deny a classification explicitly.

notes.title: Full Form Alternative
notes.text: Some writers use the uncontracted form -라 할 수 없는. Both are acceptable, but the contracted form is slightly more idiomatic in fixed expressions.

explanation: A fundamental abstract nominalization meaning 'what is called X is...' It defines, explains, or philosophizes about a concept by turning the named noun into the subject of a definitional sentence. It is the bedrock of expository and analytical prose in advanced Korean.

rules.title: N-(이)라는 것은 + Definition
rules.description: Attach -이라는 것은 to a noun ending in a consonant and -라는 것은 to a noun ending in a vowel. The structure creates a topic/subject that is then defined or explained in the predicate.

examples.breakdown: 성공(success) + 이라는 것은(what is called) + 단순한(simple) + 결과(result) + 가 아니다(is not)
examples.english: What is called success is not a simple result.

examples.korean: 행복이라는 것은 타인과 비교해서 얻는 것이 아니다.
examples.breakdown: 행복(happiness) + 이라는 것은(what is called) + 타인(others) + 과(with) + 비교(comparison) + 해서(by) + 얻(obtain) + 는 것(thing) + 이 아니다(is not)
examples.english: What is called happiness is not something obtained by comparing oneself with others.

rules.title: N-(이)라는 것은 + Clause Ending
rules.description: The predicate following -라는 것은 can be a simple adjective, a noun + 이다, or a complex clause with multiple embedded ideas.

examples.korean: 자유라는 것은 책임 없이는 존재할 수 없다.
examples.breakdown: 자유(freedom) + 라는 것은(what is called) + 책임(responsibility) + 없이는(without) + 존재(exist) + 할 수 없다(cannot)
examples.english: What is called freedom cannot exist without responsibility.

notes.title: Essential Essay Structure
notes.text: This pattern is the single most important definitional structure for TOPIK II writing. Opening an essay with N-(이)라는 것은 immediately signals analytical depth.

notes.title: Variations: -라는 건, -라는 거
notes.text: In spoken or informal writing, -라는 건 and -라는 거 are used. The full form -라는 것은 is required for formal essays and official prose.

notes.title: Frequently Paired Verbs
notes.text: Commonly followed by 이다, 아니다, 의미하다, 뜻하다, 의미하는 바이다, and complex clause endings like -기 위한 과정이다.

notes.title: Subject Marker Flexibility
notes.text: While -는 is standard, -은 is also possible after 것: -라는 것은 vs. -라는 것은. Both are acceptable; -는 is more common in modern usage.

explanation: A formal, legalistic expression meaning 'on the grounds that,' 'under the pretext of,' or 'by claiming that.' It introduces a reason or justification, often with the implication that the reason is insufficient, unfair, or fabricated. It is critical for critical writing, legal contexts, and news analysis.

rules.title: N-(이)라는 이유로 + Consequence
rules.description: Attach -이라는 이유로 to a noun ending in a consonant and -라는 이유로 to a noun ending in a vowel. The noun represents the stated reason, and the following clause describes the action or discrimination that resulted from it.

examples.breakdown: 그는(he) + 외국인(foreigner) + 이라는 이유로(on the grounds that) + 차별(discrimination) + 을(object particle) + 받았다(received)
examples.english: He was discriminated against on the grounds that he is a foreigner.

examples.breakdown: 경험(experience) + 이(subject particle) + 없다(be absent) + 라는 이유로(on the grounds that) + 지원(application) + 이(subject particle) + 거부되었다(was rejected)
examples.english: The application was rejected on the grounds that there is no experience.

rules.title: Implied Critique or Objection
rules.description: The grammar often carries a critical nuance: the reason given is presented as unfair or as a pretext. This makes it powerful for argumentative and journalistic writing.

examples.breakdown: 날씨(weather) + 가(subject particle) + 나쁘(bad) + 다는 이유로(on the grounds that) + 약속(promise) + 을(object particle) + 어겼다(broke)
examples.english: He broke his promise under the pretext of bad weather.

notes.title: Critical / Negative Bias
notes.text: While grammatically neutral, -라는 이유로 often implies that the reason is a flimsy excuse or an unjust basis for action. Context determines the strength of this implication.

notes.title: Formal / Legal Register
notes.text: This pattern is standard in legal documents, news reports, and formal complaints. It is too heavy for casual conversation; use ~때문에 or ~라서 instead.

notes.title: Full Form: -(이)라는 이유로
notes.text: The full form uses -라는 이유로. Do not confuse with -라는 이유는 (the reason that is called X is...), which is a different structure with a topic marker.

notes.title: Related Structures
notes.text: Closely related forms include -라는 명분으로 (under the pretext/name of), -라는 구실로 (under the excuse of), and -라는 핑계로 (under the excuse of).

explanation: An advanced, formal connector used to reveal the surface justification someone claims for an action, often implying a hidden or ulterior motive. It translates roughly as 'under the pretext of' or 'in the name of.' Because 명분 carries a rhetorical weight, this expression appears frequently in editorials, political commentary, and literary prose where the speaker wishes to expose a discrepancy between stated reasons and real intentions.

rules.title: Basic Attachment
rules.description: Attach to a noun. Use 이라는 after a noun ending in a 받침 (consonant) and 라는 after a noun ending in a vowel or the consonant ㄹ. The noun represents the stated reason or title being exploited.

examples.korean: 회사는 구조 조정이라는 명분으로 수백 명의 직원을 해고했다.
examples.breakdown: 회사는 (the company) 구조 조정 (restructuring) 이라는 명분으로 (under the pretext of) 수백 명의 직원을 (hundreds of employees) 해고했다 (fired).
examples.english: The company fired hundreds of employees under the pretext of restructuring.

examples.korean: 정부는 안전 점검이라는 명분으로 시장을 폐쇄했다.
examples.breakdown: 정부는 (the government) 안전 점검 (safety inspection) 이라는 명분으로 (under the pretext of) 시장을 (the market) 폐쇄했다 (closed down).
examples.english: The government shut down the market under the pretext of a safety inspection.

rules.title: Implied Criticism
rules.description: The grammar naturally carries a critical nuance. It is used when the speaker believes the stated reason is merely a cover for a less acceptable motive. It pairs well with verbs of restriction, punishment, or forced change.

examples.korean: 그는 개인적인 복수라는 명분으로 동료들을 모함했다.
examples.breakdown: 그는 (he) 개인적인 복수 (personal revenge) 라는 명분으로 (under the pretext of) 동료들을 (colleagues) 모함했다 (framed).
examples.english: He framed his colleagues under the pretext of personal revenge.

notes.title: Negative Connotation
notes.text: Although 명분 itself is neutral ('justification'), the pattern N-(이)라는 명분으로 is overwhelmingly used in contexts where the justification is viewed as a pretext. Using it in a genuinely positive sentence may sound odd.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Frequently paired with nouns like 구조 조정 (restructuring), 안전 (safety), 비용 절감 (cost reduction), 개혁 (reform), and 합의 (agreement).

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: This pattern belongs to advanced formal Korean. It is common in news articles, academic essays, and formal speeches, but rarely in casual conversation.

explanation: A formal expression meaning 'under the excuse that.' While structurally parallel to N-(이)라는 명분으로, 핑계로 uses the more explicitly negative noun 핑계 (excuse), making the speaker's disapproval even clearer. It is used to criticize someone for using a flimsy or fabricated reason to avoid responsibility or justify an action.

rules.title: Basic Attachment
rules.description: Attach 이라는 to nouns ending in a 받침 and 라는 to nouns without one. 핑계로 always follows. The noun provides the specific excuse being criticized.

examples.korean: 그는 몸이 아프다는 핑계로 회의에 참석하지 않았다.
examples.breakdown: 그는 (he) 몸이 아프다 (I am sick) 는 핑계로 (under the excuse that) 회의에 (to the meeting) 참석하지 않았다 (did not attend).
examples.english: He didn't attend the meeting under the excuse that he was sick.

examples.korean: 애들은 숙제가 많다는 핑계로 청소를 피했다.
examples.breakdown: 애들은 (the kids) 숙제가 많다 (there is a lot of homework) 는 핑계로 (under the excuse that) 청소를 (cleaning) 피했다 (avoided).
examples.english: The kids avoided cleaning under the excuse that they had a lot of homework.

rules.title: Criticizing Evasion
rules.description: This grammar strongly implies that the excuse is untrue or insufficient. It is often used when recounting someone's attempt to escape an obligation.

examples.korean: 상대방은 통화 중이라는 핑계로 약속을 어겼다.
examples.breakdown: 상대방은 (the other party) 통화 중 (on a call) 이라는 핑계로 (under the excuse of being) 약속을 (the promise) 어겼다 (broke).
examples.english: The other party broke the promise under the excuse of being on a call.

notes.title: Stronger Negativity than 명분으로
notes.text: While 명분 implies a 'pretext,' 핑계 is closer to 'lame excuse.' Use 핑계로 when you want to convey clear disdain for the reason given.

notes.title: Verb Compatibility
notes.text: Commonly followed by verbs like 피하다 (avoid), 빠지다 (get out of), 거절하다 (refuse), 회피하다 (evade), and 어기다 (break).

notes.title: Colloquial vs. Written
notes.text: Although 핑계 itself is common in spoken Korean, the full pattern N-(이)라는 핑계로 is more characteristic of written narratives and formal complaints.

explanation: An advanced conditional pattern attached to nouns, expressing that if something is limited to the stated noun or level, a negative result follows. It conveys inadequacy, insufficiency, or impossibility. The sentence that follows almost always contains a negative evaluation, such as 부족하다, 안 되다, 힘들다, or 불가능하다. It is characteristic of formal argumentative and evaluative prose.

rules.title: Basic Attachment
rules.description: Attach 이라서는 to nouns ending in a 받침 and 라서는 to nouns ending in a vowel or ㄹ. The following clause states why the current state is insufficient.

examples.korean: 이 정도 실력이라서는 국제 대회에서 상을 받기 힘들 것이다.
examples.breakdown: 이 정도 (this level of) 실력 (skill) 이라서는 (if it is) 국제 대회에서 (at an international competition) 상을 (a prize) 받기 (receiving) 힘들 것이다 (will be difficult).
examples.english: If your skill is only at this level, it will be difficult to win a prize at an international competition.

examples.korean: 지금까지의 경험만이라서는 이 직무를 수행하기에 부족하다.
examples.breakdown: 지금까지의 (up to now) 경험만 (only experience) 이라서는 (if it is only) 이 직무를 (this task) 수행하기에 (to perform) 부족하다 (insufficient).
examples.english: If it is only the experience gained so far, it is insufficient to perform this task.

rules.title: Evaluative Contrast
rules.description: Often used to contrast a current inadequate state with a higher standard that is required. It can set up a subsequent clause explaining what additional conditions are necessary.

examples.korean: 이 정도 준비라서는 시험에 합격할 수 없다.
examples.breakdown: 이 정도 (this level of) 준비 (preparation) 라서는 (if it is) 시험에 (the exam) 합격할 수 없다 (cannot pass).
examples.english: If this is the extent of the preparation, one cannot pass the exam.

notes.title: Negative Clause Required
notes.text: The following clause must contain a negative assessment. Using a positive result clause sounds grammatically and semantically unnatural.

notes.title: Common Pairings
notes.text: Frequently followed by expressions like 부족하다 (insufficient), 안 되다 (not okay), 힘들다 (difficult), 어렵다 (hard), 불가능하다 (impossible), and 모자라다 (lacking).

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: This pattern is almost exclusively used in formal speech and writing, especially in evaluations, critiques, and academic discussions.

notes.title: Noun Modifiers Allowed
notes.text: The noun can be modified by adjectives or relative clauses, e.g., 이렇게 얕은 지식이라서는 (if knowledge is this shallow).

explanation: An emphatic conditional pattern meaning 'only if it is N.' It restricts the condition to a very specific identity, standard, or name, implying that anything less would not suffice. The grammar conveys exclusivity and high standards, and is common in formal evaluations, comparisons, and discussions of qualification. It carries a slightly literary or rhetorical flavor.

rules.title: Basic Attachment
rules.description: Attach 이라야 to nouns ending in a 받침 and 라야 to nouns ending in a vowel or ㄹ. The following clause presents the unique privilege, ability, or outcome reserved for that specific noun.

examples.korean: 세계적인 석학이라야 이런 연구를 이끌 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 세계적인 (world-class) 석학 (scholar) 이라야 (only if one is) 이런 (this kind of) 연구를 (research) 이끌 수 있다 (can lead).
examples.english: Only if one is a world-class scholar can one lead this kind of research.

examples.korean: 원조 맛집이라야 이런 대접을 받는 것이다.
examples.breakdown: 원조 (original) 맛집 (famous restaurant) 이라야 (only if it is) 이런 (this kind of) 대접을 (treatment) 받는 것이다 (receives).
examples.english: Only if it is the original famous restaurant does it receive this kind of treatment.

rules.title: Emphatic Restriction
rules.description: The pattern can also be used to dismiss lesser alternatives. By asserting that 'only N qualifies,' the speaker elevates the noun to a unique status and implicitly criticizes anything that falls short.

examples.korean: 유명 대학이라야 취업이 보장되는 것은 아니다.
examples.breakdown: 유명 대학 (a famous university) 이라야 (even if it is) 취업이 (employment) 보장되는 (guaranteed) 것은 아니다 (is not necessarily).
examples.english: Even if it is a famous university, employment is not guaranteed.

notes.title: Exclusivity
notes.text: The grammar strongly signals that the condition applies to N and almost nothing else. It is a rhetorical tool for highlighting uniqueness.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Often used with nouns denoting status or identity: 국가대표 (national representative), 원조 (original), 세계적인 (world-class), 유명한 (famous), 최고의 (the best).

notes.title: Subtle Nuance Shift
notes.text: In some contexts, it can soften to mean 'if it is going to be N at all,' as in 밥이라야 밥이지 (If it's rice, it should at least be rice), implying minimum standards.

explanation: A formal, enumerative connector attached to nouns, meaning 'whether it is N or...' It is used to list two or more items as representative examples from a broader set. The pattern often appears in pairs—N1-(이)라든지 N2-(이)라든지—but a single instance can also introduce an open list. It carries a slightly bookish, deliberative tone and is common in legal, academic, and expository writing.

rules.title: Single Listing
rules.description: Attach 이라든지 to a noun with a 받침 and 라든지 to a noun without one. A single use suggests an open-ended example, inviting the listener to imagine other similar items.

examples.breakdown: 책 (books) 이라든지 (whether it is) 공부에 (for studying) 필요한 (necessary) 물품을 (items) 준비하세요 (please prepare).
examples.english: Please prepare books or other items necessary for studying.

rules.title: Paired Listing
rules.description: When used in pairs, the grammar creates a comprehensive list of alternatives. Both nouns receive the same ending. The final clause applies to all listed items equally.

examples.korean: 영화라든지 음악이라든지 예술 분야에 관심이 많다.
examples.breakdown: 영화 (movies) 라든지 (whether it is) 음악 (music) 이라든지 (whether it is) 예술 분야에 (in the arts) 관심이 많다 (has a lot of interest).
examples.english: Whether it is movies or music, I have a lot of interest in the arts.

examples.korean: 한식이라든지 양식이라든지 다양한 음식을 판매하고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 한식 (Korean food) 이라든지 (whether it is) 양식 (Western food) 이라든지 (whether it is) 다양한 (various) 음식을 (foods) 판매하고 있다 (are selling).
examples.english: Whether it is Korean food or Western food, they sell a variety of cuisines.

notes.title: Interchangeable with 라든가
notes.text: N-(이)라든지 and N-(이)라든가 share the same core meaning. 라든지 is slightly more common in written prose, while 라든가 can feel marginally softer.

notes.title: Not Restricted to Two Items
notes.text: Although often used in pairs, three or more items can be chained: A라든지 B라든지 C라든지.

notes.title: Avoid in Very Casual Speech
notes.text: While understandable, this pattern sounds formal. In casual conversation, native speakers more commonly use (이)나 or (이)든지 without the quoted 라.

explanation: A formal enumerative pattern functionally identical to N-(이)라든지, meaning 'whether it is N or...' It lists representative examples from a set of possibilities. The tone is expository and slightly softer than 라든지, though both belong to the formal register. It is frequently encountered in written explanations, survey questions, and broad categorical statements.

rules.title: Basic Listing
rules.description: Attach 이라든가 to nouns ending in a 받침 and 라든가 to nouns without one. It introduces one or more examples without claiming the list is exhaustive.

examples.korean: 울림이라든가 반향 같은 음향 효과를 사용했다.
examples.breakdown: 울림 (reverberation) 이라든가 (whether it is) 반향 (echo) 같은 (like) 음향 효과를 (sound effects) 사용했다 (used).
examples.english: They used sound effects like reverberation or echo.

examples.korean: 축구라든가 야구라든가 단체 운전에 능한 사람을 뽑는다.
examples.breakdown: 축구 (soccer) 라든가 (whether it is) 야구 (baseball) 라든가 (whether it is) 단체 운전 (team driving) 에 능한 (skilled at) 사람을 (people) 뽑는다 (selects).
examples.english: They select people who are skilled at team sports, whether it is soccer or baseball.

rules.title: Open-Ended Enumeration
rules.description: When only one instance of 라든가 appears, it signals that the noun is merely one possible example among many. This usage is common when giving suggestions or describing categories.

examples.breakdown: 취미 (hobbies) 라든가 (whether it is) 특기 (special skills) 를 (object marker) 자유롭게 (freely) 적어 주십시오 (please write).
examples.english: Please freely write down your hobbies or special skills.

notes.title: Synonymy with 라든지
notes.text: In most contexts, 라든가 and 라든지 are interchangeable. Native intuition may slightly prefer one over the other based on rhythm or regional habit, but there is no meaning difference.

notes.title: Written Preference
notes.text: Like 라든지, this pattern is more at home in writing and formal speech than in everyday conversation.

notes.title: Pairing with 뭐
notes.text: In slightly more colloquial formal contexts, you may hear 뭐 (what/something) used in the second slot: A라든가 뭐라든가.

explanation: A comprehensive, formal quantifier meaning 'regardless of whether it is N or anything else.' It dismisses distinctions and asserts that a statement applies universally across categories. The grammar is emphatic and inclusive, conveying that no exceptions exist. It is characteristic of official announcements, legal language, and forceful rhetorical statements.

rules.title: Basic Attachment
rules.description: Attach 이나 to nouns ending in a 받침 and 나 to nouns without one, followed by 할 것 없이. The noun represents a category whose internal distinctions are being erased.

examples.korean: 성별이나 할 것 없이 누구나 지원할 수 있다.
examples.breakdown: 성별 (gender) 이나 할 것 없이 (regardless of) 누구나 (anyone) 지원할 수 있다 (can apply).
examples.english: Regardless of gender, anyone can apply.

examples.korean: 직업이나 할 것 없이 모두 보험에 가입해야 한다.
examples.breakdown: 직업 (occupation) 이나 할 것 없이 (regardless of) 모두 (everyone) 보험에 (insurance) 가입해야 한다 (must enroll).
examples.english: Regardless of occupation, everyone must enroll in insurance.

rules.title: Emphatic Universality
rules.description: The pattern can be strengthened by following it with extreme or comprehensive terms like 모두 (all), 누구나 (anyone), or 전부 (everything), reinforcing the idea of zero exceptions.

examples.korean: 학력이나 할 것 없이 실력으로만 평가받는다.
examples.breakdown: 학력 (academic background) 이나 할 것 없이 (regardless of) 실력으로만 (only by ability) 평가받는다 (is evaluated).
examples.english: Regardless of academic background, one is evaluated solely on ability.

notes.title: Register
notes.text: This is a heavy, formal expression. Using it in casual conversation can sound pompous or bureaucratic.

notes.title: Interchangeable Alternatives
notes.text: In less formal contexts, speakers often use (이)나 마찬가지로 or simply (이)나 to express similar inclusivity, though without the same emphatic weight.

notes.title: Common Nouns
notes.text: Frequently paired with classifying nouns such as 성별 (gender), 나이 (age), 직업 (occupation), 학력 (education), 국적 (nationality), and 지위 (status).

explanation: A metaphorical equivalence pattern meaning 'it is the same as N' or 'it amounts to N.' It asserts that a situation, action, or state is functionally identical to the noun, often with a critical or resigned undertone. Because 마찬가지 (the same) is a noun, the entire construction predicate ends with 이다. This grammar is common in editorial and evaluative discourse.

rules.title: Basic Equivalence
rules.description: Attach 이나 to nouns ending in a 받침 and 나 to nouns without one, followed by 마찬가지다. The subject of the sentence is equated to the noun in a metaphorical sense.

examples.breakdown: 지금 (now) 포기하는 (giving up) 것은 (thing) 실패 (failure) 나 마찬가지다 (is the same as).
examples.english: Giving up now is the same as failure.

examples.breakdown: 그의 (his) 태도는 (attitude) 거절 (refusal) 이나 마찬가지였다 (was the same as).
examples.english: His attitude was practically the same as a refusal.

rules.title: Critical Evaluation
rules.description: The pattern is frequently used to criticize an action by elevating it to a more serious category. It reveals the speaker's judgment that a superficially different act is fundamentally identical to something negative.

examples.korean: 이런 식으로 일하면 해고당하는 것이나 마찬가지다.
examples.breakdown: 이런 식으로 (in this way) 일하면 (if one works) 해고당하는 (being fired) 것이나 마찬가지다 (is the same as).
examples.english: Working this way is practically the same as being fired.

notes.title: Negative Bias
notes.text: Although technically neutral, this pattern most often equates a situation to something negative. Positive equivalences are possible but less frequent.

notes.title: Tense Flexibility
notes.text: 마찬가지다 can be conjugated for tense: 마찬가지였다 (past), 마찬가지일 것이다 (future). The noun attachment remains unchanged.

notes.title: Common Pairings
notes.text: Often used with nouns like 포기 (giving up), 실패 (failure), 거절 (refusal), 자살 (suicide), 도박 (gambling), and 기만 (deception).

explanation: A comparative pattern meaning 'just like N' or 'in the same manner as N.' It draws a direct parallel between two entities, emphasizing shared characteristics, behaviors, or outcomes. Unlike N-(이)나 마찬가지다, which ends a sentence with an equivalence, N과/와 마찬가지로 functions adverbially, setting up the following clause. It is standard in both formal and semi-formal registers.

rules.title: Basic Attachment
rules.description: Attach 과 to nouns ending in a 받침 and 와 to nouns without one, followed by 마찬가지로. The noun serves as the reference point for comparison.

examples.korean: 그는 형과 마찬가지로 음악에 재능이 있었다.
examples.breakdown: 그는 (he) 형 (older brother) 과 마찬가지로 (just like) 음악에 (in music) 재능이 (talent) 있었다 (had).
examples.english: Just like his older brother, he had a talent for music.

examples.korean: 이번 사태는 과거와 마찬가지로 반복되고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 이번 (this) 사태는 (situation) 과거 (the past) 와 마찬가지로 (just like) 반복되고 있다 (is repeating).
examples.english: This situation is repeating just like in the past.

rules.title: Parallel Structure
rules.description: The pattern naturally sets up a parallel between two subjects performing the same action or possessing the same quality. It is useful for analogies and systematic comparisons.

examples.korean: 외국인과 마찬가지로 모든 신입생은 입학 교육을 받아야 한다.
examples.breakdown: 외국인 (foreigners) 과 마찬가지로 (just like) 모든 (all) 신입생은 (new students) 입학 교육을 (orientation) 받아야 한다 (must receive).
examples.english: Just like foreigners, all new students must attend orientation.

notes.title: Adverbial Function
notes.text: Because it ends in 로, this grammar modifies the following predicate rather than serving as the sentence predicate itself. The main clause contains the actual verb.

notes.title: Common Verbs
notes.text: Frequently followed by verbs like 생각하다 (think), 행동하다 (act), 변하다 (change), 반복되다 (repeat), and 작용하다 (function).

notes.title: Neutral Tone
notes.text: Unlike N-(이)나 마찬가지다, this pattern is emotionally neutral and simply establishes similarity. It is appropriate for academic, journalistic, and everyday contexts.

explanation: A formal contrastive pattern meaning 'in contrast to N.' It introduces a sharp difference between two entities, situations, or periods. The grammar is analytical and objective, making it well-suited for academic writing, comparative reports, and critical essays. It typically appears at the beginning of a sentence or clause to pivot from one subject to its opposite.

rules.title: Basic Attachment
rules.description: Attach 과는 to nouns ending in a 받침 and 와는 to nouns without one, followed by 대조적으로. The noun represents the first element of the contrast.

examples.breakdown: 작년 (last year) 과는 대조적으로 (in contrast to) 올해는 (this year) 매출이 (revenue) 급감했다 (plummeted).
examples.english: In contrast to last year, revenue plummeted this year.

examples.korean: 동양 철학과는 대조적으로 서양 철학은 이성을 중시한다.
examples.breakdown: 동양 철학 (Eastern philosophy) 과는 대조적으로 (in contrast to) 서양 철학 (Western philosophy) 은 (topic marker) 이성을 (reason) 중시한다 (values).
examples.english: In contrast to Eastern philosophy, Western philosophy values reason.

rules.title: Thematic Contrast
rules.description: The pattern can set up a sustained contrast across an entire paragraph. After introducing the contrasting noun, the writer elaborates on multiple dimensions of difference.

examples.korean: 이론과는 대조적으로 실제 현장에서는 많은 문제가 발생했다.
examples.breakdown: 이론 (theory) 과는 대조적으로 (in contrast to) 실제 현장에서는 (in the actual field) 많은 (many) 문제가 (problems) 발생했다 (occurred).
examples.english: In contrast to theory, many problems occurred in the actual field.

notes.title: Objective Register
notes.text: 대조적으로 is more analytical and less emotional than 반대로. It is preferred in academic and professional writing where neutrality is valued.

notes.title: Position Flexibility
notes.text: The phrase can appear sentence-initially or after a topicalized subject: 올해는 작년과는 대조적으로...

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Frequently paired with temporal nouns (작년, 과거) or categorical nouns (동양, 서양, 이론, 현실) to highlight systematic differences.

explanation: A contrastive pattern meaning 'contrary to N' or 'opposite to N.' While similar to N과/와는 대조적으로, 반대로 carries a stronger sense of direct opposition and can feel slightly more emphatic or even confrontational. It is used when two things move in opposite directions, hold opposite positions, or produce opposite results. It is common in argumentation, refutations, and pointed comparisons.

rules.title: Basic Attachment
rules.description: Attach 과는 to nouns ending in a 받침 and 와는 to nouns without one, followed by 반대로. The noun establishes the position or expectation being contradicted.

examples.breakdown: 예상 (expectation) 과는 반대로 (contrary to) 경기는 (the economy) 호전되었다 (improved).
examples.english: Contrary to expectations, the economy improved.

examples.korean: 부모의 뜻과는 반대로 그는 예술가의 길을 걸었다.
examples.breakdown: 부모의 (parents') 뜻 (wishes) 과는 반대로 (contrary to) 그는 (he) 예술가의 (artist's) 길을 (path) 걸었다 (walked).
examples.english: Contrary to his parents' wishes, he walked the path of an artist.

rules.title: Direct Opposition
rules.description: Use this grammar when the contrast involves a reversal of direction, intention, or outcome. It naturally pairs with result clauses that overturn an initial assumption.

examples.korean: 세계 추세와는 반대로 이 나라는 출산율이 상승하고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 세계 추세 (global trend) 와는 반대로 (contrary to) 이 나라는 (this country) 출산율이 (birth rate) 상승하고 있다 (is rising).
examples.english: Contrary to the global trend, this country's birth rate is rising.

notes.title: Stronger than 대조적으로
notes.text: While 대조적으로 implies difference, 반대로 implies opposition or reversal. Use 반대로 when you want to emphasize that something runs directly against something else.

notes.title: Fixed Expressions
notes.text: Common collocations include 예상과는 반대로 (contrary to expectations), 뜻과는 반대로 (contrary to one's wishes), and 흐름과는 반대로 (against the flow).

notes.title: Adverbial Function
notes.text: Like 대조적으로, this pattern functions adverbially and modifies the following clause. The main predicate contains the actual contrastive result.

explanation: A formal, honorific organizational pattern meaning 'led by N' or 'with N at the head.' It designates a leader or primary figure and indicates that a group follows or acts under that person's direction. The grammar carries a ceremonial, official tone and is standard in news reports, press releases, and formal invitations where hierarchy must be explicitly acknowledged.

rules.title: Basic Attachment
rules.description: Attach 을 to nouns ending in a 받침 and 를 to nouns without one, followed by 필두로. The noun is a person or organization occupying the leading position.

examples.korean: 회장을 필두로 임원진이 방문 일정을 논의했다.
examples.breakdown: 회장 (chairman) 을 필두로 (led by) 임원진 (executives) 이 (subject marker) 방문 일정을 (visit schedule) 논의했다 (discussed).
examples.english: Led by the chairman, the executives discussed the visit schedule.

examples.korean: 선생님을 필두로 학생들이 봉사 활동을 시작했다.
examples.breakdown: 선생님 (teacher) 을 필두로 (led by) 학생들이 (students) 봉사 활동을 (volunteer activity) 시작했다 (started).
examples.english: Led by the teacher, the students started the volunteer activity.

rules.title: Extended Usage
rules.description: The pattern can be followed by 하여 or 하고 to create a more explicit participial phrase, though 필두로 alone is sufficient. It can also list multiple leaders.

examples.korean: 대표를 필두로 한 연구팀이 논문을 발표했다.
examples.breakdown: 대표 (representative) 를 필두로 한 (led by) 연구팀이 (research team) 논문을 (paper) 발표했다 (presented).
examples.english: A research team led by the representative presented the paper.

notes.title: Honorific Nuance
notes.text: 필두 (頭) literally means 'head' in a ceremonial sense. The grammar automatically confers respect on the noun, making it unsuitable for describing disreputable leaders in formal contexts.

notes.title: Common Domains
notes.text: Widely used in political reporting (대통령을 필두로), corporate announcements (CEO를 필두로), academic contexts (교수를 필두로), and diplomatic events (대사를 필두로).

notes.title: Participial Form
notes.text: When modifying a following noun, use 필두로 한: 대통령을 필두로 한 대표단 (a delegation led by the president).

explanation: A sequential, enumerative pattern meaning 'starting with N.' It marks the first item in a series and implies that additional items follow in a natural or logical order. The grammar is formal and organizational, making it ideal for event programs, chronological descriptions, and structured lists. It conveys that the named noun is the beginning of a continuum.

rules.title: Basic Attachment
rules.description: Attach 을 to nouns ending in a 받침 and 를 to nouns without one, followed by 시작으로. The noun is the first element in a temporal or spatial sequence.

examples.breakdown: 개회식 (opening ceremony) 을 시작으로 (starting with) 행사가 (event) 본격적으로 (in full swing) 진행되었다 (proceeded).
examples.english: Starting with the opening ceremony, the event proceeded in full swing.

examples.korean: 서울을 시작으로 전국 순회 공연이 펼쳐진다.
examples.breakdown: 서울 (Seoul) 을 시작으로 (starting with) 전국 (nationwide) 순회 공연이 (tour performance) 펼쳐진다 (unfolds).
examples.english: Starting with Seoul, a nationwide tour performance unfolds.

rules.title: Series Implication
rules.description: The pattern strongly implies continuation. It is most natural when the speaker intends to mention or allude to subsequent items in the series.

examples.korean: 이번 달 급여 인상을 시작으로 임금 체계가 개편될 예정이다.
examples.breakdown: 이번 달 (this month) 급여 인상 (salary increase) 을 시작으로 (starting with) 임금 체계가 (wage system) 개편될 예정이다 (is scheduled to be reformed).
examples.english: Starting with this month's salary increase, the wage system is scheduled to be reformed.

notes.title: Temporal Focus
notes.text: While primarily temporal, the pattern can occasionally describe spatial sequences, especially in itineraries and logistics.

notes.title: Common Verbs
notes.text: Frequently followed by 진행되다 (proceed), 펼쳐지다 (unfold), 시작되다 (begin), 개최되다 (be held), and 이어지다 (continue).

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: This pattern is standard in official notices, press releases, and event descriptions. In casual speech, 처음으로 or 먼저 are more common.

explanation: A temporal sequence pattern meaning 'following N' or 'subsequent to N.' It indicates that one event, action, or period occurs immediately after another, often with a sense of natural succession or continuation. The grammar is formal and expository, commonly used in historical narratives, procedural descriptions, and chronologies where the order of events must be precisely marked.

rules.title: Basic Attachment
rules.description: Attach 에 이어서 directly to a noun representing a prior event, period, or action. The following clause describes what happens next.

examples.korean: 개막식에 이어서 본 행사가 열릴 예정이다.
examples.breakdown: 개막식 (opening ceremony) 에 이어서 (following) 본 행사가 (main event) 열릴 예정이다 (is scheduled to open).
examples.english: Following the opening ceremony, the main event is scheduled to take place.

examples.breakdown: 1차 시험 (first round exam) 에 이어서 (following) 2차 면접 (second round interview) 이 (subject marker) 진행된다 (is conducted).
examples.english: Following the first round exam, the second round interview is conducted.

rules.title: Continuity and Expansion
rules.description: The pattern can suggest not just temporal succession but also thematic continuation—what follows builds upon or expands what came before.

examples.korean: 이번 연구에 이어서 후속 과제도 진행할 계획이다.
examples.breakdown: 이번 (this) 연구 (research) 에 이어서 (following) 후속 과제 (follow-up task) 도 (also) 진행할 (carry out) 계획이다 (plan).
examples.english: Following this research, we also plan to carry out follow-up tasks.

notes.title: Tense Neutrality
notes.text: 에 이어서 itself is tense-neutral. The tense of the sentence is determined by the final verb.

notes.title: Common Nouns
notes.text: Frequently used with event nouns like 개막식 (opening ceremony), 회의 (meeting), 강연 (lecture), 시험 (exam), 휴식 (break), and 전시회 (exhibition).

notes.title: Synonymy with 에 이어
notes.text: 에 이어서 and 에 이어 are nearly identical. 에 이어서 is slightly more common in spoken formal contexts, while 에 이어 is more concise and typical of news headlines.

explanation: A temporal precedence pattern meaning 'prior to N' or 'before N.' It marks an event or action that takes place in advance of a reference point. The grammar is formal and anticipatory, establishing a preparatory phase. It is standard in procedural manuals, formal speeches, and schedules where sequence and preparation must be explicitly stated.

rules.title: Basic Attachment
rules.description: Attach 에 앞서서 directly to a noun representing a subsequent event or milestone. The following clause describes the preparatory or preceding action.

examples.breakdown: 회의 (meeting) 에 앞서서 (prior to) 잠깐 (briefly) 인사말씀을 (greeting words) 드리겠습니다 (I will give).
examples.english: Prior to the meeting, I will briefly offer some words of greeting.

examples.korean: 계약에 앞서서 세부 조건을 검토해야 한다.
examples.breakdown: 계약 (contract) 에 앞서서 (prior to) 세부 (detailed) 조건을 (conditions) 검토해야 한다 (must review).
examples.english: Prior to the contract, we must review the detailed conditions.

rules.title: Preparatory Actions
rules.description: The pattern naturally describes groundwork, preliminary checks, ceremonies, or announcements that set the stage for the main event.

examples.korean: 시험에 앞서서 응시 원칙을 안내해 드리겠습니다.
examples.breakdown: 시험 (exam) 에 앞서서 (prior to) 응시 (test-taking) 원칙을 (principles) 안내해 드리겠습니다 (I will guide you through).
examples.english: Prior to the exam, I will guide you through the test-taking principles.

notes.title: Formal Politeness
notes.text: 에 앞서서 is especially common in honorific and formal contexts. It pairs naturally with humble speech forms like 드리다 and 안내해 드리다.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Frequently paired with nouns like 회의 (meeting), 출발 (departure), 계약 (contract), 시험 (exam), 방문 (visit), and 공연 (performance).

notes.title: Condensed Form
notes.text: In very concise writing, 에 앞서 may appear without the 서. Both are correct, but 에 앞서서 is more common in spoken formal contexts and full prose.

notes.title: Not for General 'Before'
notes.text: For casual 'before' meaning, native speakers use 전에. 에 앞서서 carries a ceremonial, procedural weight that 전에 lacks.

explanation: A formal and literary expression indicating that one event or action occurs prior to another. It is commonly used in official speeches, news reports, academic writing, and formal announcements. The nuance is more elevated than the everyday -기 전에.

rules.title: Preceding an event or time
rules.description: Attach 에 앞서 to a noun denoting an event, action, or temporal point. The following clause describes what happens before that noun occurs.

examples.breakdown: 회의 (meeting) + 에 앞서 (before) → before the meeting
examples.english: Before the meeting, I will briefly greet everyone.

examples.korean: 방송에 앞서 주의 사항을 안내해 드리겠습니다.
examples.breakdown: 방송 (broadcast) + 에 앞서 (before) → before the broadcast
examples.english: Before the broadcast begins, we will guide you through the precautions.

rules.title: With preparatory actions
rules.description: Often used to introduce checks, greetings, or preparations that must happen before the main event.

examples.korean: 출발에 앞서 준비물을 다시 한번 확인합시다.
examples.english: Before departure, let's check the supplies one more time.

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: This pattern is almost exclusively used in formal speech and written Korean. In casual conversation, use -기 전에 instead.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Frequently paired with nouns like 출발, 방송, 회의, 시험, 개봉, 출시, and 발표.

notes.title: Noun Restriction
notes.text: The preceding noun must denote an event, action, or point in time. Abstract concepts without temporal meaning are unnatural.

explanation: A formal, written expression meaning 'following after.' It describes sequential events or people where one follows another in time or order. It carries an elevated, literary tone suitable for news, essays, and official documents.

rules.title: Following an event
rules.description: Attach 에 뒤이어 to a noun representing an event. The following clause describes what comes after it.

examples.breakdown: 1차 발표 (first presentation) + 에 뒤이어 (following after) → following the first presentation
examples.english: Following the first presentation, the second presentation will continue as scheduled.

examples.breakdown: 지진 (earthquake) + 에 뒤이어 (following after) → following the earthquake
examples.english: Following the earthquake, aftershocks are continuing.

rules.title: Following a person
rules.description: Can also follow nouns referring to people, indicating that someone enters or acts after that person.

examples.breakdown: 사장님 (CEO) + 에 뒤이어 (following after) → following the CEO
examples.english: Following the CEO, the vice president entered.

notes.title: Formal Register
notes.text: Used primarily in news reports, official announcements, and formal writing. Avoid in casual conversation.

notes.title: Common Collocations
notes.text: Often used with 순서, 지진, 전쟁, 발표, 공연, and titles of people (사장님, 대통령).

notes.title: Related Patterns
notes.text: Similar in meaning to -은/는 다음에 and -고 나서, but 에 뒤이어 carries a more elevated, literary tone.

explanation: Indicates that a particular event serves as a turning point or catalyst for a significant, often lasting change. It is formal and commonly used in essays, biographies, and news. The resulting change is usually more important than the original event itself.

rules.title: As a turning point
rules.description: Attach 을/를 계기로 to a noun representing an event. The following clause describes the resulting transformation or new state.

examples.breakdown: 유학 (studying abroad) + 를 계기로 (as an opportunity) → taking studying abroad as a turning point
examples.english: He changed his outlook on life, taking studying abroad as a turning point.

examples.korean: 이번 사고를 계기로 안전 규정이 강화되었다.
examples.breakdown: 이번 사고 (this accident) + 를 계기로 (as an opportunity) → taking this accident as a turning point
examples.english: Safety regulations were strengthened, taking this accident as an opportunity.

rules.title: 하여 / 삼아 variants
rules.description: The pattern can be expanded with 하여 or 삼아 to function as a mid-sentence connector or predicate.

examples.korean: 이번 프로젝트를 계기로 하여 새로운 협력 관계가 시작되었다.
examples.breakdown: 프로젝트 (project) + 를 계기로 하여 (taking as an opportunity) → taking this project as an opportunity
examples.english: A new cooperative relationship began, taking this project as an opportunity.

notes.title: Turning Point Nuance
notes.text: Implies a significant, often transformative change. The result is usually more important or lasting than the original event.

notes.title: Positive or Neutral
notes.text: Typically used for positive or neutral changes. For negative turning points, the pattern itself is neutral, but the context determines the tone.

notes.title: Variations
notes.text: N-을/를 계기로 하여 and N-을/를 계기로 삼아 are common variants that add grammatical flexibility.

explanation: Means 'using N as a pretext.' This pattern carries a strongly negative connotation, implying that the subject exploits a situation, event, or condition as a false or exaggerated reason to achieve a hidden, selfish, or harmful goal. Common in political commentary, critical essays, and news.

rules.title: Using as a negative pretext
rules.description: Attach 을/를 빌미로 to a noun representing a situation. The following clause must describe an action that is manipulative or undesirable.

examples.korean: 그는 약속을 빌미로 자주 돈을 빌려 간다.
examples.breakdown: 약속 (appointment) + 을 빌미로 (using as a pretext) → using appointments as a pretext
examples.english: He often borrows money, using appointments as a pretext.

examples.korean: 회사는 경기 불황을 빌미로 인력을 감축했다.
examples.breakdown: 경기 불황 (economic slowdown) + 을 빌미로 (using as a pretext) → using the economic slowdown as a pretext
examples.english: The company reduced its workforce, using the economic slowdown as a pretext.

rules.title: 하여 / 삼아 variants
rules.description: Expand with 하여 or 삼아 to add grammatical flexibility, often making the pretext more explicit.

examples.korean: 그는 병을 빌미로 삼아 회사에 나가지 않았다.
examples.breakdown: 병 (illness) + 을 빌미로 삼아 (using as a pretext) → using illness as a pretext
examples.english: He didn't go to work, using illness as a pretext.

notes.title: Strongly Negative
notes.text: Always carries a negative connotation. The subject uses something as a false or exaggerated reason to achieve a hidden or selfish goal.

notes.title: Not Interchangeable
notes.text: Do not confuse with N-을/를 계기로. While both mean 'using something as a reason,' 빌미로 is malicious, whereas 계기로 is neutral or positive.

notes.title: Common Contexts
notes.text: Frequently appears in political, social, and news contexts: 경기 불황을 빌미로, 테러를 빌미로, 질병을 빌미로.

explanation: Means 'using N as an excuse.' Like 빌미로, this pattern carries a negative connotation, but it often feels slightly more everyday and less politically charged. It implies that the stated reason is not genuine and is being used to avoid responsibility or obligation.

rules.title: Using as a flimsy excuse
rules.description: Attach 을/를 구실로 to a noun representing a reason or condition. The following clause describes an avoidance or evasion.

examples.korean: 그는 배 아픔을 구실로 일을 피했다.
examples.breakdown: 배 아픔 (stomachache) + 을 구실로 (using as an excuse) → using a stomachache as an excuse
examples.english: He avoided work, using a stomachache as an excuse.

examples.korean: 그녀는 약속을 구실로 모임에 나오지 않았다.
examples.breakdown: 약속 (appointment) + 을 구실로 (using as an excuse) → using an appointment as an excuse
examples.english: She didn't come to the gathering, using an appointment as an excuse.

rules.title: 하여 / 삼아 variants
rules.description: Use 하여 or 삼아 to make the pattern function as a predicate or to connect clauses more formally.

examples.korean: 그는 부상을 구실로 삼아 훈련을 빠졌다.
examples.breakdown: 부상 (injury) + 을 구실로 삼아 (using as an excuse) → using an injury as an excuse
examples.english: He skipped training, using an injury as an excuse.

notes.title: Negative Connotation
notes.text: Like 빌미로, this pattern implies the reason given is an excuse. However, 구실로 often feels slightly less severe and more everyday.

notes.title: Distinct from Real Reasons
notes.text: If the reason is genuine, use 때문에 or -(으)로 인해. 구실로 explicitly casts doubt on the sincerity of the excuse.

notes.title: Common Nouns
notes.text: Often paired with 병, 약속, 바쁨, 피로, and 가족 사정 to describe common excuses for avoiding obligations.

explanation: Indicates purpose or aim. It is the formal equivalent of -(으)려고 and -기 위해서, and it is the standard pattern for stating objectives in academic papers, official proposals, reports, and formal speech. It typically collocates with abstract nouns.

rules.title: With the purpose of
rules.description: Attach 을/를 목적으로 to an abstract noun representing a goal. The following clause describes the action taken to achieve it.

examples.korean: 그는 취업을 목적으로 어학원에 다니고 있다.
examples.breakdown: 취업 (getting a job) + 을 목적으로 (with the purpose of) → with the purpose of getting a job
examples.english: He attends a language school with the purpose of getting a job.

examples.korean: 건강 증진을 목적으로 운 동 프로그램이 마련되었다.
examples.breakdown: 건강 증진 (health promotion) + 을 목적으로 (with the purpose of) → with the purpose of health promotion
examples.english: An exercise program was prepared with the purpose of health promotion.

rules.title: As a predicate: 목적으로 하다 / 삼다
rules.description: When the sentence ends with 목적으로 하다 or 목적으로 삼다, it nominalizes the purpose as the defining goal of the subject.

examples.korean: 이 연구는 질병의 원인 규명을 목적으로 한다.
examples.breakdown: 원인 규명 (identifying the cause) + 을 목적으로 한다 (aims to) → aims to identify the cause
examples.english: This research aims to identify the cause of the disease.

notes.title: Formal Purpose
notes.text: The formal equivalent of -(으)려고 and -기 위해서. It is the standard pattern for stating objectives in academic papers, proposals, and official documents.
